title: F is for Formula
content: I have watched a lot of racing movies. They are hard to make. They are even more hard to make into an interesting 2 hour movie. I think the best approach was back in the 1960s and early 1970s, focusing mostly on the race itself and not a lot of claptrap. But of course they were not perfect racing movies. Thin character development diminished the story. Hollywood didn't try another big racing movie until 1990 with "Days of Thunder". I was disappointed with that film because it cut the actual races too short, where real tension could have been developed. It also was just a dumb story with dumb characters."Days of Thunder" was critically panned and unfortunately did not solve the question of what makes a good racing movie. For that reason, no epic sized movie about racing was attempted for a long time. There was "Rush", but did not feature a lot of racing. There was "Ford v Ferrari", which although had a larger scope, and made for a more in-depth film about racing, was not meant to be a big action movie. And the most recent "Ferrari" was never meant to focus on racing itself. The other is "Gran Turismo", based on a video game."F1" attempts to be that big action racing movie. It does flashy action race sequences that reminds you of the flashy action air sequences from Top Gun 2. It is edited with a flurry of different angles, no doubt a nod to the Tony Scott style. It is exciting to a degree, but does not need to be so frenetic all the time. So strike one for the formula approach to formula 1 racing. It is only at the end of the movie we get see a more extended POV shot of the race.Then comes the trope of the pressure from sponsors. The trope of the burned out mentor, the cocky young student, the trope of needing to break the rules to win, and on. There are only tropes in this entire film, with everything feeling superficial and pushed. For instance Kerry Condon is the love interest character played by Jennifer Connelly in the director's previous film Top Gun 2. Clashing with the crew chief, being a renegade, all the formula tropes are here.This film took a long time and a lot of money to make. There really was a quest to make a racing movie the right way. Yet it still doesn't feel grounded, it doesn't transcend being more than a spectacle movie. I like racing, in fact in an odd coincidence, I had just spent an extended period of time zipping around go-cart tracks a couple days before I saw this film. Some years back I got to meet famous drag racing drivers during competitions. I have a few good friends that are motorheads.Whether they initially wanted to make this movie more visceral at the beginning, the producers may have felt the need to sacrifice 'heart' for a broader demographic. Homogenized side characters can't emote beyond the obvious cliché reactions. The caring Mother accuses the reckless Mentor of putting her rookie driver son in danger. The overly eager agent. The sour mouthed guy in charge.I think there was a lot of compromise in order to attract a larger audience because of the enormous budget necessary to make an epic sized racing film like this. So maybe the perfect racing film is really impossible to make. For instance, exposition is fed to us via dry delivery by supporting characters, or by a race announcer, or member of the press, describing all the important moments in a spoon fed fashion. Yet how else can the audience really get to know the stakes involved in condensed racing sequences? A lot of drama has to be rushed through for the race to be meaningful. All that drama needs to be explained, so you are stuck.So I can't fault the formula moviemaking for a big action film that is at the mercy of the producers who are putting their money on the line. But ultimately the movie's 2 hour and 30 minute running time unnecessarily adds padding. There are no Academy award performances here. So knowing this, I think somewhere between the short and superficial "Days of Thunder", which clocked in at 108 minutes, and a more epic racing movie that clocks in at 155 minutes is a better 2 hour racing movie.The excellent racing sequences are dynamic, and I never got tired of the multiple race sequences peppered throughout the film. But the narratives of the mentor and rookie, the guy under financial pressure, the stereotypical group members and their difficulties to overcome, the "paste love interest here", and so on, diminished the film as a whole. It resembles the "Days of Thunder" piece of product with better looking racing sequences.

title: I love F1! But ...
content: Ok, so I'm a huge F1 fan. I've been following it since I knew it exists, I wrote for several F1 websites and I simply love everything about it!But ....I was so looking forward to this movie, so much that I got an Apple TV subscription just to watch this movie .... and then I watched it ....Brad is, without a doubt, a good actor, Javier is an even better actor, loved Kerry and Damson is just a young aspiring actor who had to act like he thinks he's all that and immortal up to an annoying level.But ....It wasn't what I expected, it did not grip me, it did not made me sit on the edge of my couch in full excitement. The camerawork was good but all the hype around it made me think it was going to be NEXT LEVEL, but to me, it wasn't. The only thing I saw that was new was a camera that moved it's filming angle 180 degrees, the rest of the camera work ... not new and not that exciting.The story ... ok, nostalgic, an ex F1 driver that was once on top of his game and then 'lost it all' and gets a 2nd chance .... cute but not new.I don't, it was fun to spend 2,5+ hours of my night while the family ( not interested in F1 ) was sleeping, but it's not the type of movie that I will watch again.Brad's supposedly 30+ million USD pay for this movie seems a bit over the top.7/10.

title: Basic Ingredients, no Adventure
content: I have to admit I walked into this film with much higher expectations, only to leave feeling thoroughly disappointed.And I'm really sorry to write this, but unfortunately-it is what it is.On the surface, the film has all the basic ingredients of a story: characters with goals, a past, and obstacles to overcome. Yet, none of it carries any weight, leaving the narrative shallow and emotionally hollow.I couldn't connect with the characters at all.The dialogue is pure plastic and painfully cringey to watch.Every shot is predictable -I was five steps ahead and already knew what was going to happen.The overall feeling I got from the film is that the entire budget was spent just to make the driving scenes look halfway decent.But even the driving scenes weren't nearly as good as those in the films that came before F1, like Fast & Furious.Sony is a character with a paper-thin backstory-we barely know anything about him except that he crashed as a young driver and his career ended.Then, out of nowhere, a friend shows up to convince him to get back on track. But again-why him? What makes Sony so special? Why is he the one getting a second chance to redeem a career he barely had in the first place?There's one moment where Sony starts talking about himself-how he's amazing, unbeatable, how he "flies" when driving-and it's so bad I nearly threw up.Then there's this ridiculous scene of him sitting in an ice bath with car tires in front of him and a kitchen behind him. What?! All that was missing was a barn full of horses next to the tub. Completely incoherent and utterly confusing.The only thing I know about Kate is that she likes the wind. Period.And Pearce, the young driver-his conversations with his mom are emotionless and a total disaster.There's one scene where he complains about a new coworker, and the dialogue is so empty, it feels like only he and his mom know what they're talking about-because the audience certainly doesn't.The relationship between him and Sony is so cold and flat, I honestly have no idea why the movie decided to play emotional music under their conversations.Speaking of music-poor director didn't know what to do with himself, let alone with the soundtrack.The movie starts with rock music and some dynamic sound design, and ends with modern pop and trap? Completely incoherent.The sound design just falls apart as the movie goes on. All the fast cuts and dynamic editing are packed at the start-and then vanish.Who is Sony, really? Why does he carry playing cards? Why does he gamble? Why is he depressed? Why him? What makes him special?What's his inner goal? His outer goal? What are his internal and external conflicts?Even if I magically answer all those questions, the movie still wouldn't be good-because it lacks clear focus.The only real takeaway I got from this movie is: two people need to win a race, and along the way they need to become a team.Their only obstacles? That they don't get along and their car isn't great.What this film lacks the most is the fact that the characters reach their goals through obstacles-without taking the audience on any kind of compelling journey or adventure.

Highly recommend.

title: F1: it could have been better and deeper.
content: When producing a movie, we must keep in mind its purpose and the message it brings. This isn't an unavoidable aspect. Nothing in a movie's aesthetic should follow a specific message or convey any specific meaning. But in some exceptional movies, something is being said. Even if we don't realize it. After all, we're dealing with multiple layers. But something is being conveyed. Implicitly or explicitly. Important or not. It's part of the artistic process. And it's no different here. F1 presents the idea that we should try to do what we like, what fulfills us, and not what enriches us. Whether you're the best at it or not. And this idea guides the movie.And this raises some problems (yes, good movies can have problems too). Because gaining money is necessary if we consider that without money, we can't move forward (I'm sorry to have to mention it here, but it's part of capitalist society). Not that F1 is a bad or inconsistent movie. Sometimes it's easy to forget the specter of poverty plaguing some parts of the planet, especially if it's not ourselves. So, doing what you love is sometimes not a priority, or even an option, although this doesn't detract from the movie. We should simply reflect and ponder these pressing questions.The movie's direction is fabulous, considering all the breathtaking speed involved, and it provides dynamism and solidity at the same time without compromising the story. The acting is also top-notch, and all the other layers are impressive. And what about Lewis Hamilton producing? A smart choice that adds more power to the movie. The production overall is splendid. Another detail that few notice is the editing. It had been done before, but here we see a brilliant editing that contributes to the overall picture. And the screenplay, although originally conceived by Hams Zimmer, is also well-written.F1 doesn't disappoint, but it's a simple movie with a problematic message that, while reaching the right audience, reveals itself to be shallow and underdeveloped. This leads us to conclude that F1 is a captivating movie, but very poorly explored. It could have been better and deeper.

title: Watch on the biggest screen
content:
Rotten Tomatoes gives the movie a score of 82%.Reviews from users are more divided; some die-hard Formula One fans point out problems with the story and realism, while many compliment the excitement and visuals.🔥 What Works: The movie immerses the audience in the speed, chaos, and noise of international motorsport right away with its exhilarating, high-octane race sequences. The sound design and cinematography, which were greatly aided by composer Hans Zimmer, are the film's strongest points.Star power: The supporting cast, which includes Javier Bardem and Damson Idris, heightens the emotional stakes of the narrative, and Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a seasoned racer making his final lap at glory.Set-piece spectacle: To give the film a sense of realism and scale, the production team used real Formula 1 tracks and authentic elements of the sport.⚠ What's Less Effective: The character arcs and screenplay have drawn criticism for being too formulaic or predictable. According to some critics, the plot twists and emotional depth fall flat.Certain creative liberties (about race rules or driver behavior) may lessen immersion for ardent Formula One fans who demand strict realism.Although dynamic, the runtime and quick pacing occasionally don't allow for much character development or subtle drama.The verdict:
Those seeking an exciting and visually stunning journey through the world of Formula 1 will find it in F1: The Movie. The best way to enjoy it is with full sound and immersion on the big screen. But if you want a story with a lot of layers or an accurate motorsport documentary, you might find it more style than substance.Take a seat, watch on the biggest screen possible, and enjoy the thrill, but don't expect too much from the story.

title: Excellent F1 propaganda
content: In the real world formula one is the dullest spectator event going, drivers are largely incidental as the uber rich, associated with one of a couple of constructors and with the deepest pockets win the races, whilst simultaneously torching fossil fuels to boil the atmosphere.A massive suspension of belief is therefore needed as F1 the movie tries to convince you that motor racing is interesting and exciting, which of course it isn't.It has to be said though: F1 is very watchable and despite it being a bit familiar due to significant similarities to Top Gun Maverick that extend well beyond director Kosinski... Aging film star plays aging bad boy pilot/driver drafted in for one last shot at redemption through mentorship of some talented cocky protégée, they even get to hook up with love interests played by actresses decades younger than them.Premise and predictable story arc aside though this is a good film, well worth seeing, has some excellent action sequences and gives rousing thrills even to the most cynical of viewers.The battle for the last real movie star continues between Pitt and Cruise.

title: Best film of 2025
content: This is going to be top contender for film of the year, if not the decade. It makes you question if it's based on a true story as the logic in writing has zero holes in in. Its phenomenal.Directed by the Joseph Kosinski, who did Top Gun Maverick. Score by Hans Zimmer. Brad Pitt as the star, Damson is now a household name (Probably front-runner to be the next Black Panther).Highly recommend you watch on the biggest screen possible. Its loud with lots of racing, but at a level that doesn't require earbuds. The sound editing was phenomenal and deserves Oscars for that as well.Its my number 1 film of the year. title: Go For The Brad, Stay For The Bardem
content: Javier can turn even the smallest scene into something great and interesting, and he's the only reason to stick "F1" out. He's certainly better than Brad switched to bemused, arrogant Cliff Booth mode as an old racer friend Javi recruits to help with gambling debts to his F1 team (there's a brief flicker in his eyes early on of the vulnerability of his finest acting in "Ad Astra," then gone). The film is a painfully formulaic look at Formula 1 racing for anyone who remembers a movie made before 1990, director Joseph Kosinski intent on hitting every '80s Tony Scott theme of minimum characterization and cliched 'haunted hero comes back/clashes with rival for ultimate redemption' beat, even mimicking Scott's cinematography and skimming pacing. Kerry Condon as a crew techie does the paces as the 'initially skeptical/later slamming against the wall with passion' love interest, and Damon Idris as Pitt's teammate is at least interesting for showing the contemporary multiculturalism of London, but only the most obsessed aficionados of formula racing will find the repetitive racing scenes themselves interesting.Kosinski's most human film was the firefighter epic "Only The Brave" (I was an extra in it; he called the casting director to yell at me for giving away the ending on social media - since it was based on a ten year old true story not sure what I was giving away, but anyway); since then with "Top Gun: Maverick" and this he's been determined to transform his style into some kind of dynastic revival of Tom Cruise's glory years: programmed thrills, robotic, empty. Probably a smart move financially, but a hollow one for his art, and for moviegoers. Oh well, at least Brad's name here isn't Cole Trickle (which sounds like some kind of prostate problem). Race on, Brad, race on.

title: A High-Speed Underdog Story with Heart
content: Not a hardcore racing follower, but I was keen to watch this one-mainly because of the people associated (Brad Pitt, especially).At its core, it's an underdog story-set around a struggling team and the contrasting dynamics between a returning, forgotten old-timer and a young rookie trying to find his footing and build his personal brand in the racing world.From the get-go, the film is immersive. It's backed by a well-written narrative, relatable characters, and genuinely entertaining dialogues that keep you hooked.But it's not just about driving fast or winning races. The film takes time to explore the technical intricacies, the off-track drama, and the behind-the-scenes banter of the team with surprising depth.It also gives a special yet subtle nod to the contributions of women in the sport-reminding us that building a championship-winning car isn't just a man's job. It's not a standout feature, but a thoughtful touch nonetheless.Visually, it's stunning. The cinematography places you right behind the wheel-especially in IMAX, where the experience feels even more real.For a film about speed, it's impressively well-paced (pun intended). The first half flies by, so much so that you barely notice the runtime.The second half, though, slows down a bit. It takes a more dramatic and slightly predictable turn, but still manages to bring in themes of grit, purpose, and determination-reminding us why racers do what they do.If you're a fan of the genre, you'll enjoy spotting several familiar faces sprinkled throughout. But even if you're not, the last 30 minutes alone are enough to keep you on the edge of your seat. I found myself unexpectedly cheering by the end.Definitely worth a watch-especially in IMAX. If you can experience it that way, nothing like it.Although I'd still consider it as a one-time watch for me.

title: 🎬 F1 (2025) - My Honest Thoughts as a Die-Hard F1 Fan and Cinephile
content: So yeah, I just watched the new F1 film directed by Joseph Kosinski, starring Brad Pitt, and produced by Apple and all that. I've been following this one for a long time because I'm not just a film fan-I'm also a massive F1 enthusiast. Like, I've been following the sport passionately for years now, and I've been watching all the teasers, behind-the-scenes footage, and updates about this film since it started production. Naturally, I went in with high expectations.And to be honest... I've got mixed feelings.The StoryLet's start with the story. I'll keep it simple: it's nothing special. Just another Hollywood-style redemption arc. You know the drill-retired guy, personal demons, takes on a young rookie, somehow finds himself again, saves the day, etc. We've seen it before. A lot. From Batman Begins to Ford v Ferrari, this whole narrative structure isn't new, and sadly, this film didn't do anything fresh with it. It wasn't bad, but it just felt very predictable. Very safe. And for a film that claimed to be groundbreaking, the writing didn't deliver that.Story: 6/10🎥 CinematographyNow visually? Yeah, they nailed it. The cinematography was impressive, no doubt. They shot it with custom Sony cameras and it had that IMAX look, even though I didn't get to watch it on a proper IMAX screen. The way they captured the POV shots from inside the car, the track angles, and just the overall racing energy-it all looked stunning. You can tell they really tried to do something special with the visuals.Cinematography: 7.5/10🎶 SoundtrackThis is where I expected a lot and didn't really get it. Hans Zimmer was part of the soundtrack team, and I was genuinely hyped about that. But honestly? Apart from a few good moments, it didn't really hit. There were flashes of Zimmer's signature style, but most of it felt pretty generic. Some scenes had rap, some had rock-it was trying to be diverse, I get that, but it didn't feel cohesive or powerful. I didn't leave the theatre remembering any particular track. And that's rare for a film with Zimmer attached.Soundtrack: 6/10🎭 ActingBrad Pitt was solid-he always is. I've always respected him as an actor and I think he brought the right amount of depth and coolness to the role. But the rest of the cast? Meh. Damson Idris was alright, but I wasn't too blown away. The supporting characters didn't stand out much either. It felt like the entire emotional weight was on Brad, and honestly, that's not fair in a film like this. Better casting around him could've made the character dynamics more interesting.Acting: 7/10 (mostly because of Brad)🏁 Realism & AuthenticityThis one really annoyed me. They marketed the film as the most authentic racing movie ever made, but as someone who's been in the F1 community for years-I could see right through it. Just like what they did with Top Gun: Maverick, Kosinski and the team made it seem like everything was shot practically, with no CGI, no fake stuff... but that's just not true. A lot of the racing scenes had heavy VFX, and I know for a fact that several moments could've been done practically but weren't. It's all very cinematic, but not very real. They promised authenticity and didn't deliver.Realism: 5/10💭 Final ThoughtsLook, I don't think it's a bad film. It was entertaining in parts, it looked good, and Brad Pitt definitely gave it some weight. But for me, as someone who's deeply into both F1 and cinema, it just didn't live up to the hype. When you know the sport inside out, and the film tries to sell you drama you can't buy into, it just doesn't hit. It felt like they tried to make Ford v Ferrari meets Top Gun, but without the emotional depth or realism of either.And to be honest, if they had Tom Cruise in it? We might've gotten something wild. That guy would've actually driven the car and done half the stunts himself. Brad's great, but he didn't come from this world, and that shows.🔚 Final Score: 6.5/10It's fine for a casual viewer. Maybe even great for someone who doesn't watch F1.But if you're like me-if you know the sport and love the craft of cinema-this film will probably leave you a bit... underwhelmed.

title: Slick, Shallow, and Soulless - A Disappointment on All Fronts
content: I went in hopeful - the trailers promised intensity, high-stakes drama, and a real love letter to Formula 1. What I got was cinematic mediocrity masquerading as prestige.Despite the high IMDb and Metascore ratings, this film is a masterclass in surface-level storytelling. The soundtrack fails to capture the adrenaline or emotional weight of racing. The characters? Underdeveloped and, at times, painfully wooden. Performances feel forced, as if the actors are playing dress-up rather than embodying real people with real stakes.The emotional beats are hollow. Reactions feel scripted. There's no tension, no atmosphere, no grit - just glossy shots and empty dialogue. It's dauntingly superficial, boring, and uninspiring. A story about one of the most passionate sports in the world should feel something. This felt like a commercial.In a world where AI-generated trailers and hype-driven reviews drown out substance, this movie is a perfect symbol of what's going wrong in Hollywood. It looks the part, but it doesn't mean anything.

title: The Champion!
content: The champion! A movie star, good quality movie, a subject that appeals to people outside of the main fans of race car driving. Although the latter could be tricky subject to connect to general movie goer. Brad Pitt and Apple did.A movie star like Brad Pitt brings charisma that is seldom found. Since the dawn of movies, movie stars existed as the studios knew they had something special that could get people to come to the movies.Pitt can also act and tries to bring quality movies to the cinema, which elevates the movie business. Thank you.In this movie F1, The Movie, Pitt and the rest of the cast, writing, sets, costumes, directors pulled it together to provide movie theatres and fans a reason to say "Ok you got us in the seats".I appreciate the fact that this movie star, producer and team did not try to con us into their seats via pre-relase tactics trying to tell us this movie was going to be a hit, have such a large ticket sales, that you better get your tickets ahead and all that gimmicky stuff. Including the side show antics of memes, trying to make a marketing lightening in a bottle via manufactured process, which seldom works. These things don't enhance the movie business. Rather those tactics lower the medium of movies. For when people get sucked in by those things(including the newspapers, online resources who blindly push certain studios/producers works) and people get in movie seats and realize "hey this a dumb cheesy movie that I could have saved gas, time to park and drive, and all that and STREAMED this movie" the consumer feels used and then starts to go to movies less and less.The movie is entertainment gold. Go see it. I won't provide spoilers or a book report over it! It is up to you go to see this movie and decide for yourself.

title: Fast cars, slow script, and Brad Pitt doing his best "I'm still young, I swear" impression
content: Brad Pitt's F1: The Movie is what happens when Hollywood decides to slap a redemption arc onto a carbon-fiber chassis and call it cinema. Yes, the racing scenes are slick-IMAX-worthy, even. But once the engines cool and the helmets come off, you're left with a plot that feels like it was written during a pit stop.Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a retired driver dragged back into the sport to mentor a hot-headed rookie. Groundbreaking, right? It's basically The Karate Kid in a race suit, minus the emotional payoff and with more brooding stares into the middle distance. Pitt is charming, of course - he could read a tire pressure manual and still make it sexy- but the script gives him about as much depth as a puddle on race day.The supporting cast? Javier Bardem tries to inject gravitas, Kerry Condon looks perpetually concerned, and Damson Idris plays the rookie with all the subtlety of a Red Bull marketing campaign. You can almost hear the studio execs whispering, "Just make it look cool and throw in some Zimmer music."And look cool it does. The racing sequences are genuinely thrilling, and Kosinski knows how to shoot speed like it's poetry. But once the adrenaline wears off, you're left wondering if the emotional stakes were ever real or just another lap around the cliché circuit.In short: F1: The Movie is a glossy, well-oiled machine that runs out of narrative fuel halfway through. It's not a crash, but it's definitely not podium-worthy either. Watch it for the spectacle, stay for Brad's jawline, and lower your expectations for anything resembling character development.

title: Forgettable
content: First of all, Brad Pitt is nearly 60 years old-how can he convincingly play an F1 driver? Even seasoned F1 veterans are typically in their mid-40s. It just doesn't add up. He's a talented and charismatic actor, but he seems past his prime for roles like this. If this is meant to be some sort of rivalry with Tom Cruise, please don't drag us into it.His performance, while charming, didn't bring anything innovative to the role. He felt like he was portraying the same character as Cliff Booth from Once Upon a Time in Hollywood , which is enjoyable, but I was hoping for something different.There were no real surprises in the narrative, making it less captivating. I found myself anticipating plot twists, characters development , or moments of tension, but none emerged.However, one aspect that stood out was the visuals.The cinematography was impressive, beautifully showcasing the high-speed thrills of racing. While the visuals were a treat, they unfortunately couldn't compensate for the unoriginal plot.In summary, F1 had the chance to be a thrilling and distinct addition to the genre but ultimately fell short.

title: A Breathtaking Racing Ballet
content:
F1 The Movie (2025) :
Movie Review -"I am going to win a million races, you should win at least one," says Joshua to Sonny-defining the entire movie in one sentence. F1 is more about teamwork than Sonny Hayes' journey, as he actually stays in the back seat throughout the film until the final 15 minutes. The movie follows a formulaic plot, intentionally designed for racing fans, and delivers blockbuster entertainment with a solid finishing touch. The best racing film in recent times-or even the last two decades-has to be Ford v Ferrari. You get two phenomenal actors (Christian Bale and Matt Damon), and an overwhelming climax that suddenly transforms the high-octane action into sentimental heartbreak, making it a timeless classic. F1 The Movie tries to do the same, but with a more restrained approach. It's about the main character far removed from stardom and celebration, and that moment when he says, "It's not about money. It's about racing," lands hard-and so does the film. That is surely going to hit hard to some die hard racing fans.The story tracks Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a former F1 racer, who returns to the championship for his old buddy Ruben (Javier Bardem), the owner of the APXGP team. The second driver on the team is Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), a talented but rookie racer. Kate (Kerry Condon) is the technical director-a rare woman to achieve this post-and she must build a combat-ready car as demanded by Sonny, in order to make APXGP a winning team. The biggest challenge is the incompatible bond between Sonny and Joshua, which stands in stark contrast to the teamwork needed to win an F1 championship. Their ego clashes and on-track battles for glory jeopardize Ruben's dreams, but he still has faith in Sonny, who might just pull it off using his unconventional methods. How? Catch the adrenaline rush in the film.As stated earlier, F1 The Movie has a formulaic storyline, combining artificial elements with a stroke of luck to make it all click. What truly stands out are the heart-thumping, breathtaking racing sequences, punctuated by fast-paced commentary and razor-sharp edits, made thrilling by Sonny's tactics. It's the emphasis on teamwork that makes this film a winner, both within the story and for the audience. Another win is how the movie avoids clichéd drama and time-consuming romance like Rush (2013), opting instead to stay focused on the racing and the driver like FvF. There's only one intimate moment between the leading pair, keeping the pace tight. Joshua's character arc takes a bit too long to develop, which slows the film down in the middle. Also, the car-building scenes are overly technical. Some mechanical detail would have helped. I was hoping for a scene where Sonny guides Kate through the car-building process while the main director stands impressed. I waited long for such a scene-but it never came. That magical moment of collaboration, like we saw between Bale and Damon in Ford v Ferrari, is missing here. Furthermore, that one defining, mic-drop moment is absent in the climax, though the writer offers something else-something that's likely to resonate deeply with certain viewers, as mentioned earlier.Brad Pitt completely embodies the role of Sonny Hayes. If I ever wanted to see him in a character-driven performance with mass appeal, then my wish has been granted through F1 The Movie. His selfless demeanor, silent sacrifices, and nuanced expressions make Sonny a memorable character. Damson Idris delivers a commendable performance opposite Pitt, and he'll likely gain more "followers" after this role. Kerry Condon is convincing, Javier Bardem shines as always, and Sarah Niles brings warmth as a caring mother. Kim Bodnia, Tobias Menzies, Shea Whigham, Joseph Balderrama, Callie Cooke, Abdul Salice, and others offer sincere support in their roles.F1 is powered by a superb technical crew that elevates the cinematic experience across every department. Starting with the cinematography-it's nothing short of fantastic. Claudio Miranda captures high-speed close-ups, frantic cuts, and high RPM-packed racing scenes that demand to be seen on the big screen. Thanks to the Warner Bros. India team and their PR, who arranged the screening at the best IMAX screen in the country, which definitely enhanced the experience. My final rating may even be influenced by that one reason alone. Watching on a giant IMAX screen, seated dead-center in the auditorium-truly the best view-is an unmatched experience. The sound design, background score, and music add further energy to the film. You'll find yourself tapping your foot at least 4-5 times. The film also boasts a beautiful production design, making it look grand, and the editing works well for the most part-barring the middle portion. Surprisingly, the film has its fair share of humorous moments too, and the jokes land organically. Joseph Kosinski, who earlier took our breath away with Top Gun: Maverick, does it again with F1 The Movie. He hits almost all the right notes and delivers a bona fide blockbuster. With sharp commentary and surprising cameos from real racing legends, F1 is sure to leave every F1 aficionado satisfied. Go experience this high-octane extravaganza on the big screen-because it's not meant for the small screen. "It's not about money, it's about racing." Likewise, "It's not about the movie, it's about experiencing a thrilling ride."RATING - 7/10*

title: Fast, Flashy, and a Little Off Track
content: F1, the highly anticipated Brad Pitt-led racing film, hits a homerun when it comes to capturing the sheer speed and adrenaline of Formula 1. The racing scenes are intense, immersive, and beautifully shot, fans of high-octane action will find plenty to enjoy. Add in an all-star cast and you've got a film that certainly looks the part.But while the film nails the atmosphere, it stalls when it comes to story. Compared to Ron Howard's Rush, F1 feels like it's running on fumes. About halfway through, I caught myself glancing at my watch-a personal sign that the plot just isn't gripping enough. The narrative lacks depth and, at times, drifts too far into Hollywood fantasy, often bending (or ignoring) the realities of what a Formula 1 team could realistically do or get away with.For casual viewers or newcomers to the sport, F1 will likely deliver a thrilling and stylish ride. But for devoted F1 fans, it may leave you a bit frustrated-or even disappointed-by how far it veers from the real-world intricacies of the sport.Bottom line: it's flashy and fun, but don't expect it to cross the finish line with the same heart and substance as Rush.

title: A Masterclass in Motorsport Filmmaking
content: Finally - a film about Formula 1 that truly understands the sport. F1 with Brad Pitt isn't just the best racing film in recent memory - it's one of the most professionally crafted sports dramas of the decade.From the very first scene, it's clear this movie was made with immense respect for the world of Formula 1. The attention to detail is astonishing: camera angles, pit lane dynamics, race strategy, telemetry screens - everything feels authentic because it is authentic. You can tell real F1 teams and drivers were deeply involved in the consultation process. That level of input from professionals elevates the film far beyond anything we've seen before in the genre.Brad Pitt delivers one of his most grounded, mature performances in years, but he's far from alone - the entire cast is phenomenal, breathing life into characters that feel real, flawed, and human. There are no caricatures here - just people immersed in a brutally competitive world.But what truly sets this film apart is that it doesn't glorify F1 as just fast cars and podiums. It dives into the politics, pressure, and moral compromises within the sport - the shady deals, the betrayals from within, the mental and physical toll on drivers, and the cost of chasing perfection. It's a raw, honest portrayal that doesn't flinch from the darker side of elite competition.Whether you're a hardcore fan or new to the world of racing, this film pulls you in and never lets go. A must-watch.10/10.

title: This is what people were going crazy for?
content: The technical execution is undeniably exceptional, particularly in the way the shots and frames are composed and handled, though heavily inspired by live, real world broadcasting, and the seamless integration of footage set to an understated but stirring background score deserves some commendation.However, a film encompasses more than just visual appeal; it mandates depth, it requires literary substance, and this is exactly where my praise concludes and my criticisms begin.The narrative follows a familiar racing archetype involving a seasoned, cocky veteran and an inexperienced, spirited rookie, and it tries to delineate on their respective struggles & dilemmas both on and off the track.While this formula is well-established, it is unfortunate that even within this framework, the writing fails to convey anything of significance, leaving the due resolution and overall takeaway feeling almost trivial, nonexistent!This lack of influence extends to all relationships portrayed in the story, whether it be the racing teammates, the friend duo, or the romantic subplot; none of these ingredients truly develop or resonate, resulting in a storytelling that feels painstakingly predictable and tiresomely routine.In fact, if the racing sequences were removed, the film's structure would collapse in an instant.Regarding the technical aspects, while they are indeed impressive, they may not resonate with longtime fans of the sport, including myself or those familiar with Netflix's original series, "Drive to Survive," (count me in yet again) which was referenced in one of the dialogues even.For such audiences, the film will most likely come off as rehashed, cinematic retelling of familiar themes.Where is the novelty, then?

title: F1: A Thrilling Lap Between Fiction and Passion
content: As a passionate Formula 1 fan, I was eagerly looking forward to this film. And honestly, it lived up to my expectations! F1 manages to capture the adrenaline, tension, and unique atmosphere that reigns on the circuits, with incredibly realistic in-car sequences. It truly feels like you're right there - and that's undoubtedly one of the film's greatest strengths.Brad Pitt delivers a solid and charismatic performance, as always, well supported by the rest of the cast. He convincingly portrays a veteran driver returning to the grid, and his commitment makes the character both believable and endearing. One of the most charming aspects of the film is that it was shot during the actual past season. It gives you that thrilling feeling of reliving certain Grands Prix, of reconnecting with the unique atmosphere of moments we've already experienced - but through a fresh cinematic lens. This adds a unique and immersive layer to the film, especially for fans.Of course, some of the strategic choices in the movie might raise eyebrows among knowledgeable viewers - a few decisions seem a bit off compared to how things would realistically play out in modern F1. But we mustn't forget: this is fiction, not a documentary. And in that regard, the film fully embraces its purpose - to entertain, to move, to make us feel.So yes, maybe a few tweaks could have made it even more believable, but that doesn't take away from the overall experience. For me, it's a resounding yes. F1 successfully blends cinema and motorsport passion with intelligence and style.

title: Everything You Could Want
content: F1 is fantastic. This is the type of old school blockbuster that we've desperately been needing. Director, Joseph Kosinski, has proven that his Top Gun Maverick success was not a one hit wonder. This man is at the top of his game. He goes all out in this movie. The amount of effort put into the racing sequences is insane. I was on the edge of my seat watching the races. The way the cameras follow the cars is exhilarating and it leads to truly thrilling sequences. Doing so much of this practically, on actual tracks, helps make this movie feel so real and immersive. Kosinski has clearly found his genre. Yet again this is a movie about an underdog teaming up with a newcomer and he just knows how to direct actors and get great performances out of them. Brad Pitt's movie star charm is in full effect here. His charisma lights up the screen and just makes this movie so easy to enjoy. The entire supporting cast does a very good job too, and because of that you get really invested in the characters and story. The emotional and comedic moments both work excellently, and a lot of that is due to Ehren Kruger's great screenplay. This movie also utilizes sound and music very well. The film is big and goes all out on every level. F1 is everything blockbuster entertainment should be. 9/10.

title: Furious summer fun
content: In Joe Kosinski's F1, Brad Pitt plays journeyman race car driver and former New York cabbie Sonny Hayes (no word yet on what he did with his medallion). Sonny is coaxed out of retirement by old pal Ruben (Javier Bardem) to help the latter build respectability for his struggling Formula 1 team, which includes talented young Joshua (Damson Idris). You can imagine the plotlines that emerge from there: the old versus the new, legacy and redemption, David and Goliath, conflicts, romances and front office disputes, alongside a plethora of highlights behind the wheel.Pitt remains one of our true legends. The master of very-good-maybe-not-great flicks (though he's had his share of great ones too!). F1 fits neatly in his canon. Better yet, he seems like he's enjoying himself and making the most of the moment. As with his only previous sports film Moneyball, the writing is forced to balance technical gobbledygook for insiders with oversimplified explanations for the rest of us. There's no easy way to serve both groups- and hardcore F1 enthusiasts are bound to resent the liberties taken with their sport- but Pitt and his castmates bridge the gap as well as one could expect.The performances overall are perhaps not great. Scenes veer between hits and misses, often one after the other. Thankfully Pitt holds his own throughout. In a few rocky exchanges with Joshua's mother Bernadette (Sarah Niles), he doesn't pour gas on the fire when it's not working. He stays in his proverbial lane and keeps the car moving for the both of them. Bardem has more trouble- owing to his banal character having absolutely nowhere to go, which leads Ruben into an array of cul de sacs. But again, Brad's steady hand makes him more tolerable as well. It's an impressive display of a Hollywood titan gently pulling his co-stars out of the flames of a somewhat combustible script.A greater weakness is that the editing is not always sharp, with continuity gaffes aplenty (e.g., half-full glasses that appear at a different level in the next shot; facial expressions and hand gestures that reset when the camera shifts; etc.). But if you're not a stickler for detail, that shouldn't bug you either since there's so much other flash to draw your focus.And there really is: F1 is a BIG production, which occasionally demands we suspend our disbelief. Thankfully we have that skill! When Bernadette swoons over her first sighting of Sonny - "Mmm, that's a handsome man, Joshua" - we don't jump out of our seat to remind her, "Yes honey, because it's Brad Pitt!" So it's clear we know we're at the movies, right? Ok perfect lol. It helps to keep that in mind so as to not get trapped in the implausibility of certain plot devices, events and visuals. To be sure, the on-track photography is sublime and the action heart-stopping (if not realistic). Kosinski and DP Claudio Miranda are in their element here and frame Grand Prix racing in a way that is explosive and uniquely theirs. And while I'm sure it would look beautiful on a tablet/laptop too, the theater is a must if you're a film junkie aiming to appreciate the craft. I personally saw it in Standard, but figure the IMAX experience would be the ultimate.F1 is a classic summer blockbuster. Which is to say it's not meant to be taken too seriously. You'll have some laughs, shed some tears and even take some inspiration from the story. You'll see old friends, meet new ones, and have a generally good time at the movies. As long as we remember in the end it's all F1 & games, this one's well worth the ride.

title: Adrenaline fuelled popcorn nonsense
content: A very enjoyable but silly F1 movie !!!Yes its a lot of fun with great F1 action scenes but it hits too many tropes that it becomes entirely predictable and formulaic, which for many people is perfect for a trip to the cinema.The premise is a typical hollywood fantasy one, Brad Pitt plays Brad Pitt/Sonny Hayes. Ex failed F1 driver/failed husband/failed gambler but with mild success in racing cars of various ilk.His old buddy who is now owner of an F1 team comes calling as a Hail Mary to somehow rescue the team because of some sort of plot point. None of this matters because who cares....lets get down to fast cars and grand prix !!!This film looks pretty fantastic, Pitt as usual looks fantastic and owns every scene. The rest of the cast does a good job supporting Brad in every way possible.The real stars of the show are the cars and racing that build some tension which ends up being diminished by the constant commentary/narration/exposition.There are moments when this commentary is warranted or welcome but most of the time it was a jarring nuisance put in place to explain to the audience what was happening every second of every race. Imagine watching Top Gun with a sport's presenter narrating every move like a Red Bull extreme sports show from the 00s.For me I had to mark the film down a couple of points due to this. Id love to see a version of this film with 80% less commentary, more engines, more music, and more comms between the drivers and the pit.Where Mad Max Fury Road succeeded with little dialogue this film fails with too much. My hatred for the 2 commentators was real and my enjoyment of a certain lap with no commentators was such a relief.Other than that its a solid F1 car racing movie that looks great but is also exactly what you expect its going to be.6.5/10.

title: Good Film We've Seen Many Times Before
content: I will spare the details and summarize this as your average underdog movie. This has all of the expected bells and whistles for a $200 million budget: above average writing, passable CGI, and real sets. One of the main redeeming qualities of F1: the movie has to be the strong musical score which is to be expected with the likes of Jimmy Page (Oblivion) and Led Zeppelin on board.My main issue comes down to acting. Brad Pitt is an amazing actor who has a body of work to back up his amazing legacy. To my surprise this was the highest salary he has earned to date ($30 million). His performance felt dry and devoid of emotion. The dialogue felt wholly inorganic to the point of distraction. To Pitts credit, I should note a large portion of the script was performed in formula-1 cars so some dryness is expected.The direction for this film had a very amateur feel. There was a bombardment of blunt commentary as to what was taking place in many of the race scenes which felt completely unnecessary even for somebody who isn't a diehard fan of the sport like myself. Characters would often describe their emotions and intentions rather than SHOW these things on screen, which feels like a complete blunder given the star-studded cast.To summarize, there is nothing original to this story. It is another tale of conflict between veteran and rookie. A lifetime of experience versus young ambition. A compromise of two perspectives will ultimately allow for our heroes to prevail as they learn to appreciate one another. I am once again left baffled as to how such a mediocre product got made when there was so much potential for greatness backed by a studio budget.I would recommend turning off your brain and just enjoying the music and action for what it is. 6/10.

title: Complete Disappointment
content: When I watched the trailer, I was worried that this film would turn into a big-budget production with cringeworthy storytelling and a weak storyline. Well, a dream came true.A cringeworthy storyline with a mix of this and that could give this movie a 7.9/10. What a joke. The storytelling is like a teacher explaining what "teamwork" and "rivalry" are all about to a three-year-old and basically telling them, "Oh, fighting with your teammate is bad," and "discipline is important," that kind of fairy tale. Everything in the storyline is happening for the sake of it.I sit in the theater for two hours, hoping that the movie will eventually improve as time goes by. Hoping that there's some deeper meaning and message behind it. Even a documentary about Hamilton vs Rosberg on YouTube has done a better job than this. Everything that has happened on track is just a montage of messy editing. I don't even know the grid condition, and all that has is just a bunch of overtakes. At one point, the movie is too busy with the "jokes" and the movie has sparked no emotion from me when Pierce crashed in Monza, because first, I'm not even sure if the movie is a comedy or a racing film at this point. Second, I am certain that every major character in this film has plot armor. It's like a perfectly designed recipe, but in the end, it has been used as a fast food production.I can't tell how disappointed I am with this movie. A fan-made YouTube documentary could have done a better job than this. Yes, it's great on IMAX, and all the action throughout the track has been amazing. That's why it's getting a 3/10, instead of 0/10.This movie is not like Rocky, not like Rush, not like Ford vs. Ferrari, not like Million Dollar Baby, not like Whiplash, not like Black Swan, heck, not like any kind of sports movie or any kind of "motivational" or "rivalry" movie. This right here has none of it. The plot is so easily breakable that it shatters like a thin piece of paper. The main character, Sonny, couldn't find another reason to actually care for him other than the crash he had with Senna back in '93. We have a reason to care for Niki Lauda because what a horrible crash that was back in the horrible envrionment in the 1970s; We have a reason to care for Ken Miles and Carroll Shelby because those two are not only have passion for racing, but in the mean time they are good husband, and a great friend with each other. Their love and affection are not just happening on the screen for the sake of it, but it's actually there. And once again, this movie has none of it. You had a crush on your boss, and seconds (months, to be more realistic in the movie) later, you are making out in the elevator.Gosh go watch Rush or Ford v Ferrari. Those are the movies that I had in mind when I sit in the theater. Not some junk fast food garbage.

title: Best Movie
content: From the opening scene, F1 pulls you into a world of speed, adrenaline, and high-stakes drama. Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem bring depth and tension to their roles, while Kerry Condon and Damson Idris add fresh layers that keep the story grounded and engaging.The real stars behind the scenes are Claudio Miranda's stunning cinematography and Hans Zimmer's pulse-pounding score. Together, they create a visual and sonic experience that's nothing short of immersive. Kosinski blends real F1 footage with seamless VFX so effectively, it's hard to tell where reality ends and movie magic begins.

title: F1 - 8 Stars
content: F1 is one of those movies that pulls you in fast and never really lets go. Even if you're not into racing, it's hard not to get caught up in it. The movie feels big, loud, and intense in the best way, and Brad Pitt is a big reason it works. He brings a calm, lived-in presence that makes the story feel grounded instead of over-the-top.The racing scenes are easily the highlight. They feel real - fast, dangerous, and stressful - like you're right there in the car. The sound alone is enough to keep your heart rate up, and the camera work does a great job of making you feel the speed without being confusing or flashy for no reason.What I liked most is that the movie doesn't just rely on the racing. There's a strong focus on the people behind the helmets - the pressure, the relationships, and what it costs to compete at that level. It's not doing anything wildly new story-wise, but it's done well enough that you stay invested.Overall, F1 is a really solid, well-made film that knows exactly what it wants to be. It's exciting, polished, and has just enough heart to balance the adrenaline. Definitely worth seeing on the biggest screen you can.

title: "F1" - When Even Non-Racing Fans Get Glued to the Screen for Two Hours
content: To be honest, I've never been much of a motorsport fan. I'd turn on "Formula 1" on TV for maybe five minutes - there's noise, speed, sleek cars - but that's usually where my interest stopped. So when I heard about Joseph Kosinski's new film F1, I wasn't exactly hyped. Still, two things convinced me to give it a shot: first, Kosinski himself - the man behind the visually stunning Oblivion, the solid Only the Brave, and the blockbuster revival Top Gun: Maverick. And second, Brad Pitt in the lead role. I may not be a racing fan, but I'm definitely a Brad Pitt fan - no question there.The film tells the story of a seasoned driver returning to the sport and facing a new generation of racers. Against that backdrop unfolds a story about ambition, character, and the price of victory. From a writing standpoint, it's very well done - engaging and surprisingly heartfelt.One of the film's biggest strengths is how it explains the nuances of racing. Sometimes through dialogue, sometimes visually - but it never drowns you in jargon. Even if you've never watched a single lap of F1 in your life, you'll get what's going on and why it matters.Now, about the cast. Brad Pitt plays the aging ace who still has a spark left in him. He nails that balance of weariness and thrill-seeking. Damson Idris brings youthful energy and drive - the embodiment of a new generation. The chemistry between him and Pitt works perfectly. You might expect a standard mentor-student story - the veteran teaching the rookie discipline and humility. But no - the story takes a different direction, and the duo carries it beautifully.Kerry Condon plays the woman keeping everything under control off the track - smart, firm, yet deeply human. A great character. Javier Bardem, as always, is magnetic - his team manager is both cunning and charming. By the finale, you genuinely warm up to him. Tobias Menzies delivers a restrained and icy performance that adds just the right tension. And Sarah Niles brings warmth and psychological depth, grounding this metallic world of machines and velocity.There's humor too - not over the top, but subtle and well-placed. The kind of line that makes you smile without breaking the story's rhythm. The plot feels "clean" - in a good way: no unnecessary drama, no filler. And the visuals? Absolutely breathtaking. Cameras mounted inside the cars, sweeping shots of tracks, razor-sharp editing - it's done so well that your heart skips a beat on every turn. The soundtrack is pure adrenaline - the kind that makes you want to crank up the volume.In the end, "F1" turned out to be a pleasant surprise. Even if racing is totally foreign to you, the film makes it thrilling. A balanced story, strong performances, stunning visuals, and enough energy to charge an entire theater.My verdict: a good film. Not a masterpiece, but an honest, well-crafted piece of cinema that keeps you watching till the end. And that, in itself, is a win.8 out of 10.

title: Need for Speed, But Make It F1
content: As someone who doesn't care about car racing at all - unless we're talking my Need for Speed gaming obsession era - I was fully prepared to be bored to death by F1. I mean sure, I love a good racing film (Rush is still absolute perfection), but this felt like it could go either way.Honestly? I ended up having a great time with it. The story is completely ridiculous and follows the most predictable arc ever - but somehow that didn't bother me. It knows it's not reinventing the wheel (pun intended), and it leans into the drama and cheesiness in a way that's kind of charming.The racing scenes are 100% the highlight - loud, slick, and properly thrilling. I found myself way more into it than I expected, even cheering at one point like I knew what was happening (I did not). And the soundtrack? Hans Zimmer. Enough said. It gives the race moments so much weight - half the adrenaline might've come from the music alone.Is it deep? No. Is it fun? Weirdly, yes. If you love the chaos of a big glossy action film and can tolerate a few clichés along the way, you'll probably have a good time with this one - even if you couldn't care less about Formula 1.

title: A Predictable Lap Around the Track
content: F1 is flashy and enjoyable at moments, but it leans too heavily on Hollywood formulas. The story follows a very predictable path, with little innovation or surprise. Brad Pitt carries the film with his natural charisma, yet his character feels more like a polished archetype than a fully realized person-stylish on the outside, but missing the emotional depth that could make him memorable.The movie clearly wants to balance adrenaline-filled racing with human drama, but it often defaults to surface-level storytelling. At times, it feels like the "Hollywood version" of a racing story: glamorous, slick, and entertaining, but without the grit or authenticity that could set it apart. The racing sequences are fun and the production quality is impressive, but the lack of deeper layers keeps the film from leaving a lasting impression.In the end, F1 is fine for casual viewers and fans of Brad Pitt, but for those looking for something beyond clichés, it's just another high-budget spectacle.

title: Good movie
content: The narrative, a beautifully crafted story about mention a theme, e.g., resilience, love, the human conditionis propelled by a script that is as sharp as it is sensitive. title: The movie of the summer!
content: I just watched F1 in a packed IMAX theatre!What an epic experience! F1 is tremendous - the kind of spectacle I show up to see on an IMAX screen. It's an excellent reminder of why we love seeing movies in theatres, on the biggest screen with the loudest sound possible! I loved it! Joe Kosinski is proving himself to be a master of thrill-seeking blockbuster entertainment. Thrilling, stunning, and packed with a lot of heart, this is easily the movie of the summer! The sound. The direction. The insane camera work. Everything works in unison making for one hell of a ride.F1 is solid dad-core entertainment built for the big screen, featuring genuinely thrilling, state-of-the-art racing sequences. It may not be as surprising or electrifying as Top Gun: Maverick, but director Joseph Kosinski channels the momentum of that success into another confident, if slightly overlong and predictable, supercharged ride. F1 is good old-fashioned, heart-stopping cinema.It's a pretty thin story, but the racing sequences hit hard, with thrilling action captured with a visceral lens. Paired with a stellar Hans Zimmer score, it was easy to get swept away in the race. The script leaves a little to be desired; I certainly wanted more on a character level, no question. Everything about the story felt undercooked, but those complaints (mostly) faded away when the rubber met the road. The racing scenes were just too good, I couldn't help but get swept away by the thrill of the film!F1 is basically Top Gun Maverick but with not as many jaw-dropping moments. Don't get me wrong, it's still great and the visuals are amazing, but I definitely was missing that oomph that Ford v Ferrari and Maverick had. There's a wild disconnect between the verve and ingenuity around Kosinski's filmmaking and a script that might as well have been written on cocktail napkins and post-it notes. You can see them trying to hit the same beats as Maverick, but it's a movie with no dramatic engine. Watching F1 it is distressingly obvious what McQuarrie and Cruise brought to Maverick, whereas here it's all stacked contrivances and empty characters. It's interesting how much Top Gun: Maverick is a perfect fusion of what McQuarrie, Cruise and Kosinski are great at, whereas F1 and the recent Missions (all movies I love!) reveal how they took the wrong lessons about what made Maverick great. Usually, this sort of phenomenon happens after a huge debut, and they try to repeat that success for their second movie, but all these guys are seasoned pros in the industry for decades. Maverick was just such lightning in a bottle magic that they can't help but chase its glory.But whatever, yes, the script is thin and predictable, filled with every single cliche imaginable. It's nothing we haven't seen before. It's your classic, cliche "old school vs. New school", in which the old school guy's seen it all, lived it twice, and cares about nothing. The movie as a whole and its story are very similar in nature to Top Gun: Maverick, which comes at no surprise given Ehren Kruger wrote and Joseph Kosinski directed both films, retaining a similar creative team. Make no mistake, F1 is nowhere near as good as Maverick. I think Maverick sort of shattered the mold and perhaps broke our expectations for what a summer blockbuster should be. The criteria for a good summer blockbuster, normally, is that it should be highly entertaining, often memorable, and sometimes silly enough that it usually doesn't receive massive critical acclaim. F1 fits that bill perfectly. Outside of maybe some below-the-line nominations, don't expect this film to show up at the Oscars. I say all that to say this: F1 squarely satiates my thirst for a summer blockbuster but does not go above that.F1 is a damn good summer blockbuster. The intense, adrenaline-filled racing sequences in this movie are flat-out special and some of the best scenes I've seen all year! Director Joseph Kosinski directs the hell out of this thing, literally putting you in the driver's seat during each race through world-class use of sound (which includes a great Hans Zimmer score), visuals, and real boots-on-the-ground filmmaking. From the in-camera driver shots to the overhead race track visuals, the filmmaking carries this movie and helps cover up holes in an otherwise average story. Combine that world-class filmmaking with an outstanding cast lead by a really good Brad Pitt performance, and the end result is a great time at the movie theatre. This high-octane racing epic is a pulse-racing spectacle from start to finish. The outstanding sound design, electrifying score, and heartfelt story combine to make it THE must-see movie of the summer. Rarely does a film impact the senses like this-F1 demands to be experienced on the biggest screen possible! This movie was tailor-made for me!

title: Fast Cars, Sloppy Writing
content: "What do you care what they say? It's just noise."As a lifelong F1 fan, I really wanted to love it, but certain aspects of the film just didn't sit right with me.First, the writing. I thought it was pretty lazy when it came to the racing itself. Sonny constantly resorting to crashing or dirtying the race to gain an advantage felt completely detached from reality. There were better ways to show competitiveness than having him deliberately cause chaos almost every other race and without facing proper consequences for it. The constant exposition through the commentators was also really annoying and took me out of the moment more than once.The acting didn't help either. Damson was okay at times, but I was never fully convinced by him, Kerry, or the guy trying to take over the team. For a film of this scale, I expected a lot more.The soundtrack was another letdown. I'm a huge Hans Zimmer fan, but this one felt like a miss, maybe because of the blend between his score and the pop radio hits they threw in. I could never quite understand what mood they were aiming for, and that made it hard to really connect emotionally.Strangely enough, the parts I thought I'd like the least ended up being what I enjoyed most. The racing scenes and the world-building actually worked for me. Visually, they nailed the feeling of the F1 paddock and race weekends, and that was impressive.All in all, the movie still has its fun moments, especially spotting the real drivers, team principals, and familiar F1 faces. But for what's reportedly the most expensive movie ever made, I expected way more.

title: Vroom Without the Boom
content:
Here's the good news: F1 looks and sounds like a million-dollar lap. Actually, make that $300 million. With real F1 cars, real circuits, and Claudio Miranda's camera wedged into every curve, you're practically inside the gearbox. Kosinski directs the race footage like a man who's been waiting his whole life to strap an IMAX camera to an exhaust pipe. And yes, Hans Zimmer's score has all the bass drops and brass swagger you'd expect from the guy who once made pirates sound operatic.The problem is, once the helmets come off, the drama veers straight into pit-lane soap opera.Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a retired racing legend pulled back into the sport to mentor a hot-headed young talent (Damson Idris). Cue: clashing egos, philosophical staring across garages, and dialogue that sounds like it was tuned up in a wind tunnel of clichés. Pitt gives it charm, no doubt. He's ageing like carbon fibre... still quick, still smooth. And Idris holds his own, injecting fire into a script that mostly coasts.But here's the thing: beneath the turbocharged visuals, the emotional engine never really starts. The stakes feel borrowed. The backstories are sketched. And the dramatic turns (mentor redemption, rookie pressure, paddock politics) are as predictable as a Monaco pole position.It's not bad. Not at all. It's entertaining in the way a track parade can still be hypnotic. But narratively, it's running on fumes. You'll enjoy the ride, but you'll forget who was in the car by the time the credits roll.Verdict:
F1 is a gleaming, turbocharged spectacle. But underneath the chassis, it's a story we've already lapped a few too many times.Style wins pole. Substance misses the podium.

title: Cream Without the Cake
content: I am ardent fan of f1. I have been following it since the last decade. Being an ardent fan of this sport I have watched Rush (2013). In fact, that is the film that pushed me to follow F1. Well, let me tell you that film is the gold standard for an F1 film.Now, what we have in this film is cream in the form of cinematography and music but no story to coat it around. Sonny Hayes being the underdog of the story sure does like to skirt around (putting it mildly) the rules to get what he desires. In a contemporary action film one can get behind the idea. But in a sporting context that is down right shallow and lack of character depth and story line.I wish the $300 million budget for IMAX cameras and cars would have been used for a proper feature film on actual forgotten greats of F1. Those are some stories that are worth telling with big budgets.But instead here we have Kosinski and Pitt doing the bidding of FOM and Liberty Media by endorsing their now ever increasingly popular product to the masses without a shred of sporting and technical context.All in all the theatrics is worth the trip to the big screen but sadly it does not stack up to the likes of Ford v Ferrari and Rush. Will be a great watch for those who do not follow the sport and I hope this introduces them to the world of F1.P. S For those who have not watched Senna (2010) directed by Asif Kapadia. I would recommend watching this beautiful and emotional documentary film on the career of the 3 time F1 world champion.

title: First Day, First Show, IMAX Experience
content:
Yesterday, I had the electrifying chance to witness the much-anticipated F1 movie on its first day, first show-and that too in IMAX. Let me just say this: what a ride it was.From the very first frame to the final sequence, the film grips you like a tight chicane at Monaco. The thrill of Formula 1-its roaring engines, the raw pace, the razor-sharp strategy, and that relentless spirit-was captured with such authenticity that we, the audience, could barely sit still. My friend and I found ourselves cheering, gasping, and whisper-shouting through nearly every scene.For F1 fans, this movie is a love letter-full of clever nods, cinematic recreations, and moments that feel lifted straight from the grid. Yes, a few scenes may lean into the dramatics, but never at the cost of the soul of the sport. In fact, it's this drama that brings out the emotion behind the helmet.But here's the best part: even if you're not an F1 fan, this film might just spark that fire. It's not just about cars going in circles-it's about courage, engineering brilliance, the pain of milliseconds, and the art of precision under pressure. It shows what it truly takes to sit in that cockpit at 320 km/h and still calculate a move three corners ahead.The direction is razor-sharp, staying loyal to the sport thanks to Sir Lewis Hamilton's involvement. The cinematography and VFX are just chef's kiss-you don't watch the race, you feel it. The sound design deserves its own trophy; there were moments I genuinely forgot I was in a theatre and not strapped into a W14.As the movie neared its climax, the energy in the hall reached fever pitch. Fans were screaming, clapping, and living every gear shift and overtake. It wasn't just a movie, it was a grand prix of emotions.Final Verdict:
Whether you're a devoted F1 enthusiast or someone curious about the sport-this movie is a must-watch. It doesn't just show you Formula 1... it throws you right into it.

title: I Was So Looking Forward to This Being a Great Movie!
content: I like Brad Pitt as an actor. I LOVE Formula One racing. The number of movies interesting enough for me to pay the $80+ for me and my wife to go see and have some snacks has been few and far between since COVID and several industry strikes.I had read the Rotten Tomatoes reviews about F1 and was so excited to see an excellent movie, it was on my list of things to do on vacation!What happened? The story was overly simple. The few exciting scenes were predictable. I was even disappointed with Pitt's acting.Yes, the movie continues to do well, and everyone seems to think it IS the excellent movie I was hoping it would be. I am not one for conspiracy theories, but it seems like the movie critics want to preserve their livelihoods, and if they give mediocre reviews to the few movies worth seeing, they may be contributing to the demise of their own industry - if that makes any sense.Anyway, I was glad to have a date night with my wife, but we both agree, this movie is average and would rate it as "stream worthy." Sorry to the majority of you out there. The emperor has no clothes!

title: F1: The Movie - 9/10
content:
1. Overall Plot - 8/10
The plot of this movie was very cool to watch. It was about an old F1 driver, that has since moved on from F1, returning to help a losing team. I like how him coming on humbles and teaches the rookie. This movie is a very fun watch, but just not the most realistic in terms of plot. F1 would never be doing all the stunts they do in this film.2. Entertainment / Engagement - 9/10
This movie is very fun to watch, and keeps you pretty entertained throughout. There is a scene or two in the middle that slows down the movie a lot and is a bit boring, but that doesn't make it any less good of a movie.3. Emotion / Investment in Story - 9/10
There is a lot of emotion in this. We see bits and pieces about the character's pasts, and that helps drive the story a lot. But because it is an all new story, there isn't any grounds to make that emotion hit as hard as it could. It does more than enought to get the job done still.4. Theme and Depth of Story - 9/10
Second chances and redemption are the theme of this movie. It is all about Hayes getting his second chance at winning an F1 race, and the team having a redemtion from being in last. The story goes pretty deep with some of the characters relationships with each other.5. Characters - 10/10
I thought all of the characters were done perfectly. They all have a purpose of driving the story along. I like how we see the rookie change throughout the movie, and how he grows up. It is cool to see Hayes rub off on the people on the team.6. Acting / Writing - 10/10
All of the acting was great. No complaints.7. Cinematography / Sets - 10/10
The cinematography and way this movie was shot was very, very cool. It was shot like Top Gun Maverick where they actually raced cars and used real footage instead of just CGI. During the races there was some very good and impressive camera work. And some scenes are framed together very cool.8. Score / Sound - 10/10
This movies soundtrack is pretty good. And the sound design is great. It is very fun to listen to the F1 cars the whole movie. And there is a few scenes that the way they did the sound was super cool.9. Editing / Pace - 7/10
The editing was all great. I love how the races were edited together so it was just tons of action. The pacing could have been a bit better. The movie is either rushed or slow, with no in between. But it was still very good.10. VFX/Practical Effects - 10/10
Using actual cars and races instead of CGI was amazing. If there even was any CGI in this movie, it is completely unnoticable.Average Score:
9.2/10My Overall Rating and Final Thoughts:
9/10
This movie was a very fun watch. I love a good racing movie. It is better than Gran Turismo, but not as good as Ford V Ferrari. Everyone should watch this in theaters with the sound design alone. And seeing it being completely filmed for Imax was cool too.

title: Great Movie with Great Actors
content: As the Top Gun Maverick this as well was masterpiece.F1 is an exhilarating ride that manages to capture both the blistering speed of Formula 1 racing and the human stories behind the helmets. Brad Pitt delivers a convincing performance as a veteran driver returning to the grid, bringing depth and charisma to a role that could have easily slipped into cliché. The real standout, however, is the film's breathtaking realism.Director Joseph Kosinski's decision to shoot on real racetracks, during actual Grand Prix weekends, pays off spectacularly. The racing scenes are some of the most immersive ever put to film-forget CGI-heavy car movies, this one makes you feel like you're in the cockpit. The use of cutting-edge camera technology and the sound design are nothing short of phenomenal.The supporting cast, especially Damson Idris as the young rising star, adds emotional weight and keeps the story grounded. The mentorship dynamic feels authentic and gives the film heart, even if some of the plot beats are a little predictable.Where F1 slightly falters is in character development outside the track-it occasionally leans on sports movie tropes and doesn't always explore the complexities of the modern Formula 1 world. But these are minor issues in an otherwise electrifying film.Whether you're a hardcore F1 fan or just love a well-crafted sports drama, this movie is well worth your time. It's a love letter to the sport, made with clear passion and respect.

title: F1 - The Hollywood Version
content: Having seen years of F1 on a TV screen, watching this movie was a nice, entertaining break from the actual goings on within F1. The timing of the streaming availability was just right, too, shortly after the season ended.Many of the reviewers seem to think the movie was a bit of a joke, i.e., not realistic. I couldn't disagree more. The cars were real, the tracks were real, many of the drivers who weren't stars of the movie were, in fact, real (F1 stars), as were many of the officials and teams. The story, not so real, but this isn't, "All The Presidents Men," so maybe lighten up on the criticism.Hearing the engines roaring in and out of the pits, down the straights, through the chicanes and seeing the incredible action was comforting, knowing that the season had ended and wouldn't occur again for months. And, knowing that I could stream this again, any time I needed some F1, is nice.I grew up watching, Le Mans and Grand Prix, the McQueen/Garner movies considered the industry standard of race flix. If you didn't like them, you probably won't like this, either. Those were made by people who raced who wanted the rest of us to know the good parts of racing; they were right.In this effort, the makers avoided for us the actual mess that the real F1 is; i.e., the anal retentive stewards who intervene, unnecessarily, who dish-out penalties in such an inconsistent manner that you can easily tell who the preferred drivers are. And, of course, they avoided the undeniable ways that the F1 supreme overlords break their own rules when they deem it necessary.This movie is all the good parts of F1, with some Hollywood glamour on top. Enjoy it, because it's enjoyable. At $200M, it better be.If you do, enjoy it I mean, and you choose to follow F1 on a regular basis, prepare to be disappointed. As movies go, this one is better than the real thing.
