title: Cinematic Masterclass From Masters
content: Top Gun Maverick showed us what can happen when technology meets cinematographers hell-bent on making stunning and real cinema. The line between effects and reality blur in a quality just not seen prior and F1 is a worthy cinematic successor.F1 is a pretty simple movie, and one layered with loves and traditional values. It's cool to love your kids, it's cool to love racing, it's cool to love your job and it's even cool to get into a fight with a lifelong friend and find your way through it. That's the heart of this movie, and why it can connect with racing fans and amateurs alike. Don't get it wrong though, there is plenty of diversity in the film, and handled with purpose. Every character is given something, even if brief, to make them relevant. There are surprisingly few cardboard cut-outs in the film, and they're purposely part of the scenery.While the story is serviceable and at times hammy, the reason this film will endure is because of its intense marriage between dynamic, sit-in-the-driver's-seat action and brilliant sound engineering that dominates the 9 races that make up the film.Brad Pitt is okay, not his best, but then his character is not the most likeable in the film, nor is his wardrobe. Plunging, open necklines, an unhealthy obsession with meaningless and random tattoos and a couple of terry cloth shirts that should never have seen the light of day may have been a part of his persona, or just poor taste. But Brad isn't the anchor of the film, his relationship with his former teammate and troubled owner of AXP racing is, and the supporting roles perform brilliantly. Did they know what they were getting when they cast Kerry Condon? Who knows, but she delivers, and so well that she dominated the trailer.Jerry Bruckheimer, Hans Zimmer and Joseph Kosinski are a great combination. Looking at the lengthy list of producers on this film (24), one wonders what it took to make this film. Hollywood has long been struggling with studios folding or abandoning established directors. Compare with Moana, (3) or Thunderbolts with 8, which is already well above average.Those desiring a reason to be in the theatre, F1 delivers in a way seldom justifying the expense. The cast, writers, production crew, producers and sound engineers all deserve praise for creating a masterclass in cinematic action.

title: An F1 film for people who've never watched an F1 race
content: I half expected this film to be great. The best part of it was the cinematography, the action shots during the races were brilliant and the soundtrack was perfect. Together that's what earned it the three stars for me. Unfortunately the rest of the film was terrible. The storyline was weak, the few cameos from actual F1 drivers seemed out of place and forced but the absolute killer for me was Crofty and Brundles commentary during race scenes. They literally explained everything happening on screen in such ridiculous detail that that's when it became obvious this film was not meant for actual F1 fans. It was clearly made with the new wave of F1 'fans' who have entered through DTS and treat F1 like another cheap, tacky reality show.It's a real shame. This film could have been great.

title: Kosinski Really Gets Speed!!!
content: Joseph Kosinski has mastered the blueprint for high-octane filmmaking. He just knows how to shoot speed, tension, and adrenaline...frames packed with dopamine-inducing shots. Movies like this remind you why you started watching 'hem in the first place week in, week out.Acting was great across the board. Javier Bardem and Brad Pitt both just own every scene they're in. Idris holds his ground too. The character drama around the races kept things engaging, and yeah, as an F1 purist you always want more on-track action but anyway.Those camera angles were insane, felt so damn real. You actually feel the speed, the danger, the whole aura of these modern engineering beasts. Also, the score by Zimmer during the racing scenes was incredible, some of those hits just land perfectly. It's 2.5 hrs long and I was locked in the whole time, that tells you something.Legit refreshingly beautiful for an avid F1 fan. Absolute peak cinematic spectacle of its kind. Really loved the way this came together.

title: F1 is not the best racing movie, but not the worst racing movie!
content: Racing movies can be great, but not always deliver! I love racing movies. I love racing movies that are made from video games like Gran Turismo. Unfortunately even if the action scenes are great and I'm racing movie combat the drama doesn't always deliver. So when it comes to F1 starring Brad Pitt and directed by Joseph Kosinski, does stack up to some of the all-time great racing movies. Well the answer is yes and no. If you thought you were going to get a simple answer, sorry to disappoint... sometimes there isn't a simple answer. I like the movie F1, but it's not the best racing movie and while it is a great cinematic experience, it's trying too hard to be a great race movie but falls short in a lot of ways. It does have a great cast starring Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem, along with Damson Idris, and the sensational Kerry Condon. But a great cast can't always deliver ahead, they can only make the movie better than it really is and for me that's the story of F1. Let me explain because while I do not think movies like Days of Thunder are necessarily great, they're very entertaining. Ford v Ferrari is probably my favorite racing movie and that film delivered in every possible way. But I also love Steve McQueen's Le Mans... it is a very underrated racing movie but ahead of its time when capturing actual racing from the 24-hour Le Mans race. That film doesn't get the credit it deserves. So there are quite a few racing movies that F1 is judged against. And some of those films are hard to measure up to... I admit that freely when reviewing this phone.The story of F1 is about a has-been race car driver who was once a young Phenom on the F1 circuit 30 years prior to this story. He is called in to help a rookie driver get better and to make the racing team relevant. Brad Pitt does a great job of playing this character, but I have to confess as much as I love him as an actor, he doesn't do the movie any favors by essentially playing the same character he did from Quentin Tarantino's Once Upon a Time in Hollywood. The character of Sonny Hayes in this movie is essentially Cliff Booth. But there are a lot of things going for this particular movie. You can call at the Top Gun of racing and it's definitely that. The best part of the film is the racing scenes. Just like with Top Gun: Maverick, the action is absolutely incredible while in the race cars and around the track to where you feel like you're in the race. It is probably one of the best shot racing movies out there. And it's those action scenes that prop up the story. Now don't get me wrong I'm not going to completely blast this film and say not to go see it. It's definitely fun on the big screen. It's meant to be seen on the big screen, However, the film has a lot of problems that keep it from being a great racing movie starting with the length.This is not a movie that needed to be two and a half hours. It's a story that I can easily have been told in 2 hours, if that. It's a predictable story. We know who's going to win. We know the tropes built within the story. Old veteran comes to help the young kid who doesn't understand him, but eventually they become teammates. Didn't Sylvester Stallone do a movie just like that where he was driving Formula 1. I knew how Ford v Ferrari was going to end because I knew the history and that movie didn't feel as predictable. Brad Pitt has done a lot of great movies and I definitely think he deserve the Oscar for Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, But I don't need him playing the same character, only this time he plays a race car driver instead of a stuntman. And of course his character is going to get with the main girl. Of course the younger and better driver will become a better teammate. There's nothing shocking in this movie. There's no heart. Gran Turismo turned out to be one of my favorite racing movies not only because it was based on a true story of a gamer who became a racer, but the story of his father accepting his ability when he thought gaming was a waste of time. It was very easy to root for the characters in Gran Turismo and I'm not saying that you're not going to root for Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes, but with a predictable story, why root for them at all when you know what's going to happen. This film follows the same formula as Top Gun. In some ways I think Joseph Kolinski was remaking Top Gun:Maverick but for racing. Sure, go right ahead, but at least Top Gun: Maverick had a lot of heart and a great emotional connection to the main characters especially with Val Kilmer coming back to play Iceman. There's none of that in this movie.F1 is a great action movie...that's the best compliment I can give it. It looks great on the big screen. It feels great when you're seeing it in an IMAX type of theater and you feel close to the action, but to be a good movie you have to have more than just great action scenes. Yes, Days of Thunder, Ford v Ferrari, and Gran Turismo are my favorite racing movies, but they all have that one thing that F1 doesn't... heart. It's the thing that makes you invest in the characters and root for them. You don't find that in F1. If I'm being honest you find a lot of clichés with the characters that get overshadowed by the fantastic action during the races. There are definitely better formula 1 racing movies out there. "Rush" is another great one. But don't go into this movie expecting it to be anything you need, check it out because you love to see fantastic racing action on the big screen. And I think this movie will be largely forgotten in the years to come compared to the ones I've mentioned in this review. The drama is average at best while the action sequences are great, and just makes it an just okay movie that's still fun to watch even if just once. Maybe F1 would have been better as a series on a streaming channel.

title: Top Gun: Asphalt Edition
content: I really enjoyed the movie. Story-wise, it is not original. It is very cliché. It feels similar to movies like Rocky III, Rocky IV, Rocky Balboa (2006), and Top Gun Maverick in terms of its plot. However, the filmmaking is amazing. The use of real race footage, new camera technology created to make everything look more realistic, and the fact that Brad Pitt and Damson Idris actually trained for racing and drove real F2 cars (looks like F1) made the movie feel very authentic. All of these details made it great. The story is predictable, as I said, and not very original. But just like Top Gun, it feels real and well-made. The director is the same, so that makes sense. I have never really liked or watched Formula 1 before, even though in recent years my country has also hosted a Grand Prix (in Baku.) This movie might not be perfect for hardcore F1 fans. But for people who do not watch or even dislike F1, like me, the film is still very entertaining. It does not get lost in technical details and instead delivers great action and racing scenes without becoming boring. Overall, this is one of the best movies of the year.

title: Leaves any fan with a smile and the urge to accelerate
content: As a Formula 1 fan, F1: The Movie was everything I wanted to see and more. The film manages to transform the adrenaline of the tracks, the drama of the teams, and the intensity of the rivalries into a cinematic experience that makes your heart race. There are moments when you can almost smell the hot asphalt, hear the roar of the engines, and feel that tension before the green light.The film perfectly balances action and emotion. It's not just about fast cars; it's about pressure, ambition, sacrifice, and the constant pursuit of perfection. For those who follow the sport, there are delightful details: strategies, behind-the-scenes moments, egos, human flaws, and that feeling that every millisecond counts. And that makes everything even more engaging.The direction is dynamic, the cinematography is vibrant, and the racing sequences are so well-constructed that they seem real. I left the theater feeling like I had watched an entire race, complete with risky turns, epic overtakes, and moments of pure emotion.And best of all: the film opens doors to more stories within this universe. It makes you want to keep following these characters, these teams, and this incredibly addictive world.A film that does justice to Formula 1 and leaves any fan with a smile and the urge to accelerate.

title: Decent watch but not great
content: Good watch but I had high expectations from this one. For me, in any sports based movie, you have to be careful how much sport are you actually showing vs the drama. The movie must be primarily about the drama between lead characters and the sport should be a backdrop or theme. F1 race sequences are great but the movie lacked a solid story arc with logical blocks and follows a linear and flat storyline with a series of races with some good dialogue and a bit of drama between the race sequences. Rush which released in 2013 has set a very high bar for me in this genre. Hans Zimmer composed the music for Rush too and it is still one of the best scores ever. This film couldn't beat Rush either in drama or the music. However, I still had a good time watching this movie and Brad Pitt is great.

title: A Visually Striking, Yet Familiar Ride
content: Joseph Kosinski's "F1: The Movie" (2025) attempts to capture the high-octane thrill and drama of Formula 1 racing, and while it delivers on visual spectacle, it ultimately feels like a somewhat familiar cinematic experience. The film excels in its stunning race sequences and the immersive sound design, putting viewers right into the cockpit. However, its narrative often treads well-worn sports drama territory, following predictable arcs without introducing significant new insights into the world of F1 or its characters. The performances are solid, but the dialogue can occasionally lean into clichés, preventing a truly fresh or deeply engaging emotional connection. Instead of a groundbreaking sports epic, it offers a competently made, visually impressive, but ultimately safe portrayal of the racing world.Is it worth watching? If you're a devoted Formula 1 fan or simply enjoy visually impressive action sequences, it might offer some entertainment. However, for a general audience seeking a truly innovative or emotionally profound sports film, it might feel a bit uninspired.

title: Best sports movie of the decade
content: F1: The Movie is the first film I can think of that really gets the Formula One racing scene right. It is a formulaic sports film (no pun intended) and not directly based on real characters nor 100% accurate, but it is very well done for what it is (honestly, many fans have been waiting for a film like this) and well worth watching. It is dramatized and there is back story and conflict with the main characters to keep the audience engaged and even a little romance, but it is a race car film at its core. The racing and crash scenes are very well done, and some scenes were even filmed at real Formula One races. The cast is top notch, with great directing and believable performances. It may be just a tad long for some viewers.

title: A masterpiece
content:
F1 isn't just a racing movie, it's an immersive, pulse-pounding experience that puts you right in the cockpit, emotionally and physically. From the first lap to the final scene, I was completely hooked.Direction & Cinematography:
Joseph Kosinski proves again that he's a master of immersive action. The use of real F1 cars, the real F1 champions, combined with innovative camera placement inside the vehicles, delivers visuals that feel breathtakingly real.The soundtrack is phenomenal.Acting:
Brad Pitt delivers a nuanced, magnetic performance as the seasoned driver. His presence commands every scene, but it's the chemistry between him and Damson Idris that truly stands out. Their mentor-mentee dynamic is heartfelt and believable, adding depth to the high-speed chaos.Racing & Action:
The race scenes are simply epic. You can feel the tension, the speed, the danger, it's all there, and it's all real. There's a grit and intensity that's rare, even in big-budget racing films. The use of practical effects and real racing locations makes a world of differencePacing & Story:
The story is classic but told with fresh energy.

title: High-Speed Thrills , Low-Depth Story
content: F1 is a thrilling ride that throws you straight into the driver's seat of a Formula car. The cinematography is by far the movie's strongest element - dynamic, stylish, and genuinely immersive. It's refreshing to see a racing film shot in such an exhilarating way, all while staying grounded in realism and successfully conveying the emotions, themes, and chemistry you'd expect from this world.Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem, and Damson Idris all deliver solid performances that fit the tone of the film well.Unfortunately, the story and characters are painfully generic. I expected the relationship between the veteran and the rookie to have much more depth, but it's handled in a very barebones, superficial way. The plot as a whole is forgettable, even uninspiring at times, and most character motivations - aside from Sonny (Brad Pitt) - are either unclear or simply vanish in the rush of high-speed driving.I found myself wishing for more meaningful character development and stronger writing, rather than the film putting unnecessary focus on "representation" at the expense of natural, organic growth for its characters.Still, despite its shortcomings, F1 is fun and delivers on the excitement it promises. For me, it earns a respectful 6.8/10.

title: Some good moments, but just not compelling enough.
content: My partner is a big Formula One fan, so we were looking forward to 'F1', but unfortunately both of us were disappointed in the movie. I can't quite put my finger on why exactly we didn't enjoy the movie as much as we'd have liked to, but I think the movie in general just wasn't thrilling enough.Brad Pitt stars as Sonny Hayes. His back story tells us he was a promising racer whose career was abruptly ended by a crash. Now, 30 years later and after winning the 24 Hours of Daytona Race, he is approached by his old friend Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), who owns a racing team and wants Sonny to race for him.Sonny meets his team mate Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), a cocky and ambitious rookie driver. Instead of working together on the track, they are competitive resulting in both crashing. And so the tension between these drivers never really eases up, and although both were meant to be protagonists, I found myself rooting for neither of them. Ruben was the only character I enjoyed and rooted for, and is so wonderfully portrayed by the charming Javier Bardem.Female technical director Kate (Kerry Condon) serves as the film's romantic interest, whom Sonny becomes romantically involved with, but with very little chemistry between the characters, I found this love story forced into the film and not believable.I know nothing about racing procedures but found it odd that Sonny was able to compete in the Grand Prix after just one test race (in which, by the way, he didn't even do so well). Several other events in the movie also caused me to raise an eyebrow, questioning its authenticity.Thanks to newly developed camera technology we feel part of every race, and the cinematography is fantastic. The film has a good score compliments of Hans Zimmer, and the editing is great.The action sequences are good, but I guess I expected more. 'Gran Turismo' for instance delivered far better crash sequences, and it was a much better paced movie as well. 'F1' was entertaining enough for a once off viewing, and I believe it would have been faster paced and possibly more interesting if it was about 30 minutes shorter. The constant bickering between Sonny and Joshua started feeling like deja vu.

title: A Little Above Average !
content: Visually stunning with incredible racing sequences and seamless integration of real F1 teams and racers. title: Oh boy! What a blast
content: I had the pleasure of watching this in IMAX. Not sure how the experience holds up at home, but I'll update this if needed.Let's break it down into camera work, CGI, and story.Starting with the story - it's pretty solid.Yes, some might say, "This would never happen in Formula 1," but I beg to differ. In the past, we've seen drivers go from last to the podium in a single race. It takes a lot of things going right, and the film uses various tricks to add just enough drama to keep it engaging. For me, the whole "he's young and reckless" angle was a bit overdone at times - especially when it crossed into "now he's also lying to himself and others." But overall, the story works. It's entertaining, and I'll definitely watch it again. Way better than any F1 movie before - no question about that.Now, camera work & CGI: Boy oh boy. What a blast.How did they even do this? If you've followed real races, you'll recognize certain maneuvers - and yes, in some moments they clearly used original footage and CGI-ed Sunny or Josh into the car. But in many scenes, you can tell: this isn't CGI.This is pure racing. Pure cinematography.A 20 out of 10. Absolutely mind-blowing. Hopefully this sets a new standard for high-quality racing films.Overall: What a blast!I'll be watching it again at home - surround system turned all the way up. Awesome!

title: Fast Cars, Slow Plot
content: Brad Pitt and Damson Idris share decent chemistry, but their arcs are straight out of the Racing Movie 101 playbook: grizzled mentor clashes with rebellious prodigy, complete with daddy issues and conflict that builds toward predictable high-speed peril. Kerry Condon brings warmth as the no-nonsense team engineer, though her role is sidelined for track action. Meanwhile, Tobias Menzies hams it up as the corporate antagonist demanding "win at all costs."The racing almost makes up for it-cars scream through Monaco and Baku with beautiful drone shots-but the drama between laps feels like placeholder dialogue. Even Lewis Hamilton's cameo as himself can't distract from the undercooked rivalry subplot.F1 2025 is a solid popcorn flick for racing enthusiasts, with hyper-realistic sound design and camera work that puts you right in the driver's seat, but those hoping for a gripping narrative might leave the theater feeling like they've seen this race before.

title: Solid
content: The only reason I checked this out was because it was made by Joseph Kosinski, the guy who directed Top Gun Maverick. And it didn't disappoint. Great underdog story. The build-up, progression, and pacing was very well-done, and took it's time to introduce us to the characters, the way F1 works, and was structured in a way where even the average layperson with no F1 knowledge, could follow and enjoy. Hans Zimmer - a name now synonymous with quality - did an amazing job with the score, complementing and enhancing the scenes perfectly. It wasn't overbearing where it took you away from the visuals, but not so subtle that you wished there was more music. And the style of music he chose was perfect. Like Top Gun 2, the love story was done subtly and with class. Wasn't the focus of the movie, and was approached in a mature, light, and realistic way, with the chemistry initially apparent, but had a natural progression to it. Overall, great directing, and you walk away feeling like it was a solid movie overall, just like you did in Top Gun Maverick.

title: F1 Fan Review
content: As a huge F1 fan, this was honestly hard to watch. A big missed opportunity.

title: F1 Redefines What It Means to Win
content: I walked into F1: The Movie expecting speed and spectacle. What I didn't expect was a quiet, emotional depth that left me reflecting on life, ambition, and what we often misunderstand about success. Brad Pitt's portrayal of Sonny Hayes-a seasoned racer chasing not a title, but a fleeting moment of peace-delivers more than thrills. It offers wisdom.The film flips the usual racing narrative. Winning here isn't about aggression or dominance; it's about stillness and letting go. One of the most powerful scenes shows Sonny describing a moment that feels like flying-calm, weightless, untouched by pressure or ego. That moment, more than any trophy, is the victory.As someone who studies stress and performance through something I call Tension Theory, I saw in this film a striking parallel: that we are often pulled by the illusion that success comes from constant striving. But as F1 beautifully shows, the real power is in knowing when to slow down, when to support others, and how to return to what truly matters.I wrote in detail on this in a Psychology Today article: "Why F1: The Movie Changes How We See Success."

title: F1.. #1
content:
Brad Pitt delivers a powerhouse performance in F1 - effortlessly cool, emotionally raw, and totally in command. The moment he enters the screen, you're locked in. Whether you're a hardcore Formula 1 fan or just here for the thrill, this film pulls you in from the first rev of the engine.The racing scenes are next level - intense, beautifully shot, and real enough to make you feel the G-force. Every corner, crash, and comeback keeps your heart racing.The dialogues hit hard, never dragging, always pushing the story forward. It's not just about racing - it's about pride, loss, redemption, and grit.Direction and production? Smooth like a pit stop in Monaco. Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) brings his sleek, cinematic style with some of the best racing visuals ever put on screen. The camera work makes you feel like you're inside the cockpit. The sound design? Chef's kiss.A movie for all ages, especially the younger crowd who love speed, action, and strong character arcs. But it's more than adrenaline - there's heart, drama, and moments that stay with you long after the credits roll.Final Verdict:
F1 is a cinematic joyride - fast, fierce, and full of soul. One of the best racing movies in years.

title: F1 Review
content:
8.5/10
After surprising us with a legacy sequel that ended up being one of 2022's best, Joseph Kosinski felt like the right choice for a sports drama of this scale. Diving into the people behind these impressively fast machines. It explores how isolating it can be to dedicate your entire life to a career that demands everything from you, and how hollow that devotion can feel when your time at the top comes to an earlier end than expected.I'm not a big car guy, though exciting race sequences are universal. After countless F&F entries it can feel like there's little left to do differently. That's where Kosinski enhances the racing with stunning visuals and precise direction. From shifting driver seat POVs to alongside the cars, then cutting to pit crews and crowd reactions. He captures the emotional roller coaster of racing just as much as the physical danger, making each lap feel consequential rather than repetitive.The film plays like a fusion of Top Gun and Days of Thunder, which is fitting with Jerry Bruckheimer producing all four. There's really a perfect opportunity here for a future crossover where Cruise's character from Days and Pitt's, face off one last time while mentoring a younger rising star. Incorporate a historic feud where maybe he's part of how Pitt made it to the top in the past. Kosinski at the helm, that kind of legacy driven combination of films feels less like fan service and more like destiny.With Hans Zimmer on the soundtrack and Brad Pitt on the racetrack, F1 evolves into a story about legacy, risk, and what it costs to keep chasing the finish line long after the world expects you to slow down. Even if you don't care about the sport, it makes sure you feel the speed and mindset that come with it.

title: Predictable? Yes. Boring? Never.
content: F1: The Movie, on paper, seems like a formulaic and clichéd underdog story packaged as an elaborate promotional campaign for F1, but it pulls it off so well that you have to give it credit.The film, much like an actual F1 race, has its twists, sharp turns, and thrills, keeping you on the edge of your seat. At the same time, it leans unapologetically into one of the oldest cinematic tropes: the classic underdog story. Some might call it predictable, but it's undeniably fun. After all, what's entertainment if the hero doesn't win in the end?The movie creates an atmosphere that transports you from the audience's seat straight into the cockpit of an F1 car. It never tries to sound overly clever or alienate viewers with excessive technical jargon. Instead, it strips the experience down to its bare essentials, speed and drama, enough to hook both die-hard F1 fans and those who've never watched a single race.Zimmer et al.'s thumping score, paired with stylish cinematography, makes the whole experience thoroughly worthwhile.

title: Stunning cinematography with an overcooked story.
content: I'd give the movie Formula One a solid 7 out of 10. Visually, the film is outstanding. The cinematography is impressive, with dynamic camera angles, immersive on track shots, and beautifully composed scenes that truly capture the speed and intensity of racing. From a visual perspective, it is a pleasure to watch and clearly the strongest aspect of the movie. However, the story itself feels overly performative and exaggerated. Many moments are designed purely for drama rather than realism, which hurts the authenticity of the Formula One world. There are too many crashes, too much forced tension, and too many dramatic plot elements that do not reflect how the sport actually operates. Instead of focusing more on real racing strategy, team dynamics, and technical depth, the film often chooses spectacle over substance. While this makes the movie entertaining, it also makes it less believable. Overall, it remains an enjoyable experience thanks to its stunning visuals, but its lack of realism prevents it from reaching its full potential.

title: I Expected A Better Movie
content: After seeing the relativity high rating on IMDb and knowing Brad Pitt was going to be in the movie, I went to watch F1 (2025) thinking it was going to be awesome. But it ended up being just another average movie.If you truly like racing movies, I recommend you watch the best racing movie ever made called Days of Thunder (1990) with the King of Hollywood himself, Tom Cruise.Days of Thunder has NASCAR, but if you specifically want to see an F1 movie, then the best one I can think of is the incredible true story depicted in the movie called Gran Turismo (2023).F1 (2025) has a few racing scenes that were memorable, but other than that, it was a very forgettable movie. One major thing this movie is missing is a real antagonist. They somehow added an executive villain near the end of the movie, but it was poorly done, and it was the main reason why this movie sucked. It would've been way better had they added a bad guy racing against the main characters. Instead of doing that, the writers turned this film into a "how to manipulate the racing rules to finally win something" type of movie.

title: Enjoyable movie, but lacks character depth
content: Movie overall is quite enjoyable. There are plenty car and race scenes, so no complaints from that side. Very often those sport movies are lacking in showing the actual sport and rely too heavily on the characters. Funnily enough this movie could have profited a lot from more character depth. The characters are there, but their growth isn't really earned. Especially the mentoring of the younger driver should have been more at the centre of the movie. His growth. His journey. At the same time Brad Pitt's character stays basically the same through the entire movie. I liked the character of the female engineer, but she as well would have profited from some more growth ("I know that much about building cars, but now I have this experienced driver, whose knowledge I can profit from, to build an absolute monster of a car"). Why is this story just glossed over? Show more of it. That's what F1 is. A team sport.... This movie could have been an absolute spectacular blast, but unfortunately it's just "good". Still a good watch, but could have been great. The ingredients are all there....

title: F1: The Movie is a cinematic adrenaline rush
content: F1: The Movie is a cinematic adrenaline rush that earns every bit of its 9/10 IMDb rating. Having witnessed its IMAX glory in Manhattan, I'm blown away by the technical mastery-from the visceral roar of engines captured via 15 cameras embedded in custom F2 cars to Hans Zimmer's pulse-pounding score that syncs with every gear shift. Director Joseph Kosinski's immersive approach, including "nine-minute" during real races , makes every turn feel like a gamble with destiny.Brad Pitt, as washed-up legend Sonny Hayes, delivers a career-defining performance. His weathered charm and raw vulnerability breathe life into a character teetering between redemption and ruin. But the true revelation is Kerry Condon as Kate McKenna. Her portrayal of the team's brilliant technical director transcends the film's masculine bravado. Whether dismantling ego-fueled rivalries with icy pragmatism or sparking chemistry with Pitt in a smoky bar scene , Condon embodies quiet power, proving she's the emotional engine driving the story.While the third act leans into formulaic sports-movie tropes, the sheer spectacle-rain-drenched Spa races, pit-stop chaos, and IMAX's 360° cockpit views-overwhelms any narrative flaws. For F1 enthusiasts, it's a love letter; for casual viewers, a heart-racing joyride. Condon and Pitt's magnetic partnership, paired with groundbreaking filmmaking, cements this as a modern classic. 9/10-a triumph on and off the track.

title: An adrenaline rush full of twists and energy!
content: F1 - Movie ReviewDirected by Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun Maverick & Tron), I had high hopes to get pumped up and have an experience of a lifetime, and I was correct! The movie had so much life and energy that I couldn't help but sit in awe and watch, my eyes fixed on the passing Formula cars as if I were part of it. Brad Pitt did an amazing job as the old veterinarian racer, finding his lost glory. With just the perfect hint of Hans Zimmer's masterpiece score (first time I heard a Hans score on the big screen), and the beautiful yet fast cinematography, I was just excited to experience this in this lifetime. The amazing duo story of new with old working together while having their banter was everything today's movies needed.This movie has literally piqued my interest in F1 with its amazingly creative writing, grueling, ruthless racing, and of course, passion to tie it all up. This might be the closest most of us get to experiencing an F1 race.PROS:Creative writing.Simplistic plot for non-F1 fans to follow.Close-up, energetic cinematography.Hans Zimmer's goosebumps soundtracks.Good bit of metaphors and character growth.CONS:Bits of timelapses/compilations thrown in between, which in my opinion could've been edited better.RATING: 9/10.

title: Entertaining, but rather FORMULA1-C !!
content: The movie was paced well and entertaining, often with good drivers' vantage point cinematography, but it was too well-worn a story, of a young upstart versus a grizzled veteran, a conniving quasi-villain, and an "against-all-odds" narrative. As we watched it, my son and I readily predicted many scenes in the sequence of scenes, albeit with a few, unexpected surprises. Whether you enjoy the film will depend on your expectations - it was somewhat alluring as watchable light fluff, but, also, as watchable light fluff, there were a lot of hack, "cardboard" characters to fill out a somewhat predictable narrative. Ironically, a few days before watching it, there were conversations in the household about how good an actor Brad Pitt is, especially in "Snatch" and in "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood", but his performance in this movie was pretty pedestrian, but, hey, who wouldn't do it for the $30M salary for a mailed-in performance. Wasn't in any way a bad performance, rather just a passable perfunctory one, and in a cliche-riddled movie. Same comment regarding Haviar Bardem - two stars who generally are top of their field, but who, in this case, delivered narrowly-scripted formulaic roles comprised of quick shifts between joy and consternation with little depth behind either. All in all, worth a watch for its entertainment value, and all the moments of "Look - there's so and so from the true F1 field of drivers, team principals, and commentators", but hardly a film that would leave an impression that lingers beyond its watching.

title: Soundtrack and speed make it worth watching
content: My review of "F1" (2025)What motivated me to watch F1 was mainly its soundtrack featuring artists like Tate McRae, Madison Beer, Ed Sheeran, and Myke Towers. Plus, I think Brad Pitt is really handsome, and my mom likes him too, so it was a movie we were both curious about.Brad Pitt's performance was good - even though he's older now, he still has that charm he had when he was younger. He carried the movie well, and his presence made it enjoyable to watch.My favorite parts were definitely the racing scenes. They were exciting, suspenseful, spectacular, and impressive. The way the film captured the tension on the track kept me intrigued throughout those moments. However, outside of the races, some parts of the movie felt a bit boring or slow, especially the dialogue-heavy scenes.The movie made me feel tension and excitement mostly, but there were times when I found it a little dull. I think the balance could have been better with fewer long conversations and more action.Overall, I wouldn't recommend F1 to just anyone. It's really for people who enjoy Formula 1, car racing, and that kind of adrenaline. If you're into those things, you'll probably enjoy the movie, but if not, it might not grab your attention.

title: Perfect isolated pieces
content: The film impressed me overall, but I couldn't shake the feeling that something was missing -- like there were many great elements working in isolation.. take Hans Zimmer's score... especially in the final 15 minutes it was striking and evocative, clearly echoing the emotional pull of Interstellar. Brad Pitt, however, didn't quite deliver one of his top five performances here. My favourite character however was Javier Bardem's Ruben - but maybe it's just because I love seeing this man in every single movie he is in.What I also loved were the small unexpected moments that made me feel like it was a bit more difficult to predict the exact outcome of the story.

title: This movie succeeds at everything
content: Let me get straight to the point: the race scenes in this movie are incredible. They gave me goosebumps. They made me sit forward with anticipation (I noticed others doing it too). They are filmed beautifully. I feel like it's the closest I can come to actually experiencing it.Let's talk about the musical composer Hans Zimmer. He's the GOATest GOAT of all GOATs. I legitimately wish he scored every movie. I'm glad he did this one. It's an awesome score that elevates every scene and intensifies every emotion.Aside from those standouts, F1 succeeds at everything it tries. The characters, the drama, the emotions, the pacing, etc. And I could not have been more invested/engaged. During the climax I found myself tapping my feet so fast.I had an awesome time with this movie and I think most will, racing fan or not. It's just a great movie.Advice: This is a movie that benefits more than most from IMAX. The entire runtime has an expanded aspect ratio only on IMAX screens. And the larger screen/louder speakers enhance the experience significantly.It was also made for ScreenX with two extra screens on the sides in use for certain scenes. I think this movie would be amazing in that format, and I plan on finding out :)EDIT: I tried it in ScreenX and I think I like it even more than in IMAX. The feeling of speed with the peripherals flying by. Or when the camera swings and your entire vision is moving.(2 viewings, early screening IMAX 6/25/2025, ScreenX 8/21/2025)

title: A High-Octane Triumph of Grit, Speed, and Redemption.
content: Start your engines-F1 isn't just a film, it's a high-octane adrenaline shot to the heart of the sports drama genre. Directed by Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) and filmed for IMAX, F1 delivers on every front, heart-pounding racing sequences (several that put you behind the wheel), nuanced performances, and a grounded emotional story that elevates the spectacle beyond mere speed. It's a rare sports film that feels both authentic and cinematic, with a story that burns rubber but keeps its soul intact.Brad Pitt roars back into the spotlight as Sonny Hayes, a once-great Formula One driver lured back to the grid to mentor a young phenom. It's a role tailor-made for Pitt, who brings charisma, gravitas, and that signature edge of vulnerability.Opposite Pitt is Damson Idris, who delivers a breakout performance as Joshua Pearce, the rising star under immense pressure to prove himself-not just to fans and sponsors, but to himself. Idris more than holds his own against Pitt, and their evolving mentor-protégé dynamic becomes the emotional engine of the film. Their chemistry crackles with tension, pride, and ultimately, respect.Created in collaboration with the FIA, the film feels like a fully immersed experience in the world of Formula One. This is one I had to see in IMAX and I will tell you it is well worth it too. Maybe the best IMAX experience I have ever had. The racing sequences, shot using real cars on real tracks with Pitt and Idris behind the wheel for real, are absolutely breathtaking. Kosinski's mastery of immersive camerawork-often placing the viewer inside the cockpit-makes each race feel dangerous, exhilarating, and visceral. You don't just watch the action-you feel the G-forces.Another thing that stood out to me and was perfectly paired with this film... The sound design, score, and editing are all world-class. Every gear shift, tire squeal, and engine roar hits with bone-shaking intensity, and the musical choices are kinetic without being distracting. F1 is crafted for the big screen, where its speed, scale, and story truly shine.F1 is a turbocharged character driven sports drama that balances realism with style, emotion with spectacle. It's Rush meets Top Gun, with a heart as big as its horsepower. Whether you're a die-hard Formula One fan or a newcomer to the sport, this is one cinematic ride you won't forget and easily cracks my top 5 films for the year.

title: Great, but missing Days of Thunder
content: Stephen King once remarked that the film adaptation of his novel The Shining was akin to "a hot rod without an engine." In doing so, he vocalized an elusive sentiment that many might have felt but never dared to express aloud. His words resonate with my experience watching F1-a cinematic spectacle that dazzled with its visual bravado yet left me yearning for something more profound beneath the surface.Walking into the theater, my expectations were sky-high, fueled by the promise of high-octane thrills and a narrative that would grip both heart and mind. On screen, F1 delivered an exhilarating display, a veritable feast for the senses. It was, without doubt, a cinematic masterpiece, its artistry woven into every frame, each race scene pulsating with life and energy. Yet, amid the roaring engines and sweeping panoramas, there lingered a sense of hollowness-a depth the film aspired to but never fully reached.Sunny, the protagonist, radiated charm and likability, his character crafted to be both relatable and enigmatic. However, his past, hinted at through fleeting images-a solitary photo of him as a boy standing beside his father-felt like an unopened chapter. Perhaps the filmmakers intended this mystery, believing that not every story needs to be told in full. Yet, for me, that photograph was a symbol of untapped potential, a narrative thread left dangling, whispering of a backstory that could have added layers to Sunny's journey.Despite these musings, I found myself enamored with F1 . Its magnetic pull lies in its ability to entertain effortlessly, to sweep the audience into the adrenaline-fueled world of racing where every turn holds the promise of victory or defeat. I would watch it again without hesitation, relishing the spectacle even as I ponder the depths that remained unexplored.Yet, as the credits rolled and the theater lights brightened, my heart drifted back to Days of Thunder. A film that, despite its age, still holds a sacred space in the heart of this race car aficionado. It is there, amidst the echoes of roaring engines and the scent of burning rubber, that my cinematic soul truly resides.

title: Back After 30 Years
content:
Storyline:
The plot is quite predictable, following familiar sports-drama arcs of a fallen star, a comeback, and mentoring younger talent. While it can feel formulaic, the presentation of Sonny's life, struggles, and pursuit of glory remains deeply resonant. It teaches that dreams are worth chasing - as for jp success, fame, and money are just noise compared to doing what you love best.Racing:
The racing sequences are visually and technically stunning, filmed on real circuits with high-end cinematography that delivers an "in-the-cockpit" thrill reminiscent of Top Gun: Maverick. Even non-F1 fans will find it engaging for its sheer speed and immersive experience.Performances:
Brad Pitt brings charisma and gravitas as Sonny Hayes, while Joshua Pierce adds emotional tension and sharp banter. Their dynamic evokes shades of Maverick and Goose. The film succeeds in spectacle and energy, but some of the pit crew characters lack emotional depth - the plot hits expected beats yet sometimes leaves you wondering "why."Pacing:
At around 2 hours and 35 minutes, the runtime feels long and uneven. Some scenes rush by, while others drag, stretching the overall experience. Tighter editing could've made it more impactful without losing momentum.Final Verdict (7.4/10):
If you're in the mood for a high-octane spectacle, F1 delivers pure racing adrenaline with style and scale. However, if you seek a more grounded, gritty, or character-driven motorsport drama, you may find it slightly underwhelming. Still, it's a thrilling and visually immersive ride worth the watch.

title: Masterpiece 🌟
content:
I recently watched the movie F1 and I loved it so much! It was an amazing experience from start to end. The movie is about the world of Formula 1 racing, and it really shows how fast, dangerous, and exciting this sport can be. Everything looked so real, I almost felt like I was sitting inside the car with the racers!One of the best parts of the movie was Brad Pitt's acting. He was just perfect for the role. He played a retired F1 driver who comes back to help a young team. His expressions, emotions, and dialogues were all on point. He didn't feel like an actor, he felt like a real F1 driver. I could really feel his passion for racing. Brad Pitt made the story emotional too, not just about cars and racing. He showed what it means to never give up and follow your passion, even when people say you're too old or not good enough.Another thing I enjoyed was the background music. The music was done by the famous Hans Zimmer, and honestly, it was so powerful and beautiful. Every time the cars were on the track, the music made those scenes even more exciting. It added so much energy and emotion to the movie. Sometimes it was fast and thrilling during races, and sometimes it was soft and emotional during sad or deep moments. Hans Zimmer really knows how to make us feel the movie with his music.But the best part of the whole movie was the racing scenes. Oh my god! They looked so real. The camera work was incredible. You could see the speed, the danger, and the pressure the drivers feel. The sound of the engines, the tires, the wind - it felt like I was actually sitting in the pit stop or driving on the track myself. My heart was beating fast during those scenes. I was so into the movie, I forgot I was watching it on a screen!The movie also shows behind-the-scenes of the F1 world - like how the teams work, the mechanics, the strategy, and the training. I learned a lot, and it made me respect the sport even more. It's not just driving fast - there is a lot of science and teamwork behind it.The story was also inspiring. Brad Pitt's character teaches us that age is just a number, and if you love something, you should go for it. The movie shows both the fun and the hard sides of racing - the wins, the losses, the pressure, the dreams.Overall, F1 is one of the best racing movies I have ever seen. Brad Pitt gave a great performance, Hans Zimmer's music gave me goosebumps, and the racing scenes made me feel like I was in the driver's seat. I would love to watch this movie again, and I totally recommend it to anyone - even if you're not a big fan of racing. After watching this, you might become one.And all the cameos in the movie was awesome!Max Verstappen
Lewis Hamilton
Charles Leclerc.

title: It may not be authentic, but it sure is fun!
content: Hollywood seems to have a slightly tortured history with motor racing films. It seems like the reality is never quite enough, so scriptwriters feel compelled to abandon the pretense of accuracy in favour of hyperbole, presumably assuming that audiences just don't have the patience for a more realistic portrayal.In this respect, F1 falls into many of the same traps as its predecessors in this genre, although F1's approach is perhaps slightly more surprising given the efforts to associate itself with the sport. This loose association with reality was probably apparent to many from the start with the idea of a 60-year-old Brad Pitt being a racing driver, but of course the movie goes further than that. The suspension of disbelief required for an F1 fan to watch this film is considerable.This is not the movie's only problem. Brad Pitt's portrayal of the returning prodigy Sonny Hayes has charm but lacks any real depth. Damson Idris is more compelling as his rookie rival Joshua Pearce, but again the character is myopic and lacks much of a sense of purpose. Javier Bardem does much of the heavy lifting as Hayes' one-time teammate and now source of narrative tension, and Kerry Condon is engaging as the car designer Kate McKenna, but the movie starts to suffer when it ventures away from the racetrack. This is made worse by a plot that manages to be both predictable and nonsensical.And yet, none of this really matters, because the movie is such a lot of fun to watch. The driving sequences are expertly delivered and produce some genuinely exciting, even thrilling moments. You aren't really going to care that much when a car spins out, but you are going to enjoy watching it happen. The commentary adds real dynamism to the action, even if it is obviously absurd that the commentators are apparently obsessed with a team of backmarkers.No doubt this film isn't everything the filmmakers hoped it would be, but we can accept it for what it is: a great visual spectacle.

title: Everything is great, but...
content: As a huge Formula 1 fan, I had been eagerly waiting for this movie - and it didn't disappoint. The cast is fantastic, the cinematography is top-notch, and above all, it's a film about F1, which already makes it special for fans like me. Yes, there are some unrealistic moments that wouldn't happen in real racing, but this is a feature film, not a documentary. You need some drama and storytelling, and the filmmakers delivered just that. I was completely engaged from start to finish.I really want to highlight the soundtracks. title: ABSORBING EXPERIENCE!
content: 'F1: The Movie' is an undoubtedly impressive experience that races to the finish line in victorious form! Brad Pitt commits to the role by commanding the main stage as 'Sonny Hayes' with an underdog passion and Damson Idris' 'Joshua Pearce' adds a witty bite to the film in his back-and-forth dynamic with Pitt. Javier Bardem is always a pleasure to watch on the big screen, as is Kosinski's authentic approach when it comes to crafting grandiose visuals. While there are predictable moments of triumph with lofty bits that linger too long, the racing elements are truly a marvel to witness and do a great job cutting into the pacing of slower sequences. It's the tried-and-true comeback story you want with an added blockbuster quality, and it further solidifies Joseph Kosinski as a truly one-of-a-kind action director. 'F1: The Movie' has everything in its supply to create new racing fans and still wow the loyal ones carrying high expectations, through an absorbing experience on the racetrack.

title: Silly, but looks fantastic
content: Firstly, as a motorsport fan, I will view this film differently than an uninitiated audience member. It's impossible not to.Im sure there are films that I love, but have little knowledge of the source material or its basis in reality. title: Must see in IMAX
content: This is a movie that was made for watching in IMAX. Do yourself a favor and at least watch it the first time in that format. You won't be disappointed.So, I'm a casual F1 racing fan; I appreciate the technology involved as well as the immense skill required to excel at this sport. However, I don't have a favorite racing team or driver, etc. Even so, this movie will turn you into an F1 fan.The move itself is beautifully shot, with fantastic action scenes that many times put you right into the driver's seat. The plot is classic summer blockbuster, loads of fun and only gets a bit heavy a few times, but even those scenes are played well and with purpose. One standout that I immediately noticed apart from the cinematography is the fantastic music throughout. Zimmer has come up with a perfect score for this flick and it left me wanting more.Pitt is excellent as an (allegedly) aging racing phenom that is still chasing the thrill of the race. He portrays a confidence from experience, and even a little cavalier attitude about it, but you can see the desire to win in everything he does.Idris does a great job portraying the up-and-coming future star, that has all the fire and guts of youth - and all of the impatience. He does well by the role without overdoing it.I was really impressed with Javier Bardem as the former racer and team owner. He shows he still has a lot of fire for winning, yet knows that winning isn't everything, especially when it comes to the safety of his friend.All in all, I found this to be a fantastic way to kick off the summer movie season.

title: There is a 'participation trophy' joke in this movie
content: This movie makes the absolute most out of a boring and lazy script. You can see and feel the amount of care and work that went into getting these amazing shots. The editing, the sound, all working at peak efficiency, not unlike an actual F1 crew. But unfortunately, on a script level, this is just shallow Top Gun Maverick Boomer Bait about how much we all have to learn from boomers.From a business perspective, boomers and babies are the ones still going to movie theatres so I get it. That doesn't make it any less lazy or boring.This is even more frustrating because of the irony that, not unlike the boomers we all contend with in our real lives, the boomer in this movie, Brad Pitt (not technically an actual boomer I believe, but you get what I'm saying) is a failure. He's got an infinite amount of smugness and confidence in his own correctness and ability to know better than everyone else, and yet he is at the tail end of a life full of nonstop failure. Meanwhile, anyone in the audience under 50 has probably spent their entire lives being similarly lectured by baby boomers who have objectively made the world worse, who has, at least in the US but also many other places, relative to their parents, slowed GDP growth, increased wage disparity, increased national debt, made homeownership and a house with one income impossible, they're the first generation in America who will leave their kids with less than they got, and they were left with maybe the best cards anyone has ever had following their parents. I can go on and on, wars and the environment, but I think people are generally familiar with the idea that baby boomers screwed the world over.Again, they make a joke about participation trophies, that's all you need to know about the level of 'depth' in this movie. Not to mention, participation trophies were invented by baby boomers. I remember getting participation trophies as a kid and hating them, we all did, we all know it was a symbol of our failure. The only reason we got them is because our boomer parents would freak out at the school board or the coaches if we didn't get a trophy. Participation trophies are so parents can feel like they're good parents, or at least so they don't have to have conversations with their children about how it is ok to fail. We as kids never wanted, nor had the power to ask for participation trophies. They were never for kids, they were fragile boomers.Just the same way in this movie, ultimately, the much younger guy offers up the glory to the older guy, the older guy who should be mature and adult enough to never want that and to let the younger guy have it. Not unlike real life, boomers will not let go of the torch. Gen X, Millennials, and now Gen Z, all have their hands on the torch but boomers will not let go of it. And they're so immature we let them do it because, since boomers never group up, us younger people have often had to take the high road and deal with them and placate them. It's just crazy to see a sports movie about the kid making a sacrifice for the old man, the old man who isn't a team player and who doesn't listen to anyone and has failed his entire life, who is ultimately so pathetic that someone half his age feels the need to sacrifice for him and give him, essentially, the participation trophy.Again, there is so much about this movie that works great. Technically it is amazing. It is for sure going to win some editing and sound awards. It's just a shame that all this creative and interesting stuff is built on a foundation of safe and boring boomer bait. Maybe when you spend a lot of money on a movie you want to play it safe and have the type of script that has been proven to make money. But that doesn't make it any less disappointing to watch a movie where some many elements are operating at their peak and so much innovation is occurring, where you feel like you're on the track, and then afterward to have a conversation where a baby boomer mentions 'participation trophies'. Just knowing the script was probably read by a hundred-person-committee of script doctors and producers and not one person was like 'let's figure out something better to say than 'participation trophy'', it barely even makes sense, it's like we're just saying it because we know the people we're pandering to will laugh at the word.Idk, you get it. Technically great but boomer bait.

title: Go see it now! 🏁
content: F1 isn't just a movie-it's a full-throttle experience that blows past every expectation and redefines what a modern racing film can be. It might be the insane realism captured from actual Formula One tracks, or it might just be Brad Pitt's deathly handsome good looks and undeniable charisma-but whatever it is, F1 is the adrenaline shot the summer box office desperately needed.This high-octane Apple Original Films production is revving with power both behind and in front of the camera. Produced by blockbuster maestro Jerry Bruckheimer-who's never met a spectacle he couldn't turn into a crowd-puller-F1 is reportedly made on a budget around $200 million, with some insiders whispering it could've reached as high as $300 million. And guess what? It's already zoomed past the checkered flag with a massive $140 million global opening weekend. That's not just momentum-that's cinematic velocity.Brad Pitt stars as a veteran driver returning to the grid, and from the moment he appears onscreen, he owns the track. But he's not racing alone. Javier Bardem brings his signature gravitas to the role of a wily team owner, while rising star Damson Idris turns in a breakout performance that practically hums with electricity. Together, this trio transforms the story into something far deeper than a simple comeback tale-it's about legacy, risk, trust, and the raw edge of competition.As a longtime fan of racing films, I walked into F1 cautiously optimistic, hoping it could live up to the legacy of John Frankenheimer's Grand Prix (1967)-a film still revered for its groundbreaking race sequences. Frankenheimer also gave us the jaw-dropping car chases of Ronin (1998) and the underrated French Connection II (1975). To my surprise and delight, F1 doesn't just match Frankenheimer's legacy-it may actually surpass it.Director Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) brings a modern precision to the chaos of F1 racing. He told the F1 Explains podcast how the custom car for Pitt was developed with input from none other than Mercedes Team Principal Toto Wolff and seven-time world champion Lewis Hamilton-who also serves as a producer on the film. Their fingerprints are all over the authenticity of this project.Kosinski's mastery behind the camera is on full display. With the use of GoPro tech, track-mounted cameras, and some of the most inventive race cinematography I've ever seen, he puts you right in the cockpit with Pitt. You don't just watch the action-you feel it. The screech of the tires, the vibration of the engine, the breath between turns-it's all captured with jaw-dropping clarity. This isn't just high-definition; it's high emotion.F1 isn't just for racing fans-it's a must-see for anyone who appreciates bold filmmaking, sharp performances, and storytelling that never lets up on the gas. It's fast. It's loud. It's stylish. And best of all, it's got heart.F1 is a modern marvel of action cinema. If Grand Prix invented the car racing movie, F1 just perfected it.🏁 Buckle up.

title: Not bad, but not a masterpiece either.
content: I watched the movie recently, and while I can't say it was bad at all, it didn't quite leave the impact I was hoping for. The film looks amazing - the shots, the atmosphere, and the overall quality are really well done. You can tell a lot of effort went into making it visually stunning.What kept me from fully loving it, though, was the story. Instead of focusing on a young talent versus an experienced veteran, I personally would have found it more exciting to see two drivers around the same age - both in their prime, both willing to do absolutely anything to win. That kind of rivalry feels more intense to me, and I think it would have drawn me in even more.The movie also had a very American touch, which isn't necessarily a bad thing, but it made it feel a bit less raw and authentic than I expected. In the end, I did enjoy it, but for me it was more of an average experience rather than something unforgettable.

title: Exactly what I expected: great cars towing a silly story to a high placing at the finish line
content: Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), an aging ex-F1 driver hired to save a foundering racing team brings his unorthodox 'win any-way-you-can' style to the track, slowly winning the respect and/or affection of the team's young hot-shot rookie driver (Damson Idris) and the team's less-young but fine-looking technical director (Kerry Condon). The plot and script are an entertaining blend of sports-movie clichés and meet-cute romances/bromances and cast is perfectly suited to their aesthetic but 2D characters. The on-board driving footage is state-of-the-art and the Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA) was heavily involved in the film's production, so there are lots of great pit- and track-footage and cool cameos, but no 'villains' and the potential dangers of the sport seem downplayed (or the FIA's safety measures up-played - a slightly injured driver is soon back on the track after his car dramatically flies off the track and disintegrates in a ball of flame). Despite being predictable, implausible and a bit goofy, the story is entertaining and gear-heads will enjoy the racing scenes (if they don't get too nit-picky). F1 makes a great companion piece to 1966's 'Grand Prix' which also featured state-of-the-art (for the time) on-board cinematography, a 'meh' plot, and lots of fabulous cars (that look more like automobiles and less like 'robots in disguise').

title: Fast cars, weak story
content: It's a checkered background veteran, Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), and a young upstart, Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), in a racing drama set in modern times, featuring various Formula 1 racing locations in Europe and elsewhere. Can a controversial aging driver, who hasn't driven Formula 1 cars for 30 years, help mentor a cocky but thus far unsuccessful young driver for an obscure racing team headed by Sonny's old competitor, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem)? There are nine races left in the season, and Ruben may lose his team if it doesn't dramatically improve."F1" follows Sonny as he tries to instill a more aggressive spirit in the team while staying within the rules with mixed results. There's a female technical director, Kate McKenna (Kerry Condon), and a suspicious board member, Peter (Tobias Menzies), to add spice to the story.The last time I watched car racing was stock cars back in the 1960s. "F1" shows off the technology that supports modern car racing and includes lots of high-speed track scenes. The action stuff is impressive, but the story is barely believable. Brad Pitt does his darndest playing the grizzled loner who plays it his way while also helping the youngster, but the viewer never forgets this is Brad Pitt. The obligatory romance is not convincing. Maybe race fans will like it; I was looking at my watch before it ended.

title: A fun pop corn summer spectacle
content: So, the formula (pun intended) is as follows: take the director who directed Top Gun: Maverick, take the biggest star in the world for the lead role and make a film on a similar basis to Top Gun: Maverick. The audience loved that film, so they will surely love this one too. It seems to have worked this time too. This is a typical summer blockbuster, a spectacle for a wider audience that can enjoy the film. Brad Pitt as the lead is great, charismatic and you simply can't help but love him. I personally found it annoying that he is a little too much of the main character in the film, I saw him more as Brad Pitt than the role he played. The rest of the film crew is at a high level, and the relationship between teammates and the rivalry is brilliantly portrayed and you can see the evolution and changes in these two characters (khm khm Top Gun: Maverick khm khm). The formula racing scenes are great, exciting and tense, exactly what a cinema hit needs. I was bothered by the various dances about the rules and violations in Formula 1 because I'm not sure that everything is so realistic and accurate. I think they went more for a movie drama than reality here, which is not a big mistake.A fun pop cron summer spectacle that is also of high quality, that's not something we see often lately.

title: Fun yet predictable movie
content: I went into this expecting beautiful visuals and a predictable story, and I wasn't disappointed.It's cool to see F1 in IMAX on the big screen, the visuals were beautiful and the action scenes felt intense. Suspend your disbelief and all that.The cameos were fun to see. There was less of the real drivers than I expected (I don't think they had any dialogue), but in the end I don't think we needed that anyway.Overall as good as I hoped for.

title: Hollywood in a bad way
content: This film encapsulates everything Hollywood typically aims for-high stakes, emotional manipulation, heroic underdog arcs-and, as a result, it becomes painfully predictable. The outcome is never in doubt (of course they win), the runtime is excessive, and the plot stretches believability far beyond its limits. It feels less like authentic storytelling and more like a formulaic checklist of recycled tropes. There's little originality or risk.Audiences deserve more than comforting repetition. Common people, raise the bar. Demand stories that challenge assumptions and offer something genuinely new.

title: Great F1 based movie
content: In essence this is a story we've seen many times before - bad boy loses everything, is humbled, gets a second chance, spoilery things happen, lots is at stake, can he come through for a friend?I've seen it criticized for not being a real F1 movie - nothing for F1 fans. Its not a documentary! Its a Hollywood action movie set in the F1 world. It uses F1 as its backdrop, but its more about the human condition that whether, say, DRS is the solution for wheel to wheel racing. That said, it does get fairly technical, including tyre degradation, DRS, safety cars, aerodynamics / the dirty air problem and lots of racing.F1 drivers interaction. There are lots of F1 drivers around, but very little (I think just Alonso) direct communication between the actors and drivers. This does feel a little odd. The drivers are seen a lot and feature in the races - but no interaction. As an F1 fan it was great to see actual drivers on the track, racing with the fictional ones. Theres a few segments involving team principles that are great.Top Gun on wheels. Yes. Its been described as Top Gun on wheels. It is, a bit. But stylistically and emotionally is quite different from those movies.Brad Pitt plays ultra cool Brad Pitt very well here.Kerry Condon is great in her designer role.Damson Idris is good. His character is the most predictable - its the story - not him, but he plays it very well. There are various other mostly British character actors who do a great job (Will Merrick, Samson Kayo, Tobias Menzies, Sarah Niles and others).Its been said its unrealistic. Its a dramatisation of F1. It looks very exciting. Its realistic enough, focussing on the exciting bits.The music is great.I really enjoyed every minute of it.Its not about the drivers championship points, not about the constructors championship. It focussing on the drama around the fictional F1 team and Brad Pitt. You don't get bogged down in F1 itself but theres enough fast cars to keep the Dad's happy!

title: 🏁🔥High Octane Dreams And Burnt Rubber 🔥🏁
content: The first thing that hit me was the sound. Not the polite whirr of luxury cars gliding past beach houses, but that chest rattling snarl that makes your bones feel rented. The movie opens like it wants to remind you that Formula One is violence with sponsorships, and honestly, I was grinning like an idiot within seconds.Sonny Hayes walks back into this world carrying the specific weight of men who never planned on surviving their own legend. There is something deeply appealing about watching an older driver strap himself into a machine clearly designed to replace him. The film understands that tension instinctively. Every close up of Sonny in the cockpit feels like a negotiation between muscle memory and fear, and Brad Pitt plays him with a relaxed weariness that reads lived in rather than nostalgic. He is not chasing youth, he is chasing silence, that rare quiet you only get when everything else disappears at 200 miles per hour.Then comes the rookie. Loud, gifted, impulsive, and vibrating with that unearned confidence that only the very young or very fast can afford. Their relationship never pretends to be gentle. It is prickly, competitive, occasionally cruel, and refreshingly honest about how mentorship often starts as mutual irritation. Some scenes crackle with a kind of locker room tension, the kind where nobody says what they mean because saying it would admit vulnerability. I loved that the film lets them be wrong together for a while.Visually, the movie is drunk on speed. Cameras cling to tires, scrape asphalt, and float just long enough inside the cockpit to make your palms sweat. One qualifying lap, shot with a sudden drop in sound where the engine fades and the breathing takes over, stuck with me far longer than it probably should have. Another moment, a pit stop gone slightly off rhythm, turns into a miniature horror scene, all frantic eyes and trembling hands. Even the lighting does quiet storytelling, with night races glowing like pressure chambers while daytime tracks feel almost too exposed.Not everything lands cleanly. A few emotional beats arrive exactly when you expect them, and there is a stretch in the middle where the film seems briefly more interested in mythology than mess. Still, it never loses the thread of why this story matters. This is not about winning a championship. It is about learning when to let go of the steering wheel just enough to trust someone else.By the end, I felt that familiar itch. The one where you want to rewatch certain scenes immediately, not to analyze them, but to relive the feeling in your gut. This is not a perfect racing movie, but it is a deeply satisfying one. Loud, sincere, occasionally corny, and powered by real affection for the sport and the people who ruin their lives for it. I will absolutely take another lap.

title: Physically uncomfortable cheesy dialog. Enjoyed the inside look into F1.
content: A few minutes into the movie, Brad Pitt's character says the first of many lines of dialog that is so corny, instantly predictable, clichéd, and just enough degrees off of authentic human communication that Pitt's eyes and facial muscles betray the actor's real feeling, that he knows these lines are childish and formulaic and that he's suffered through weeks of trying to act these lines without gritting his teeth and rolling his eyes. For children or those who enjoy World Wide Wrestling level of melodrama, no problem. But for cinema fans who love subtlety, it's a viscerally painful experience, you'll feel for Brad's situation, and it will pop you out of the movie experience into that familiar "oh, I'm now watching actors trying to emote correctly."The insider look into the F1 world was fun and times exhilarating. They did a great job at conveying the speed, the view for the cockpit, and in those scenes, it was great to watch Pitt act, even though you could only see his eyes through the massive safety helmet and padding. It ultimately made the movie worth watching.

title: F1 fans need not fear - it's great!
content: Not been to the cinema much in years, but was very much looking forward to this film after two seasons of filming at real F1 events and constant buzz from real f1 drivers, teams and fans.We weren't disappointed.It's high action pretty much from the start, in fact as somebody who hates long films, l'd say there's not a flat, spare or boring minute in the entire 2+ hours of F1 the Movie.The racing scenes are superb, even though the story ignores large parts of real racing rules and the physics of the universe we race in. It doesn't matter that Brad Pitt looks a bit old or that the car he's in isn't even an F1 car (they're f2 cars with CGI added, l believe).It's well worth seeing on the biggest screen possible, though l bet I'll also enjoy it on good old fashioned DVD later, too.

title: Watch it in IMAX, it's worth it
content: A fictional movie about Formula 1, directed by Joseph Kosinski, has been generating significant buzz, and for good reason. The film is a thrilling ride that captures the essence of high-speed racing while delivering a compelling human story.The action sequences, particularly the driving scenes, are nothing short of spectacular. Kosinski, known for his work on "Top Gun: Maverick," brings his signature style to the F1 track, creating a visceral and immersive experience. The sound design is a key component, with the roar of the engines and the screech of tires making you feel like you're right there in the cockpit. The on-track battles are intense and realistic, a testament to the effort put into making the racing feel authentic. The film also features a fantastic soundtrack that perfectly complements the high-octane action and emotional beats.The character development, while leaning into some familiar tropes, is effective. We see the journey of a veteran driver, played by the ever-charismatic Brad Pitt, as he mentors a young prodigy, played brilliantly by Damson Idris. Their dynamic is the heart of the film, and both actors deliver powerful performances. Pitt brings a veteran's gravitas and emotional depth to his role, while Idris captures the ambition and vulnerability of a rising star. The supporting cast also shines, adding layers to the world of F1 and its high-stakes environment.While the film takes some creative liberties with the reality of F1 racing, it's a forgivable sin. The filmmakers' goal was to create a cinematic experience, not a documentary. The drama and high-octane action wouldn't be possible if they adhered strictly to the often-strategic and less-dramatic nature of real F1 races. The result is a film that is more entertaining and accessible to a wider audience, and it's a compromise that pays off in spades. However, the film could have benefited from a tighter edit; shaving off around 15 minutes would have made the pacing even more effective.In conclusion, this film is a must-watch for anyone who loves high-octane action and compelling character-driven stories. It's a testament to the power of filmmaking, proving that a well-crafted movie can capture the spirit of a sport and make it feel more exciting than ever before.

title: A cinematic thunderstorm powered by story, speed, and Zimmer's genius
content: F1 isn't just a film-it's a visceral, pulse-pounding experience that grabs you by the collar and hurls you straight into the heart of Formula 1 racing. Even as someone who walked in knowing nothing about F1, I was completely hooked from start to finish.Visually, it's stunning. The cinematography puts you inside the car rather than just behind the camera. Every turn, overtake, and crash feels like it's happening to you, especially if you're lucky enough to watch it in 4DX or IMAX. It's not just immersive-it's transportive.But what lingers most is the emotional core of the story. The film doesn't just showcase speed, it humanizes the people behind the wheel-their obsessions, rivalries, and vulnerabilities. It's beautifully paced, never overindulgent, and incredibly engaging even for non-racing audiences.And then there's Hans Zimmer. His music doesn't accompany the film-it drives it. The score is thunderous, aching, and electrifying all at once. It makes your chest thump during the races and squeezes your heart during quiet moments. It's easily one of Zimmer's finest in recent years.Also, "Lose My Mind" by DOLIVER is the unexpected anthem of the film-it hits hard, sticks with you, and captures the movie's adrenaline-soaked spirit perfectly. It's one of those songs that'll live rent-free in your head for days.Whether you're a fan of racing or just love damn good cinema, F1 is a ride you don't want to miss. It's technically masterful, emotionally grounded, and musically unforgettable. A modern-day classic in the making.
