title: Top Gun 3 Rocks!
content: Joseph Kosinski's F1: The Movie is more advertisement than art. There is so much product placement during the film's 155 minute run time that when Shaquille O'Neal saw the film he was overheard saying, "Stop, stop, that's too much." With all of this selling going on F1, at times, comes across so bland even the main black character is pretty vanilla.Kosinski knows how to make soullessness look achingly pretty - see Oblivion for example. The technical prowess of the driving scenes is beyond reproach. All of the night races in particular look spectacular with their far flung exotic locales. Unfortunately the best race scene is the first race scene. After that F1 goes to the same wheel wells a little too often.Starring the Handsome Man and Damson Idris F1 mimics all the character conflict of the two Top Gun movies. It does so with about as much character development. The movie is not without its charms. Who doesn't love Led Zeppelin, a lilting Irish brogue or a laughing Kim Bodnia? The script, while short on substance, does allow the Handsome Man's charisma a chance to shine through.Two years ago Gran Turismo told the same story with 1/5th the budget but five times the heart. Where F1 caught me out was with the ending. I was positive they were going to go in a different direction and am much happier with the choice that was ultimately made. Walking out of the theatre a few of us had a debate about how many stars to give. I said 5. My companions suggested 6 would be more fair. I'll dole out the extra star just for that ending.The day after watching F1: The Movie I went on a road trip and drove much faster than I normally do. Chalk one up for the power of advertising.

title: F1 Movie - A Thrilling Ride from Start to Finish
content: Before going to the cinema, I had seen some comments from real F1 drivers saying the movie was made more for general audiences than for hardcore Formula 1 fans. That made me lower my expectations a bit. They said the story might feel predictable to those who follow the sport closely, since fans already know what happens on the track. But in reality, if you're not deep into the world of Formula 1, you will enjoy the movie, although you might don't understand a few references here and there.That said, it's not a problem at all. The visuals, cinematography, and sound effects are simply perfect. The character Sonny is a complicated guy, and while his issues are touched on in the story, they're not the main focus, and honestly, that was the right choice in my opinion. We were there for the action, and believe me, the movie delivers.It's nearly three hours long, but time flew by, and my session started at 9 PM! I felt like I was right there in the cockpit during many scenes. The entire production contributes to an intense sense of immersion. Of course, some parts are far from realistic, especially some of the racing strategies, but in the end, that won't matter.

author: JurijFedorov

title: No, please
content: Having been a hardcore formula 1 nut for the best part of my life ( since I saw Ayrton driving at silverstone in 1985 as a mere little boy), I decided to go in the theatre with an open mind about this movie, I decided to give it a fair shot as a piece of entertainment, regardless of the formula 1 angle. Unfortunately the movie is badly let down by many many many other bad aspects which have nothing to do with formula 1. The entire movie is deeply nonsensical with cardboard cut out as characters and extreme suspension of disbelief at every step of the plot. I always liked Jerry bruckheimer movies but this one is a far cry from his best... even accepting the signature cheers and svelte cuts of the scenes I cannot condone the bad acting that infected even great actors like Javier Bardem and Brad Pit. Especially the dialogs between anyone else and the girl technical director are phenomenally flat and all over the place with the actors clearly confused by the girl giving them contrasting emotions and unable to keep a straight face. In this movie the formula 1 is a veneer, a skin suit over a generic and boring has-been plot which most of the time does not know what to do with the next scene, characters motivations reverse at the flip of a coin, people who seem important for the story suddenly get forgotten... I guess this is the type of writing we are accustomed to these days. No thanks! Now I will go and rewatch steve McQueen and 917 winning Le Mans ( yes I am a car nut and not only a formula 1 fan...) to wash the bad taste out of my mouth.N. B.: sir Lewis was a producer to this movie, one can clearly tell by the plot and the relationships between the characters ... what a great movie that would have been if they decided to tell his tale of struggle, dedication, passion and victory... I am even madder now.

title: Cinema in Pole Position
content: Boy oh boy, F1 was an absolute blast. Ten minutes in, I knew this was going straight into my "movies to rewatch when I need a hit of adrenaline" list. It's got major Top Gun vibes - and I mean that in the best way possible. The opening montage, the high-octane score, the vibe of being on a mission - all very familiar, all very cool.Brad Pitt? Effortlessly slick. The movie moves fast (duh), but it still finds time to sneak in some solid emotional moments without slowing things down. If you're a Formula 1 fan, you'll love how real the tension feels. It's not trying to be deep or life-changing - it's just here to entertain, and it does that full throttle. This one's a total grand slam for me.

title: Absolutely Captivating
content: First off, I should have seen this in IMAX. If you're considering buying tickets, buy them now and get them in IMAX. From the opening shot I was wishing I had booked the IMAX screening.Second, the cinematography was phenomenal. I was captivated and constantly impressed by the shots. In most of these sport movies, I leave the theatre wishing for more sport scenes. Thankfully, this film found the middle ground between too little and too much. The high stakes, thrill inducing racing scenes left me feeling content.Personally I am frustrated when a blockbuster sports film is overwhelmed by a complex and dramatic story. On the contrary, having a weak and unfulfilling story can also cripple a film. F1's story serves its purpose, and while a little more could be desired, it gets the job done and doesn't take anything away.Go see this film, it was entertaining and a great way to spend our afternoon.

title: Vrooom!
content: Greetings again from the darkness. I can't explain why auto racing movies have always appealed to me. This dates back to such classics as GRAND PRIX (1966, with James Garner), WINNING (1969, with Paul Newman), and LE MANS (1971, with Steve McQueen). Toss in the more recent films like SENNA (2010), RUSH (2013), and FORD V FERRARI (2019), and that's a lot of roaring engines and squealing tires on the track and the big screen. Writer-director Joseph Kosinski is no stranger to movies with stunning action and movement and speed as evidenced by his TOP GUN: MAVERICK (2022), OBLIVION (2013), and TRON LEGACY (2010). Kosinski co-wrote the story here with Ehren Kruger, his screenwriter from that high-flying 2022 gem.Led Zeppelin's "Whole Lotta Love" blasts through the IMAX sound system as we are introduced to brash driver Sonny Hayes (Oscar winner Brad Pitt, ONCE UPON A TIME ... IN HOLLYWOOD, 2019) during 24 Hours at Daytona. We learn quickly that Sonny marches to his own drummer and is a bit of a loner living and traveling in his van. When Ruben (Oscar winner Javier Bardem, NO COUNTRY FOR OLD MEN, 2007), his long-ago friend and one-time racing rival, shows up to offer him a seat for the Formula One team he now owns, Sonny politely declines, despite his friend's financial struggle and desperate need to win a race or risk losing his team. Of course, we know Sonny will show up.Sonny discovers that the other driver on the team is an arrogant and talented rookie named Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris, "Snowfall"). Pearce represents a certain generation with arrogance and entitlement beyond his actual success (along with a supportive mama, Sarah Niles, "Ted Lasso"). He certainly wants nothing to do with an 'old man' who hasn't driven F1 in decades. The two polar opposite drivers provide the conflict in the film, but it's Sonny's backstory that makes things click. As an immensely talented young driver, his dreams and expectations came crashing down, leaving him a broken man with no home - other than his life as a gypsy driver bounding from one racetrack to the next.If you've seen BULL DURHAM (1998) or THE COLOR OF MONEY (1996), you know how it goes when the grizzled veteran mentors the cocky (and talented) newcomer. It's a rough start and slowly the two begin to work as teammates. Sonny's sixth sense on the track stems from knowledge and experience. His swagger initially rubs former aerospace-engineer-turned-car-designer, Kate (Kerry Condon, THE BANSHEES OF INISHERIN, 2022), the wrong way, but of course anyone who has ever seen a movie knows where they are headed.As with everything these days, especially when big money is at stake, politics and power play a huge role. This time it's sleazy investor Peter Banning (Tobias Menzies, YOU HURT MY FEELINGS, 2023) who provides the ugly underbelly of greed and corruption. Still, none of that much matters ... same with the dialogue that often veers into pure cheesiness. What matters is that the race scenes are exhilarating ... the best we've ever seen on screen (and make that an IMAX screen if possible - for both sight and sound). Cinematographer Claudio Miranda won an Oscar for LIFE OF PI (2011), and I'm guessing the filming during actual Formula One events proved even more challenging than that one. Definitely deserving of attention are co-editors Stephen Mirrione (Oscar winner, TRAFFIC, 2000) and Patrick J Smith, as well as the entire Sound team. I'll admit it's not my favorite Hans Zimmer score, and I'm certain that neither he nor his two Oscars care about my thoughts on the matter.As an actor, Brad Pitt no longer survives simply by existing - he actually emotes (and does so quite well). He's a big movie star in a BIG movie that strives to wow viewers. The mystery is how many will be drawn to the theaters for fast cars. For those that do, this one delivers. On the other hand, if TALLADEGA NIGHTS (2006) or Pixar's CARS franchise is more your speed, well there's no shame in that.Opening in theaters nationwide on June 27, 2025 followed by streaming on AppleTV+

title: Best movie of the year (so far)
content:
There are no words to describe how much I enjoyed watching this movie, but I'll try anyway.First of all, I came into it without high expectations. On one hand, it's Brad Pitt - an amazing actor whose projects almost always succeed in a big way. Plus, the film's director, Joseph Kosinski, also directed Top Gun: Maverick, which I absolutely loved.On the other hand, I'm not a Formula 1 fan, so I was afraid I wouldn't follow the plot.Let me cut to the chase and say this: it's the best movie of the year so far.The stunning directing and cinematography deserve an Oscar nomination, the acting is top-notch, the soundtrack is phenomenal, and overall it's just a high-quality film. Even if you don't like Formula 1 (like me), you'll still enjoy this movie.I didn't feel time pass at all, which is definitely a big compliment to the film.It's the kind of movie I'd happily watch again right now,and that's not something I say often on the same day I watch a film.Here's a quick breakdown of what I especially loved:
First, every time there's a race scene, you as the viewer feel like you're part of the race - as if you're sitting in the driver's seat - and that feeling is incredible! The camera angles are truly unique, and the direction, as I mentioned, is stunning.The actors also do a great job. Brad Pitt once again proves what a great actor he is, even at 61 (and he really doesn't look it!). But the surprise of the film for me was Damson Idris, who plays Pearce - he delivers an outstanding performance. I didn't know him before, but now I think I'll start following his work.To sum it up: two and a half hours of fun, laughter, tension, and tons of action on wheels.Don't eat your popcorn during the trailers - it's worth the wait.

title: No Lane Chosen
content:
Rating: 7.5/10Summary:
F1 is a strange film - at once a love letter to racing, a Hollywood underdog story, and a glossy behind-the-scenes peek at the paddock. With Brad Pitt front and center, real tracks, real commentators, and even real drivers appearing, it feels like it should be an authentic deep dive. Yet the script tries to be accessible for casuals while still marketing itself as the ultimate F1 fan film, and in doing so never fully commits to either lane. There's strong acting, some solid rivalries, and undeniable spectacle, but also muddled plotting, unrealistic racing choices, and a lack of clarity about who we're meant to root for. It's good fun, but it falls short of greatness.Full Review:
First off, the name "F1" feels too broad. For a film that's supposedly about the heart of racing, it's odd that it wasn't given a title like Apex or something that reflects speed, danger, or passion. And that sums up the central problem: the movie doesn't quite know which lane it wants to be in.On one hand, Lewis Hamilton's presence as a producer means the film gets incredible access. Sequences were shot on the actual grid at real F1 weekends, Silverstone to Monza, Spielberg to others. We get Martin Brundle and Crofty on commentary, team principals in the garages, and even cameos from current drivers like Bottas and Lawson. You see McLaren HQ standing in as the Apex team's factory, which is a thrill for fans. For diehards, this is gold.On the other hand, the script is written to explain the sport as if the audience has never seen a race. Commentary is exposition-heavy, full of explanations that would never be part of a real broadcast. As a result, it doesn't feel like authentic F1 commentary at all, and the movie struggles to balance being accessible to casual viewers with being credible to fans.The performances are strong. Brad Pitt does well as Sonny Hayes - yes, it's a familiar type of character for him, but he brings weight to it. Damsan Idris as Joshua Pierce is excellent too, starting subdued and building up to something stronger. Javier Bardem's Ruben Cervantes and Kerry Condon's Kate McKenna are solid as the leadership core of Apex. I didn't buy into the romance between Kate and Sonny, though - it felt unnecessary. The garage crew, Jodie and Dodge, are fun, and I liked their contributions to the team dynamic, even if it was underdeveloped.There are weak points. Sarah Niles feels out of place, her delivery poor as in other projects, and her subplot fizzles. And while the cast overall tries hard, the writing doesn't give them much to work with.As for the racing, it looks slick but lacks the sense of awe and intensity you'd expect. Compared to Joseph Kosinski's Top Gun: Maverick, which made flying jets visceral and thrilling, here the race sequences feel like any other racing movie. There are flashes of brilliance, but I never felt blown away by the visuals the way I hoped to.The storylines are muddled too. One moment Sonny Hayes sacrifices his own race to help Pierce win, the next he's back to undermining him. Characters flip-flop in motivation from race to race, leaving it unclear where we should place our allegiance. Apex itself feels like an island with no meaningful rivals - it would have been far richer if the film had fleshed out another fictional team as a proper antagonist, rather than isolating all the drama within one garage.And then there are the unrealistic details that pull you out. Drivers drinking the night before races, staying up until 1am, strategies that would rack up endless super license penalty points - no F1 driver would ever be allowed to get away with that. For fans, these missteps are distracting.All of this comes together into a film that's entertaining but compromised. It clearly loves the sport - Hamilton's influence shows - but it doesn't fully understand what makes F1 tick. Top Gun: Maverick worked because it embraced its roots and amplified them into pure spectacle. This tries to do too many things at once: an underdog story, an aging-driver story, a romance, a mentor-protégé tale, and a glossy fan-service reel. In doing so, it never nails one of them completely.Still, it's a good time. Pitt and Idris give it heart, the access is thrilling, and for general audiences it'll be a fun ride. But for fans, it's a mixed bag - as much to love as to hate. With more focus and a clearer identity, it could have been a classic. Instead, it's a solid but muddled movie about a sport that deserves something sharper.

title: This ain't just racing-it's ego, legacy, and chaos at 300km/h.
content: This film is insane. From the first second, it grips you by the collar and drags you straight onto the grid. The roar of the engines, the blinding speed, the ego, the pressure, it's all there and it's all loud.Brad Pitt is back and cooking as Sonny Hayes, a washed-up might have been legend with nothing to lose and one last shot at glory. He brings that effortless cool mixed with quiet desperation. Damson Idris is a total scene-stealer as the rookie with fire in his veins. Their dynamic? Straight-up fireworks.Joseph Kosinski directs the hell out of this. The visuals are crisp, intense, and give you a full-blown sensory overload. And big shoutout to Claudio Miranda on camera-this man puts you in the cockpit. Every turn, every near crash, every millisecond decision-it feels like you're strapped in.But what I didn't expect? The film's got heart. It's not just about speed-it's about legacy, pressure, pride, and what it means to risk it all when the world thinks you're done. It's also funny in that smart, dry way that hits right when the tension gets too real.This isn't some sugar-coated underdog story. It's fast, feral, and fully loaded with chaos. This film doesn't slow down, and neither will your pulse.

title: Fun action but completely unrealistic
content: If you are an F1 fan and, like me, have been watching since childhood, you know the rules and how things work during a race and behind the scenes. As portrayed in this film, which is understandable for entertainment purposes, it is completely illogical and impossible.If you're not an regular F1 watcher, it's great entertainment, but if you know a little bit about the ins and outs of F1, you'll be constantly annoyed by something that is completely impossible.Nevertheless, the images are beautiful and the race scenes are fun and entertaining. Good for a Saturday night's entertainment, then quickly forgotten.

title: Top Gun with Wheels - F1 is Pure Rush
content: If Top Gun: Maverick had a racing twin, F1 would be it. The cinematic experience is breathtakingSure, the movie moves so fast that character depth takes a back seat. Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and Kerry Condon all deliver solid performances, but you don't really get to sit with them. The emotional layers-if any-are left in the pit lane. That said, it never really drags. The fast pace works in its favor, keeping you hooked even if you're not deeply invested in the characters.It's not a documentary, and it doesn't pretend to be. F1 is about speed, spectacle, and sensation-and in that, it absolutely delivers. Whether you're an F1 diehard or just there for the thrill, it's a cinematic ride worth taking.

title: "F1": The Must-See Theatrical Experience of the Year
content: "F1" is the epic blockbuster that everyone should experience in theaters this year.It's grand, mesmerizing, adrenaline-fueled, and incredibly realistic-so much so that you'll want to watch it again the moment the credits roll.While die-hard F1 fans might notice a few inaccuracies in the rules or design choices, these are only minor flaws that do nothing to diminish the film's excellence.What truly sets it apart is its ability to draw in a wider audience, allowing even those unfamiliar with motorsports to feel the full force and thrill of the world's most elite racing competition.To put it bluntly-if a film like this isn't worth seeing in theaters, then what is?

title: F1 Life in the Fastest Lane..!
content:
I watched it tonight at a full-house IMAX Laser theater! I was pretty sure I'd enjoy it just like I enjoyed Top Gun: Maverick in a 4DX theater, which was honestly one of my best movie experiences ever. This one? Just a notch below, but still totally satisfying.It's like Joseph Kosinski knows how to make a film that's built for the big screen.Until I saw this movie, I thought "speed" was only for fiber-optic internet :))
But this isn't just about roaring engines and tire smoke F1 dives deep into the lives of the drivers: the pressure, the tears, the fierce competition...Seeing Lewis Hamilton involved as one of the producers was a nice touch too.And Hans Zimmer's score? Another story entirely. It fits perfectly with every single scene. Funny enough, I just went to his concert last month on Yas Island the same location where the film's final race was shot! Too bad the score from this film wasn't part of his setlist :))All in all, definitely one of the best movies I've seen this year.

title: Enjoyable in bits, but overall lacks the thrill that was expected
content: I liked it in parts, though it never felt like an all-out adrenaline rush. The racing sequences were shot really well, even if some maneuvers were too impractical, they did give the film its thrills. What didn't work for me was Brad Pitt, who just looked bored throughout, and the romance angle, which felt totally unnecessary.The biggest letdown was the music. Hans Zimmer usually elevates films, but here it felt flat and forgettable. Honestly, it gave me the vibe of Top Gun with F1 cars, but without Tom Cruise's energy. It also makes comparisons to Rush inevitable, and this one doesn't come close to that level.Still, despite the void I felt, it's a good film, enjoyable, rewatchable, but never hitting top gear.

title: The first F1 I've watched
content: This is literally the first F1 I've watched, but it's fiction. Tbh, never really interested to watch people race over each others for hours. I watched this movie because it's recommended by a radio announcer, he said the cinematography makes you feel like you're inside the car and being part of the race itself. He also added that it should be watched on cinema, not on your TV or laptop or worst your phone, because it would feel different. Then I know that I should watch it on ScreenX to make get the best experience.So here I am, just finished the movie, on ScreenX and so happy. The story is simple, and old guy, Sonny Hayes, been out of circuit around 30 years, then got back to the circuit to help his old pal from going bankrupt and drowning in debts. He got a partner, a younger guy, Joshua Pearce, who got skills, but with a lot of ego. This plot kinda reminds me of Top Gun Maverick. But the difference is Sonny treats the F1 like his playground. He challenges every F1 rules. Even though I don't know the rules exactly, I can understand when he's playing in the grey area, breaking the rules only to get the benefits for his own / his team, and most of the times in a hilarious way.Overall, I love love loveee this movie. Storyline easy to understand, good amount of humour, teach us valuable life lessons, and most importantly is the cinematography really makes you feel like you're inside the F1 car. Kudos to all the team. One more thing, the ending is sooo beautiful. I got excited and teary watching it. I'm not gonna spoil it because I don't want to ruin the surprise. Please watch it and prepare to be amazed.

title: Top racing movie at the moment
content:
Delivers exactly what fans hoped for: jaw-dropping race sequences, a powerhouse Brad Pitt performance, and enough emotional stakes to keep the drama engaging beyond the track.The Good:Cinematography & Visuals - Exhilarating, immersive, and sleek. The races feel both authentic and cinematic.Performances - Pitt is magnetic, and Damson Idris provides a strong counterbalance. Their dynamic drives the film as much as the cars do.Sound & Score - Every rev, skid, and crash rattles your bones. IMAX is a must.The Not-So-Good:
Predictable story beats-mentor vs rookie drama is familiar.Occasionally leans into cliché sports tropes.Final Word:
A thrilling blend of spectacle and heart. While the story isn't revolutionary, the execution makes F1 one of the best racing films ever put to screen. Highly recommended for both Formula One fans and moviegoers who just want a ride that leaves them breathless.

title: All Style, Little Substance
content: Maybe it's because I went in with high expectations, or simply because I really wanted to like it... but although it didn't disappoint me by any means, it ended up being just okay.Let me start with the positives. The performances are one of the film's strongest aspects, with three in particular standing out: Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and-above all-Brad Pitt, who powerfully conveys the emotional stages his character goes through. The visual work also deserves praise: the cinematography and staging are spectacular. The racing scenes are so well shot that at times the entire theater fell completely silent. That says a lot.Now, what didn't fully convince me was the plot development. I get that the film draws from classic cinema and follows the formula of the seasoned veteran guiding the arrogant rookie until he becomes exemplary. But that progression felt forced and rushed to me. The editing, especially in terms of character development, feels uneven-like it constantly prioritizes visual spectacle and grandeur over genuine emotional evolution.It's a bit like Rush, but with a much more superficial execution.And lastly, I have to say the music took me out of the film more than once. Aside from a few specific tracks, the soundtrack felt off.All in all, a good movie within its genre-possibly the most visually impressive-but very basic when it comes to story and character depth. It won't leave a lasting impact, but it works well as solid weekend entertainment.

title: Brad Pitt Shines, But the Script Doesn't
content: While "F1" delivers a sleek and polished visual experience with impressive race sequences and high-quality cinematography, particularly in the Abu Dhabi scenes the film ultimately falls short in narrative substance. As someone familiar with the city, I appreciated the local shots, but they weren't enough to compensate for a predictable and formulaic script.Brad Pitt brings charisma and credibility to the role, and his performance is arguably the film's strongest asset. Unfortunately, the story unfolds with little surprise or emotional weight, relying on worn-out tropes that undermine the film's ambitious presentation.In the end, "F1" is a technically impressive film that lacks the narrative fuel to cross the finish line memorably.

title: More PLEASE.

title: Formulaic yet thrilling
content: There is something special about the simplicity of a classic food that is particularly enjoyable to me, similar to how this movie doesn't reinvent the wheel (heh), yet still provides a satisfying portion of soul-warming goodness.The opening sequence is a highlight, introducing a soundtrack that perfectly complements the roaring sounds of racing cars throughout the duration, blending an original score with impeccable selections of licensed tracks, some written for the movie.Down and out Sonny Hayes, a man whose life could have been great on account of a racing career that was cut short, seems to have lost most of his juice by the time an old rival approaches him with an opportunity that just might rescue the both of them, in more than one way. With a rookie driver thrown in the mix, the has-been mentor vs the hotshot apprentice story feels a bit too middle of the road (heh again), but the human stories here are mostly serviceable enough to keep you engaged - though some are more developed, thus working better, than others, particularly in the case of the side characters.The racing action is pure, and it is a testament to the sport itself that there is little need for extra manufactured Hollywood drama (I say extra because there already is manufactured drama in the real thing), bar a few sequences in one of the early races, where Sonny Hayes crosses a line that even the more combative drivers in real life would not cross. But the action sequences thrill like in almost no other movie in this genre, and they more than do justice to a sport that is organically cinematic.Finally, and this might sound like it's a bad thing when it's not, was this just Top Gun: Maverick but on four wheels? Kinda, sorta, and I really enjoy that about it, just like that good plate of honest, unpretentious pasta that I will never get tired of.

title: Speed never felt so intimate...
content: Just saw F1, the Brad Pitt vehicle (pun intended), which he also co-produced.I am not a fan of F1 or racing in general. So why did I go see this film which is clearly and inescapably about Formula 1, a subject of which I know next to nothing? Because it's directed by one of the most underrated directors of his generation - Joseph Kosinski, director of Oblivion, Only the Brave and 2022's Top Gun: Maverick, a film which delivered one of the best cinema experiences I've ever had.Kosinski is excellent at crafting a competent, if clichéd, story mixed with truly amazing visual prowess, and it's clear that F1 is no different. Breathtaking, edge of your seat filmmaking is coupled with a by-the-numbers know-where-it's-going 'underdog, one last chance' sports story. Yes, the premise and characters have all been seen before, but not once throughout a 2-and-a-half-hour run time did it feel tiresome.Brad Pitt is a wonderful choice for the main character of Sonny Hayes - he brings this cool elegance to the character and lends the film a much needed humanistic focal point. Additionally, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon and Damson Idris all serve to break free of their thin, well-worn and 'seen umpteen times before' character tropes to elevate the material like a great supporting cast should.Yet despite the surprisingly strong and impassioned acting on display here, the real star of F1 The Movie are the cars and races themselves. Never before in the history of cinema has the intensity, intimacy, power and terrifying speed of Formula 1 or racing in general been captured on screen quite like it is done so here. The sound is intense, the editing is as sharp as a katana and the visuals make you feel like you can almost feel the G forces bursting through the screen. The cherry on top of the cake is that all of this is backed up by an 'as-per-usual' incredible score by Hans Zimmer. Make no mistake - this is epic filmmaking that transports you right into the seat of some of the fastest machines on earth, and it is utterly terrifying and exhilarating to experience.F1, overall, is a brilliant movie, with what it lacks in storytelling and original characters being more than made up for in white-knuckle intensity and hypnotizing, can't-look-away filmmaking. Experience this film and marvel at it. Very much a recommendation from me.

content: The technical details are very good. The cinematography is well done and captures the racing scenes very nicely. Hans Zimmer's score is strong and fits the mood; seeing his name in the opening credits definitely raised my expectations. The music helps build adrenaline throughout the film and movie uses suspense and camera work effectively to create tension during races. The adrenaline is one of the best parts of the film, even though it's quite unsurprising, it was nice how they managed to do that.The story, however, is clichéd. It is anticipated and follows common sports drama patterns. After some time, the plot becomes repetitive. It keeps going over the same points without much change. If you know anything about F1, the film is unrealistic in many ways. The way the characters act and how events unfold do not match real-life F1. The dialogue is unnatural and awkward. The American accents are also very American and very annoying, but that's not really on the movie. The film looks too polished, with very clean camera quality. This makes it feel fake or like an advertisement.Despite these problems, the movie keeps the adrenaline going well. It does not fail completely. It manages to keep a lot of excitement through its technical aspects. The movie is good technically but weak in story and realism. It is enjoyable if you focus on the action and music. It was also very funny seeing the real drivers randomly showing up. Also, it was odd how they had almost everything based on real events aside from the main story. The predictable plot and polished look make it less convincing, but very fun, 7.7/10.

title: It is not indispensable viewing, but should one watch it, it will not weary the audience. (66/100)
content:
Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) is "a man who lives his life by constantly beginning anew." Just as his career at the Daytona races reaches its peak, he abandons it and sets off in search of something else. When he is later offered the chance to return to Formula 1 -a career cut short years before by a crash- he does not refuse. In the intervening years, he has endured three failed marriages and bankruptcy brought on by gambling debts. He also finds himself in serious conflict with his teammate, Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). Yet these frictions, in keeping with the zeitgeist of our own time, are resolved through proper communication. :)
I have never truly found any beauty or thrill in Formula 1 racing. With the aid of new technology, they have brought to the screen a storyline more befitting of the 1980s. It must also be said that Hans Zimmer's score stands as one of the film's essential supporting pillars.

title: I M A X
content: One of the best movie experiences I have ever had. IMAX documentaries have been around for a very long time, however it wasn't until 2002/2003 when Fox and Lucasfilm released Star Wars : Attack of the Clones in 2002 in IMAX... that Hollywood took notice. Now, nearly 25 years later the audience has been still listening, viewing, and experiencing all the majestic beauty that only the IMAX experience can offer.Because of this, F1 is gripping from the opening moments all the way through to the credits rolling. Amazing cast ensemble, action, cinematography, and pace. Even when the story steps away from the track... it still keeps you right where it is needed. Bravo to all involved getting this film made. Lastly, of the countless films I have seen in IMAX... this was the first, to shoot the entire film with the IMAX ratio. Absolutely stunning. TAKE NOTES to all the other film makers. Spare no expense... it is... without a doubt, worth it!

title: technically superior racing
content: Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) owns the troubled APXGP F1 Team. His rookie lead driver Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) hasn't scored a single point. His technical director Kate McKenna (Kerry Condon) is the first woman in that job in F1 history. He decides to go outside the box again and recruits his former teammate Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt). Sonny is a veteran who has been bouncing around since dropping out of F1 30 years earlier due to a devastating crash.The racing is absolutely exciting. The technicality of F1 strategies can be daunting, but the movie is able to navigate it. I especially love the cockpit filming with the actors' faces. Anything without their faces is assumed to be not them. That's why it is so exciting to see our two stars in their driver seats. The racing scenes are superior work.The actors are all terrific. The story is pretty simple and a familiar one. The old guy comes in to teach the youngster how to be. They're also the big underdogs which is standard for a sports movie. The only thing that truly bugs me is the 30 years. It is a big number. I do understand that they need work around Brad Pitt's real age. He is over 60 and 40 would be an even bigger number. There is no way he could race at that age. If he was a rookie when he had the accident, twenty years would be the maximum that I could give it. Even legends last only about 20 years.

title: Edge of a seat experience
content: Once in a lifetime movie . Verdict: 10/10.F1: Too Good isn't just a movie. It's an experience. For fans of Formula 1, it's a love letter. For film lovers, it's a masterclass. For everyone else-it's a pulse-pounding, soul-stirring journey into one of the most beautiful obsessions mankind has ever created. Visually, it's a marvel. IMAX cameras inside the cockpit offer a perspective never seen before in F1 filmmaking. The transitions between real race footage and reenacted scenes are so seamless, it becomes impossible to distinguish where reality ends and cinema begins. The color grading, especially in night races like Singapore, gives a surreal, dreamlike quality-like you're racing through the heart of a fever dream, where milliseconds mean everything.

title: The Hollywoodification of Formula 1
content: F1: The Movie is a flashy, race sports drama directed by Joseph Kosinski with screenplay by Ehren Kruger. It was produced by Jerry Bruckheimer and a few others, Brad Pitt and Lewis Hamilton being amongst them. Kosinski, Bruckheimer and Ehren Kruger had also produced and directed Top Gun: Maverick - which was a stellar film. This one had all the backing to be considered one of the best, but it felt hollow aside from the racing. We are introduced to a retired racer and formula 1 prodigy, Sonny Hayes. He discontinues racing after a crash nearly kills him and prefers to operate nomadically as a racer for hire. Until one day he is approached by a former teammate Ruben Cervantes who is the owner of the APXGP Formula 1 team. He offers Sonny a chance to regain former glory by filling a spare seat on his team as an experienced racer, however Sonny reluctantly agrees as he is still reeling from the trauma of his past. He sashays onto the scene, bringing with him an unusual way to train for racing. The young, arrogant rookie racer Joshua Pearce is threatened by his addition and seeks to make that known at any moment he can. Though as seen in similar sports drama movies, the young whippersnapper refuses to heed correction from the old, wiser pro which results in disaster. Yes, we have seen this kind of film before and that is the problem with this movie; it brings nothing fresh to the table. There is also a throwaway romance between Hayes and the technical director, Kate McKenna, which we could have done without.Regarding the casting, Sonny Hayes is portrayed by Brad Pitt in a lackluster, nonchalant manner. His character seemed inauthentic and disinterested - it really seemed like it was just Brad Pitt in a racing film. Joshua Pearce headlined by Damson Idris provided more appeal with his character yet on account of the simple dialogue, there is not enough depth to his character. Javier Bardem plays a convicted Ruben Cervantes that is concerned about his friend as well as the well-being of his team. Kerry Condon portrays a passionate technical director, but her character remains at the surface level in favor of developing a romance that is entirely unnecessary.The massive appeal of the movie comes from the immersion it provides with stunning cinematography and racing sequences. It took a while to gain footing but gradually got better. Its immersive experience, especially when viewed in IMAX, allows the audience to feel as if they were on the sidelines of a formula 1 race. Paired with an original score by Hans Zimmer, it almost seemed unstoppable. However, a few choices within the film reduced the potency of the soundtrack. Despite Zimmer's score, the music seemed overbearing at times when silence would have given a greater pay-off in contrast to the loud terrain of a racetrack. The deafening sounds of the track were paired with equally blaring music and detracted from a lot of those prominent moments. Dialogue in the film was littered with car jargon which the audience had to get acquainted with before fully engaging - but this is not a critique, merely an observation. The dynamic between Brad and Damson was neat, though it felt like it lacked a bit of authenticity between characters. Once again, because the dialogue struggled, most of the characters suffered from inauthenticity and it did not help that most of the set design was pristine and sterile. It pains me to see a set design that looks too clean, because it reminds you that you are watching a big, Hollywood produced movie. The cameos from real-life racers like Lewis Hamilton - who was one of the producers - and Charles LeClerc and more helped to sell the immersion as it was shot during the 2023 Formula 1 season. The film could have done without romance as it seemed irrelevant to the story and should have rendered more time into focusing on the relationship between Bardem and Pitt.The true stars of the film were the race sequences and cinematography, apart from that F1: The Movie, feels like an over-produced Hollywood film with shallow characters and a formulaic storyline.

title: Absolutely Blown Away
content: So I got around to seeing the F1 Movie a bit late in theaters and with not much hitting the cinema this week I decided it was time to see what the hype was about. I went into this one with no expectations just looking for a good looking movie and wow - I'm simply blown away. This movie absolutely delivers and then some with almost every single aspect here. I love sports, but I never truly got into F1 or racing in general and this movie shows you don't have to be an F1 mega fan or even semi fan to get almost everything this film offers. On the surface it's about F1 racing and that's for sure but the deeper you get into this movie it really sucks you into this racing world and intrigues you and does this sport absolutely right. I can see how some might not completely get behind it but the characters here are just amazing - the development here and the subtle moments that show there growth are just absolutely brilliant and the acting job here was good.The cinematography was definitely stand out, and it just simply looks beautiful. The overall themes and messages of this movie were played into the plot perfectly and they definitely resonated with me and I'm sure they will/did with a lot of others who've seen this.Closing thoughts after seeing the movie is it's special and absolutely worth seeing for the amazing visuals alone on top of having non F1 fans come to appreciate the sport and deliver great characters and a simple plot for all to follow. Incredible movie.

title: The ride of the summer
content:
Joesph Kosinski isn't a stranger to the summer blockbuster. In 2022, he brought us the legacy sequel that had everyone running to the theater to experience it the way it was mentioned to be seen. Now we have
F1. While isn't breaking any new grounds in terms of story and characters, feeling very similar to the director's previous work Top gun maverick , but Joseph Kosinski crafts an immersive, intense, audio visual experience that brings both newcomers and already existing fans of the sport, into the driver's seat for the crowd pleasing blockbuster that is surely to be the talk of the summer movie season.

title: An Absolute Masterclass in Sports Drama! (With Stellar Cinematography & Music!)
content: Having just experienced "F1: The Movie," I can confidently say this film is an absolute triumph. While I initially approached it with some reservation, joining 5 minutes late after an Assamese movie ("Rudra" - also featuring Ravi Sharma), the first 15 minutes seemed to confirm my fear of a slow burn. However, from that point on, this movie absolutely ignited!This isn't just a sports drama; it's a profound exploration of rage, ego, the power of a second chance, the complexities of good versus bad character, the essence of teamwork, the weight of maintaining a legacy, and the fervent dream of achieving the pinnacle of a sport for the very first time. It encapsulates the raw, unadulterated emotions of winning and losing, showcasing how sometimes, a loss can be the most beneficial outcome. It truly captures that visceral, real sports feeling. The brilliant banter and chemistry between the two main characters, cleverly fueled by their age difference, add another layer of enjoyment.The cinematography in this film is absolutely top-notch, immersing you in every thrilling moment, and the music perfectly complements the emotional beats and high-octane action.Overall, "F1: The Movie" is simply outstanding. It's the kind of film that lingers long after the credits roll, its narrative echoing in your mind. While the film itself isn't about a local sport, it strongly inspired me to envision a similar powerful story centered around a grassroots competition like the Coochbehar Trophy - a sport that feels authentic and pure, unlike the often-scripted feel of something like the IPL. This movie not only inspires you to write a review but also sparks the creative fire for a screenplay of your own. The film is peppered with short, impactful dialogues that are pivotal to its emotional punch.From a technical standpoint, the screenplay is a masterclass. It flawlessly employs the 6-Act structure, meticulously charting the hero's journey, both inner and outer. The turning points are perfectly timed, creating a gripping and cohesive narrative. For anyone aspiring to learn screenplay writing, this movie is an invaluable resource and one of the best films to study."F1: The Movie" is a must-see for anyone who appreciates compelling storytelling, authentic emotion, and the thrilling world of sports. Highly recommended!

title: FAST Cars, Great Cinematography, Below par Story
content: F1: The Movie is a treat for the senses, especially if you're a fan of motorsports. The film delivers an undeniably immersive cinematic experience - one that truly comes alive in a theater setting. From the roar of the engines to the sweeping drone shots of the tracks, it captures the sheer intensity and speed of Formula 1 like few films have before. The racing sequences are choreographed with precision, giving viewers a front-row seat to the action, and the sound design makes you feel like you're right there in the pit lane.Visually, it's stunning. The cinematography makes excellent use of modern camera tech - onboard shots, slow-motion tire changes, and aerial views of circuits like Monaco, Silverstone, and Spa really stand out. If nothing else, this is a film that begs to be seen on the biggest screen possible.However, once the cars slow down, the film loses momentum. The story follows a fairly standard underdog-versus-establishment formula, with little in the way of surprise or emotional depth. The characters - including the ambitious young driver, the grizzled veteran, and the pressure-cooked team principal - all feel like familiar archetypes. While the performances are competent, they aren't given enough depth or unique development to make them memorable.The personal drama and behind-the-scenes politics that could have added emotional stakes feel rushed or glossed over. Compared to other great racing dramas like Rush or Ford v Ferrari, this film lacks the character-driven storytelling that makes you care beyond the checkered flag.Still, for pure spectacle, F1: The Movie delivers. If you're a fan of Formula 1, fast cars, or just want to enjoy some eye-popping race sequences on the big screen, this is worth a watch. Just go in expecting more style than substance.

title: What could have been...
content: F1 (2025) delivers exactly what its marketing promised - thrilling racing, big-screen energy, star power, and broad entertainment value. But despite that massive budget and great cast, it rarely reaches the deeper emotional heights or originality that would make it a future classic in the genre.It sits firmly in the category of "solid, exciting, definitely worth watching" - but not quite "legendary." If you go for spectacle and fun, it delivers; if you go for a transformative story about motorsport, it might feel a little safe.

title: Desperate
content: This film reeks of desperation and a need to be relevant. Jerry Bruckheimer desperate to be relevant again as a blockbuster producer but still just a pedlar of empty spectacle which this most certainly is. Brad Pitt desperate to be relevant as a handsome leading man and taken seriously, his last decent effort was Ad Astra in 2019 and the shine has definitely worn thin. Javier Bardem who was last relevant in 2021's Being The Ricardo's and who is just one of those acceptable Hispanic clichés ready to take any pay cheque. Hans Zimmer stopped being relevant quite a few film scores ago and the generic braying sound delivered here could have come from anybody. The plot is simplistic and stupid, failing F1 race team managed by Bardem hires old former F1 burn out, Pitt, to partner with young, arrogant hot shot driver, Damson Idris, to save the season. Along the way we have clashes of testosterone, sage wisdom, clichéd reflection on life, a woeful dalliance between Pitt and Kerry Condon - funny how in film a 20+ age difference between characters (and the actors) is fine yet out in the real world it's a massive problem, a ridiculous boardroom financial power struggle and more car crashes in half a season of racing that the real past 5 years. Nothing works in this overlong mess, everything to do with F1 is wrong, not one piece of the portrayal of the team or the sport is right. There is zero chemistry between any of the principal players, they could literally have been on different sound stages, and this compounded by an inane script and empty character development. The direction by Joseph Kosinski is just like his hit Top Gun Maverick, a series of loud set pieces stitched together with thin plot lines. At the end I felt nothing for any of the characters, Damson Idris's 'JP' was as insufferable as he was at the start, Pitt's old hand was still an unbelievable joke, nobody else developed at all. Amazingly the film made decent box office but I have no idea why, audiences must be just as desperate as the makers. Don't pay good money for this and if it is on your streaming service watch something else.

title: 'BAD AS I USED TO BE....!'
content:
I saw this early in the best IMAX chair! Treat yourself to the big screen! This will please everyone in the crowd, bring your friends.What was awesome:
1. Story of aging longshot Sonny Hayes (Pitt) vs prodigy Joshua Pearce (Idris). Pitt was especially great playing the charismatic veteran. Follow him from his surf van back to the track! His story gave me new personal inspiration. I like the now older Pitt better than ever. Bardem was also PERFECT as the high-roller team owner.2. Racing scenes were amazing on the big screen. Movie goers have never experienced racing like this before. A MUST on IMAX, hold on to your seat!3. The Soundtrack! Everything fit... I bought Stapleton's 'Bad as I used to be' after the show! I was actually moving to the different tracks in my plush AMC rocker.What wasn't that great:
1. The announcers narrated the races as though the audience had never seen one. This diminished the experience for actual race fans. The racing scenarios also tilted too heavy toward not believable.

title: Thrilling on Track, Flat off Track
content: I recently had the chance to watch F1 in IMAX, and I'll start with the biggest positive - the IMAX experience itself is absolutely stunning. The cinematography, camera work, and the way the races were shot were nothing short of spectacular. From onboard shots to wide race views, the movie captures the thrill of Formula 1 beautifully. The race sequences are gripping, suspenseful, and engaging, even if you already know what's likely to happen. That immersive presentation is easily the highlight of the film.Unfortunately, everything outside the racing falls flat. The acting from most of the supporting cast feels weak and unconvincing, with the exception of Brad Pitt and a couple of key characters. The writing is a real letdown - the dialogues are cheesy, often cringy, and don't land the way they're supposed to. There's virtually no character development, especially for Brad Pitt's character, whose backstory and personal struggles deserved more depth but were left unexplored.As for the racing strategies shown in the film, they lean heavily into cinematic drama. While some of it feels over the top, I can accept that as part of a movie experience. Non-F1 fans will likely find the final race strategy exciting and unpredictable, but if you follow the sport closely, you'll probably see the ending coming a mile away. Still, thanks to the presentation, the race sequences manage to keep you engaged.Overall, F1 is a film carried almost entirely by its visuals, sound, and IMAX experience. If you go in for the spectacle, you'll have a great time. But if you're looking for strong writing, layered characters, or a meaningful story, you'll be disappointed.It's enjoyable, worth a watch for the racing spectacle, but far from the 9s and 10s some people are giving it.

title: Great F1 Movie (especially for the real fans)
content: A thrilling ride for Formula 1 enthusiasts .F1 delivers on sound design, gripping cinematography, and a compelling story rooted in racing passion. While it captures the sport's intensity and drama, the film does drag at times and could've been tighter in pacing. Still, a must-watch for true fans. The attention to detail in the race sequences and pit stop dynamics is impressive, making it an immersive experience. Performances are strong, and the behind-the-scenes look at team rivalries adds depth. Despite its length, the film rewards patient viewers with emotional highs and authentic motorsport moments.

title: A enjoyable sports movie nicely done overall
content:
I had a good time watching this movie in Imax :)
It's been a while since i've went into an Imax room, since Avatar 2. I was a bit disapointed by the experience, you kinda get use to the incredible sound 30 minutes into the movie, and don't pay attention to it by the end when you're really grabbed by the plot.Regarding the movie, I was quite happy because it could have been a whashed up virilist car race movie like Gran Turismo seemed to be. Sure there were some parts that are totally cliché but that are adressed with enough lightness that you can easily get over it and even laught about it.Brad Pitt is great, amazing charisma (and physic, my god, They are having a battle with Tom Cruise for the 60+ physical actor)
The rest of the cast is also doing great !The races are amazingly filmed, and you expect that of course if you remembered Top Gun 2I was surprised that the F1 that I imagine had a big influence on the script insisted that much regarding the tactical aspect of the sport with lots of cunning.But that's overall a great movie done with tons of talent. title: F1 is a total adrenaline rush - and way more than just racing!
content: I just got out of the theater and I'm still buzzing. F1 completely blew me away. From the very first scene, I felt like I was in the driver's seat-every corner, every overtake, every heartbeat of the engine had me locked in. It wasn't just fast cars and flashy crashes-it had heart, conflict, and real stakes. The characters were layered, and the tension both on and off the track was gripping. This isn't just a movie for F1 fans-it's a film for anyone who loves a good story, well told.Forget what you've heard about racing movies being boring. F1 is fast, emotional, and totally immersive. Easily one of the best theater experiences I've had in a while.

title: Watch it, I guess
content: First and last time I'll write a review of any sort.Everyone has their own opinion. Mine... This movie is a classic.It's not mind-bending, yet bended my expectations.It has a little cliche sparkled in, yet life is nothing but that; dark backstories, inner-demons, adversity, and those few who have what it takes to use that to fuel their engines. If you disagree, then I suppose you're just not one of those. I personally didn't need a story about that, yet I didn't mind it at all. Most importantly, it's exactly what the movie needed, just how Top Gun: Maverick needed it.Both visual and audio designs were spot on. Immaculate, in fact. The same adjective could be used to describe the music. The names of the professionals responsible speak for themselves.A movie that paid attention to the story of the sport and to details, with several references to the past. One happens during the Mexican GP. In the first corner, Sergio Perez's car lifts off the track, exactly how it actually happened in 2023. The entire F1 environment was phenomenal, including the participation of actual riders and professionals from Formula 1. Lewis Hamilton helped produce the movie, so I guess that's that.I'm rambling, I guess. If you want and can go to the cinema to watch the movie, go. It's a classic.

title: F1 (Hollywood style) wasn't too bad.
content: Firstly, to all those who posted about this movie not being true to F1 with too many Hollywoodisms that ruined it - F1 was never about thrilling and wooing existing fans. It was about bringing the thrill and excitement of the sport to a NEW audience and potential new fans!Personally, I've been a fan of Formula 1 for almost 50 years and this movie was very enjoyable. Compared to other movies (such as Driven) it sits head and shoulders above the rest. Yes, there are elements and events which, to an avid F1 follower, are laughable and surreal. There are also situations which bring back bad memories and horror stories to those who've been around long enough to have seen the real life events play out. But the thrill of the race and the team pulling together and winning is in there - and that's what motor racing is all about. I gave F1 7/10 for the entertainment factor. If it had focused more on the racing than the dramatic private lives of the characters then it would have been a 9/10.One way that F1 could have been better would be to serialize it into episodes rather than a 2.5 hour movie. It did feel a bit rushed when the end credits were rolling by.

The plot is interesting and designed so that even those unfamiliar with Formula 1 can understand and enjoy every aspect of the sport. The performances, especially that of the lead character, bring realism and charisma, while the direction and race scenes are visually stunning.I liked how the film explains the rules and technology of F1 without becoming tedious, allowing any viewer to become involved in the story. It's ideal for both sports fans and those looking for a good movie full of action and drama. In short, an exciting and well-made film that I highly recommend for those seeking quality entertainment in sports cinema.

title: Worth The Watch!
content: First there were Steve McQueen, James Garner and Paul Newman. Then Tom Cruise starred in the noteworthy racing film, "Days of Thunder". Now Brad Pitt stars in the spectacle, "F1: The Movie", which I watched in IMAX, wanting to capitalize on the audiovisual extravaganza that is reputed to have cost $200 million.I was somewhat wary, because I had read reviews that it's all spectacle, not much of a story. But I am pleased to say that the story does develop its characters, it does contain emotional moments, and it does have plot twists. Actually, the writing is quite good.Sonny Hayes (Pitt), to the racing establishment, is a has-been, an outsider, an unconventional individualist in a team sport. But, like Ted Lasso, he thinks outside the box, he pushes boundaries, and just when you think he is a walking disaster, he reveals a native wisdom that might lead to success.Pitt is very likable in this role, and he is surrounded by a cast who work together well. You have to like what Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and Kerry Condon do-even if Michael Rooker, Robert Duvall, and Nicole Kidman were given meatier roles in "Days of Thunder"-and Sarah Niles is quite fun to watch as the mother of Sonny's teammate in a smaller role.I recommend IMAX, if it's available. When the camera takes you inside the cockpit, and the track is whizzing by, it's a thrilling taste of what the world must look like for an F1 driver.

title: Reformulated Top Gun without the history
content: The film is in premise similar to Kosinski's last major block buster Top Gun Maverick. Young hot shots and skilled old timer butt heads in some adrenaline filled activity where they have a huge challenge in front of them.Unlike Top Gun, these characters don't have a history with audiences. Little is done to build that for either of the leads, making the overall arc of the movie and its tensions a low investment.The plot is spoon fed to the audience with the turns being obvious. The movie felt stuck between targeting an existing F1 fan base and bringing along non-F1 viewers for the ride with plenty of dialog from the track announcer and various other characters during every race sequence. Like Top Gun it's not meant to be a film to deliver a thought provoking story so this it's hard to knock it.Visually the film was flashy and it's hard not to be in the world of F1. It was difficult to tell where the film needed the more vertical aspect ratio of IMAX. F1 cars and racing are naturally a fairly low to the ground sport. Many of the shots were similar to what you'd find in a traditional F1 broadcast which isn't necessarily bad (they were done well) but for a feature film I expected more than what the Drive to Survive series would offer.Overall it's an F1 fan movie with a good soundtrack, plenty of racing scenes and lots (lots!) of F1 promotion.

title: Literally a "HIGH-OCTANE" movie
content: I'm really glad that the summer blockbuster season this year is kicking off with "F1 The Movie".The word "high-octane" feels like it was invented just for "F1 The Movie". The two and a half hours I spent in front of the IMAX screen flew by just like an F1 car. This film is, without a doubt, the best commercial movie of the first half of 2025. I absolutely loved Joseph Kosinski's commitment to shooting on location at major F1 races to enhance the film's realism. I appreciated even more how he once again utilized and improved upon the cinematography techniques from "Top Gun: Maverick", seamlessly bringing them into the visual language of "F1 The Movie". He masterfully delivers the speed and intensity of the F1 world in an incredibly immersive way, allowing the audience to experience the thrill and passion of every race right alongside Brad Pitt and Damson Idris.In terms of storytelling, while the overall structure is relatively traditional, the character development and growth are clearly crafted with care. Many people might compare "F1 The Movie" to "Top Gun: Maverick", but to me, the two films handle character portrayal differently rather than simply applying the same template. Brad Pitt remains undeniably charismatic on the big screen, and Damson Idris holds his own without being overshadowed. Their performances complement each other perfectly.This movie had a huge sensory impact on me. Paired with Hans Zimmer's score and original songs created by various artists for the film, the viewing experience was elevated even further. Producer Lewis Hamilton also made significant contributions to the project. "F1 The Movie" is one I would gladly watch again and again, and it's absolutely a film that demands to be seen on an IMAX screen. After watching it, I've become even more interested in F1 and want to learn more about the sport!

title: Style or Substance?
content: F1 The Movie looks and sounds amazing. It has the Netflix Drive to Survive aesthetic and all the official F1 production templates including real teams and drivers. The racing scenes are exhilarating for the most part, we get to visit several cool locations around the world and all of this is backed by a Hans Zimmer score. Despite all of these good things, I still struggled with the movie...The story is extremely cliché which in itself isn't a bad thing but the dialogue is the main downfall here, at times coming across as very cringy. As a result, the characters aren't particularly memorable and sometimes not likeable. The romance lacks feeling and the twist is an unfortunate one. At times, the dialogue also impacts the enjoyment of the racing scenes when the words and actions appear unrealistic. Maybe this impacted me more as person who has some knowledge of F1 albeit not the most hardcore fan.For me this dampened my enjoyment of the movie when compared to the likes of Ford v Ferrari, Rush and Senna but for many others the sights, sounds, racing and the stars on screen will be enough to make this an enjoyable summer blockbuster.

title: F1: The Movie - A Visceral, Soulful Masterpiece Racing to New Heights
content: "The roar of engines becomes the heartbeat of cinema itself."Joseph Kosinski has done it again. After revolutionizing aerial combat with Top Gun: Maverick, he's now conquered the asphalt with a film that doesn't just capture Formula 1 racing; it becomes Formula 1 racing. Every frame pulses with the raw energy of 200mph dreams and the weight of human ambition. This isn't merely a sports film; it's a visceral experience that grabs you by the throat and refuses to let go until the checkered flag falls.Brad Pitt's performance as Sonny Hayes is nothing short of extraordinary. At 61, Pitt embodies the weathered veteran with such authenticity that you forget you're watching a fiction film. His Sonny is a man haunted by what was, driven by what could still be, and desperate to prove that experience trumps youth when it matters most. Every wrinkle tells a story, every glance carries decades of racing wisdom, and when he grips that steering wheel, you believe this man has lived and breathed Formula 1 for his entire existence. It's career-defining work that reminds us why Pitt remains one of the greatest actors of all time.The mentor-student dynamic between Pitt and Damson Idris crackles with genuine chemistry. Idris brings youthful fire to his role as the rising star, creating a perfect foil to Pitt's seasoned veteran. Their relationship evolves from skepticism to respect to something resembling family, and you feel every beat of that journey. The script wisely avoids cheap sentiment, instead earning its emotional moments through shared victories, devastating losses, and the universal language of speed.Kosinski's direction is simply breathtaking. He understands that Formula 1 isn't just about cars going fast; it's about the human drama unfolding at impossible speeds. His camera work during the race sequences is revolutionary, placing you inside the cockpit, on the track, and in the very soul of the sport. The way he captures the ballet of pit stops, the chess match of strategy, and the raw terror of high-speed crashes creates an immersive experience that makes you feel like you're wearing the helmet yourself.The cinematography during the race sequences defies description. Every turn of the track becomes a work of art, every overtaking maneuver a dance of death and glory. The cameras capture not just the speed but the feeling of speed, the way the world blurs at the edges, how milliseconds stretch into eternity, how a single decision can mean the difference between triumph and tragedy. Monaco has never looked more beautiful or more dangerous. Silverstone becomes a cathedral of speed. Abu Dhabi transforms into a neon-lit arena where legends are born.Hans Zimmer's score is pure emotional rocket fuel. His music doesn't just accompany the action, it is the action. The way his themes weave through the roar of engines, building to crescendos that make your heart race faster than any V6 turbo, is masterful. There's a moment during the final race where his score swells as Sonny makes his move, and I swear I stopped breathing. The emotional weight of every note perfectly captures the hopes, dreams, and fears of everyone who's ever dared to chase the impossible.What struck me most was how the film captures the essence of motorsport itself. Like Top Gun: Maverick did for aviation, this movie understands that racing isn't just about winning, it's about the pursuit of perfection, the acceptance of risk, and the brotherhood forged in the crucible of competition. The film respects the sport's history while making it accessible to newcomers, never talking down to either audience.The emotional impact hit me like a freight train. I found myself cheering during the victories, my heart breaking during the setbacks, and ultimately feeling inspired by the reminder that it's never too late to chase your dreams. The film made me want to believe in second chances, in the power of mentorship, and in the idea that sometimes the greatest victories come not from crossing the finish line first, but from proving to yourself that you still belong in the race.This movie made me feel like a kid again, watching wide-eyed as these modern gladiators dance with death at 200 miles per hour. It reminded me why I fell in love with Formula 1 in the first place, not just for the speed, but for the human stories unfolding within those carbon fiber cocoons.F1: The Movie is essential viewing for racing fans, but it's equally powerful for anyone who's ever faced doubt, anyone who's ever needed a mentor, or anyone who's ever wondered if they still had one more fight left in them. It's a film that respects its audience's intelligence while delivering pure, unadulterated entertainment.This isn't just a film, it's a race to the heart.

content:

Cinema that vibrates.

title: A High-Octane Spectacle for All Audiences
content: F1 manages to be an entertaining ride even for those with no particular interest in motorsports. I don't follow racing, yet the film held my attention from start to finish, enhanced by the immersive experience of a Dolby Atmos screening that elevated every engine roar and tire screech.The plot follows a well-worn formula - a retired legend returning for one last shot at glory - and while predictable, it's competently executed. The performances aren't groundbreaking, but they serve the story adequately. What the film lacks in originality, it makes up for with energy, pacing, and a clear understanding of its genre. A bit overlong, perhaps, but certainly engaging, and likely even more rewarding for fans of Formula 1.

title: A Fun, Flawed, Formulaic Look at F1
content: "F1" is a thrill ride of a film that centers on Formula 1 racing. But it's also, um, formulaic.Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) is a talented former F1 driver who never made it to the top. He's recruited by Reuben (Javier Bardem), a former racing rival who now manages the hapless APXGP team on the F1 circuit. Sonny is brought in to mentor Joshua (Damson Idris), the company's talented rookie driver, teach him valuable life lessons and demonstrate that a driver should race for the love of the sport, not the fame and acclaim that accompany success at this level.Co-Writer/Director Joseph Kosinski, who directed "Top Gun: Maverick," has involved many members of that team on his current film. Co-Writer Ehren Kruger is back. Composer Hans Zimmer delivers another propulsive score that perfectly fits the action taking place. Cinematographer Claudio Miranda has substantially improved on the already-excellent cinematic techniques he employed in "Top Gun." Editor Stephen Mirrione, who was not involved in the previous project, also deserves mention for contributing to a story that starts out fast, then accelerates.The technical aspects of this film are simply amazing. F1 champion Lewis Hamilton signed on as a producer for the film. Because of his involvement, many current F1 drivers - Oscar Piastri, Lando Norris and others - along with Toto Wolff (principal of Mercedes' F1 racing team) participated in the film and served as technical advisors. Many current F1 drivers took part in the racing sequences.Director Kosinski outfitted six F2 race cars with cameras, recorders and transmitters. During the racing scenes, Pitt and Idris drove on actual F1 racetracks (the scenes were shot during Grand Prix weekends throughout 2023 and 2024). During the racing scenes, Cinematographer Miranda sat at a monitor with sixteen screens, directing the camera operators and selecting shots. Most of the cameras were on motorized mounts, allowing the cameras to be readjusted to track the action in close-up. As a result of this technical expertise, "F1" is realistic and exhilarating.In addition to its substantial strengths, this film has major flaws. Calling the story formulaic is actually charitable. The gruff veteran who has problems with authority (and secretly has a heart of gold)? Check. A gifted, self-absorbed younger man whose hubris exceeds his talent and who needs to learn the value of teamwork? Yep. Setbacks caused by unsavory outside forces? Sure. A period of self-doubt before ... You get the idea. You can see it all coming from wayyyyy down the track.Then there's casting Pitt as a Formula 1 driver. Pitt was 60 during much of the filming. The retirement age for the average F1 driver is 37. Although Pitt is as charismatic and charming as ever, the stereotype of the worldly-wise, wily veteran that worked for Tom Cruise as a pilot in "Top Gun" doesn't seem nearly as plausible here. For me, there was a melancholy element to all this. Pitt, at times, seems to be literally straining to reassert his relevance in a Hollywood often prone to move on without sentimentality to the newer, younger next big thing.It's easy and appropriate to recommend "F1" as flashy, sleek, adrenaline-pumping entertainment. It's a fun afternoon at the movies. You have to look quite closely to see the wrinkles.

title: Pitt Stop
content: No film genre is more formulaic and cliche-ridden than sports, and to be sure F1 doesn't break any new ground. But formulas become cliches for one simple reason. They work.The old saying "What's old becomes new again" definitely applies here. In an age where Hollywood continues to offer up the tired comic book film and unneeded and often unwanted remake, what audiences are thirsting for-even if they don't exactly know it-is a return to the red meat and potatoes "Good guy overcomes challenges and wins/saves the day." type of film. No capes flapping or heavy-handed messages. Just likeable people we can root for. It's so simple you wonder why studios can't grasp it. But hey, I'm just a paying customer. What do I know?F1 is an appropriately fast-paced (if a tad bit over long) movie that is perfect for Summer movie season. It looks spectacular, eschewing the CGI cheats of so many Summer "event" films these days, and delivers white-knuckle action that feels tactile. Director Joseph Kosinski, who directed the excellent Top Gun: Maverick (a film that should have woken up Woke Hollywood and showed them how to make movies-and money-again), really knows how to make these kinds of films. I wish he'd been named director of the new Bond film instead of the overrated Denis Villeneuve and his soulless, joy-deficient style.Like Top Gun's Tom Cruise, Pitt is one of our last real movie stars. He's surrounded here by a capable cast, but make no mistake this is his movie and he carries it across the finish line.

title: We Need More Films Like This
content: After seeing the first trailer for F1 over a year ago, I thought to myself this will be a total misfire or one of the best racing films I have ever seen. Fortunately it is the latter.I went to see F1 opening day in Dolby and within the first ten minutes I knew Joseph Kosinski had done it again. Unlike all the sequels, superhero films and live action remakes that studios have been churning out, F1 is an original film which is such a rarity nowadays. While it is a fairly predictable story that we have seen a million times in other films, it somehow works.Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon all do a great job here as well as the supporting cast. The score by Hans Zimmer really elevates the film in certain moments and there are multiple songs that play throughout the film that are perfectly placed.The racing sequences are incredible and very realistic. You can tell the entire crew gave it their all to make sure everything was as authentic as possible and there is a moment toward the end of the film that brought tears to my eyes as a lifelong car enthusiast.Hollywood should take notes. We need more feel good films like F1 that don't have a million different messages being shoved down our throat. F1 is hands down the best film I have seen so far in 2025 and even if your not into Formula One, I would highly recommend this film.

title: Visually stunning, but overall forgettable
content: This movie is like a one night stand you never see again. Looks - 10, substance - 2. Overall I thought it was enjoyable enough for what it was. title: Go see it
content: By far, one of the best movies I've seen lately. Perfect casting and great acting. All of the actors played by the book, and it's nice to see Brad Pitt doing what he really does best, fully immersing himself in a role and carrying the film with charisma, intensity, and authenticity.F1 is not just a racing movie; it's a film that blends adrenaline, storytelling, and character development in a way that makes it accessible even to those who are not die-hard Formula 1 fans. From the very first scene, the energy is gripping. The sound design, the cinematography, and the pacing put you right in the driver's seat.What really elevates the film, however, is the balance between high-speed action and human drama. Brad Pitt's performance is magnetic, portraying a veteran driver facing both personal and professional challenges. The supporting cast does an exceptional job as well, giving the story depth and emotional layers. It's refreshing to see how well each actor fits their role, there's no weak link in the lineup, which is rare for a film with such high expectations and pressure.Director Joseph Kosinski deserves credit for capturing the thrill of Formula 1 in a cinematic style that feels both authentic and larger than life. The collaboration with real F1 teams and drivers adds credibility, while the storytelling keeps it from being just a spectacle. It's a film that respects the sport but also appeals to a broad audience.Overall, F1 delivers on every front: acting, direction, emotion, and sheer entertainment value. It's more than just a racing movie, it's a story of resilience, passion, and the relentless pursuit of excellence. Without a doubt, a must-watch.
