title: Masterful big screen entertainment
content: F1 reunites most of the Top Gun: Maverick crew to deliver another incredible roller coaster ride in the form of a summer blockbuster. It's built on the classic underdog story of a sports movie and executes it flawlessly, enhanced by the technology used to push it further. It shows that Formula 1 is a team effort and contains some of the best racing scenes ever put to screen. By being Top Gun with race cars it offers a welcome reminder of why we go to the movies.Brad Pitt is excellent in a leading role tailor made for his rugged movie star charms. He has chemistry with pretty much everyone he comes into contact with and is instantly likeable. Damson Idris brings his own brand of charisma to his star-making performance and only gets better when he eventually warms to his teammate. They feel like equals and the time given to each of them to navigate their own personal and professional struggles allows both of them to properly stand out.The major players in the supporting cast are all given enough to work with so it's easy to root for them. Javier Bardem has never looked better and makes it abundantly clear how big the stakes are. Kerry Condon is able to be won over by Brad Pitt without losing her independence or confidence in her own skill set and Tobias Menzies is a suitably slimy board member. Kim Bodnia, Callie Cooke and Abdul Salis turn their smaller roles into vital and likeable characters.Joseph Kosinski's direction crafts a gorgeous looking film that has ridiculously slick visuals and never goes too long between races. Claudio Miranda's awards worthy cinematography accomplishes goosebump inducing stuff and Stephen Mirrione's editing ensures those racing sequences are always extremely clean. The film strikes a fantastic balance between a soundtrack containing the ideal songs for any given moment and a beautiful 80s style score by Hans Zimmer.

title: Might End Up Being My Favorite Movie Of Summer 2025
content: I had little expectations coming into F-1. I have zero knowledge of that sport and Brad Pitt isn't usually enough of a draw for me in and of himself. But after what director Joseph Kosinski did with Top Gun Maverick in 2022, I was intrigued to see what he could accomplish here. That proved to be the key-as Kosinski (and writer Ehren Kruger) do here for Pitt what Maverick did for Tom Cruise.For a very basic overview, F-1 tells the story of Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), an old race-hound (any vehicle will do, really) brought onto a new F-1 team-led by Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) & Kate McKenna (Kerry Condon)-to help mentor young driver Joshue Pearce (Damson Idris). With collapse seemingly imminent at every turn, the team must learn to work together or be removed from the sport altogether.Trying to explain exactly why F-1 works so well is actually a little difficult, because it is extremely straightforward from a plot perspective. You will have seen this narrative before: older athlete must decide between chasing one last shot at glory versus mentoring those around him. It really is that simple from a thematic perspective here.But it is of course anything but simple to make that all work in a blockbuster film, and that's where Kosinski comes in-understanding exactly what is needed:-Building the entire picture around Pitt (like Maverick did w/Cruise) is the first masterstroke, as Pitt is a true movie star (now a bit of a throwback term) when given the right role.-Filling the entire cast with competent actors and-perhaps more importantly-giving them all an emotional stake in the proceedings. Even a member of the pit crew is given an angle that will have you rooting for success!-Adapting the Maverick jet-filming technology to F-1 vehicles. This is a film that begs to be seen in premium visual (IMAX) or audio (Dolby Atmos) formats. An absolute thrill-ride when on the track.Perhaps the hallmark of F-1 is its overall ability to get viewers invested in the APX team. All the racing bells-and-whistles are great (wonderful here, even), but the investment in the main characters all the way down to the basic team members is what will have viewers emotionally hooked all the way to the end credits. The screenplay even toys with those emotions-showcasing some nice feints even within the basic sports movie formula.All in all, I was absolutely blown away by F-1, especially as a "newbie" to the entire concept. Director Kosinski has the storytelling and tech down to a science, which allows the principals (especially Pitt & Condon) to elevate to stratospheric acting heights. This may well end up being my favorite movie of Summer '25.

title: Fueled by Greatness, Driven by Pitt and Bardem
content: The Movie is one of those rare sports films that manages to feel both grounded and larger than life at the same time. What really worked for me was that the fictional characters placed so seamlessly into the real Formula 1 world that at times you forget this isn't based on a true story.Brad Pitt delivers a standout performance as Sonny Hayes. He brings grit, depth, and a quiet vulnerability to the role that pulls you in from the start. Javier Bardem is equally phenomenal..commanding every scene with a mix of intensity and charisma that only he can pull off. Together, they're magnetic. In fact, their acting is so strong it makes the rest of the cast look like background extras. You're watching a movie about racing, but it feels like you're witnessing two masters at work on a different level.Visually, the movie is stunning. The racing scenes are immersive, but what stood out even more is how the film captured the emotion behind the sport..the pressure, the culture, the obsession with perfection. It's not your typical "win-the-race" plot. It's more reflective, personal, and human.

title: Honest review
content: Cinematography, soundtrack, the shots-everything in this movie is a pure race to win all the Oscar categories. It's fast, fierce, and visually stunning. title: Not bad, but not real F1
content: Ok, the racing camera angles are great. And it is nice to see real F1 teams, drivers and cars participate in the movie.Given the plot they have to work with, the actors do a decent job, although some of the dialogues are quite silly.But please, do we really need the Hollywood treatment of a self-induced car crash and explosions every second race? Like it's another Marvel movie. And Sonny Hayes (Pitt's character) never gets penalty points for the deliberate crashes he causes? Curbs launching cars into the air? Drivers who can talk to eachother now? And what happened to qualifying, arguably one of the most exciting parts of F1 in reality?I understand the audience the movie is made for, and that it is purely there to get more people come to races, subscribe to F1 services, etc, but it would have been nice to make it a bit more realistic. It is the camerawork that makes it a 7 out of 10, just the story itself would be a 6.

title: F1: A thrilling ride of passion and redemption
content:
"It's not about money"!Beyond the finish line of life, F1 explores the essence of redemption and second chances. Brad Pitt shines as Sonny Hayes, a veteran driver, driven by more than just speed & glory of the F1 world. His racing journey started with a fire of passion kindled by his father, turning him into a sensational beast and
spanning over 30 years, is fueled by a deeper purpose. When recruited by former rival Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), Sonny gets a chance to revive his fading dreams, which he lost after a crash on one of his racing laps, where he fractured his cervical, thoracic and lumber spine and puts him out of the F1 racing world, shackled his dreams and passion to be the No.1 in the world.The film masterfully weaves together themes of perseverance, passion, and the pursuit of glory. With heart-pumping racing sequences and poignant character moments, F1 is an unforgettable ride.Let's get one thing straight - this isn't a film review, so if that's what you're looking for, you're in the wrong place. This is about my connection with Brad Pitt, an actor I've been a huge fan of for years. It took me 15 long years to finally watch him on the big screen, and it was absolutely worth the wait an incredible, nail biting, and absolutely thrilling experience.I'm not much of a cinema goer I preferred to watch films in my own mini cinema, but when a movie catches my eye and deserves the big screen, I'm always eager to jump into action. You might wonder what's taken me so long to watch William Bradley Pitt on the big screen. Well, here's the thing: when I started watching him back in August 2012 with Troy which was an old film, he was already fading into more obscure roles and Killing Them Softly was released at that time. I missed out on some of his notable films like Fury and World War Z, which I would have loved to see in theaters, but I wasn't sure about going to the cinema back then even the tickets of Bullet Train slipped through my fingers as I have planned to watch in Cue Cinema & I asked a friend to join me multiple times, but he never showed up, and the movie was pulled out after its regular screening. Despite the wait, my enthusiasm for Brad Pitt never faded as I watched him in horrendous "The Lost City", "Babylon" & "Wolfs".When I heard about F1 in development, I was determined to watch it in the cinema, no matter what and this time, I wasn't waiting for anyone to join me. I searched online for nearby cinemas and found a suitable show at 4:30 pm at Cinepax, Amanah Mall on June 27, 2025. However, just as I was about to go, an emergency patient appointment came up, and I had to cancel my plans.Luckily, I got another chance, and on June 28, 2025, I arrived at the cinema at 4:30 pm, buzzing with energy and enthusiasm to watch Brad Pitt. I even hoped to find posters to take a photo with, but unfortunately, there weren't any. After getting my ticket, I entered the cinema hall, ready to watch Brad in action. But what I got was so much more, I met Sunny Hayes, a veteran driver fighting to stay relevant, and his story of shattered dreams and broken ambitions left a lasting impact.F1 has redefined Brad Pitt's legacy, touching the very core of our being, especially for those who dare to dream. I dared to dream and fallen badly and It's a poignant tale of a fading star, once radiant with promise, now teetering on the brink of extinction, a powerful metaphor for the human experience, which I experienced as well, and It's heartbreaking to let go things off your chest as now they started to burdened you but F1 is different as the film masterfully captures the essence of chasing fallen dreams, those we nurtured with loved ones who left us too soon, leaving behind unfulfilled aspirations. It's a testament to the fragility of human potential, where the whirlwind of ambition can both propel and paralyze us.Through Sunny Hayes' story, we're reminded that life is a delicate balance of perseverance and surrender, of holding on to our passions while confronting the harsh realities of our limitations. F1 is an ode to the resilience of the human spirit, a reflection on the dreams we've lost, and the dreams we continue to chase.F1 is a masterclass in classical filmmaking, showcasing pure cinema in every frame. Joseph Kosinski's direction is a stroke of genius, weaving thrilling racing sequences with emotional depth. Following Top Gun: Maverick, F1 solidifies Kosinski's reputation for crafting high-octane films that captivate audiences.The film's dynamic score by Hans Zimmer is a work of art, perfectly complementing Stephen Stephen Mirrione's elegant editing and Claudio Miranda's frenetic cinematography. The music pulses through the film, immersing viewers in the heart pumping world of Formula 1 racing. As a fan, I'm already planning to watch F1 again, it's a cinematic gem that deserves repeated viewings. The film's ability to put you in the driver's seat, feeling the rush of adrenaline as you throttle through tight curves, is truly exhilarating.Despite its predictable plot, F1 zooms by like a thunderous flash, keeping you thoroughly engaged. Sonny Hayes' journey resonates deeply, If you're a fading dreamer, and a story of testament to resilience and passion. As he rises from the ashes, driven by love and determination, it's impossible not to be moved. In the penultimate scenes, I found myself wanting to cheer and give a standing ovation, swept up in Sonny's triumphant moment, shed some tears with him though the audience around me remained stoic, I appreciated the immersive experience, letting the story's emotional impact speak for itself.As summer unfolds in Pakistan, I highly recommend watching F1 in cinemas near you. This film is a masterful storytelling experience that explores the dynamics of passion, love, and ambition.It's a great opportunity to take your kids and have a meaningful conversation about what truly matters in life. In a world where fame and fortune often take center stage, F1 reminds us that passion and love are the driving forces that bring fulfillment. Sometimes, it's better to tune out the noise and focus on what truly matters.

title: Aspect Ratio is tiresome
content: The film is solid. Like others have said its Top Gun with race cars. Pitt and Bardem bring some weight and give the fairly thin plot some weight. It all works, its enjoyable.Now.I know, I'm sorry I can't always get away to my IMAX screen and wade through traffic 2 hours each way, pay exorbitant prices to sit in a theater where a kids on TikTok the entire time. I like to watch stuff at home. So why (even when it was reported to be on the physical disc) are we not getting the IMAX aspect ratio?Is it so they can re-release later? Saving it for a new streaming service launch?Nolans films switch ratios all the time. It's the same thing with Dune. These amazing properly used IMAX camera scenes are now just regular 2:39 the entire time.It's BS and I feel more people should be very annoyed by it. Its a slap in the face to the customer.The ENTIRE selling point of this film is again TOP GUN WITH RACE CARSI want the aspect ratio on my TV to reflect the reason very, very, very expensive cameras were used to film the GD scene. You're telling me the guy who made Top Gun Maverick that had a very well selling video release with switching aspect ratios was handcuffed in the release of this film?Tom Cruise would never.

title: Want realism? Forget it.
content: If you want realism, walk away now. This is not the film for you - as made evident by my F1 nut of a dad who pointed out all the plot holes and problems with the story. For example: "They wouldn't just let him come off the streets and race in an F1 car. He needs a special licence and while he might have had it all those years ago when, supposedly, racing Senna - it would have lapsed and he'd have needed to get a new one".That being said, if you want a silly but entertaining popcorn movie with some great racing scenes (although I preferred the racing in the film RUSH) then, you'll get a blast out of this. Sure the story is predictable and acting so-so (Pitt is, essentially, just Pitt but that's all he has done for his whole career and we still go and watch him so - fair play).The long running time felt like it rushed by and - of course - every scene was enhanced tenfold by the music of Hans Zimmer.Not sure how well the film will translate on a smaller screen but in the cinema? It was fast, loud and in your face... just as it should be.

title: Joseph Kosinski understands cinema
content: This is a theater must watch for any movie lover. Absolute cinema. Every shot on the racing track is incredible and brimming with talent behind the screen. Every actor in front of the camera also does a wonderful job. Brad Pitt should be seen as a top 10 all-time actor by now. Every role he takes he OWNS. Damson Idris is going to be a big star in Hollywood for decades to come. Kerry Condon and Javier Bardem are always great in every role as well. There wasn't a single weak point from the talent pool in the cast or behind the camera.The only issue is that the story is a bit too familiar perhaps by now. I mean Cars 3 did this exact same story eight years ago. That being said though, by the end, the only appropriate response to this story are tears. I enjoyed every second and I will not forget how good the engines revving up sounded in the cinema.

title: Worth it for IMAX
content: F1 has two great things going for it, and that's mostly the racing scenes and the pumping soundtrack. The story is pretty basic, not too many surprises in that department. Although I must say that some outcomes of the races did surprise. Pitt does a solid job, he seems to channel Robert Redford, getting by mostly with his charme. The romance I found a little bit contrived and unnecessary to be honest (similar as in Top Gun Maverick). In general, there are a couple of parts and story beats that made the whole thing a little bit longer than it should be. Especially the middle sags a little. To be fair, the majority of viewers are showing up for the race scenes and especially the first two and the last one are worth the admission alone. Some of them are breathtaking in an IMAX cinema where a pumping bass can literally make your seat shake. Some camera angles were a bit overused and repetitive like the quick camera pans from the race track to the cockpit. I personally preferred the simple POV shots, but it's just nitpicking. The last race is pure cinema: sound design and camera work go hand in hand and create pure cinema. In general I can say that the movie offers a very good immersive experience, especially when entering the first F1 race. You can not go wrong checking this movie out, but if you have the means to watch it in IMAX, you shouldn't think twice.

title: Great Movie - Exceeded all expectations
content: The Movie is an outstanding film that truly exceeded all expectations. Brad Pitt shines in the role of a veteran driver making a comeback, bringing depth and charisma to the screen. The racing scenes are breathtaking-shot on real tracks with impressive attention to detail, they deliver a level of intensity and realism that makes you feel like you're right there in the action. The cinematography is stunning, and the sound design pulls you into every roar of the engine and screech of the tires. The story balances high-stakes racing drama with heartfelt moments, offering a narrative that's both thrilling and emotionally resonant. With strong performances, a great supporting cast, and a pulse-pounding score, the film appeals not just to Formula 1 fans, but to anyone who enjoys a well-made, high-energy sports drama. It's a cinematic ride worth taking-fast, fun, and full of heart.

title: Cockpit Cinema: Kosinski's F1 Delivers Adrenaline with Style
content: Man, I wanna criticise F1: The Movie so hard!But how can I? Joseph Kosinski has done a wonderful job. Tron, Maverick and now F1, he has now cemented a brand around his name. He has delivered a cockpit-level filmmaking once again, with a hell of a theatrical experience. This Kosinski-Kruger duo is working frighteningly well, delivering a perfect paced tight framework. I'm not fully happy with the dialogue cause it was good, perhaps was on the verge of great, but, sadly, not a classic one. Make no mistake, it was top-tier, but I expected more from this type of classic-ish movies. As for Cinematography, the Life of Pi runner produced some visceral, adrenaline rush of driving carved into light. It is a technical marvel, perhaps a visual symphony both from the director and cinematographer. Hans Zimmer, another crew of Maverick, provided a noticeable track as well.Each and every one of the technical and creative team delivered within their expertise, and it was brave. Nicholas Barber from BBC stated that the movie delivers exactly what you guessed, except that your version might have some more high-stakes jeopardy than the actual film, which is somewhat true. But that doesn't stop it from being the best film contender of 2025. And again,IT'S THAT KINDA MOVIE YOU GO TO THEATERS FOR.PS: Ford v Ferrari is still the best :3.

title: good watch
content:
Great watch
gave me good insight into the F1 world especially the behind the scenes. Never thought it was that huge that it's scientifically and technologically advanced.I'll discuss things i like and dislikeI like how realistic it is.. really captures the world of F1 authentically. Showcasing real events and drivers .. even some shots were filmed in real events. Very Impactful for the movie.Also, racing scenes are exquisitely captured... they really portray the "speed and adrenaline rush" aspect of f1... really felt it.Brad Pitt... he's one of my favorite actors of all time. Instead of allowing the dialogue to flow naturally, the camera constantly cuts back and forth between characters - sometimes even mid-sentence. title: F1 (2025)
content: F1 is a film about a professional environment where speed is not a spectacle but a working tool. Kosinski deliberately moves away from the romanticization of racing: there is no familiar drama of a "sports comeback," no artificial tension around a final showdown, no glorification of risk. Instead, the film attempts to show how motorsport actually functions from the inside - technically, organizationally, and emotionally.The protagonist is former Formula 1 driver Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), brought into a young team not to chase records but to support their new lead driver (Damson Idris) on the track. Their dynamic isn't a rivalry or a clash - it's a working partnership inside an overloaded industry. Hayes is a transitional figure: not a mentor in the traditional sense, but someone positioned between his own past experience and another person's future. This makes the film not a tale of triumph, but an observation of how an older professional transfers knowledge without grandeur and without insisting on being the center of the story.Kosinski and cinematographer Claudio Miranda build the film's visual language on a mix of documentary authenticity and measured directorial restraint. Race sequences are shot in real conditions, using embedded cameras and actual track logistics. Their purpose is not to dazzle but to convey the environment as it is - negotiations, miscalculations, technical failures, fatigue, and repetition. In F1, speed isn't treated as an event - it's the background against which people emerge.Hans Zimmer's score, contrary to expectations, almost never dramatizes the action. It emphasizes the rhythmic structure of team operations rather than emotional peaks. This reinforces the film's central focus: routine, process, and internal tension rather than packaged emotional payoff.Pitt's performance is built on precise control: the character exists in a state of constant inward evaluation. This isn't the return of a "forgotten legend," but a portrait of a man who understands his place in the system and doesn't need his status reaffirmed. Damson Idris is convincing as a young driver whose main goal isn't to win at any cost but to maintain himself in an environment that burns people out quickly.Kerry Condon's role deserves special note. Her technical director is a key figure in how operations are run. Without attempts to "brighten up" her character through dramatic flourishes, she conveys the management side of motorsport with clarity, logic, and an economy of words.This is a professionally assembled film about a professional system, one in which victory matters less than the ability to function under overload, make decisions under pressure, and maintain precision when everything around you is built on speed.F1 is one of the few sports films that truly understands what a professional environment is. No artificial drama, no imposed meaning. An honest, technically meticulous, restrained film about people who don't engage in heroic gestures - they simply do their job. Strongly recommended.

title: Movie is like a big sugar rush
content: Let's face it, this film was never going to be some layered, cinematic masterpiece that left you with questions. It's just a racing movie, and that's okay. But it's not a film that is going to linger with you, it's just a quick sugar rush. It's fun, but not really in a way that leaves a memorable impression. The story (or what there is of it) has typical sports movie clichés: old guy gets one last shot at glory, fails to get along with teammates at first and then they become incredible together.Kate got really annoying after a while. She was the whole professional woman sort of thing, but ends up inevitably falling for the charming guy who plays by his own rules. You wanted to make a female character in a different kind of role, great. Then you throw her back into a stereotype. Pick a lane (no pun intended). And her husband left her because she took this job? Really?It's a watchable movie that you can enjoy if you just turn your brain off for 2.5 hours. But like sugar, there's not much substance here.

title: I Came for Brad Pitt, Stayed for the Pit Stops
content: Listen, I know exactly nothing about Formula 1. My entire understanding of the sport boils down to fast cars, terrifyingly expensive champagne showers, and apparently, a shocking number of tire changes. Engines? Couldn't care less. Drivers? Meh. Unexpected, electrifying, and honestly kind of inspiring.

title: Enjoyable ride, with some clichéd techniques to make it work
content: My first impression of this film was, beyond basic enjoyment ultimately, was that the filmmakers knew the task before them, which was two-fold. First, explain Formula 1 to an audience who mostly wouldn't have much of a clue, myself included. And secondly, manufacture cinematic excitement on screen from what may be enthralling live, but is little more than cars zipping across the screen. What they did was fairly ham-fisted, but ultimately effective.First, the use of the race commentary (almost universally diegetic, with characters and the audience all hearing), is shamelessly expositive, violating basic 'show-don't-tell' filmmaking philosophy. However, it does help the audience understand what strategy is being employed, why it's being done, and possible ramifications. These would totally be lost on a non-F1 fan without the commentary. And to be honest, I suspect they played a little fast and loose with the 'chaos' and 'combat' strategies, portraying them like this was the first time anyone had every thought of doing it, or that greater penalty contingencies hadn't been put in place.Secondly, is the music. It seems clear the filmmakers were between a rock and hard place, choosing between quick cut action (which would look like a jumbled mess), and long takes for actors to emote (which would ruin the pacing). Again, the sledgehammer solution was to put aggressively motivational music in to manufacture excitement in the audience. In fact, right from the start with Zeppelin even before the opening credits.But even with the obvious ham-fisted techniques, I have to admit, they probably were necessary. And even more, they worked. It's a nice film, a bit conventional in story (I promised myself I wouldn't use the word 'formulaic', but there, I did anyway). But a nice time out at the movies.

content: When I sat down in my home theater to watch F1: The Movie, I honestly didn't know what I was getting into. The sound design was next-level-the engines, shifts, and tire squeals filled the room perfectly. I swear I could almost smell the burnt rubber. The editing and pacing kept me glued the whole way through.What made it feel even more special was how personal it was. Because it was shot on an iPhone, the camera angles were bold, close-up, and super immersive. At times it felt like I was right there in the cockpit with the driver.F1: The Movie isn't just a racing film-it's proof that with the right vision, even a phone can create something truly cinematic. Definitely a must-watch, whether you're into racing or just love movies. Easy 10/10.

title: It is a movie at the end!
content: Visually, the F1 movie delivers - the cinematography is striking, with vivid colors that pop and a score that complements the high-speed drama. It's stylish, sleek, and clearly crafted with a strong visual sensibility.However, when it comes to the story, it's fairly average. There's excitement, yes, but it follows a predictable Hollywood formula. If you're looking for a deep, raw dive into the world of Formula 1, this isn't it. It's not a documentary - it's a dramatized, polished version of the sport, designed more for spectacle than substance.Still, if you enjoy fast cars, great visuals, and cinematic flair, it's a solid watch - just don't expect too much depth.

title: High-Octane Thrills & Brad Pitt's Magnetic Performance Make F1 a Cinematic Rush!
content: Watching F1 on the big screen is nothing short of a visceral thrill ride-an adrenaline-pumping, heart-racing cinematic experience that puts you right in the driver's seat of the world's most elite motorsport. From the moment the lights go out on the grid, the film launches into high gear and never lets up, delivering one of the most immersive racing experiences I've seen in a long time.The cinematography is jaw-dropping. The roar of the engines, the screech of the tires, the dynamic camera angles-from inside the cockpit to the sweeping aerial shots of the track-make you feel like you're actually racing at 300 km/h. The sound design is equally intense, amplifying every gear shift and collision with such precision that you'll probably find yourself gripping your seat unconsciously. If you've ever wondered what it truly feels like to be in an F1 car, this movie comes about as close as you'll get without a helmet on.But the real engine of F1 is Brad Pitt. He delivers an exceptionally handsome, charismatic, and deeply compelling performance as the lead driver. His screen presence is magnetic-effortless yet emotionally grounded. Pitt's portrayal is not just about looking good in a racing suit (though he does that too); it's about conveying the passion, pressure, and personal grit that define the life of a professional F1 driver. Whether he's giving an inspiring pep talk in the pit or executing a daring overtake on the track, Brad Pitt owns every frame he's in.

title: A True Thrill Ride
content: One worry I had going into F1 was whether or not Joseph Kosinski could capitalize on what he delivered in Top Gun Maverick. Would he prove that much of the film was Tom Cruise going the extra mile or was that thrill of a film his to own. F1 proves that he just may be a master of summer blockbusters. His new movie is a thrill-a-minute ride that constantly puts you on the edge of your seat. While it's story and characters are fairly standard, the racing is worth the price of admission. You've never seen racing on film like this and the movie, once again, shows us what a summer blockbuster is all about.As stated, we get a fairly standard tale here of underdog and mentor, with Brad Pitt playing Sonny Hayes, an over-the-hill racer who once had a shot at F1 called in by his former partner (Javier Bardem) to help win several races to dig his partner's company out of the red and avoid it being sold off. He is partnered with a brash rookie, Damson Idris' Joshua Pearce, and the two go on about as predictably as you could imagine. They are joined by the teams technical director and obvious love interest, Kate McKenna, played charmingly by Kerry Condon. The cast is good and entertaining throughout. Despite the fact that you know at almost all points of the film where it's going, it doesn't feel dull. Everyone really steps up and you're never bored.This is in part due to the fact that the film focuses mostly on the racing and it really delivers as well as you could hope. It almost feels like Kosinski took his cues for action from Maverick and replicated the type of technical work it would take to make you feel like you're right there in the race. If that seems familiar to people, it's because Maverick's cinematographer Claudio Miranda returns here and delivers another smooth and precise job that I have no shame in saying deserves an Oscar nom. Every race puts you on the edge of your seat and is about as exciting as it gets without being in the seat yourself. Even beyond this, the races are smartly written and executed as they provide more than just one race after another. Creative strategy from the characters to win adds to the intensity as the rogue-like Hayes seeks out ways to win that don't conform to typical racing. It creates a slight bit of mystery as you start to see things unfold and realize what it is that is actually happening. This is coupled with excellent editing that is just as Oscar worthy and a pulsing soundtrack from Hans Zimmer that helps propel each scene without being overbearing. This is entertainment, pure and simple.F1 achieves what it sets out to achieve. It's simple in it's non-action details, but entertaining, the racing is phenomenal, and smartly executed where it might otherwise just be typical racing shown through technical precision. Anyone looking for a purely entertaining movie won't be disappointed here.

title: A Formula For Exciting Racing
content:
LIKES:
Good Pace
Character Development
Good Character Usage
Good Acting
Funny At Times
Good Drama
The Exciting Races
Great Cinematography
Fantastic Sound Editing
The Awesome Musical Score
Editing
The Balance
Summary:
I had my doubts when I saw a lot about this movie, another racing film joining the likes of many in the last few years, and I wasn't excited to see it without something special to it. Once again, I was humbled. F1 is a long film, but the pace and entertainment value work in tandem to cut through that time with ease, as I became enthralled in the film. One part of it, which was the special factor, is the character usage in this film. With this film, you don't just get the racers or even just the owner of the team, like other films do. No, you get just about every team position you can think of in this film, helping to give a large, immersive experience that pulled me into so much of the film. Sunny, JP, and the rest of the cast have approachable personalities, dramatic personas, but are grounded enough in reality to get hooked on and take the ride. It's got great character usage for the most part, handling a decent number of stories that flow well to give this film a lot more depth than I expected. A strong acting game by the crew helped to further breathe life into the characters, as the chemistry seemed to resonate between the group to make this quirky team feel alive. Pitt may not have the most dynamic range, but this desperado personality works well, with just some slight hints of vulnerability to help give some edge and stakes to the film. Idris' character has the most emotional scaling, a fantastic portrayal of his character's status that puts loads more pressure on giving a Hero's Journey story some strong vibes. And Bardem... well, he always manages to use his accent and vocal work to such elegant levels of smoothness, unleashing that level of charisma that brings so many together. I could go on about so many other characters, but in the sake of time, let's move to the strongest element for me in this film...the presentation.Like Maverick, this film has a style that utilizes so many things to precise levels of spectacle and artistic vision. F1's comedy, drama, and action are well-balanced, Kosinski and his writers helping find those excellent moments to bring in comedic timing to offset the intense dramatic points, and often blend the two styles into a cohesive presentation that uses the best of both worlds. When this gets integrated into the action scenes, I found some of the most engaging sequences I've had in a long while when it comes to a sports or vehicle movie like F1. These moments involve some more unique racing, dumping the straight, clean-cut races that Gran-Turismo and Ford Vs. Ferrari established and instead gave us some new levels of strategy that were clever, fun, and far beyond the usual tactics these films fall into. I can't say much, but the aforementioned balance of drama, characters, and comedy is shrewdly blended into this thriving organism that I think will be a worthwhile candidate for some of the most dynamic racing I've seen in a film. All of this is maximized by the incredible cinematography at work in this film, fast, dynamic visuals that come from a variety of angles to give you every element you can for that full-on, Hollywood magic, spectacular vision that pulled me into every moment. A great sound blend further complements the film, the ferocious roars of the metallic beasts a solid background to which other usual sounds erupt to help paint the illusion of being in the driver's seat. These sound effects work well, but never encroach on the dialogue, so that you get every element you can with the impressive film editing that they did. Add in a great musical score, which is one part Zimmer orchestra work and another a mixed tape of styles to establish the edge and setting, and you get impressive scene transitions that keep the movie moving. All of these elements play well together, creating a balanced film that may not be perfect, but accomplishes so much and with such attention to detail that I felt like I had a crash course in F1 racing 101.DISLIKES:
Some antagonism needs development
Could Have Used Some Other Character Times
A Subplot That Needs Work
The Romance Aspect Seems Unnecessary
Not The Most Unique Story
Summary
Now, is the movie perfect? We know the answer is no, as no film is, and F1 had a few things I could see being less impressive than other racing films. Some subplots need working, character relationships, and involvement that could have been deeper, more invested, and perhaps having a few more lines to give their inclusion more meaning. Many of the secondary characters get hints at holding some potential for inspiration, and though they have their moments, most feel like a comedy aspect or throwaway. This is primarily true for a lack of a rival, something that so many racing movies have established in some regards. F1 kind of has that in one aspect (the rivalry between teammates), but they try to throw a few more in that feel a bit too late or subtle, almost forgettable until they stop the cogs with a sudden wrench. It has enough bite at times to get a nod, especially during the race scenes at times, but that particular plot felt a tad out of left field when it comes to needing this inclusion of the big, bad, boss man. Another subplot that didn't feel fully fleshed out or needed was the romance aspect that comes into play. Sure, it starts cute, has some merit when it's executed right, and brings some definite laugh-out-loud moments. However, the further you get into the film, the more this relationship starts to get buried by the other plots, a wrap-up that feels true to the lead character, but only makes the inclusion that much more irrelevant in the grand scheme of things. Finally, I agree, it's not the most unique story, something that many people struggle to make these days with the amount of content we have access to. Thus, if you are looking for that originality spark, you might find this movie too generic for your tastes. However, I had plenty of the mentioned character, excitement, and spectacle to help distract and enhance this component so that I did not feel too disappointed.The VERDICT:
For me, F1 is one of the best movies for the theater I have seen in a very long time. Though it is like some of the other films in this genre, this film found a unique factor in the amount of immersion you get from so many members of the modern-day racing team. It's got a lot of character usage and some good handling of plots to create characters who escape the stereotypical characters this genre creates. Yet it is the exciting craftsmanship of the action scenes that I think pulled me in, this exciting pinnacle where all this build-up continues to come together, that is the biggest selling point for seeing this film in theaters as soon as you can. True, the movie still needs some more time for full character immersion, especially with several subplots that could use some redesign or be dropped altogether. And yes, the originality is still not all there, nor is this the newest look at a movie. However, the good outweighs the bad for me, and I implore most to get out and see this film for how many people it should appeal to. When I balance all of this, I give F1 the following scores:Action/Drama/Sport: 8.5- 9.0
Movie Overall: 8.0.

title: Almost Great
content: F1 is a movie that seems like it has all of the components necessary to be memorable: A star studded cast helmed by Brad Pitt, a reliable director in the form of Joseph Kosinski, and extremely competent cinematography. Yet, in spite of these good qualities, which I give the movie points for nonetheless, it is extremely bogged down by an average script that is uninterested in telling a compelling story. If one were to go into this movie with a checklist of "comeback kid" sports movie tropes, there wouldn't be a single thing unchecked. While the movie lacks meaningful substance, it does an extremely good job with its high-octane racing sequences, which are elevated by a IMAX/Dolby viewing experience. This movie may not be in the Top 5 best of the year, but the driving sequences alone make it a must-watch.

title: F1: A Flashy Misfire That Misses the Spirit of the Sport
content: As an F1 enthusiast, I approached the F1 movie with curiosity and cautious optimism. Unfortunately, what unfolded felt more like a stylized detour than a tribute to the world's most prestigious motorsport.While the film is visually slick and packed with adrenaline-pumping sequences, it ultimately sacrifices authenticity for cheap thrills. The storytelling leans heavily into dramatic clichés and shallow rivalries that feel unsportsmanlike, illogical, and far removed from the discipline, precision, and respect that define real-world Formula 1 racing.From unrealistic on-track behavior to forced character arcs, the movie often prioritizes spectacle over substance. For true fans of the sport, the emotional integrity and technical finesse that make F1 so compelling are glaringly absent.It's disappointing to see a film with so much potential fall into the trap of style over soul. If you're looking for a genuine reflection of the F1 spirit, you'd be better off rewatching Rush, Senna, or even a well-made Drive to Survive episode.The 2 stars are for brilliant performance from Brad Pitt and Javier bardem.

title: F1: The Movie is Why We Go to the Movies
content: I usually don't write movie reviews and I probably never will again. But some films... they're not just movies. They're experiences. Pure, immersive, soul-shaking experiences. F1 was exactly that for me.This was my first time ever watching a movie in a theater, and I'm absolutely sure I made the right choice. I'm beyond glad that this was the film I picked to define that milestone because it defined it perfectly.I won't get into the plot (because let's be real, the title says it all). But I remember two years ago, when I first watched Top Gun: Maverick , a film so visually stunning and emotionally charged that I stopped caring about Tom Cruise and started obsessing over Joseph Kosinski, the man behind the camera. So when I heard Kosinski was teaming up with the GOAT Brad Pitt for a Formula 1 film? That was enough to get me hooked. Then came the cherry on top: Hans Zimmer doing the score.That lineup alone is enough to make any cinephile lose their mind.When July 21, 2025, came around, I had doubts. The early reviews were strong, but I still wondered: How far can a movie about cars really go?Bro. That was the biggest misconception of my life.Two hours and thirty minutes passed, and I didn't blink. Not once. The aura Brad Pitt brought to the screen? Unreal. The cinematography? Each shot felt like a million-dollar painting. This wasn't just storytelling, it was visual poetry. And that theater... it didn't just project a film. It transported me.Since that day, I've been binge-watching F1 reels, looping the soundtrack, and following Max Verstappen, Lewis Hamilton, and Charles Leclerc like I've been a fan for years. This movie didn't just entertain me, it introduced me to a whole new world.And now I finally get it.F1: The Movie isn't just about racing. It's about passion, vision, sound, and speed blending together into cinema. And this... this is why we go to the movies.

title: he's flying
content:
A visually stunning film in every sense. The cinematography is breathtaking - the camera angles make you feel like you're right there on the track. The colors are vibrant, full of energy, and they perfectly match the film's adrenaline-fueled atmosphere from start to finish.Brad Pitt? His performance is exceptional. He doesn't just act - he lives the moment. His presence in every scene is magnetic. He perfectly balances the weight of a seasoned, worn-out driver with the quiet hope he carries in every lap. From the very beginning, I was fully invested in him. It felt like watching a real legend come back not just to win a race, but to defy time, doubt, and everyone who underestimated him.The drama, however, didn't meet the same standard. The story follows a very familiar path, with no real surprises. Many events felt predictable, and even the moments that were supposed to hit hard were telegraphed well in advance.(Spoiler)
Even though it was predictablethe ending was the film's weakest point. Brad Pitt's victory felt like a win for us as an audience - a true payoff - but the scene that followed ruined the emotional climax. It should've ended right there, at the height of the moment, letting us fully feel that rush of triumph.

content: F1 delivers a pure cinematic experience, seamlessly blending breathtaking visuals with impeccable sound design. From the very first frame, the film immerses the audience in the high-octane world of Formula 1 racing, with a title score that sets the tone-bold, intense, and unforgettable. Brad Pitt shines as Sonny Hayes, adding another memorable character to his already iconic filmography. His performance is both grounded and electrifying, perfectly capturing the seasoned grit of a former racer back in the fast lane. Every moment on screen feels purposeful. The final race sequence, elevated by Hans Zimmer's powerful score, is a true cinematic spectacle. It builds tension and emotion in equal measure, delivering goosebumps and sealing F1 as a thrilling ride from start to finish. In short, F1 is not just a movie; it's an experience-a must-watch for racing fans and film lovers alike.

title: Good action and well made
content: F1 : The Movie (2025) It's pretty difficult to avoid using the "F" word when discussing Joseph Kosinski's racing movie, whose full title would read "Formula One' (emphasis on the first word). Of course, no one is going to see F1 for it's intricate plot. It's a popcorn flick pure and simple (emphasis on the latter). It's produced by noted action film legend Jerry Bruckheimer (ARMAGEDDON, PIRATES OF THE CARIBBEAN), Directed by TOP GUN MAVERICK's Kosinski (not coincidentally also produced by Bruckheimer) and has a top star in Brad Pitt. Taking creative risks here isn't a top priority.Pitt is Sonny Hayes, a once hotshot driver who's career crashed and burned decades ago. In a last ditch effort to save his racing team (APXGP) that is dead last on the F1 circuit, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) hires his old racing buddy. Of course, there's a new hotshot who is APXGP's numero uno - JP (Damson Idris). Kerry Condon is Kate, the team's tech adviser who can't help but notice the rugged 'old man' Sonny (heck, even JP's mom eyeballs him!). His helmet pressed tight scrunches his face, giving Pitt a few 'wrinkles' bring his character a dash of maturity.All the usual tropes are here: the aging man trying to recapture past glories, the unlikely romance, internal rivalries, a corporation on the ropes, well timed obstacles, the big game/race, and, naturally, multiple montages set to stirring music (Hans Zimmer does the honors - abetted by a lot of songs). There are more literal fireworks here than in any sports film since THE NATURAL. Cinematographer Claudio Miranda and his team capture the track action with zeal and it's all edited to the nines. Pitt has a natural charm and Condon and Bardem are fine, even if it feels at times as if they are over-qualified for their stock roles.The reason these cliches are so often used is that they usually work. The movie never fully escapes it's thin screenplay (Kosinski collaborated with Ehren Kruger). It's competent enough.There are four levels to Formula One racing with F1 being the top tier, this one is more F3 - F2 if one is being very kind. Call it F2 ½ .

title: Shut up and drive.
content: 'F1 The Movie (2025)' is pretty much exactly what you expect it to be, but that isn't inherently a bad thing. Just because you know what it's going to do doesn't mean it isn't fun to watch it do it, and "fun" is exactly the word I'd use to describe this branded racing movie. It's undeniably forgettable and never makes any choices that feel even remotely close to inspired, but it's also consistently entertaining and it never so much as stumbles on its way to the finish line. It's a really solid movie from start to finish, capable of keeping you totally hooked with its petrol-chugging, gear-crunching, rubber-burning set-pieces. There's a lot of racing going on here, which is exactly what you want from a movie with this title. The affair does just a hint more than the bare minimum in-between its tactile on-track scenes, but the constantly kept promise of further sequences of that kind is more than enough to keep you seated throughout this fairly fast-moving yet over-two-hour-long feature. I saw a fellow reviewer say something along the lines of Joseph Kosinski being great at directing machines, but not so great at directing humans. I have to say I agree, but when he's this great at directing machines, it doesn't matter that his human element is somewhat lacking. There's enough basic but appreciated character work here to make you care about the sequences in which said characters are barreling along tarmac at over a hundred miles per hour, narrowly missing their opponents and putting their lives on the line in pursuit of that all-important champagne shower on the podium. The lead is also a bit of a lovable rogue, and it's entertaining to see him bend the rules to his and his teammate's advantage by doing things such as intentionally damaging his vehicle to force a pace car out onto the track or baiting the other drivers into a pitstop he doesn't intend to actually take. This makes the racing less conventional than it otherwise would have been, and - as unrealistic as it undoubtedly is - it's a joy to see this cheeky fella get away with so many blatant on-track crimes. The drama between Brad Pitt's old-timer expert driver and Damson Idris' hungry up-and-comer never quite feels fully capitalised on, and their relationship progresses and regresses in sometimes quite random ways. There's also some relatively unfulfilling - or, at least, uninteresting - human conflict in the form of a shady board member and the looming threat of a sale to take the team away from Javier Bardem's owner, and the central romance between Pitt and the always-delightful Kerry Condon feels rather tacked-on. Still, despite its weaker story aspects, this remains compelling for its duration. It's really enjoyable, the sort of thing you'll forget fairly quickly but will never regret having seen. It's better than it could have been, thanks mainly to the pulse-pounding race sequences that are as believable as they are exciting, and the charm of its cast (who basically elevate their stock roles by sheer will - and charisma - alone) . Hans Zimmer's score also strikes a balance between cheesy and stirring, capable of pulling you forwards in your seat and increasing the likelihood of you cheering alongside the characters. Ultimately, what this lacks in depth it makes up for in fun. Its skilled execution is to be applauded, its simple but effective story does exactly what it needs to, and its set-pieces are genuinely fantastic at times. It's a really good effort that I like a lot more than I thought I would.

title: Tons of great moments
content: I had been eagerly waiting for this movie, but it left me quite disappointed. The lead actress seemed disengaged, as if she wasn't fully committed to her role, which made several key moments feel shallow. The choice of music also felt jarring - using rap tracks outside of club scenes completely broke the mood and didn't fit the tone of those moments. On top of that, Brad's practice scenes were noticeably missing. Showing his preparation could have added depth to his character and made his journey far more convincing. A stronger connection to the audience through thoughtful set design and ambiance could have completely changed the game, making the experience far more immersive and impactful.

title: Intense, Thrilling, Nail-biting: Formula One Might Just Have a New Fan
content: You see Rush (2013) and think no racing movie could ever top it as your favorite racetrack movie and then F1 comes along.Intense, thrilling, visually stunning, and full of energy. An expensive looking movie that looks every bit like its outrageous $300m budget.The camera work in the racing scenes so excellently done you feel you're right there on track with Hamilton, Verstappen, and Leclerc. The raw engine sounds of the cars combined so well with the music to create such a thrilling atmosphere.For the non-Formula One fans (like myself), this is perhaps the movie that arouses your interest in the sport. For the Formula One fans, this is your "now you see what I've been raving about" moment.I didn't track this movie in the lead up, so I went in not knowing what to expect-but should I really be surprised it's this good considering it was directed by the same guy who directed Top Gun: Maverick (2022)?I don't know what tops F1 this year, but right now it's the best 2025 has put out. No one's winning any best actor/actress awards for this, but it's truly a well made movie.Score: 9/10P. S: Hans Zimmer might just be the GOAT composer.

title: Okay story, great visuals
content: F1 is about an old racer whose aim is to be the best in every type of motorsport. He is called into the worst-performing F1 team, where he has to team up with a young pilot and manage to win. It is the classic underdog becomes the #1 story. It doesn't show anything new; most of its components were reused many times by other movies, so the plot is totally predictable. This was foreseeable from the trailer, but the exact implementation with the F1 dressing was quite decent. While nothing was surprising, the movie stayed exciting for most of its runtime. The beginning was strong with the introduction of the two competing protagonists, which resembles a rivalry between old school and new school. The characters were built up well:Sonny Hayes, the aging daredevil who doesn't care about his image or others' opinions. He makes his way, doesn't talk much, and generally seems like a cool dude. He arrives early, puts in the effort in the old school way.Joshua Pearce, who resembles the young. He uses everything technology offers to gain as much edge as possible. He also works hard but differently. He is constantly self-aware and tries to maintain a positive social media presence. He tries to stay calm and polite, but part of it is just a facade.Both of them are competitive, selfish and arrogant. Hayes tries to fit into a team that desperately needs something new to help them, while there is an ongoing rivalry between the pilots. This drives the first half of the movie. This part was done well. We see how they train, their different approach and the conflicts gave a lot of material for the movie to work with. Unfortunately, this moral aspect wasn't brought to its potential. The middle of the movie was uneventful and the dialogues were deflated. By the end, the characters both grew and started to work as a team, but given the lengthy runtime, this happened too suddenly and easily. While there were some nice lessons about putting away personal beef and rivalry in favour of playing in a team, or the importance of selfless sacrifices, the morality mostly exhausted in this. The movie has a mostly diverse cast, and it does discuss tangentially being a woman in a mainly men-dominated sport, but it wasn't overdone. What they portrayed was not into the face and represented reality, so it was good to see a movie without the propaganda.The whole story of a loser team becoming a winner would be ridiculous even in a movie, so they use a lot of dirty tricks to gain an advantage. This makes the entire plot totally unfeasible in another way. In reality, they would have amassed a mountain of penalties before being banned from racing. As such, the movie stays true to the action movie genre when it comes to feasibility. From a drama standpoint, they used the F1 specific technicalities well to create excitement around the races themselves. This was one of the reasons why the story stayed watchable despite its ridiculousness. On the other hand, it portrayed very well that F1 is a team sport. It showcased many important aspects of it, like the pit crew and the job of the engineers.After the stagnant middle part, the ending of the movie sped up, and while it managed to become exciting again, it wasn't as interesting as the beginning. It was predictable, and while there were some minor plot twists and an ecstatic finish, it had some kind of emptiness. This is mostly coming from missing the things that could have happened, like deeper character growth or a less predictable story with a morale-centric twist. But they went with the safe route that has already proved to be working. It was directed by Joseph Kosinski, who was also responsible for Top Gun: Maverick, and he definitely has his fingerprint, since the two works share a lot of similarities.Acting was good. Brad Pitt steals the show. He continues to be the signature manly chill guy who doesn't worry about anything. He just confidently and effortlessly walks towards his goal. While his stylish daredevil nature is enjoyable, his character isn't a good role model, especially when it comes to his love life. The love subplot was entirely unnecessary and only took away from his value. Damson Idris did well as his rival, and while Kerry Condon wasn't bad, her character mostly serves as a love interest, and for such a short time that it would have been better to leave this character out entirely. Her acting was similar to his performance in Breaking Bad, which worked well there, but here she just looked constantly concerned without a reason. Her delivery was quite dry and didn't fit the tone. The movie also has plenty of cameos from well-known F1 pilots and team principals.Visually, the movie is beautiful! This is its major strength. The camera angles were amazing. They showed an F1 race from viewpoints that you don't see in a regular broadcast, and the colors with the lighting were stunning. It did a great job showing what pilots feel with the point of view shots and close-ups. IMAX is highly recommended! If somebody is interested in F1 or cars in general, it's worth a watch only for the picture.The soundtrack has lots of original music made by contemporary musicians. It is a hit or miss depending on one's taste, but it reflects the current music landscape pretty well. The best scores were from the great Hans Zimmer. His songs had the unmistakable Zimmer element, and his music significantly enhanced the dramatic nature of the most epic parts of the movie.Hungarian dubbing was fine, but if you can, watch the original.All in all, it is a generic but still watchable story in F1 dressing, with the greatest offering being the amazing visuals. If you can look away from its unrealistic nature, it comes recommended, especially for F1 fans.

title: Amazing
content: I don't usually write reviews, but this movie truly deserves it. As an F1 fan, it thrilled me from start to finish - every scene brought out the intensity, speed, and emotion of the sport. What impressed me most is that even people who don't follow Formula 1 can still find it exciting and engaging. Yes, there are moments that wouldn't happen in real life, but let's not forget it's a movie - and those dramatic, cinematic touches make it all the more enjoyable. The atmosphere, the soundtrack, the performances - everything works together beautifully. It's a film that delivers both excitement and heart. For me, it's a solid 10/10.

title: One of the best films I've seen this year and I don't even like F1
content: Meet Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) a washed up racer who had the potential to be the best F1 driver but now lives in his van, loses most of his money to gambling and has no chance of winning a F1 race, right? Wrong, meet Rubin (Javier Bardem), Sonny's old friend who offers him an opportunity to join a failing F1 team and hopefully get them some points.I really enjoyed this film, the camera work makes you feel like you're sitting in the driver's seat and it even got me excited about watching formula 1 racing which I haven't done since Michael Schumacher raced. But what I didn't like about the racing was the lucky technique that Sonny and his team mate Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) kept using in the races. At first it was quite funny and some of the lucky flukes made the film intense but after a while it got a bit repetitive.Sonny's witty personality was also funny and Brad Pitt also still looks fit and healthy film so I hope he gets a chance to do an action film before he retires.I also hope that Hollywood doesn't make a sequel to this movie, I think it's better as a standalobe film. However overall I really enjoyed it so I would love to own it on DVD so I can watch it over and over again.

title: Popcorn and Fireworks
content:
I was hoping for Ford v Ferrari for the 'feels' and emotion and RUSH on steroids for action. I was disappointed. Don't get me wrong, there is some amazing cinematography and action going on, but...
The dialogue and script is appalling. Damson Idris was terrible, his 'manager' even worse.The story unbelievably predictable. At best, it''s like watching a string of commercials and yes, there is plenty of product placement.But Pitt is charming, as always, Bardem (always good) and there was the eye twinkle from Condon.I like F1 but I am not an expert, and yet, even I found some of the scenes just ridiculous. The pit dialogue ludicrous and any authenticity just seemed missing....
BUT - if you have some popcorn, turn the sound up and just treat this as a fun ride, you will be rewarded. It's Top Gun on a racing track.

title: Very solid story!!!
content: This film delivers a truly robust and thoroughly entertaining narrative where every element feels masterfully interconnected. The visual storytelling is a standout achievement; the shots are not only original but are also seamlessly combined to create breathtaking race sequences that blur the line between cinema and the visceral reality of the track. The way the camera captures the intricate dance of the cars-their positioning, the daring angles, and the insane track-level perspectives-is nothing short of spectacular. As a recent Formula 1 enthusiast with a foundational understanding of the sport's technicalities, I can appreciate the deliberate choice by Warner and Apple to craft a film that is accessible and thrilling for a mainstream audience. However, this accessibility comes with a trade-off. A part of me longed for a deeper dive into the more technical aspects that make F1 so fascinating. I would have been captivated by sequences that visually explain the science of downforce, the nuances of achieving grip, the sheer power of the braking systems, the immense G-forces drivers endure through corners, and the strategic art of tire management. Including these details would have elevated the experience from a fantastic movie to an absolute masterpiece for fans who crave that extra layer of authenticity. Nonetheless, the director has undeniably crafted a brilliant film that serves as a powerful and welcome introduction for this genre to the wider market.

title: More like a 6.5 imo.
content: As an F1 fan, I'd say this racing movie is just okay. Visually, it's great; shots are awesome, the transitions are smooth, editing is subtle but effective and the soundtrack fits well. The plot is pretty simple but gets the job done and it's accessible even for non-F1 fans since they explain key terms and keep the racing easy to follow, mostly by focusing on ApexGP. That said, if you actually watch F1, you'll spot quite a few inaccuracies; some moves and moments just aren't realistic, which takes you out of it a bit. It definitely feels like an American movie, with the usual over-the-top exaggeration and acting. Also, in my opinion the love story felt unnecessary and honestly did more harm than good when it comes to how women are portrayed in the sport. Still, I really enjoyed the ending; it felt more grounded, emotional and actually got me invested. And it was obviously awesome to see real drivers take part in the film.

title: F1
content: With Sir Lewis Hamilton and F1 media veteran Ian Holmes amongst those at the helm of this production, you can be certain that it's going to be as an authentic story of all things F1 (can't be bothered finding the trademark symbol) as you're ever likely to see. It's all about team "Ruben" (Javier Bardem) whose cars couldn't beat an egg and so is looking at ending up broke! His remaining driver is British rookie "Joshua" (Damson Idris) but he's nowhere near experienced enough to make any impact on the problem. For that, he needs to coax back "Sonny" (Brad Pitt) who did drive these cars twenty years ago but hung up his helmet after an accident and then became an itinerant driver eking a living across the USA. Can he be tempted? Well we'd have no film if he wasn't so the next question is: can he manage to salvage this operation before it's sold for scrap? True to form with this sport, what now ensues sees all the glitz and glamour of circuits around the world; money no object cars, training and equipment; testosterone galore both in the car and with team tech director "Kate" (Kerry Condon) as well as a whole slew of rule manipulating, crashes, downright cheating and I have to say, some of the best action and in-car photography I've seen on a big screen. Indeed, I could cheerfully have lived without the dramatic characterisation here altogether, and just stuck to the tensely directed and really compelling action sequences that, especially using the in-car cameras, is genuinely quite exciting to watch. You really do get a sense of the speed of the cars racing round the tracks and of the phenomenal engineering that goes into the design and manufacture of these cars that at times look no more substantial than an Airfix kit. Pitt is in his element here delivering a character that's a lovable rogue meets smug git whilst Idris comes across entirely naturally as the youngster out of his depth but willing and determined to learn. The soundtrack from the likes of Queen and Led Zeppelin livens up what I'd have to say isn't Hans Zimmer's most memorable score, and with the last half hour largely devoid of the romantic and emotional clutter, we watch something that shows us this circus in full pomp. You don't need to be remotely interested in this topic to enjoy this film, you only really need a pulse and a great big screen.

title: Crowd pleaser from start to finish
content: As "F1" (2025 release; 155 min.) opens, we are at the Daytona racetrack and the Bard Pitt character, a guy named Sonny, pulls out an unlikely win in an endurance race. Not long afterwards, Ruben, once a racing buddy of Sonny and now the team owner of an F1 racing team, offers Sonny an open seat on that team... At this point we are 10 minutes into the movie.Couple of comments: this sis the long-rumored Hollywood film on F1 racing. We follow a fictional F1 racing team, mingled with real life F1 drivers and staff and owners. To be clear: the movie was made with the full cooperation of the FIA (the F1 governing body), and it shows. No detail is too small. In particular the racing scenes are insanely well done and form the heart of the movie. The story line is quite predictable and frankly almost irrelevant. Far more important is that Brad Pitt puts in one of his best performances EVER. He truly takes the film on his shoulders, and he simply is super likable and one cannot help but root for that character. The film is directed by Joseph Kosinski, who also directed "Top Gun: Maverick". Kosinski is the perfect match to bring us "F1". The movie may be a tad too long for its won good, but I must admi I enjoyed it far more than I had expected. Lastly, please note that this film is not called "F1: The Movie", as noted here on IMDb and many other sites. Per the film's opening credits, it's called "F1", nothing more, nothing less."F1" opened in theaters this past weekend and did quite good at the box office, with a global haul of $155 million. The movie will need to have long legs as the production budget of "f1" is rumored to be $300 million. It is currently rated 83% Certified Fresh on Rotten Tomatoes, and that sounds about right to me. Whether you are an F1 racing fan or not, I'd readily suggest you check this out, and draw your own conclusion.

title: CAR GO VROOM!
content: Brad Pitt is such an outstanding actor that I have no idea where to even begin. I know that a LOT of people were very sceptical on how his acting would be, how the movie would turn out and if it really would be able to stand out against other releases this year. But when I tell you that F1: The Movie might just be the best Blockbuster movie to release during this first half of 2025, I mean it.F1: The Movie is about Sonny Hayes (played by Brad obviously), a retired F1 driver that had a pretty rough way of life growing up (even during his older years), switching different races and racestyles just as often as I switch underwear.However, everything changes when his former F1 teammate finds him and asks Sonny to drive for him and his team, APEX GP (a bottom of the table team with no points EVER won in a race).In the team races Joshua Pearce, or JP (portrayed by Damson Idris). An egostic, and in the beginning very annoying character. However, I grew to like him even more as the movie went on.The two drivers start of as "rivals" (atleast in JP's mindset), but Sonny does everything to try and help Joshua during the races, and with teamwork comes the inevitable (which in this case are Championship points).The 2 hours and 35 minutes that this movie delivers to us flies by just as fast as an F1 racecar (haha, get the pun?), and quite literally every character in the film give perfectly played performances and a fantastic script, however the real drivers that also got to take part in the film were quite stale, giving off an "I don't want to be here" kind of vibe. (This was pretty much my only flaw.)I loved this movie from top to bottom, and it's most certainly my favourite racing movie ever (literally Live Action Cars).10/10 (and an extra 1 for being flippin' fantastic.)

title: more depth than you'd expect from a sports movie
content: I have seen F1 as a kid so I might be biased on the racing end. Which is nicely done, but apart from that the movie is actually very inspiring and rich beyond just that.What you do, you can do it for the money, the "other" people, whoever that might be, to make your family proud, to make your "fans" proud or to proof somebody wrong. All this is doing something for somebody else and not for yourself. Even money, it's just a proxy, do it for the money and it's an indirect action, if you don't know what you want to do with the money.Then you have Sunny, and the experience of chasing a feeling, chasing what joy means to you, figuring out what it is that drives you, and doing exactly that. What ever makes your forget about time, gets you into the moment, and forget about all that noise: be truly there.Okay I admit that sounds rather philosophical, which is not intended, but I really love how this movie has a real take on life from that perspective.

title: A Love Letter to Racing
content: As a lifelong Formula 1 fan, let me just say this: the new F1 movie absolutely delivers. It's not just a film about racing - it's a full-throttle immersion into the heart-pounding, high-stakes world that we love.The racecraft in this movie is next-level. From the first lap to the final checkered flag, the attention to detail is astonishing. They nailed the feel of modern F1 - the dirty air, the tire degradation, the split-second strategy calls, and that mental and physical grit that separates the elite from the merely talented. I found myself gripping the armrest like I was watching a real Grand Prix.What truly blew me away was how authentic the racing sequences felt. There's no over-dramatized nonsense - just razor-sharp onboards, surgical overtakes, and the kind of wheel-to-wheel battles that made me sit up and yell. You can tell they worked closely with the teams and drivers because the nuances - from brake bias changes mid-corner to radio chatter during a safety car - are spot-on.Brad Pitt's character fits seamlessly into the F1 world. He doesn't try to outshine the sport - he embodies what it means to live it. But make no mistake: this movie doesn't rest on star power. It's the racing that's the real star. And for fans like us, that's exactly how it should be.In short: if you're an F1 fan, this movie is everything you hoped for and more. It respects the sport, captures the essence of what makes it beautiful and brutal, and wraps it all in a cinematic package that leaves your heart racing.Can't wait to watch it again.10/10. Full send.

title: Gripping, even for non-racing fans
content: Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, an aging racer with a troubled past. Brought onto a struggling F1 team by an old friend, there is friction with the young rising star racer who is his teammate. Can they come together to bring the team back from the brink of complete failure?I am not a racing fan. Honestly, I cannot think of anything more boring than watching cars going round and round a track. Having said that, this film makes the drama completely accessible to someone who is not remotely interested in F1 racing.The technical aspects are explained with just enough detail to convey their relevance to the story, but without getting bogged down in exposition.The commentators seems to spend all of their time discussing Pitt's team, which is a way of providing information to the audience, but is a bit unrealistic.There is drama and character development both on and off the track. The cinematography is excellent and the cast all bring top performances. Despite its runtime, the film does not drag.I only give it a 7/10 and not higher, purely because the subject matter doesn't grab me. For racing enthusiasts, this is pure joy.

title: Great for F1 Diehards and Newcomers Alike
content: I think the first question that will come up for anyone deciding whether or not to see this movie will be "I don't know anything about Formula One or even racing in general. Is this movie worth watching?" And the answer is emphatically yes. There are definitely bits here and there that reward serious F1 fans, but for this movie to be successful outside of that niche it needs to be approachable for everyone, and they did a great job. The trackside commentator voiceover throughout the races essentially acts to hold your hand and guide you through the deceptively complex rules, regulations and technologies governing F1, in a way that's not at all how they do it in real life, but so that even a child could understand and follow along.Sonny Hayes and Joshua Pearce are a perfect match for each other. Sonny is calm, confident, and very experienced. It's my way or the highway. Joshua is young, confident, but cocky and inexperienced. While Joshua is running on a treadmill in a coolsuit with a thousand probes monitoring his breathing, heartrate, etc, Sonny is jogging trackside in sweatpants. While Joshua is practicing in a state-of-the-art $100,000 simulator, Sonny is watching race recording and taking notes on pen and paper. Watching the two go from hating each others guts to working as a real team is a treat.Great movie, and I didn't even mention that Lewis freaking Hamilton is a producer. 7.5/10.

title: One of the best sports drama films ever made..
content: F1 is truly amazing from start to finish, it is a drama story about once a legend racer who destroyed his career in arrogance and has gotten another chance to make a name for himself and fullfill his dream of finishing 1st on the finish line.. well that's the basic story, but the way it was presented on the big screen will keep you engaged for nearly two and half hours.. Brad Pitt is one of the greatest actors of all time and he showed it here by delivering an great performance.. Now of you're expecting a masterpiece like Top Gun Maverick which had similar premise you're setting yourself for a disappointment because this film is not even close to that and it's a good thing because it never meant to be . The race sequences were thrilling to watch on big screen, at some scenes audience can feel the intensity and tense moments which characters are going through during races.. The sound effects are amazing and enhances the experience.. Only thing I found bad was it felt little bit long at some times but runtime is totally justified at it has a great pacing which develops their characters throughout the entire film beautifully filled with human themes of getting a second chance, making peace with the past and feeling of self fulfillment of the dream.. without any doubt F1 is one of the best films of this year that everyone should catch on the big screen even though you know nothing about the real world car racing. title: Does for F1 what Days of Thunder did for NASCAR
content: Driven by Brad Pitt's effortless magnetism and featuring a turbocharged boost from Joseph Kosinski's kinetic direction, F1 delivers vintage cool with elegant finesse. A complete display of audiovisual power and rhythm, a party worth joining without hesitation, like a pulsating Formula One car rushing down a straight line at 200 miles per hour. There's little in terms of doom and gloom; instead, it offers an abundance of vroom and zoom, a thrilling throwback to racing movies of yesteryear packed with excitement, tension, spectacle, and just the right amount of humour and hope. As the living embodiment of Tony Scott's heavily underappreciated legacy, I'm eager to see what Kosinski does with Miami Vice next. Good luck, Joseph, I'll be rooting for you.
