title: Enjoyable summer fare with hot stars and hot cars.
content: Will the current Joseph Kosinski F1: The Movie clobber the successful Frankenheimer Grand Prix (1966), Ron Howard's Rush (2013), or Mihael Mann's Ferrari (2024) ---I don't think so, but it can stand with them as one heck of a fine racing movie with performances to match the quality of those other three. The director of the Cruise missile, Top Gu: Maverick, shows the great directors a thing or two about up close and personal in and out of a costly Formula 1 racing car.When lead Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayse is behind the wheel as an aging racer coming back for one more run (yes, clichés abound as in most not quite first-rate stories), Kosinski is superior to his rivals by pasting in real footage from Formula 1 races that include people in the stands along with the super-charged tracks. Help along the way comes from numerous cameras mounted on the cars to give 360-degree coverage of Pitt and his rival Joshua Pearce (Damon Idris) inside and out and small helmet cams to completely immerse us in the experience.Such technical bravado is what American cinema does so well-transporting us from the plush chairs to the cramped compartment of a multi-million-dollar machine and racer. Besides the visual delights that include Pitt's well-wrought 60-year-old body, Kerry Condon brings just the right underplayed romance as the feisty chief architect of the car and Javier Bardem as Pitt's old racing rival. It all works as summer fare that provides you with air conditioning and action in weather that is usually as blasted hot as the engines on the screen.For a more intense collaboration with the stars, Kosinski and crew pay little attention to other rival drivers as Pitt and Pearce race around the world, leaving us with our stars and the plot centered around their team work, the inescapable ingredient of success. Better than its cinematic rivals, F1 lets us experience the rivalry and eventual brotherhood at which American filmmaking and its stories excel.

title: Pitt lane
content: Heard reviews saying it was cliched & simple with good racing footage. It totally has top racing footage. Not just footage but very well thought out shot sequences obviously with assistance from experienced drivers. When you can merge a sports imagery with emotional content on the big screen, you're onto a winner. Was that what Kosinski, director of Oblivion and Top Gun 2, had in his mind from the outset? Probably so.But what about the story; was it simple & cliched? For a 2 hour long story about how to drive a car quicker than anybody else, I'll say it actually has depth. And it's only clichéd as much as each of us is a walking cliché in our daily lives. We are not always and only complex balls of knotted emotions & issues that need groundbreaking analysis for resolution. Very key aspects of us are, in fact, simple. The movie celebrates this. It whittles down a person to ultimately reveal that single thing that has become their life's driving force.All roles, small or key, are executed impeccably by the cast, both familiar or new, old(er) or young.One of the year's best blockbusters, for sure.

title: A very secular F1 tale
content: I'll start out by disclosing I am a very hardcore Formula 1 fan. The type that never misses a practice broadcast. Not even during off season. So......There are a lot of issues with this movie from my perspective. A lot of the in-race communications would never have been accepted in real racing. I understand they need some of it for the drama, but still. A lot of the "helping hands" given between drivers goes way beyond what's accepted as team orders and the rules of the sport. Brad Pitt's character, Sonny, would have spent most of this season being suspended, with his antics. It feels like they cut the corners a bit too much to create drama. There are plenty ways to generate drama in races, without essentially making the hero a blatant cheater. But this bit made it a bit too obvious that they avoided using the real drivers in the movie scapegoats for events to happen. In this sense, the movie probably needed one more fictional team, to make the on-track drama more natural.About the story told, the characters did feel a bit linear and shallow. Some moments did make me visually cringe.It also felt like they used way, way, way, waaay to much commentator talk. Most of what happened could easily have stood on its own feet. This bit, I hope they fix in a alternate blu-ray edit. Like Ridley Scott gifted us a better, narrator-free cut of Blade Runner.So, there has to be some good parts, for me to give this a solid 7/10? The racing was filmed really well, giving a real sense of speed. Even in shots where the cars were inches apart. Some scenes were obviously overly cinematic, like one of the big crashes. The spray-free racing in rain didn't look too convincing. But overall, the racing sequences were shot really well.Maybe 7/10 is a but generous from me? Probably. But, the time passed really fast, despite the near three hour runtime, and I think a casual F1 viewer might enjoy most of the events more than I did.

title: I don't watch F1, but I watched this
content: I'm not a big fan of motorsports - especially Formula 1 - but even for someone like me, this film turned out to be surprisingly engaging and entertaining.In fact, I'd even say it might be more geared toward viewers who aren't hardcore fans. According to my friend, who follows more Formula 1 closely, many of the racing events and dramatic turns in the movie are improbable and unlikely to happen all within a single season. So true F1 enthusiasts might not enjoy it as much.Other than that, it's a solid movie - just don't expect any deep philosophical ideas or emotional exploration. There's no real romantic storyline to speak of, and friendship is only touched on lightly.As always, special credit goes to Brad Pitt and Hans Zimmer's music- those two alone make the film worth watching.

title: Thrilling Racing, Familiar Story
content: F1 shines when the engines take over. The racing scenes are spectacular, with sound design and cinematography that put you right on the track. Hans Zimmer's score adds intensity and Brad Pitt delivers a strong, charismatic performance. Watching it in IMAX makes the action feel even more alive.The weak point is the writing. The story feels predictable and relies on familiar sports-movie beats. Longtime fans of Formula 1 may also find the depiction of modern racing less exciting compared to the raw grit of earlier eras. Even so, the film remains entertaining, heartfelt in parts, and a solid entry point for new audiences.

title: "They have a new set of soft tires available"
content: A has-been (but experienced) formula 1 racer is invited to save a friends company from going under by winning at least 1 point.An expectedly fun watch.It is quite realistic (with a giant budget), great execution and good camerawork. Brad, charming as usual (number 1 reason to watch) and good supporting cast. Personally, I prefered this over the blander "Gran Turismo" film. Better personalities and character interactions (but VERY Brad-centric), and a tad more unpredictable in the developments (as far as something like this can offer).Reminded of the anime One Outs, where they take advantage of their sport's rule knowledge. Not only do you learn things you didn't know, it also comes across as clever! And then there's the script, that builds-up different elements and you don't necessarily know what is going to pay off and in what way exactly (which would be spoiler territory). As someone interested in storytelling, my expected resolution wasn't even close to what happened, and that's a plus in my book.Hard not to recommend. Box Box.

title: Pretty awesome
content: Racing in general is a sport I'm more or less neutral on, and the same is true about movies based on it. It probably says a lot that my favorite racing movie is Talladega Nights. I had pretty decent expectations for this film considering its main star, and I'm pleased to say it delivers. It's a straightforward, classic scenario with a modern edge and some solid twists. The film is extremely well and consistently paced; you barely feel the length. There are actually more than a few decent laughs. And the cast is absolutely phenomenal. Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem are exceptional and have great chemistry with each other and the rest of the cast. Damson Idris and Kerry Condon are especially fantastic, and the way they work off Pitt make for some of the best scenes.The editing in this is some of the best I have ever seen. Director Joseph Kosinski seems to have taken all the lessons he learned on Top Gun Maverick and applied them in full force to the Formula One racing scene. Every time there's a race, I feel like I'm right there with them, through every up, down, success, and failure. Any information you may have missed verbally is thoroughly made up for through the visuals, and every time the tension picks up, you bet I'm on the edge of my seat, especially during the final race. The music is pretty awesome, too.Overall, F1 is surprisingly my favorite film of the year so far. In a summer that also gave us new Mission Impossible and Jurassic Park movies, the fact that this is my favorite, again, says a lot, doesn't it?

title: Top Gun Meets Formula One
content: From the director of Top Gun: Maverick comes yet another fast, furious & adrenaline-fuelled piece of blockbuster filmmaking that steers with breakneck speed, swerves with exceptional control, and refuses to take the pedal off the metal while it is on the race track. Crafted with passion, told with energetic flair and further bolstered by its lead star's charisma, F1 is a full-throttled, top-gear & high-octane spectacle that amps the thrills & excitement to max levels without slowing down the speedy goods.Directed by Joseph Kosinski (Oblivion), the story follows the redemption journey of a former F1 prodigy who comes out of retirement to lead a struggling Formula One team & mentor a young hotshot driver. The film at its core is about Brad Pitt's character chasing one final shot at glory but what drives the plot forward is the friction, competition & eventual teamwork that develops between the two drivers & their crew while navigating the themes of veteran vs rookie, wisdom vs inexperience & tradition vs modernity.Kosinski directs the film with verve and makes sure that the dramatic moments carry the same charged intensity that the racing segments do. The technical execution is first-rate throughout, for the dynamic camerawork, expert editing, lightning pace, stellar sound, neat visual effects & electrifying score, all work in harmony and help sustain the narrative momentum throughout its 156 mins runtime. Performances from the entire ensemble is top-notch, with Pitt's star power & magnetic presence propelling the story past the finish line.Overall, F1 is as impressive on storytelling front as it is accomplished in technical aspects and further improves Kosinski's stock as a rising filmmaker worth investing into, for he never really allows the plot to veer off the track nor takes the foot off the accelerator. A lean, mean & fast-paced machine that's devoid of almost everything it could do without, Kosinski's latest outing delivers all the frenetic delights that one expects from Formula One in spades and effortlessly makes for one of the finest genre entries while ranking amongst the best all-round films of the year.

title: A classic "style-over-substance" no-brainer
content: Almost anything you'll see here is a cliche. There is a thin story, ready-made characters and action (as in "movements"). Most people find these quite enough for their entertainment. Despite being shallow and hollow, this piece of work has some good elements (Javier Bardem, and Brad Pitt up to a point) and one outstanding and truly masterful: editing (hence 7 stars; otherwise barely 5). Direction is well done, serving the purpose perfectly. Also, so many "product placements" make this the longest commercial in history (they even managed, via "flashbacks", to smuggle tobacco and booze in). A great marketing effort. You'll watch it, have fun and forget it pretty soon (except products, maybe). Nothing more to say.

title: Unexpectedly Riveting: A High-Octane Surprise
content: I didn't know what to expect going in. I wondered what kind of story could possibly be told around professional car racing and honestly, the Jerry Bruckheimer formula isn't something I crave anymore. But I was pleasantly surprised. After watching this film, I might actually start following F1 and NASCAR races more closely.I saw the movie in XD with the D-Box experience, and if D-Box was made for anything, it's films like this. The racing sequences felt incredibly real. The way they were shot, the opulence of the venues, and the high-paced action pulled in even an audience with no previous connection to racing. You could feel the intensity of the competition and the massive machine, both literal and corporate, behind every team.The soundtrack was fantastic. Hearing Afrobeats included brought me so much joy, especially considering the diverse cast and the celebration of African and Caribbean accents. The film makes a clear effort to feel global, not just in casting, but in its locations, music, and respectful cultural nods throughout.Brad Pitt's eternal swag truly needs to be studied. His charisma commands the screen, and his chemistry with Javier Bardem is so natural that I would love to see them star together in a buddy cop film someday.The supporting cast is also excellent. Idris brings charm and energy as the hotheaded new-blood driver, and Niles is a total delight as his mother. The pit crew members are so well-written and performed that they feel like main characters themselves. Watching them work together is one of the film's most rewarding elements.I laughed, I cried, and I felt like I was on those tracks. I've never been more invested in a race. The victories and the exquisitely shot celebrations that follow were thrilling and moving. I didn't know what I was expecting, but I absolutely loved what I got.

title: Action on four wheels
content: F1 is 150 minutes of fun on four wheels that doesn't try to be more than it is and thank you to the creators for that. Lacking in the pretension and political messaging that most content is soaking in today, it's a good old-school thrill ride that hits the points of racing movies that have come before it yet keeps you on the edge of your seat.The plot follows Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), an ageing driver who doesn't do teamwork. He's good, he's fast, but he's only interested in winning and the next challenge. His old pal Reuben (Javier Bardem) makes him an offer to join his ailing Formula 1 team as a secondary driver to the young rising star Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). Hayes joins and so begins the clash of heads and cars between the two drivers. Pearce's modern, media-focused attitude does not work well with Hayes' tough and unorthodox style. Further to this, Hayes' apparent lack of ability to listen to the team grates the rest of the crew, but does this old warhorse have an ace or two up his sleeve?When I heard that this was directed by the same guy who made Top Gun: Maverick (Joseph Kosinski), I was in. These filmmakers know their audience and give them what they want - a film about Formula one is going to be all about cars, speed and danger and F1 delivers in spades. They give us viewpoints from every angle available, putting you right there with the machines moving at breakneck speed around racetracks that have become household names, yet they still save the best visuals until last. Hans Zimmer's pumping techno score is electrifying, and kudos for bringing in the 80s theme, it works.Best actor award has to go to Brad Pitt, his portrayal of a macho rebel with a past is delivered like the pro that he is. Even when his face is scrunched into a racing helmet, he still lets us in. Bardem is magnetic as the stressed and struggling team owner and Kerry Condon who plays the car's designer shows us her struggles of being a woman in a male dominated world without going into girl boss mode. Damson plays the ambitious, yet insecure Pearce with subtlety, showing great skill and talent, though I felt that he missed a trick by not playing it up a little more.F1 is a popcorn flick that's got big visuals, sound and action. See it on the biggest screen with the biggest sound system you can. Formula one fans will dig the incorporation of the sport's funky rules into the plot and will get a kick out of the countless cameos of the real-life racing stars. The only other thing I'd say is that this film leads the competition for product placements - see if you can keep up with them all.

title: Formula Hollywood
content: Let me start by saying that Brad Pitt had been phoning it in for big bucks for over a decade. Now, as for the character, if he drove against Senna, as the film tells us, then he's somewhere between 50-55 and hasn't driven in F-1 for decades. Absolute rubbish. He would know nothing about these sophisticated cars, his reflexes wouldn't be close to race worthy, and his body's ability to withstand the G forces would be sorely lacking.So what we have is a totally implausible popcorn flick, that is more like a video game, starring Brad Pitt.If you want to see a good F-1 film, watch John Frankenheimer's 1966 classic 'Grand Prix.'

title: CINEMA, Not Content
content: I rarely post reviews here, but this film demands it. This is the kind of experience IMAX was made for. In an era flooded with formulaic superhero movies, this original story is a breath of fresh air, thrilling, emotional, and visually breathtaking.For the first time in a long while, I felt like I was watching real CINEMA on the big screen. Joseph Kosinski crafts a gripping narrative with characters that feel deeply authentic. Brad and Javier deliver masterful performances, and Damson Idris is a revelation, a star in the making.But the true MVPs? Claudio Miranda's stunning cinematography, Hans Zimmer's powerful score, and Stephen Mirrione's sharp editing. Together, they create a cinematic experience that stays with you long after the credits roll, a must-watch.

title: Perfect Film
content: This film was glorious. It was perfect. I loved everything about it - the visuals, the cinematography, the sound, the adrenaline, the storytelling. Just... everything.From the opening shot, F1 (2025) grabs you by the collar and throws you straight into the world of motorsport at its most raw and unfiltered. The roar of the engines, the shimmer of heat rising from the tarmac, the sheer velocity of the cars - it all feels real, visceral, and thrilling. You're not just watching Formula 1; you're inside it. Director Joseph Kosinski, best known for his work on Top Gun: Maverick, delivers a masterclass in cinematic immersion, using practical effects and real race footage to blur the line between fiction and reality.Brad Pitt's performance as Sonny Hayes, a veteran driver coming out of retirement to mentor a rookie, is magnetic. He brings a quiet intensity to the role, a man haunted by past crashes and driven by a need to prove that speed and skill don't fade with age. He doesn't play the role as a superhero, but as a man who understands the beauty and brutality of racing. His chemistry with Damson Idris, who plays the young, talented rookie Joshua Pearce, is one of the film's most rewarding elements. Their dynamic - respect, rivalry, mentorship, and eventually brotherhood - gives the story real emotional weight.The film also doesn't shy away from the darker side of F1. Behind the glamour, it shows the politics, the relentless pressure, the danger that lurks around every corner. But it does so without dragging down the pace. The editing is tight, the race sequences pulse with tension, and the cinematography? Sublime. Every frame looks like a painting - from the Monaco sunset to the rain-soaked streets of Singapore, each shot elevates the film beyond sports cinema and into something poetic.Hans Zimmer's score deserves special mention. It pulses and surges with the rhythm of the track, a sonic heartbeat that never lets up. It doesn't just accompany the racing - it becomes part of it.What's truly impressive is how the film respects the sport's legacy while pushing it into cinematic territory that feels fresh. Real-life F1 drivers make cameos, real circuits are used, and you can tell this wasn't made as just a "racing movie" - it was made by people who love F1.In the end, F1 (2025) is more than a film - it's an experience. A celebration of speed, risk, legacy, and human resilience. It's easily one of the most authentic and exhilarating sports films I've ever seen. A love letter to racing, and a must-watch whether you're an F1 fan or not.Glorious, indeed.

title: A wonderful film.
content: The unsung heroes of Hollywood are the writers, technicians, artists and the film crews that forge productions. Nowhere is this fact clearer than in this film. A joint venture by Warner Bros. And Apple Originals, F1 finds award winning cinematographer Claudio Miranda at his best; proving, beyond a doubt, that an industry hell bent on "improvising" and "innovating" thru colored lenses and shaky cams has it all wrong. His efforts, coupled with excellent performances, a wonderful soundtrack and an unpredictable narrative elevate F1 to near cinematic perfection.Mimicking Joseph Campbell's Hero's journey, F1 details the exploits of a down and out race car professional. F1's moral of determination is especially vital in the current climate of unforgiving cancel culture and constant criticism of masculinity: we're all human beings and we all make mistakes. In tarot, "The Magician" card perfectly illustrates a man's calling in life: to bridge Heaven and Earth (to create and improve one's life and surroundings) despite any setbacks along the way.Under the watchful eye of producer Jerry Bruckheimer, F1 is indeed a very masculine venture. It's about excellence and the effort it takes to get there. Whether thru clever gumption or sheer training, this film aptly portrays what it's like to be a man (or daring woman!): to fail and recover, accept criticism and defy the odds in the face of danger. The racing car scene is, itself, a relentless pursuit of excellence, both for the vehicle and the driver. Bruckheimer's crew make every effort to honor Formula One thru technical authenticity, including seven-time world champion Sir Lewis Hamilton as co-producer.In typical Hollywood style, some aspects of the tournament are inflated for dramatic flare. The story surprises, generously applying aspects of race car driving in cinematic fashion, while also keeping an excellent pace and an upbeat tone.Cinematographer Claudio Miranda and director Joseph Kosinski manage to keep the action clear and concise thru the use of custom-built cameras and modified F2 cars. The fantastic filming is immediately noticeable, even to the untrained eye. Overhead shots are absolutely gorgeous, delivering a sturdy sense of setting. Brad Pitt delivers a believable character who, at times, had me forgetting who the actor was. It's difficult for a part to eclipse a major celebrity, and Pitt manages to achieve this, briefly. Thru the casting of Damson Idris, Sarah Niles, Samson Kayo and others, F1 also proves how diversity can be included organically in film, with proper character development, culture and background.Make no mistake: F1 is excellence in filmmaking. What's more, despite its rigorous requirements, this level of production isn't hard for Hollywood to achieve. F1 highlights how a man forges his own fate, independently and thru teamwork. It teaches the importance of living in the moment, as well as the real reason to live life: not for fame or fortune, but for the experience. To simply be.

title: The theater should have been packed but.........

The sound design and Zimmer's score push it over the edge.This isn't just a racing movie-it's a full-throttle experience. In a summer of slow movies, F1 should break records but it may not. Search for the haptic trailer from Apple, that will get u excited.

title: Yeah...still got it
content: I am not one of those who admires action genre and movies. I appreciate those like the next guy, but when I understand Joseph Kosinski is behind this movie I only think about his beloved: "Only the Brave", "Tron: Legacy" (which is a peak, from my perspective and point of view for his career) and even "Oblivion". "Top Gun: Maverick" is probably a movie that will technologically impress me but will not make me feel like I've felt on other movies of him.Yeah...never saw and of "top gun" movies and I will probably get along without those. This movie's script is its weakest side, but the rest is so impressive and made me regret I've never got the chance to see this movie on the theatres. It is impressive and takes the audience a real life experience as the driver.Joseph Kosinski got the extra mile to make this movie as a film that is worth buying a ticket for. The cast is fantastic, and the visual aspect wins every time, but it seems too long and without any base for a feature that is two and a half hours long. The races all seems the same and the plot is not strong enough to get the viewer fully engaged.Thanks to the fact that this movie was viewed like a series, I didn't feel that the movie is too long and managed not to be bored out of it, but probably the experience of watching it on the big screen will balance between its impressive content and its over exaggerated length, that might take any normal person to be impatient.Brad Pitt is the veteran. He shows once again his strength by getting out of the compliments of his good and young looks. He is a phenomenal actor and proves it once again. Damson Idris gets the job done and Kerry Condon completes a great and underused cast, that within the runtime of the movie could have gotten a much more compelling deep digging to each and every character.Hans Zimmer is in charge of the music and Kosinski is the grandmaster of this visual, ear dropping spectacle. All is done correctly, but it is will not be a movie that will be remembered as unique or groundbreaking. It is done fantastically in its genre - sport drama (with the special sub-genre of car racing movies), but lack's the originality, while leaning heavily on it's special effects and drilling down to technical details and feeling as if we are actually in the cockpit.At the end of the day. It is one hell of a movie to go through to the cinema. It is important for the purposes of preserving audience in the movie theatres and also preserving Joseph Kosinski as one of the masters in his field, but it will probably be forgotten in the pages of history of the genre.

title: F1: Fast on Wheels, Slow on Execution
content: F1, starring Brad Pitt, attempts to tell a straightforward story-so simple that it could have been effective-but even this modest ambition is undermined by nearly every other aspect of the film. Pitt's performance is painfully underwhelming; he moves through the role without conviction, intensity, or presence, leaving one to wonder if he was ever truly engaged with the material. The film's environment feels hollow and underdeveloped, failing to immerse the audience in the high-octane world it seeks to portray.The music and soundtrack do little to compensate, lacking the energy or emotion needed to lift the film, leaving the pacing and rhythm sluggish. Without tension, nuance, or a sense of urgency, the story feels flat, almost amateurish. It's a production that seems hastily assembled, with little attention to detail, poor world-building, and a cast that delivers performances far below their potential.In short, F1 is a film that promises speed and excitement but delivers monotony and missed opportunities. Its simplicity might have worked, but only if it had been executed with care-and it isn't. Instead, we are left with a weak, uninspired, and disappointing effort, as though the filmmakers were racing to finish without ever slowing down to craft something compelling.

title: He is flying and so do we
content: Let me get this out of the way. I'm not an F1 fan. I know the basics, I know some drivers, I could sit, watch and understand but not a formulahead or anything like that. This is an absolute masterpiece of a movie. I will start with the movie's best virtue in my opinion which is of course, the wildly amazing cinematography and the soundtrack along with the ambient. These 3 together mix up and make something you can only understand once you feel. The plot is nice and the way it unfolds is equally nice. The character development is brilliant and the bonding/change of relationship between the protagonists is not done out of nowhere or too suddenly. You actually feel the changes and you yourself change your opinion on the characters as the movie goes on (that's when you know a movie has achieved good character development). Lastly the acting was nothing but terrific with Brad Pitt, Damson Idris and Javier Bardem putting on unforgettable performances. In conclusion this movie reaches the vicinity of perfection and in my opinion may even slightly touch it. Just so you know, if you are prepared to go see a documentary-like film about F1, with cars screaming for 2.5 hours (even though there indeed is a lot of this too) and extreme acuraccy on the things the movie presents to you about the sport then be unprepared. This movie is a classic drama that does it's own thing and at the same time touches the topic of F1 without you knowing it is able to do both at the same time. Absolutely recommended (on a Dolby hall if possible)P. S To those of you who DARED make the mistake of doubting Brad Pitt and whether the fact he is too OLD to play ANY character and mocked him as well, now you can cry in your bathtub humiliated.

title: NOT A FORMULA 1 FAN, BUT ABSOLUTELY LOVED IT!!
content: I'm not really a Formula 1 fan, but this film completely won me over. From the very first scene, I was hooked and those two and a half hours just flew by.The photography is stunning, the soundtrack lifts every moment, and the racing sequences are so intense that you actually feel the speed and adrenaline. It's not just action for the sake of action - it pulls you into the world of Formula 1 and makes you understand it better, even appreciate it.What struck me most is how clearly it shows that racing is a team sport. No driver ever makes it alone, and the film really brings that truth to life. I also loved the contrast between the modern, gadget-filled training and Brad Pitt's old-school approach: running the track, feeling the ground, connecting with the car and the road. It's such a powerful metaphor that sometimes the classics still work best.And then there's Brad Pitt himself. Critics are right when they say this is one of his strongest performances in years. He feels authentic, like someone who truly belongs in the paddock, moving and reacting with the ease of a veteran racer. At the same time, he brings that unique mix of charisma, rugged charm, and emotional depth that makes him magnetic on screen. It's the kind of performance that reminds you why he's still a true movie star.Even if you don't follow racing, this is an exciting, heartfelt, and beautifully made movie. For me, it's one of the most surprising and rewarding films I've seen in a long time.

title: A fun time
content: While F1 didn't completely blow me away, I definitely was entertained throughout the whole film.Story-wise, it really isn't anything special. The whole setup has been done plenty of times before: A seasoned driver with something to prove, a talented but raw rookie, and a struggling team caught in the middle. It's simple and occasionally predictable. The film also tries to bring a lot of drama into these relationships. Let's just say some of it works, while other moments feel a bit forced.Where the movie really shines is in the racing itself. Joseph Kosinski clearly knows how to stage large-scale spectacles. Even as someone who isn't a hardcore F1 fan, I found myself fully locked in during the races.Brad Pitt brings a strong presence, even if the performance itself isn't that deep. He's charismatic and believable in the role. Javier Bardem, for me, steals a lot of scenes: His energy and charm add warmth to a film that otherwise feels a bit mechanical. The rest of the cast is solid, though not particularly memorable.Overall, F1 doesn't reinvent the genre (and definitely isn't as great or deep as Rush or Ford v Ferrari), but it's a well-made film with strong direction and thrilling race scenes.

title: what movie am I going to see - F1
content: F1 is one of those movies that grabs the viewer right from the start. It has very well-done direction, with impressive visuals and a soundtrack that really stands out - intense, engaging, and always at the right pace. The emotion is there from beginning to end, without any breaks.I highlight the way the film addresses teamwork and the need for experience, which is often undervalued. It goes far beyond the drivers - it also shows the effort of the garages, the engineers, and all the staff who work behind the scenes. We've become accustomed to good performances, but here we see again why he's one of the greats. He brings charisma and intensity to the character, and that only enriches the film even more.Overall, F1 is an excellent mix of adrenaline, emotion, and drama. Even for those who aren't big fans of racing, it's well worth watching.

title: Visually Stunning, But Weighed Down by Frustrating Character Choices
content: "F1" is undeniably a cinematic spectacle. The racing scenes are intense and beautifully shot, and Hans Zimmer's return to musical scoring adds a powerful emotional layer that elevates the experience. Visually and sonically, it's an epic thrill ride.However, the film stumbles when it comes to character development-especially with Brad Pitt's character. His mindset is frustrating: afraid of success, unwilling to embrace being a champion, and consistently sabotaging the very team fighting to keep their jobs. His constant crashes and deliberate losses become repetitive, making it hard to root for him at all. It reaches a point where you wonder why he deserves to win in the first place.The team drama adds tension, but it drags the story out unnecessarily, making the film feel longer than it should. While the action and production value are top-tier, the emotional payoff falls short due to a main character whose self-destructive attitude overshadows the stakes.Overall, while "F1" delivers on the thrills, its heart feels lost in the wreckage.

title: Great if you assume it's a cliché sports film, but they could have done more.
content: This surprisingly exceeded my expectations, even though the overall result was a bit of a letdown considering its massive box office success. It's incredibly tough for a sports film to feel original because they almost always follow the same old underdog formula: lose, lose, have a small win, lose again, lose bigger, and then finally win it all. This movie managed to break that mold, which I really appreciated.That said, the characters felt a little thin. I wish they had delved more into Sonny Hayes' backstory. I get what they were going for with Brad Pitt's performance-the camera work, the music, and his whole vibe were meant to show his passion and regrets as a driver. But honestly, I just didn't connect with his emotions. Maybe it was the acting itself; I felt like I was watching a performance rather than a genuine person, which pulled me out of the film.Joshua's character was even less developed. I didn't get any sense of personal interpretation or feeling from Damson Idris. It's like any actor could have played the part. His performance felt average and safe, and it's a real shame. I don't know much about him, but if he's a rising star, this felt like a missed opportunity-especially since he was acting opposite Brad Pitt in what will probably be the biggest film of his career.

title: It is not a masterpiece, but it is entertaining enough to keep you hooked until the end.
content: F1 (2025) pulls you in with its sheer spectacle. The driving sequences are electric, the CGI blends almost seamlessly with the real racing shots, and the score ties it all together with an energy that doesn't let up. The editing is sharp, keeping the film moving at a steady pace, and the cinematography puts you right on the track in a way that feels immersive. Performances across the board work well enough to keep the story engaging, with Brad Pitt and the rest of the cast bringing a natural presence to the screen. On a production level, it nails what it sets out to do and delivers a thrilling watch.Where the film falls short is in the script. The dialogue and overall writing feel flat, almost like it was stitched together quickly without much care. It leans too heavily on clichés and predictable moments instead of developing deeper characters or giving the story more weight. That said, the technical achievements outweigh the weak writing, making it a fun ride if you go in for the spectacle rather than the story. It is not a masterpiece, but it is entertaining enough to keep you hooked until the end.

title: Some eye-rolling moments but overall fun film
content: Being a life-long F1/Indy Car/NASCAR racing fan, there were certainly some eye-rolling moments here, but the movie achieved what it set out to do - be entertaining. Many filmmakers forget the purpose of a film is to entertain, and having an entertaining film requires a few things - a plot, character development, flow, and viewer engagement. This film checks those boxes.For those who are F1 fans, there are definitely areas in which one must check their brains at the door and just go with it. The biggest area is that a 60-year old man can just jump back into an F1 car after nearly 40 years and pick up where he left off, especially a man whose spine has been surgically repaired with more screws and bolts than a pickup truck. It may not appear to the casual observer, but F1 drivers are athletes. Their bodies must endure several hours of extreme G-forces on their necks, shoulders and arms while taking tight turns at 130 miles per hour with track temperatures hitting 140 degrees. Five minutes in such a car would wipe out most people, physically, even a regular athlete. The cars have also drastically changed over the past few decades, this isn't your dad's race car. There's a reason drivers progress through F3 and F2 before getting an F1 seat, which few drivers do. It's the major leagues. And driving NASCAR vs Baja vs 24-hour vs F1 are totally different things. Many drivers attempt the F1-NASCAR crossover in both directions and most fail at it. F1 cars are also very low to the ground; the driver's butt is literally about two inches from the pavement and the pounding the driver's spine takes is jarring, never mind to a guy with a spine bolted together. But hey it's Hollywood, move on.In the film, an F1 team is on the verge of being kicked off the grid for their inability to score a single point after several years. In F1, there are 10 teams with two cars/drivers each. Points are only scored for the top 10 finishers in each race; the bottom 10 cannot receive any points. This is unlike NASCAR where you can still receive points even if you crash out, get repaired, and finish 30 laps down. In F1 races once you're a couple laps down and you have a problem, the car is retired because there is no point in risking the car and driver when it's impossible to gain any points. The cars are also far more delicate and in most cases, any damage is fatal to that race. You can replace a front wing but that's about it. That leads to the second eye-rolling moments, in which Brad Pitt's character continually deploys crazy strategies and scenarios that no one has ever seen in any F1 race which puts the car and driver in hazardous situations that will take out the car and possibly the driver. As you'd expect, these cars are incredibly expensive to repair, and Pitt's character churns through them like a smash-up derby driver. The team is already broke but what's rebuilding another car? No problem.For F1 fans, the fun part of the movie is the cameos by the many actual F1 drivers, team principles, and pit crews. Fans will have fun picking them out of different scenes. The film was shot during actual F1 races and fans will remember the days they were on-site during the various races depicted during the film, which were actual races. Fans will also appreciate the scenes that focus on off-race meetings and design troubleshooting and discussing technical details. While the in-car view of races will be fascinating and new to non-fan viewers, F1 fans are already familiar with these views as every single F1 race shows these views all race long, so there is nothing new there, but fun to watch regardless.In character development, there is a progression and focus on back stories, so the viewer is not left wondering why a particular character is doing what they are doing or what their motivation may be, it's always clear. But much of it is entirely predictable so there are no surprises, everyone appears to be who they are with no head-scratching moments. Some of the character devices can become a bit tedious, and in a two and a half hour film, some of it could have been trimmed without affecting the movie, and this is the only real ding on the film.Overall, the film is engaging, it pulls the viewer into the action and plot, provides plenty of action and flow, and you don't really notice the time until the very end when it extends past the two hour mark. As implausible as some of it is for actual F1 fans, it's still enjoyable which is what any film should do. While it may not create any new F1 fans (go watch the series "Drive To Survive" instead) it should appeal to fans and non-fans of racing.

title: 2.5 Hour Music Video
content:
There was, maybe, one racing sequence in the 2.5 hour movie that did not have loud, pounding music detracting from what could have been exciting racing.Instead of giving the audience the full racing experience, hearing a Formula 1 race car screaming around the track or the visceral experience of sitting in the cockpit, we get a disco club at full volume.Not for racing fans...I just wanted to experience the start of the engines, the way the cars sound from the edge of the track or exiting the pits...
But, it was very disappointing.This will not go down in the annals of great racing films. Le Mans it is not.

It's entertaining, emotional, and absolutely worth watching on the big screen today..

title: Great movie for F1 lovers
content: The race scenes are absolutely thrilling. It's shot from inside the front wing, and you can really feel the rubber meeting the road, the pressure and intensity - they picked the most exhilarating F1 moments and showcased it. I was on the edge of my seat for the races.Obviously some parts are not realistic (60 year old being invited to race in F1), but the movie mechanics seem authentic - inspired by Renault crashing a car to win a race in similar manner to Sonny. Cameos of Verstappen and Hamilton were neat. The training regiment, drag reduction modes, mistakes captured on the fly added more realism to what was already an engaging movie.Brad Pitt is funny, race shots are incredible, and overall this movie left me missing competition and a greater respect for F1.

title: It's lights out and away we go
content:
No spoilers.Easy start - to not spoil - the acting. Excellent.Javier Bardem plays an excellent team owner. Given that F1 is a largely European his accent was simply mellifluous. He hit every emotion perfectly.Brad Pitt plays an aging driver. I was very very skeptical and the opening had me fearful, but he turned it around and by the end I was routing for him and interested to see what would happen and I was pleased with the outcome.Damson Idris plays the rookie driver. Played a very good part sometimes overly cocky, as rookies can be. At times I disliked him, or rather his character, but we were supposed to do that, so this too was well done.Kerry Condon plays the aerodynamics team lead. Without spoiling all I can saw is A++++. I heard Bernie Collins voice every time she spoke - IYKYK.Cameos - cannot be overrated, just awesome. Not just the people, of which many drivers, team bosses and others scattered about the F1 paddock. The tracks and the paddocks and the pits the pit in and the pit out. All sparkling in authenticity as it was shot on location 2023 and 2024. The cinematography excelled on all points. Enough helo and drone footage intermixed with in-car camera and scenes from the pits were just so very cool. Again, from someone who watched many, many hours of practice, qualifying and a lot of racing, this was greatly appreciated.Announcers. Without doubt - if I had to pick one thing, and that's hard, this is it. Having the Sky Sports announcers with Will Buxton in the pits was absolutely great. The first time I heard in a theater setting "It's lights out, and away we go!" 100% gave me chills.On to the racing: good racing footage, as one might imagine, quite a bit of drama and theatrics (this is a theatrical production in case you didn't know that) that would almost assuredly incur penalties and likely some points incurred on your F1 Super Licence. They did show a lot how strategy plays into the race and the tire management and how crucial pit stops, over cuts and under cuts can be. They make good drama out of "team orders" and a lot of other F1 unique stuff like DRS, battery energizing and the like. While some have noted cringeworthy at times, the positives far outweigh the negatives.Music is great. An awesome score from an awesome composer.Plot - predictable. Not quite Hallmark but not far from it. The racing overcame the weakness of plot to dazzle and impress.Acting - *****
Cinematography - *****
Plot and Story - ***
Support cast including tracks - ********
Musical Score - ****Overall - *****Will absolutely watch again. Definitely in Top 10 all time.

title: It was alright, just not nearly as good as Gran Turismo
content: Wanted to like the movie. Was looking forward to it after the stage set by Gran Turismo, but it fell short. I know, different type of racing, but racing all the same. Unlike Gran Turismo, F1's story isn't based on a real story, which is probably why the story didn't feel nearly as good. Not only was he annoying with no redeeming qualities, but he even lied. Never told Carlos about the girl who wanted to meet him.6/10 feels generous but Brad Pitt is cool so it's fine.

title: Not pole position, but lands some points
content: F1: The Movie is an entertaining, visually dazzling ride that may not break new ground narratively, but still delivers a fun and engaging watch-especially for motorsport fans. The plot is predictable and follows a well-worn formula, but the energy and excitement of the F1 world keep things moving at full throttle.The film's true strength lies in its technical execution. The racing sequences are stunningly shot, capturing the speed, tension, and atmosphere of Formula 1 like few films before. It's clear that a lot of care went into getting the look and feel just right-and it pays off.Brad Pitt's performance is fine, if occasionally distracting. His fake laugh doesn't quite land, and he doesn't always feel fully immersed in the role. It's not his best work, but it's serviceable. (It does make you wonder again how he snagged that Oscar-but hey, everyone has their off days.)Special mention goes to F1's marketing machine: combining a star-studded feature film with the popularity of Netflix's Drive to Survive is a genius move. It's a clear attempt to bring new fans to the sport-and honestly, it just might work.F1: The Movie may not be a cinematic masterpiece, but it's a slick, adrenaline-fueled companion piece to the growing F1 media universe. Worth a watch, especially if you're racing-curious.

title: Good for the genre of racing films
content: Disclosure- I am an F1 fan. This was a well-made movie from a production standpoint. The actors played the roles well and the cinematography was top-notch. The plot is unrealistic and at times drips with cheese, but it seems like all racing films have the same basic tropes. Young driver, old driver, crazy risk-taking driver to pull off the impossible, driver wins over reluctant girl who "knows better", driver overcoming mental flashbacks of former wreck that nearly killed him, etc. As long as you understand this is what you are getting then this is a film that is done better than most in the genre and is enjoyable to watch.

title: Prime Blockbuster Movie
content: F1 is a sports film about a nomadic race car driver who is brought back to Formula One racing to save his old friend's racing team. This movie is from the director of Top Gun: Maverick and yeah, the story structure is a Top Gun: Maverick but with cars. Instead of Tom Cruise, Brad Pitt fits the racing boots as Sonny and he plays the smooth chaotic cowboy the entire time. It's Brad Pitt so he is always charming but don't expect much emotional depth for this movie. Damson Idris plays the cocky young racer and he does a good job bouncing off Brad Pitt for most of the movie. Javier Bardem plays the old friend to Brad Pitt and they also have great chemistry. They do have a love interest for Brad Pitt who plays her role good although she mostly just serves as a romance interest device more than anything. The story is formulaic sports film. They have an obvious third act villain that was so bad to deal with. Now where the movie excels is the camera work. This movie is definitely worth the IMAX or Dolby as they keep you in the car one way or another during the races and it is extremely engaging. There were some moments my adrenaline was pumping because I felt like I was in the car. This is a prime blockbuster film where it is not for the story but the spectacle.

title: F1
content: Rubén Cervantes (Javier Bardem) persuades veteran racing driver Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) with a past to bring his experience to his struggling APXGP team. Hayes joins and stirs things up which annoys young, upcoming no 1 driver Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). However his experience begins to show and the crew and eventually Pearce start to listen to him - although is he a bit too maverick for his own good?Terrific, old fashioned racing thriller with a cool superstar turn from Pitt, well supported by a great cast including Javier Bardem, Tobias Menzies and Kerry Cordon. Whilst the plot is a little cheesy, predictable and rather fanciful, it is very well done and hugely enjoyable with a great score from Hans Zimmer. The racing sequences are unparalleled and even the amazing technology that adds so much these days is strangely riveting. Sharp and glossy, you just get carried along with it - nail biting stuff.

title: Good Thrilling Blockbuster with Classic Old-School Style
content: "It's rare, but sometimes there's this moment in the car where everything goes quiet, my heartbeat slows. It's peaceful, and I can see everything, and no one, no one can touch me. And I am chasing that moment every time I get in the car. I don't know when I'll find it again, but man, I want to. I want to, cause in that moment I'm flying."Brought to you by the director of Top Gun: Maverick, he returns with another thrilling blockbuster in this movie. This is a movie with an original storyline of a team named APXGP who hires a former driver named Sonny Hayes to save their season. The plot isn't really extraordinary. It's just simple and fun, as it seemed more focused on entertaining with its action sequences rather than forming a gripping plot.We are back with another old "loser" character arc in Sonny. I actually really like these because it gives the character a chance to find their purpose, which we see when we reach the ending. Seeing his routines, such as card throwing, practicing with tennis balls, and running, was really fun to watch. I did wish to see the film explore more of how his past choices and failures affected him. I liked seeing Bardem as Ruben. I think he was the best side character. He was realistic but compassionate. Joshua Pearce was an alright character, I guess. He wasn't as strong as Ruben or Sonny. He was just this young and immature brat who learns and matures. I honestly don't know why people found Kate McKenna to be poorly written. Other than her sudden love interest for Sonny, she wasn't that bad. Maybe they could have made the love interest a bit of a slow burn by adding more scenes and development instead of rushing it. Seeing her work with the team was pretty nice, though.The visual effects and the cinematography were stunning. I liked how they used practical effects for the races. Some hardcore F1 fans may complain about some of the stuff being too artificial, though. This may include them using F2 cars, among other things.Bringing back that old-style filmmaking, F1 is filled with adrenaline, entertaining racing sequences, a beautiful soundtrack, and a good character arc. While it may be unrealistic and some of the plot feels too familiar, it's a rare summer and sports blockbuster that works and makes you lose a bit of your mind.

title: An adrenaline-filled, action-packed thrill: "F1"
content: Sports like basketball, baseball, or football are usually a vital interest in a boy's childhood. However, much like me, most people forget the sport of Formula 1 (F1) racing - a world I was once unfamiliar with but now all the more interested in.That's exactly why when I first heard about the new "F1" movie, I didn't really think anything of it until I decided to give it a chance. After watching the trailer, I was pleasantly surprised, and it completely sparked my interest not only in the sport but in this potential summer blockbuster.Directed by Joseph Kosinski, who is most known for "Top Gun: Maverick" (2022), along with Jerry Bruckheimer and seven-time F1 World Champion Lewis Hamilton, "F1" succeeds in providing the audience with an authentic and unique experience truly like no other.In "F1," Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), an F1 driver, comes out of retirement after a career-ending crash to mentor and team up with a young, talented, and promising new driver.This film features a fictional 11th team in the F1 league and was filmed during the actual racing season, with production integrated with real races and filmed on various iconic tracks around the world.Lewis Hamilton's involvement in this film extends far beyond just an appearance. Hamilton served as a co-producer and crucial consultant in regard to driving skills, script accuracy, and authenticity. Everything is done to perfection, even to the most miniscule detail.Additionally, this film highlights a dynamic and incredible soundtrack with original songs from top tier artists, such as Ed Sheeran, Doja Cat, Tate McRae, Rosé, Chris Stapleton, and many more.The impressive score for this film was composed by the immensely talented and highly skilled Hans Zimmer, who holds two Academy Awards, three Golden Globes Awards, five Grammy Awards and one BAFTA Award.The film explores and develops different themes of second chances, redemption, teamwork, overcoming challenges, the mentor-rookie relationship, and the classic underdog storyline.It also showcases the true demanding, dangerous, and life-threatening nature of F1 racing.The casting director, Joesph Balderrama and his crew succeeded in recruiting Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes, Damson Idris as Joshua Pearce, Javier Bardem as Ruben Cervantes, Kerry Condon as Kate McKenna, and real drivers from the current F1 season.Both Pitt and Idris spent months preparing and training for their respective scenes in the cars, experiencing the brutality and intensity of the G-forces while driving at extremely high speeds.Pitt fully embodies and perfectly portrays his character, effortlessly capturing the natural charisma, charm, and humor of Hayes. His ability to make certain situations or events in the film more appealing and elevate the stakes is nothing short of extraordinary.Idris is also very successful in his portrayal of Pearce, and throughout the film, allows the audience to connect with the character on a deeper level. His performance demonstrates the rivalry, competitiveness, and the need to become better than Pitt's character.Other notable mentions include Bardem and Condon in their supporting roles, which add to the overall depth and emotion to the film.As a whole, the cast and crew seamlessly integrates the world of F1 racing into this film and showcases the various elements of overcoming addiction, struggling with past trauma, and the ethics of pushing the boundaries in racing.In its entirety, "F1" is pure entertainment, providing the audience with an incredible, immersive experience. The heartfelt, suspenseful film is filled with so many adrenaline and seat-clenching moments that left me gasping at every turn, as the racing is extremely visceral, cinematography was stunning, and the action was pulse-pumping.The specific movie format utilized in "F1" emphasizes and reflects the speed, energy, and intensity of F1 racing. In fact, Kosinski and his team had to develop a completely new camera system and camera mount that was much smaller and lighter so it wouldn't affect the actor's performances in the car. The result of this innovation is a series of dynamic angles, emphasizing a stronger connection with the actors and their driving.By the end, "F1" left me absolutely stunned, amazed, and excited for what's to come.I couldn't recommend this film enough, but in order to really understand and appreciate the nuances and hard work that went into making "F1" a reality, you need to experience it for yourself on the biggest screen possible.Not to mention, "F1" has already earned positive reviews from both audiences and critics, earning the best CinemaScore of Pitt's career.So whether you are an avid F1 fan or a casual car lover, this film offers something for everyone to enjoy."F1: The Movie" is in theaters now.

title: Brad Pitt vs F1
content: Unfortunately, despite the many good scenes from real Formula 1 races, which is the only value of this film, we basically got another empty-headed production with Brad Pitt, so whoever likes to watch him, go ahead. His films have long since become just a decor in which our favorite actor has something to do. Everything in the film is completely wrong, together with the story, which is both completely predictable and worthless, and everything in the film is as it is not in reality.The real question is why would Joseph Kosinski, who we remember as having directed the likeable Oblivion in 2012, as well as the recent hit Top Gun Maverick in 2022, sign under such an unimaginative product? But okay, I guess it is quite legitimate these days to swim or float in the Hollywood toadstool, as everyone chooses for him/herself.In short, while Formula 1 fans have nothing to look forward to, fans of the famous actor have reason to watch it, if that's good enough for them.

title: Well put together
content: Another movie that has all the requisite component parts put together in the right order - drama, tension, regret, chance at redemption, the underdog, hanging on by a thread, betrayal, romance and... an angry threatening mum. Plus a lot of crashes. I watched it in 4dx and I would recommend everyone to do the same. It was a thousand times more exciting than watching the actual boring formula one races on TV. A real emotional roller coaster with worry, dread, anticipation, concern... really worked. Well done Brad Pitt. The only criticism I would give is that there wasn't a single character with any degree of depth. Everyone was very superficial at best.

title: F-anstastic Thrill Ride
content: Just ignore the haters, that won't give a film more than 20 minutes and seem to delight on claiming to "walk out" of films that they've already pre-judged. Kosinski and Bruckheimer know how to make a high-octane, adrenaline-filled, audience-pleasing summer blockbuster, and F1 delivers in spades. "Over-the-hill" Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) is a wonderful "foil" to young rookie and upstart Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) with enough drama between the two to make things interesting. And, the interplay between Sonny and Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) shows that a long-standing friendship and mutual respect is most important to the two former teammates. Just watch, cheer and enjoy!

title: Good looking cheesie Hollywood tosh of a dramatic sports action blockbuster
content: Dramatic sports actioner "F1" is glossy & well cast... but boy is it Hollywood cheesie. Javier Bardem, manager of Formula One's worst team (with lead engineer Kerry Condon (great actress) & hot-head rookie driver Damson Idris), coaxes washed-up & forgotten ex-driver Brad Pitt (now knocking around Le Mans etc) back to drive for him. Cue clichéd young guy / old guy rivalry, predictable romance, implausible tactics to give the underdogs a fighting chance (all corny guff from writer Ehren Kruger), and a wealth of actual F1 cameos (as well as Shea Wigham's)... everything set against Joseph Kosinski's beautiful, polished direction. Looks great - but is just tosh.

title: Amazing
content: Honestly, F1: The Movie blew me away. He wasn't just acting - he became the character. You could see the experience, the pain, and the passion in his expressions without him saying a word. His calm intensity gave the whole film a grounded, human side that made it way more than just a racing movie.The mix of real F1 footage and narrative scenes was seamless, and everything just felt real. This movie made me appreciate the world of Formula 1 in a way I never had before. Whether you're into racing or not, this is a film worth watching. Big respect to everyone involved.

title: At least it's better than Driven....
content: So, this is an interesting mix. The actors are great, every one of them. The story is good - when they are off track. Nothing too complex, nothing will surprise you, but its a good Hollywood flick which avoids several of the clichés that we all get used to in this genre. The old driver didn't live under a rock for decades. He isn't a misunderstood genius or infallible. The young driver isn't from the slums, his family is actually obviously wealthy, and he isn't a diamond in the rough waiting for the master to polish him. Every character has personal flaws and that makes them more relatable.And they integrated the movie extremely well into live footage. They even follow the events of some of the races properly! Cinematography is great, although sometimes the CGI is screamingly obvious, especially when you look back at the rear wing of the APX cars to see the following cars.But gosh, when the cars go onto the track, this movie becomes ridiculous. The speed is there perfect, unlike in Rush, when you could tell these prop cars drive at 30 KPH tops. The drivers really send these vehicles, and it shows. But the events. Oh. My. God. The events, are dumb as dirt! Real life drivers would be banned for a lifetime for the fraction of what APX drivers do in this movie. The moves they pull sometimes doesn't make any sense. And yeah, cars still fly into the air by sneezing at them and they burst into a million pieces when they shouldn't and survive huge shunts with minor damage when they should be incapacitated.So, yeah... If you look at it as a old champ, new champ movie, it is good. The actors are great, the visuals are good. Just don't think it has anything to do with real Formula 1.

title: Much better than expected
content: As someone who isn't a fan of Formula One I gave this a try on the back of some positive reviews and it works well as long as you go with it and don't look for realism. I get that people have criticised the use of race commentary to explain the basics in circumstances where the commentators would never speak like that but that works too! In essence Brad Pitt gives an engaging performance in the lead role as the ageing former F1 driver returning for another shot and Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon add to the entertainment. It's perhaps appropriately formulaic, and it's all been done before- troubled veteran with something to prove pitched alongside a rookie with great potential, both working against impossible odds, but it takes you along with it for a fun ride. It's effectively Top Gun Maverick on a race track, with a different A list actor.

title: A real "action" movie
content: I'm going to write this review as if I hadn't watched F1 in this lifetime- because the hype is a bit overblownThe cinematography of the film is super cool. Well-shot frames combined with SFX that really impose this action vibe (a feeling I haven't experienced this strongly in a long time). I like that the film starts with one filming style and ends with the same, without switching it up a million times. The story is good, and I like that it has 3-4 plot twists. :))) It's like even the plot twist has its own plot twist. On the dialogue and character interaction side, it's pretty weak. This was actually the second time I've watched a movie at the cinema in Constanta, and I have to say the loud people in the audience spoiled the experience a bit.Aside from the amazing visuals, the soundtrack really stands out (you can tell it's Hans Zimmer's work).The acting is fairly average-in my opinion, there are films where Brad Pitt and Javier act waaaaaay better.But to conclude: the movie is a good one (something rare nowadays), a true action film with a story that blends everything together-action, comedy, and a bit of romance. Good watch!

title: F1: Fast, Flashy, but Falls Short on Story
content: F1 brings the high-octane world of Formula 1 to the big screen with stunning visuals and the magnetic presence of Brad Pitt in the lead role. As expected, Pitt delivers a solid performance - his charisma and screen presence continue to stand out even after all these years. However, the film leans heavily on visual spectacle rather than a gripping storyline.The racing scenes are undoubtedly the highlight. Shot with thrilling precision and enhanced by the IMAX experience, the movie immerses you in the F1 perspective like few have before. But beyond the track, the story feels flat - predictable and lacking emotional depth or insight into the real struggles of a Formula 1 driver.One noticeable drawback is the age factor - while Brad Pitt does his best, it's hard to suspend disbelief seeing him in the role of an active F1 racer competing at the highest level. Perhaps if this film had come out a decade earlier, it would've felt more believable and impactful.Still, F1 is worth a watch, especially for racing enthusiasts or anyone curious about the adrenaline-fueled atmosphere of motorsport. Just don't expect a deeply layered narrative - go for the visuals, stay for Brad Pitt.

title: Dissapointing
content: I don't know how this movie has such high score on Imdb. I am an F1 fan and didn't have high expectations to begin with but wow, it's quite bad. The story line is very predictable, a lot of cringe moments that leave you confused. Idris's character itself was super cringe and annoying, he just irritated me with his attitude the whole movie. I think they could have explained his background and character (especially given his annoying tantrums) a lot better story, otherwise he just acted as a really annoying and bratty "rookie", despite the fact he lives in a posh apartment and drives a posh car (how did he get all that money as a rookie?!). Trying to throw a brief romantic scene which didn't add anything to the plot. Honestly, there are so many unnecessary things in this movie! Given that Lewis Hamilton was involved in this, how did that even get his approval or some kind of encouragement to think oh yeah, sounds like a great thing to add in there?! Some of the racing things were good, but you could visibly see that it wasn't an F1 car in close battles, but fair enough, they made it look okay. Overall, I would say the movie is okay for a casual viewer, but obvioisly if you are a fan of the sport, you will laugh or be annoyed at some of the scenes in the movie. There are a lot better racing movies out there and given such hype for the past two years for this movie, it was an absolute waste of time.

title: Just a great big-ticket ride of a Blockbuster
content: Ok, full disclosure.. I am an real F1 fan (been to a dozen live races including Silverstone, Monza and Monaco, and once had lunch with Michael Schumacher.. not flexing, just saying) not a Drive to survive late adopter...SO I approached this film with a combination of excitement and trepidation, would Hollywood mess it up with too much glitz and a lack of understanding about the multi-faceted parts of F1 that make it tick... or would they succumb to the techno-bores who love to quote F1 stats like tyre pressures etc - well the finished product is a big budget action film which manages to satisfy both audiences.I loved Brad Pitts early films, Fight club Se7en, Kalifornia etc but the past 15 years or so it's felt like he was dialling-in his performances with a perma-smug expression and a half asleep delivery... (see the 2024 dreadful Wolfs and you'll know what I mean!) but this performance is a return to his best, delivering a character battling his own Demons while trying to look like he's on top of his life. The action scenes are superb and will satisfy any real F1 fan and having been in team garages and the Paddock club, they also capture the glamour and atmosphere of both.The entire cast are excellent, Idris Damson is great as the cocky upstart, Kerry Condon is sublime and Javier Badem chews the scenery perfectly in every scene he appears in!Yes there's a relatively predictable storyline - washed-up once-great old timer finds redemption, arrogant youngster learns wisdom and patience from old timer, female chief engineer finally gets recognition for the amazing work she does etc etc... but so what.. most films are predictable - like being on a train where you know the destination but you are enjoying the scenery on your way there.Yes there is a chunk of suspension of disbelieve - at 61 Pitt is old enough to be the dad of EVERY driver on the grid - including the Oldest Fernando Alonso @ 43 - or even grandad of Kimi Antoneli @18. And he makes an almost seamless return to F1 after 30yrs away from the sport (when everything has changed technically on these cars) ... but he does look MUCH younger and his current racing credentials are established in the opening scene.The film's real ace up its sleeve is that all the action takes place at Real circuits and often in the middle of real races with the actual drivers- this makes it all feel VERY authenticJust like in F1 you find yourself rooting for the underdog - the scrappy team at the back who suddenly deliver a great car and have an amazing season. (And anyone who says this is unbelievable - go and watch the Keanu Reeves documentary about the BRAWN 2009 season - a bankrupt team with a great car design and 2 unpaid drivers fighting for their careers comes back and beats all the big budget teams to win the world championship - a story no-one would believe if pitched as a movie!)My one small criticism - which may annoy other F1 fans is the constant explaining of the techincal aspects of the sport - embedded as commentary by David Croft and Martin Brundle eg "the safety car is now out.. so all the cars must slow down and line up behind it until the debris is cleared"... or "when the tyres are worn out the cars drive slower and lose grip" made me roll my eyes... BUT I get why they did this for the non-F1 fans who are there to see an action film and may not understand the sport fully.In Summary GO SEE THIS MOVIE, it's a great action rump that gets your heart pumping while seeing Brad Pitt back to his best ... what's not to love about that!?

title: Great acting by Brad Pitt
content: Brad Pitt delivers a grounded, charismatic performance as a washed-up F1 driver seeking redemption in a sport that's moved on. F1 blends adrenaline-fueled racing with emotional depth, exploring themes of passion, wisdom, and mentorship. The racing scenes-filmed at real Grand Prix events-feel immersive and authentic. While the story treads familiar ground, the execution is sharp, stylish, and heartfelt. A worthy watch for racing fans and drama lovers alike.The film also benefits from strong chemistry between Pitt and Damson Idris, with their mentor-mentee dynamic adding real emotional weight. Combined with Lewis Hamilton's behind-the-scenes input, F1 captures both the spectacle and soul of the sport.

title: "We have the driver."
content: The aged Sonny Hayes, once considered a prodigy is now dubbed as "never was", is a racer for hire. He is going from one race to another, not doing it for the money and he is approached by his friend Rubén Cervantes offers him to join his APXGP F1 team. They have never won and are at the last position with the investors threatening to sell the team. Sonny Hayes is brought into work with the team and the young rookie driver Joshua Pearce to score that much needed victory out of nine Grand Prix remaining. With the troubling cars, inexperienced fellow driver, how does Sonny make them all work hard with him to achieve the impossible, forms rest of the story.As all sports film go, this one too sticks to the template while balancing out the realism as well as cinematic liberties needed to sell this underdog story. The film is terrific visually and watching it on IMAX screen was an experience to cherish. With it's main lead proving why he is at his charismatic best, Joseph Kosinski won half the battle with the able supporting cast too. The narrative doesn't falter even once as the race keeps the pace intact and give enough space for the drama to flourish. The internal rivalry between Sonny and Pearce is well executed but is resolved in quite a filmy manner. Their dynamics work out well and so does the mandatory speech. As it ticks off the genre template, the climax paid off big time, making F1 a worthy theatrical experience.

title: Worth the watch...
content: Though Brad Pitt's acting didn't bring a whole lot to the table, and the script felt a bit clanky (particularly in the beginning), the movie surprisingly drew me in. I even held my very full bladder for over an hour so as not to miss anything.Let me preface by saying I'm not a formula one fan, and really have zero interest in racing. And after watching this movie, I still have zero interest in racing. But somehow, despite a subpar script and a lack of facial expressions and nuance from Brad Pitt, I cared. I cared about Sonny Hayes, and I cared about whether they won or lost. I don't know if it was my emotional positioning going into the film, or the feat of the filmmakers to achieve this, but gosh darn if it wasn't inspirational and moving. Adrenaline pumping, immersive, and ultimately a fun movie with which I was able to connect. It's always fun walking out of a movie theatre with that larger than life feeling, your head filled with possibilities and hopeful longings, and "what if" questions. Really enjoyed it, and I think it hit on a lot of points. Go see it in the theatre if you can; such a great movie experience.

title: Ultimately forgettable, but a thrilling enough ride
content: STAR RATING: ***** Brilliant **** Very Good *** Okay ** Poor * AwfulIn the 1990's, Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) was a an upcoming Formula One sensation, until an accident put an end to everything. Thirty years on, and he's scraping a living racing a Porsche. Suddenly, he finds himself approached by old friend and Apx GP manager, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), who proposes Sonny travels with him to Silverstone in England, to rein in current hotshot Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), but their conflicting styles soon have them at odds with each other.One of the great sorrows of my life is never being drawn to follow any sport of any sort, and never getting caught up in the broad appeal of following any team with any sort of tribalist zeal that sucks in so many with such great passion. And that would include Formula One, a sport where the players are hidden behind the windscreens of giant metal machines, rather than the direct exposure cricket or football players receive. But that's not to say I can't understand the excitement and adrenaline the game can generate, and here Top Gun: Maverick director Joseph Kosinski attempts to realise its exhilarating big screen potential.Whilst Kosinski remains the director, producer Jerry Bruckheimer reliably makes his mark known, with his slow panning camera shots running over the proceedings at key interludes, endearingly unaware of how corny and outdated it looks these days. It certainly doesn't cover an overlong, dragging script, with some weak dialogue and improbable plot developments that only add to the eye rolls. In the lead role, Pitt does his pretty standard mumbling, swaggering tough guy act, while a support cast including Idris, Bardem, and Kerry Condon as Sonny's love interest get buried amongst a host of underdeveloped side characters.All that aside, the motor racing scenes are reliably thrilling and exciting, and neatly stylised into proceedings, even if they don't really make the nearly three hour runtime pass any quicker. This is a big package, but no one can say it doesn't deliver what it promised. ***
