title: FAR FROM WHAT I WAS HOPING FOR
content: I've waited anxiously for this movie to become available to me for about three months. I watched it last night and thought I might share my feelings about it here.I can't imagine, going back to the 1980s and the early 1990s, that there was anyone who was really more of a fan of Formula 1 motor racing than I. For all that time, I was also a huge fan of Porsche cars. So, naturally I followed the McLaren team which was using TAG Porsche engines in their cars. The drivers at McLaren at the time. Alain Prost, Ayton Senna and Niki Lauda.I lived in Australia at the time so, whenever there was a race on a Sunday afternoon in Europe or wherever, I video taped the race because of time difference and then had the problem of getting through the next day at work without some b**tard either telling me the result or accidentally giving me some clue as to whether I was going to be happy or not, depending on whether Lauda or Prost and team McLaren had done well.Now, back to F1: The Movie.I would agree that it was very well filmed and that the action/racing scenes were very well done. But, that's about where my praise will end. The script was NOT good, full of cliches and the acting, especially by the Joshua Pearce character was also pretty bad. The depictions of modern F1 racing with its many new and stupid rules were dispiriting to anyone like me who loved F1 at the time when drivers were drivers rather than computer technicians, when the cars were still sometimes lethal and, most of all, when F1 was about real racing and driver skill. F1 these days has become an exercise in occupational health and safety and I find it incredibly dull compared to how it was in the 80s and 90s. F1: The Movie was NOT exciting to watch for all those reasons and perhaps a couple more that escape me now.Why is modern F1 racing dull? One small example from about 10 years ago, circa 2015. I think it might have been the Hungarian GP where there was a multi car stack at the very first turn. In the old days the yellow flags would have come out and many martials would have come and removed cars and cleared the track of debris before the cars came around a second time and the race would have continued at full throttle in just a few minutes. WHAT DID HAPPEN at the Hungaroring? First, the safety car came out, everything slowed to walking pace and, soon after, racing was stopped for a ridiculous 90 minutes while every tiny scrap of debris from the crash was removed from the track and the martials had sprayed Mister Muscle everywhere to make sure the track wasn't slippery. THAT is why modern F1 is boring and unwatchable to me.It's an interesting exercise to think about how well the modern day F1 drivers would do, given the same cars, against my two all-time favourites, Prost and Lauda or indeed against the other greats like Fangio and Jim Clark and Graeme Hill and Keke Rosberg and Senna and Mansell and Schumacher and many others. The modern drivers would adapt I'm sure but they'd effectively have to learn to drive again. F1 driving to them in the old cars would provide a very steep learning curve and acquisition of a whole new set of driving skills. The old F1 cars did very little to assist the driver, modern F1 cars do nothing else.I had been looking forward to seeing this movie for some time and, to me at least, it was a great disappointment. With the best will in the world and taking into account the few good elements, I couldn't give it more than 7 out of 10.Jmvscotland.

title: I started to falling love f1
content: I recently watched the F1 (2025) movie, and I must say, it left a strong impression on me. As someone who enjoys high-speed action and emotional storytelling, this film delivered on many levels. From the start to the finish, it was an intense ride, not just in terms of racing but also in terms of drama, ambition, and personal growth.The movie centers around the life of a young and talented Formula 1 driver who is trying to make his mark in the highly competitive world of motorsports. The story does a great job of balancing the technical side of racing with the emotional challenges that come with such a high-pressure lifestyle. We see the character struggling with self-doubt, rivalry, team dynamics, and personal sacrifices-all while trying to remain focused on winning.One of the most impressive aspects of F1 (2025) is the way it captures the speed, danger, and excitement of Formula 1 racing. The race scenes are incredibly well shot. You can feel the power of the engines, the sharp turns, the sudden crashes, and the tension on the track. The cinematography is top-notch, and the sound design is equally powerful. When the engines roar, you feel it in your chest. It felt like I was sitting right inside the car, racing at 300 km/h. The use of practical effects and minimal CGI added to the realism, which I really appreciated.But what made this film more than just a racing movie was its emotional depth. The main character, played brilliantly by Brad Pitt, is not just a talented driver-he's a human being with fears, dreams, and flaws. His journey through the film is full of challenges that test his mental strength and dedication. One of the most touching parts of the film was his relationship with his father, who also used to be a racer. Their bond, filled with both pride and conflict, added real emotion to the story and gave the character extra motivation to succeed.The supporting characters also played an important role. The team principal, engineers, and even his main rival all added depth to the story. They weren't just background characters-they had their own goals, emotions, and moments of development. I especially enjoyed the rival character, who wasn't a typical villain but rather a fierce competitor who pushed the main character to become better. Their rivalry was intense but respectful, which made it more realistic and meaningful.The pacing of the film was well-balanced. There was a good mix of action, personal drama, and slower, reflective scenes that allowed you to understand the characters more deeply. The film also touched on themes like perseverance, sacrifice, and the price of success. It shows how racing is not just a sport, but a way of life that demands total commitment.If I had to mention one small downside, I would say that some parts of the plot were predictable. As with many sports dramas, you kind of know how it's going to end. But even then, the journey was so emotionally engaging that it didn't matter much. The final race scene was thrilling, and I found myself holding my breath as it reached the climax.Overall, F1 (2025) is a powerful and exciting film that goes beyond just racing. It's a story about ambition, pressure, and human connection. Whether you're a fan of Formula 1 or not, this movie offers something for everyone-action, emotion, and inspiration. I highly recommend it to anyone who loves well-made films with strong characters and great energy. It's definitely one of the best sports dramas I've seen in recent years.

title: Review of F1: The Movie (2025)
content: F1: The Movie, directed by Joseph Kosinski and starring Brad Pitt, is a high-octane cinematic experience that brings the world of Formula 1 racing to the big screen. Pitt delivers a compelling performance as Sonny Hayes, a veteran driver making a comeback to mentor a young talent in a struggling team. The film boasts impressive racing sequences and stunning visuals, enhanced by Hans Zimmer's dynamic score and IMAX cinematography. While the plot follows familiar underdog tropes and may lack depth in character development, especially concerning female roles, it succeeds in delivering entertainment and excitement. The movie's success at the box office, grossing over $627 million globally, indicates its broad appeal. However, purists of the sport may find certain aspects unrealistic. Overall, F1: The Movie offers a thrilling ride for both racing enthusiasts and general audiences seeking an action-packed film.

title: One of the best racing films ever made.
content: This film is the definition of a summer blockbuster. From the action, to the cinematography, to the story, this film had it all.Brad Pitt and Damson Idris were both terrific as Sonny Hayes and Joshua Pearce. As were Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon as Ruben Cervantes and Kate McKenna. I love to see a fellow Irishwoman in a big scale blockbuster like this. It's another big achievement for Ireland from an acting point of view. And the fact that she played an Irish character was just the icing on the cake.Jospeh Kosinski really knows how to deliver summer blockbusters, first Top Gun Maverick and now this. Both films are very similar as well. They both have a similar filming style and cinematography, they both have similar stories (older veteran mentors younger rookie), and they both have very similar vibes and music/score. Nevertheless, I love the things that the two films have in common.The soundtrack was just superb. All the songs perfectly fit the vibe of the film. My favourite song was probably 'Lose My Mind' by Don Toliver featuring Doja Cat.The score was just incredible. Hans Zimmer has composed a masterpiece once again. The piece used in the beginning of the film and in the credits called 'F1' is by far my favourite, it hits like ecstasy. You can even hear the certain notes that can also be heard in the 'Time' piece from the Inception score. He also used a similar instrumental from 'Lose My Mind'. Just pure genius.Overall, this was an incredible film and a massively enjoyable and captivating watch. I would definitely recommend seeing it on the biggest screen possible. I had to see it on a normal screen as I'm on holiday right now and the cinema I went to didn't have an IMAX, but when I get back I'll definitely be seeing it again in IMAX. As a big F1 fan, this film means a lot to me."I'm offering you an open seat in Formula 1, the only place you can say, if you win, you are the absolute best... in the world."Ruben Cervantes.

title: Well produced, but not a racing film
content: F1The summer blockbuster of 2025, F1 has the production values, showcasing great cinematography with the racing sequences and international setting. But let's not mistake this for a racing movie. This movie is not about F1 racing. It's about passion, learning from mistakes while taking risks, and also discovering that you need not be alone to do so. Any setting would have done to tell the story and it would have turned out the same.I won't lie. I wasn't excited to see this because I'm not a fan of the sport. I'm happy that I'm not because I don't think that true fans would have appreciated it used as a prop, or as scenery. It just isn't believable and it is riddled with clichés.But I did like it. Watching the racing was quite exciting and nail biting, yes, but I really connected with the theme of getting knocked down, going on a self discovery and then making a come back. When Brad Pitt describes the zen moment of really flying on the track as he drives, I got chills because I know how to reach that moment in my own life. I know what it's like to lose that ability, and then come back. I know what it's like to believe I can do it myself, but I couldn't.Is this movie perfect? God, no. It was cringeworthy watching Kerry Condon's character become one dimensional. The whiplash treatment of APX by the board that owns them was so cartoonish in its villainy I thought that there would be mustache twirling. Plus, near the end, there's a subplot I won't spoil that was completely irrelevant, but appeared to be headed towards something. And finally, deus ex machina, pun somewhat intended, rears its ugly head.I still would recommend it, though. You'll find enough, like me, to connect with. But if you're a hardcore racing fan, I think you'll pick this apart. That's why I say again, this is not a movie about F1.

title: Perfect for a new F1 fan!
content: I am personally an F1 fanatic! Been watching the sport religiously since I was a small kid, well over 25 years. So I was cautiously optimistic about this film. Obviously saw the actors and crew integrate into the actual season when they shot it. Could they manage to make a coherent and faithful portrayal of what F1 is like.The answer is yes and no. However I do believe that this film does have something in it for everyone. Whether you are a hardcore fan of the sport or somebody who has never watched it, you'll enjoy this film. Although for very different reasons.Is this an honest portrayal of F1, nope. It is highly "hollywoodised" and I sort of expected that and can't really blame the film for that. Going in not expecting that would be stupid. Some of the story elements and character decisions come across really chessy and unlike what F1 is like normally. That also goes for the major narrative arc of the film, which is very unrealistic but charming in a "Hollywood" kinda way. Retired racer coming back.....give me a break, but Pitt does it with charm.The thing, as a hardcore fan, I got out of it was the action. More importantly the realism of that racing action adding seamlessly into the real sport. That was something that was even better than I thought it was going to look. Also a small detail but to someone like me goes a long way. All the tracks that were raced at during the film were shot and portrayed in order of the track. I know that's a small detail but it shows care and passion for what the creators are trying to accomplish. It could have been easier to cut all the shots out of sequence and made it look cooler. I'm very glad they didn't.Overall it's 70% out of 100. Brad Pitt is a star as is Damson Idris. Love seeing them in the cars shot for real. Edge of your seat action. Although a lot of the story isn't that realistic, you can still have a great time watching it. Awesome sport and racing film. Joseph Kosinski take a bow.

title: Awesome, Enjoyable but Safe, even Sterile.
content: This movie is basically a mash up of top gun maverick in terms of plot and days of thunder in terms of subject matter. It is very safe and pleasing. What you want to happen you can be sure will happen. It is well crafted in the technical sense - beautiful locations, impressive stunts and camerawork. I am sure f1 fans also enjoyed the real f1 pilots being in the movie. The acting is alright - not much of a place to shine in a movie like this. Hans Zimmer on the score does the job.But it is also very sterile. The story is very straightforward and unimpressive. It is just meant to press your buttons and make you feel good. Days of thunder did the exact same thing - but it had one thing this movie lacks - style - in terms of both sight and sound and there is a difference between flashy and stylish. And I am not saying the visuals in f1 are unimpressive. But days of thunder... Tony Scott manages to electrify that movie - yes the story is shallow - noone is contesting that - the story in both movies is equally uninteresting and serviceable. And that is fine - not every movie needs to be(or can be) groundbreaking and genre defining. But you cannot rehash the same thing every time and not add something in any department. The music in days of thunder is much better handled and integrated - it doesn't feel like background - it makes the film even more dynamic and energetic. Go watch the last race on youtube.Here it fades. The smoke in days of thunder is almost a plot device . It is a way for Scott to enhance visuals(who doesn't like some smoke) - it serves the story. This movie lacks any standout factor like this.Still f1 gets a lot of thing right - just nothing exceptional.

title: Very nice marketing tool
content: As such, F1 works perfectly. This movie is made for the Drive2Survive audience that wants a D2S XXL episode. And having said that, it delivers on all ends.But, just like the Netflix show, it does not even remotely capture the reality of Formula 1, nor does it cater to long-time fans, as many things just couldn't happen the way they do in the film. It's a loud, fast and colorful spectacle that fulfills all the criteria of a summer blockbuster. As such, it is wonderful. Great entertainment, you will have a good time. But don't expect a deep story, a defining twist nor solid character development - it is all sacrificed for the flashy and hyperinflated mass appeal that it is supposed to convey.The best Formula 1 film yet is definitely not F1: The Movie, it's undoubtedly Rush.

title: Adrenaline, Ego, and Redemption: F1 Races Beyond the Finish Line
content:
Did I expect a thoughtful character study when I sat down for a Joseph Kosinski-Brad Pitt racing movie?Absolutely not.But F1 delivers something few sports dramas manage: speed and soul.Yes, the action's electric. You feel every gear shift, every tire shred, every helmeted stare down the straight. But beneath the spectacle, this is a movie about aging, relevance, and the brutal beauty of second chances.Brad Pitt is Sonny Hayes, a legend with dust on his legacy. He's not chasing trophies-he's chasing peace, purpose, and maybe a final exhale that means something. At his side (and sometimes in his way): Damson Idris as Joshua Pearce, the hotshot rookie who thinks F1 is just a faster version of his sim rig. Their tension carries the film, not just on the track, but in locker rooms, garages, and long silences.And it works. Because F1 doesn't force drama. It lets it breathe.Kosinski directs like he did in Top Gun: Maverick-technology meets humanity. The racing shots are vicious and poetic. POV cams inside cockpits. Pit crews moving like dance troupes. IMAX screams.Hans Zimmer's score simmers and surges. One moment, it's heartbeat. The next, it's thunder.What surprised me most? The dialogue. Sharp, lived-in, and sometimes laugh-out-loud good."Hope is not a strategy.""What's wrong with the car?" - "The guy sitting in it.""Plan C is for Combat. Combat! Combat! Combat!"Yeah, some moments echo Ford v Ferrari-but in the best way. Think Senna meets Creed with a bit of Moneyball moodiness thrown in.Brad Pitt? Still magnetic. Damson Idris? One to watch. Kerry Condon? The glue. Javier Bardem? Underused, but every scene counts.This is not a film trying to be deep. But it ends up being exactly that by not pretending.Final lap:
If you love racing, you'll be thrilled.If you don't? You'll still find yourself leaning forward.Because F1 isn't about the car. It's about the driver.And sometimes, the thing we're really racing... is ourselves.

title: Hamilton academicals
content: Despite getting quite into a race whenever I happen across one, I can't say that I'm particularly a fan of Formula One - or indeed any form of racing. I did go and see this film though, lured in by a strong cast, skilled filmmakers and impressive visuals.With half a season of the F1 championships to go, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) needs a win, his team's first ever, or the terms of his contract mean that his board can replace him as owner/operator. In an act of desperation, he turns to Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) his former teammate, who left F1 following a crash that curtailed his promising career. Cervantes current driver is Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) a talented young driver, but one who quickly comes to resent Hayes' unearned position alongside him."F1" was, I thought, a generally positive piece of escapism. It is, in storyline terms anyway, the classic "sports movie" trope. Hayes is Rocky Balboa, not so much a 'has been', but more a 'might have been' given a last chance to prove something, utilising the practical skills that he's learned in decades of competition, on a higher level. The performances throughout are good, aside from those names in the above paragraph Kerry Condon is another of the films leads, as a technical director altering the cars to fit Hayes' dangerous style of driving and doing as much work on her drivers personality clashes. From a British perspective, the rest of the cast is a fun who's who of regular TV talent, with Samson Kayo, Callie Cooke, Will Merrick and Kim Bodnia all appearing - and a typically slimy turn from Tobias Menzies.I'd be fascinated to know what F1's governing body really feels about the film. As much as I would imagine they like the publicity, they have worked so hard on their safety record for this film to basically say that the trick to winning is to drive into your rivals off the course. I did appreciate that the film worked really hard to keep us on side with Pearce though, understanding his motivations and reservations with having Hayes has his teammate. I also think that the film really is quite something just to look at, really exciting and dynamically shot.Undeniably cool and fun, if perhaps a bit clichéd, I won't want to rewatch it very often, but I enjoyed "F1".

title: Definition of a Summer Blockbuster.
content: This is what a summer blockbuster should be.In a time when it feels that every movie being released is a remake, reboot, or sequel, "F1" emerges forward with something new. New, yet familiar. I saw this film two days in a row, and I grinned ear to ear the entire duration of both viewings.The story is easy to follow and fun to invest in. There is something here reminiscent of "Top Gun", which should come as no surprise since it's being directed by Joseph Kosinski, produced by Jerry Bruckheimer, and shot by Claudio Miranda. The plot outline follows a simple, yet strong formula, and takes the audience into the thrills of racing, rather than fighter jets. Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, an older, wiser, and superstitious racer that bounces from race to race, not for the money, but for the pure enjoyment of the sport. After a failed attempt 30 years prior to win in F1, Hayes is given a second opportunity, but it comes with having to work in a team, something he hasn't done in years. His goal is simple: help get his team to win a race so that it is not sold out from under his former racing partner turned owner, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem). He'll also have to mentor a rookie driver while simultaneously proving that he isn't a washed up has-been.The performances here are nothing short of phenomenal. Pitt brings his normal cool, but with age and more humility. Bardem steals every scene he is in, up-and-comer Damson Idris shows off serious chops with his layered performance, and Kerry Condon shows us that her character is more than a typical love interest.The production quality is perfect. The cinematography was stunning all the way through and the race sequences not only make you feel like you are there, but they will have you gripping the edge of your seat with intensity. The attention to detail and realism that has been inserted into every aspect of this film makes this spectacle grounded, but just as epic. A common complaint of blockbusters these days is the overuse of CGI - a complaint which this film will never receive. The production does a great job of also showing the difference between times, as the dialogue, music selection, and wardrobe highlight the two different eras associated with our characters.I also have to give praise for the film for its use of runtime. Many will see the 2 hr 35 min runtime and turn away. However, this film's pacing is excellent. At no point does it ever feel slow, and I never checked my watch during my viewing - either time I saw it for that matter. Every scene has its place and belongs in the narrative, contributing to a story that comes full circle and will have you cheering right along with the fans.You will feel everything in this movie - the thrills, the danger, the triumph, and the love for the sports of both Formula 1 and filmmaking. This movie is the reason we go to see movies on the big screen, and I wouldn't change a thing about it.

title: Brilliant film with high adrenaline rush movements!!
content: Movie Review: F1 screening in cinemas near u!!A must watch for all F1 Fans !!Story : A fictional story where Brad Pitt plays a retired Formula One driver, Sonny Hayes who returns to F1 racing after 3 decades at the behest of his dear friend Reuben ( Javier Bardem) to mentor, partner with a fiesty ,rookie teammate Joshua Pearce, played by Idris & revive Reuben's ailing Grand Prix team - Apex Grand.One word review for this movie would be "brilliant"👍😊👌. High octane adrenaline rush movements galore with slick editing cinematography will keep u hooked from start to finish . So fast that u can't even look away for a moment in fear that u might miss something. Interval nahi hota to "su-su" break bhi lena mushkil ho jaata , it is that fast !!Brad Pitt , the guy is absolutely brilliant, absolutely droolworthy ( for the ladies) even at 61 . His portrayal of the brash , arrogant yet brilliant and empathetic Sonny Hayes is nothing short of brilliance .. love the guy. Pitt's emotional scenes with his dear friend Bardem , his verbal sparring with his team mate and humourous banter with the head of the design team Kerry Condon are relatable and touch ur core!!This film is not just about the races but the drama , excitement and tense moments happening behind the scenes too with the support teams doing their best to keep the drivers alive in the race.How Sonny & Joshua belonging to two different eras sort out their differences, work together in the end and snatch a final victory that is nothing short of a miracle is what this film is all about. The film is also about the unconventional approach adopted by Sonny , sometimes unethical,yet within the boundary of the F1 rules to help his team stay afloat and survive a turbulent season has also been well thought of!! Sheer brilliance 👍 🙂F1's theme song, composed by the legendary Hans Zimmer is truly awesome and worth a mention !!Watching this film is like watching an entire F1 season with its nail biting movements all packed in its 2 hr 36 mins runtime!!Watch it in theatres and i can assure u that u will come out exhilarated with the adrenaline rush lasting a long time after exiting the theatre.Going 5/5 for this one ... title: Like an early 2000 blockbuster
content: Typical Jerry Bruckheimer production and when you see his name in the opening credits you know what movie you're going to watch.It's quite predictable, shallow, stereotyped, the characters are cocky, the jokes tongue-in-cheek... and that's all fine.It's a well-done family blockbuster with decent acting, excellent special effects, an obsessing Hans Zimmer soundtrack and a proper narrative arc. After the endless orgy of reboots, spin-offs, sequels, etc, of the last 20 years, it's almost refreshing to see a movie like that again. One that puts the big bucks on pure entertainment, while being its own thing.

title: Action Packed Advert for F1
content: F1 - The Movie is a complete throwback to Jerry Bruckheimer produced films from the late 80s and early 90s. It was a smart decision too, as was the casting of Brad Pitt at his peak coolest, think Redford in The Sting meets McQueen in Le Mans. It's so corny at times but in a totally good way following in the footsteps of Maverick. This could actually have starred Tom Cruise although it would have lost a great deal of charm I suspect. The action is well paced, and we have a coherent(ish) story told against the backdrop of race day at worldwide F1 circuits. It's not bad at all, the very definition of a blockbuster.Ultimately it's an advert for the sport and some of the sponsorship was in your face, suspect Geico paid a pretty penny towards the movie making costs. I've taken a mark off for excessive virtue signalling which alas is all too common now. F1 may want us to believe this inclusion but the truth tells us otherwise, that said it certainly didn't ruin things due to the lack of depth of some of those characters. Hope they don't make another though, quit whilst you're ahead!

title: Awesome
content: This movie set out to be the "Top gun - Maverick" of formula 1 driving, and it has definitely achieved that. All 3 of us that went in loved the movie, and that very rarely happens.The film sees Brad Pitt as the "Rocky Balboa" of racing. Convinced to give it all another shot. With his teams youngest and most promising member Damson Idris becoming his rival, or perhaps prodigy. It's a straightforward plot but really not as predictable as you'd think.Infact the well captured racing scenes are edge of your seat. As you don't always know where they're going, and the fact that they're genuine and done for real gives the audience (still bored of CGI) what they want. As the footage captured really captures the speed and adrenaline of the cars, and the racing.It's not like there's only a couple of races either. The movies action packed, and has plenty of comedy in there too. Mostly coming from Brad Pitts coolness. Overall its a feel good 90's feeling movie which remimds modern film makers how its done.

Perhaps an F2 movie focusing on JP could be the next installment.

title: Solid film; you don't need to be a racing fan to enjoy it
content: I left the theater about an hour ago. Overall, I found F1 to be a solid film.I think the movie has 2 big upsides. The first is that Brad Pitt is just an awesome lead protagonist, as always. Some critics have claimed that he simply plays himself and just looks / acts cool, with some "I-can-do-nothing-wrong" vibes. I didn't get that sense at all when I watched the film. He clearly plays a formerly-great racer who's down on his luck. In his performance, he conveys coolness and charm, but there's also weariness and disappointment too. He definitely delivers here. It's also so impressive that he can look so great in his 60sThe other big upside is that the film nails the racing scenes. The sound is well-done, the cars come off like magestic rockships at times, and the races correctly appear intense and skillful. The racetracks, uniforms, and venues all look aesthetic and credible. The racing scenes are definitely made for the theaters. And as someone with no knowledge about professional car-racing (I've never watched F1, Nascar, or any of their ilk) - I'm glad that the movie basically explains what's happening during the races. While the characters are racing, for example, there's mention about car-racing maneuvers (i.e. "should I box them in") or techical details (e.g. Soft tires vs hard tires). During those parts, the event broadcasters and/or various characters will walk us through it with concise dialogue. You don't need to be a fan of professional car-racing at all to enjoy the racing scenes in this movieIn addition, I like Javier Bardem's character: his friendship with Pitt's character is touching at times. Damson Idris is OK.The movie isn't a 10/10 though -- the big issue is that the film is a tad bloated. The 2-hour 35-minute runtime wasn't necessary to tell the story they wanted to tell. There are 2 subplots that didn't work out so well. The first one is the subplot involving Javier Bardem's company, which involves Board of Directors and such. The film didn't need this subplot to create high stakes. The other weak subplot is the romance between Pitt and Kerry Condon, who plays a car-designer. Pitt and Condon didn't have chemistry, and the movie would have lost little in plot and emotional depth if their romance wasn't there at all. The feeling is that the movie jammed Pitt and Condon's romance just to check a box8.0/10.

title: Wish side quests were expanded
content: To see the races in Europe and the European cast was interesting. And the team behind the car racing was very interesting. It was cool to see such an advanced technology for just a little car race. And it was cool to see them talk shop and also to see their very advanced and high-tech laboratory or garage or whatever they're calling it.It was kind of cool to see the teammates and their families and friends and just like the whole dynamic of socialization between them.The racing was cool. It was a lot of racing though. And I'm the kind of person that likes watching Dance, figure skating, gymnastics. So. Anyway.I think the movie was trying too hard and that's why I don't rate it higher. It was trying to capture the magic of a movie that's trying very hard. But it tried too hard so no magic was captured.Let me explain. Conflict. They created tension and conflict between cast members. But it seemed a little bit for show to make the cinema more engaging or interesting. And it would have been fun or just to see them get along and like collaborate and think together instead.There is too much weird racing drama that seemed too dramatic and unrealistic. And I know they're trying to make racing more interesting for the audience. But it comes off as unrealistic and silly. And it's a little too try hard. I know they're trying to entertain me and everything but they need to calm down and just make a realistic script. I know, I know. Movies aren't supposed to be realistic and that's why they're there. But it was too exhausting. Instead of entertaining.Then finally there's too much of an underdog thing in this movie. And I know, that tell you get an audience hooked. He would have the underdog trope. But it was like rushed and again mixed with the silly racing plot it was just ludicrous.Can't we just have a normal movie with normal people and them collaborating as a team and just getting to know technical stuff and think together and it would have been more interesting?Instead it's this try hard underdog, pseudo conflict, and too much unrealistic racing drama.It's like they're trying to take elements from other movies that made them very successful and engaging and throw those elements at this one thinking it will work. They should have worked with what they had instead. The technology. The charisma. The European exoticism. The charisma of Brad and then his European teammate. Just Go with It. Don't Force It. title: Cars 3 Live-Action Adaptation
content:
This movie is a mixed bag of good and bad elements that overall create a decently enjoyable movie-going experience.The movie itself is technically impressive. Considering that every racing sequence is essentially just shots of cars on a track, they do a lot to keep you engaged with the races themselves. They also do a decent job at adding both of our deuteragonists' emotions and personalities into how they race. Brad Pitt's character obviously steals the show and he's the main reason this movie works but I also really appreciated how they treated Damson Idris' character.The real issue is that the story is essentially a paint by numbers plotline. I'm not kidding when I say this is literally just a live-action adaptation of Cars 3, because in a lot of ways they have the same story. After the opening sequence at Daytona I knew exactly how this movie was going to end. Nothing was surprising, everything was predictable. It really felt like the writers were actively trying to fit every cliche and trope that they could into this movie. I honestly feel like they should've cut out the forced romance sub-plot and the surprise twist villain and this would be a stronger and more concise film because of it. A racing movie doesn't need to be 2.5 hours long.That being said, the acting and directing are strong enough to stand on their own and I think that the technical aspects of the movie turn a dull story into an enjoyable viewing experience.Story: 5/10
Acting: 7.5/10
Cinematography: 8/10
Score: 7/10
Overall: 6.8/10.

title: Fast, Loud, and Thrilling
content: "F1: The Movie" is a high-octane ride that captures the intensity, drama, and spectacle of Formula 1 racing. From the roar of the engines to the razor-sharp precision of every turn, the film succeeds in delivering the adrenaline rush fans crave, while still being accessible to those who know little about the sport.The racing sequences are the clear highlight-shot with energy and clarity, they make you feel as if you're right on the track. The sound design is equally immersive, bringing every gear shift and tire screech to life. Performances are strong across the board, with the cast selling both the thrill of competition and the personal stakes behind it.The story doesn't reinvent the wheel, but the sheer spectacle, combined with enough heart and passion, makes "F1: The Movie" an exciting watch. It's fast, fun, and definitely worth the ticket for anyone seeking an adrenaline-fueled cinematic experience.

title: Good modern F1 film
content: Well, there's finally a film with modern F1 at the time of 2023, so also a feature film. So is it good? Definitely yes. So much so that it can be recommended to everyone? Maybe yes, maybe no, and here's why.Let's start with the good stuff, the F1 atmosphere is well conveyed. You can see from the beginning of the film that it's done well. The racing is done great. There's plenty of action. The sound is excellent. Music of Hanz Zimmer as usual to the place. The story in general is interesting, how the drama develops and so on. Everything looks quite dynamic. The comedy is amusing by the way!However, the film and the story itself is generally more suited to the format of a "good" film, that is, it is designed to be enjoyed for a single viewing. There's nothing supernatural to recommend it for. The antagonist is kind of weird. I didn't like the music from the other performers much, as they cheapen the film, though there should have been something inserted in the editing spots. There are some not the most interesting dramatic dialogues in places.It's a great feature film about F1 that will entertain, but it makes amateurish mistakes that diminish the film's rating. The film has various believable moments, but it also has the opposite in the service of spectacle. A realistic rating for the film would be somewhere around 7.5 in my opinion. Something on the level I would and wouldn't recommend, depending on what racing and F1 is to you. I think I'll rewatch the film after some time at home to more objectively and accurately say whether the film pulls closer to an 8, or closer to a 7 in terms of rating.

title: Speed, style, and a touch of soul
content: After Top Gun: Maverick, Joseph Kosinski trades fighter jets for Formula 1 cars in a film that's as sleek as it is high-octane. Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a retired driver returning to the track not to win, but to mentor, and maybe to rediscover something of himself. What unfolds is a visually arresting ride, full of adrenaline, camaraderie, and quiet transformation.Kosinski captures motion with precision, and every turn, close-up, and engine roar pulses with energy. Claudio Miranda's kinetic cinematography and Hans Zimmer's pulse-driving score elevate the experience, bringing the audience so close to the action you can almost feel the engines in your chest. It's immersive without being overwhelming, and the film balances tension with just enough humor to keep things buoyant. For seasoned fans, clever nods to real teams and drivers add an extra layer of delight, sharp enough to spark a knowing grin.Pitt leans into the calm, charismatic persona he's carried since Inglourious Basterds. It's a performance that glides, but rarely grips. His chemistry with Kerry Condon, as technical director Kate McKenna, adds warmth and texture. Their scenes are grounded and occasionally dry-witted, offering a welcome counterpoint to the noise.But it's Damson Idris who quietly steals the show. As rookie driver Joshua Pearce, he delivers the film's most sincere and emotionally resonant performance. With understated conviction, Idris charts a believable arc from raw ambition to quiet maturity. His presence gives the film its emotional anchor, even when the script doesn't fully capitalize on it.Some storylines feel underdeveloped, particularly the dynamic between Hayes and team principal Ruben. Javier Bardem brings gravitas, but the role never quite lands - a missed opportunity, given the character's potential. Had the writers focused less on the boardroom subplot and more on the tension between Hayes and Ruben, the film might have shifted into a deeper emotional gear.F1 isn't a character study, but a stylish action film with heart. Still, beneath the spectacle, there is a modest thematic thread. Sonny's journey is not just about racing, but about relinquishing control and learning to trust. His transformation from lone wolf to collaborator suggests that real progress only comes when we open ourselves to others. It's not as emotionally layered as Ford v Ferrari or as psychologically charged as Rush, but within its own framework, F1 knows exactly what it wants to be and executes with confidence. Not everything works, but what works, works hard.It may not reinvent the genre, but it knows exactly how to own its lane.

title: A Cinematic Masterpiece That Speaks to the Soul
content:
Let me start by saying that *F1: The Movie* gave me one of the most profound and exhilarating cinematic experiences I've had in years. As someone who often navigates life as a "middle man" and a lone wolf, this film spoke directly to me on multiple levels-delivering not only riveting storytelling but also a deeply meaningful reflection on life.**Storytelling at Its Finest**
This movie masterfully intertwines action-packed racing scenes with a heartfelt narrative, showcasing the art of storytelling in a way that is both entertaining and profound. The journey of Sonny Hayes (played brilliantly by Brad Pitt) as a retired driver coming back to the track to mentor a younger talent isn't just about racing-it's about redemption, purpose, and human connection. The script draws from the raw emotions of the characters, reminding the viewer that life's most meaningful stories aren't about the finish line but about the road we take to get there.**The Meaning of Life**
At the movie's core, it reminded me that life is a race we navigate in our own unique ways. Sonny Hayes, stepping out of retirement, reflects the idea that it's never too late to find meaning and re-align our purpose, even when we've felt lost. The dynamic between Sonny and Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) beautifully represents mentorship, second chances, and the importance of leaving a legacy-not just in a professional sense, but in human relationships. The film also subtly explores themes of perseverance, embracing change, and finding strength in vulnerability, which hit me deeply as someone who often feels like a lone traveler navigating life.**A Visceral Movie Experience**
Let's talk about the cinematic artistry. The sound design, especially when experienced in IMAX, had me gripping my seat-the roar of the engines, the tension on the track, the musical score swelling with emotion. Every race scene was a feast for the senses. Yet, what made this movie stand out was its emotional depth-balancing the adrenaline-pumping action with moments of quiet reflection. Joseph Kosinski's direction and Claudio Miranda's cinematography are absolute perfection. Each frame felt crafted with love and precision, pulling me deeper into this high-octane world while keeping me emotionally grounded.Ultimately, this isn't just a movie about Formula 1-it's a meditation on life, purpose, and finding connection in a fast-paced, often isolating world. It's a movie that made me feel that being a lone wolf doesn't mean being alone forever; sometimes, it's about finding the right team, even if it's just one person who believes in you.For anyone who loves cinematic storytelling, breathtaking visuals, and a story that uplifts as much as it entertains, *F1: The Movie* is an absolute must-watch. This is more than just a film-it's an experience that stays with you long after the credits roll.10/10. A pure adrenaline shot to the heart and soul.

title: A Masterclass in Speed and Story
content: From its very first roar, Formula One pulls you into a world where speed is only half the story. Brad Pitt anchors the film with grit and heart, leading an ensemble of characters-each given a fully fleshed backstory that brings unexpected depth to every lap. What could have been a straight-ahead racing flick instead breathes with vivid humanity: you feel the weight of each driver's dreams, the sting of near-misses, and the fierce camaraderie that binds them.What truly dazzles is the movie's perfect balance of drama and pure, unfiltered thrill. The on-track action is visceral and breathtaking, but the emotional stakes keep you tethered to every twist and turn. I walked in as someone who "didn't get" Formula One-and walked out a believer, heart pounding long after the checkered flag. Hands down, this is the best film I've seen in 2025.

title: Good entertaining, but not more
content:
Definitely worth viewing, although under different mindsets, if you are already a F1 fan or not.For these last ones, I guess it will be highly entertaining, while may be a bit too long. The plot is simple but enjoyable, very good acting by Pitt, Bardem, Condon and most of the remaining cast (Idris...not that really), excellent music and awesome footage. For short, a good movie about motorsports and a balanced glimpse over the technical complexity and business environment that run on the back office of F1.Do I think it will draw a lot of extra fans to F1? Not that sure... I think Drive to Survive is doing a much better job. This said, go and see it, it will be more than worth your while.Now to F1 fans. Several details that are impossible (the first and, probably, the more cringing: no one having driven a F1 in the 1990's will be able to get to speed in a modern F1 just like that; Pitt would stall in 2 meters when trying to get out of the garage). Anyway, forget about those and just enjoy the film, although what I was expecting was not in it (although the use of the McLaren Tech Center for APXGP was surpising and welcom).Footing may be fantastic for newcomers but, for F1 fans and, specially, those who see races through the F1TV platform, have already seen it and more - for instance, 360º views and on-helmet driver's view. I was somewhat disappointed and surprised, as Lewis Hamilton is a producer.Top Gun Maverick, in this respect, is much more impressive, although there is no TopGunTV to compare to...
To close, my list of motor racing films:
1. Le Mans 66 - mostly accurate, true story and fantastic film in every aspect
2. Grand Prix - innovative, good plot and fantastic footage, especially for that time
3. Rush - too much CGI, but the (also true) story and the acting makes for it
4. F1 - as said
5. Le Mans - if seen as a documentary.

title: Racing drama is a fun--if formulaic--ride
content: As a casual viewer of Formula 1 racing but a big fan of director Joseph Kosinski's last film "Top Gun: Maverick," I was looking forward to this high-octane film set in the world of Formula 1 racing. While not perfect, I'm happy to say that it's a solid and entertaining summer movie.The film centers around Sonny (Pitt,) an F1 driver whose career was sidelined by a bad crash in the 90s. He is convinced to return to racing and help turn around a struggling Formula One team. The film's racing scenes are well-shot and thrilling, and are pretty engaging and entertaining to see in a premium large format. The performances are mostly fine but nothing super special (although Javier Bardem is fantastic as always in a supporting role.) At 2 hours and 35 minutes, the film feels well-paced and never overlong or boring. What holds the film back from being great, though, is the generic nature of the story. Without giving anything away, this is pretty much a formulaic "sports underdog story" and I think you can pretty much guess the story beats and plot points. For that reason, I do not think this movie is a must-see for those who are not F1 fans. But it is an entertaining and feel-good summer movie that provides solid enough thrills for what it is. 7/10Note: I saw the movie in Dolby Cinema. The picture and sound quality were excellent, and the Dolby Atmos sound was immersive and impactful during the racing sequences.

title: The passion of racing
content: This movie is an homage to racing and to racers with a passion for speed and strategy in the world of Formula 1.Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a former Formula 1 driver from the 1990s, is convinced to return to racing after decades away to help a struggling team. He joins a team with an uncompetitive car, a determined female technical director, Kate McKenna (Kerry Condon), and his teammate, the hot-headed rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris).Over the final races of the season, the team grows closer, rivalry and suspicion give way to trust, and true teamwork emerges.This movie is a masterpiece-an IMAX-made racing film that kept me hooked from start to finish. The soundtrack and score by Hans Zimmer is phenomenal.A must-see for Formula 1 and racing fans -maybe this was the spark I needed to start following F1 more closely again.

title: Stunning on the Track, Stalled in the Script
content:
Cinematography: 5/5
Visually, F1 is a masterpiece. From the glint of sun off carbon fiber to the slow-motion tire changes mid-pit stop, the cinematography captures the raw speed and tension of Formula 1 like never before. Sweeping aerial shots of iconic circuits-Silverstone, Suzuka, Spa-are breathtaking, and the in-cockpit sequences are thrillingly immersive. Every frame is polished, dynamic, and electric. It is clear the filmmakers spared no effort in making sure the film looked like the ultimate racing experience.Story: 1/5
Unfortunately, the narrative does not keep up with the pace of the visuals. The plot is formulaic (no pun intended): a predictable underdog arc, thin character development, and dialogue that often feels like it was written with the engine running. The emotional beats do not land, and key rivalries lack depth. What could have been a gripping human story behind the visor ends up feeling hollow and cliché.Verdict:
F1 is a triumph of visuals and technical brilliance, but it stalls in the storytelling department. For fans of the sport, it is worth watching for the spectacle alone, but those looking for a compelling narrative may be left in the gravel trap.

title: Fast, Loud, and Pretty Good - But Nothing Legendary.
content: I'm not an F1 aficionado, so I went in knowing very little about the sport-and that actually worked in the movie's favor. The plot is refreshingly simple: don't come for a complex story, come for the visuals. F1 delivers on that promise, making you feel like you're strapped into a real Grand Prix cockpit. Unfortunately, missing out on an IMAX screening dulled some of that immersive punch.Brad Pitt gives his usual charismatic turn, but the romance subplot with the female lead feels tacked on and unnecessary. At times I yearned for the emotional heft of Ford v Ferrari or Rush, which balance their racing thrills with stronger character arcs. Here, the film leans so heavily on race footage that it almost feels like an extended documentary rather than a full-fledged drama.That said, if you simply want to experience F1 at breakneck speed-hairpin turns, roaring engines, and all-F1 delivers. As someone who barely knows the sport, I found myself genuinely caught up in the action, even if I couldn't tell you the difference between a DRS zone and a podium ceremony.

title: "You're just worried I'm quicker than you"
content: Vrooooom!!! All speed, flash and brazen attitude, "F1" is another race car flick destined to get your motor running in the flashing blaze of wind sprinting acceleration and incendiary fatal crashes. Led by the all star duo of Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem, the movie hurtles at breakneck motion to the finish line.Pitt is Sonny Hayes, a forcibly retired Formula One star who saw his racing dreams dashed into flames following a career-ending accident. Further challenges reduces the once promising light to working class purgatory until one day a former racing associate and by then racing mogul Ruben Cervantes (Bardem) invites him out of retirement to coach a rising hotshot in the auto racing scene and participate himself. Hesitant and reluctant at first Hayes agrees and what follows is a trope-ridden feel good extravaganza undiluted in Hollywood popcorn and soda style. Snarky dialogues, cool cars, an erratic soundtrack (this film quite painfully and awkwardly shows how Led Zeppelin, Queen and auto racing don't mix) and the customary whirlwind cinematography of angles, shots and breathtaking panoramas of the auto racing world where sleek and nimble speed-fuelled machines careen, jostle, maneuver and explode all in service and worship at the altar of that one strip line at the end of those long and winding roads.Of course, you can't have a big budget flick without Mr. Pc selling his wares throughout (Oh, the boxes ticked!). Running at a two and a half hour length this blazing behemoth is far too epic for a F1 soap and my number of nods and snores as the finish line was in the horizon attests to this. And as much as I love seeing the charming Kerry Condon onscreen she fit into this macho testosterone-driven fest like a 400 pound unfortunate in a Red Bull RB19.One of the highest-grossing films of the year and deservedly so, "F1" satisfies a universal need for the adrenaline rush and high of speed and the liberation and freedom it brings. Throw in the driving lure of competition and the energy swell of a stadium packed with screaming and adoring thousands primal human needs are heightened and satisfied. So ready the snacks as you hop into the driver's seat and enter a world of fast cars and cool cats as that accelerator pedal pushes further into blazing glory!

title: A great summer blockbuster.
content: F1 is a total blast from beginning to end with stunning state-of-the-art technicality and racing scenes. Brad Pitt gives a movie star, surprisingly emotionally stable performance as Sonny Hayes, and he delivers on every level from delivery, charisma, chemistry with others, and even soft vulnerability. Kerry Condon, Damson Idris, and Javier Bardem give worthy supporting performances that match Pitt in every way and they all have fantastic chemistry with him. From Hans Zimmer's expectedly great score to the gripping and masterful editing (you don't feel the length at all) to the stunning cinematography to the layered and complex sound design, the technicals in this film may be the best of the year so far. They're so complex and well put together that you find your mouth gaping open at every shot. And the plot surprisingly has some dense emotional weight to it and actual character analysis; they're not super complicated, but they have decent layers. Yes, it's super predictable and formulaic, but who gives a crap if you're having this much fun in a movie theater? You're never not engaged and it's very consistent with everything it utilizes. This is a great piece of work. One of the best of the year.

title: inspire! ...almost
content: Definitely one of the big picture highlights of the year and a box office success to boot. Hats off for the courage to tackle this niche topic; the cashback wasn't necessarily to be expected. Cars? I don't care! Formula 1? I don't care! 'F1'? I was thrilled! At times, the middle section is a bit slow. So it's an underdog story, a modern cowboy who goes from bad to worse and finally achieves great success. True, it's formulaic, every cliché is used, but ultimately that's exactly the stuff we love. The theme of sport is perfect for this, but the story has to be skilfully tailored to mass taste rather than niche sports enthusiasts. So far, so good. Unfortunately, the film also has its weaknesses, as it seems to have been determined to be modern in terms of image and sound and not to remind us of its predecessors from the 70s and 80s. This is also successful, but even further removed from reality, and that's saying something. The choice of co-star is no less unrealistic, but in keeping with the spirit of the times, or rather to avoid any controversy. In this context, the soundtrack is disastrous, presumably 'the other guy's' playlist, to make the stranglehold of production constraints even more obvious. This time it's not successful, but it's still a Brad Pitt film, so he'll surely pull it off. Or rather, ride it off, as I aptly said earlier, 'Cowboy'. Well, without that, no cinema success would have been possible here either; the audience decides, and it doesn't seem to have been lost yet.

title: This is a Driven Remake with Stallone
content: I was really looking forward to a top-tier motorsport film, comparable to Ford v Ferrari or Rush, where the drama, racing, and pacing combine into a true epic about speed and rivalry. Instead, F1 (2025) turned out to be almost an exact remake of Driven (2001) with Stallone - the same clichés, predictable dialogue, and questionable characters. Unfortunately, in terms of quality, the film deserves about as many Razzies as its predecessor.What is particularly disappointing is that the Red Bull team was almost completely ignored in the film. Apparently, this was related to Mercedes' involvement in building the race cars for the shoot. According to insiders, the producers spent several years convincing Red Bull that the team would not be portrayed as the "villain," and to avoid potential conflicts of interest, they decided to leave the team out of the storyline. The cars used were based on Formula 2 chassis with Mercedes-AMG logos, which obviously tilted the film slightly in favor of Mercedes.

title: Traditional Filmmaking is back!
content: Honestly, I felt like I should've watched this in theaters. The racing is intense and this has some brilliant direction, cinematography, and sound. I mean, I get why I didn't. I'm not the biggest racing fan and I know absolutely zero about F1. But even with that, I went into this movie with an open mind today. I heard the good reviews and I wanted a pallet cleanser from War of the Worlds. I'll say this, most of the negative/mixed reviews I've seen are from people that think this movie is formulaic. And I'd agree with them. But here's the thing. This film wasn't made to reinvent the sports/racing genre. It wasn't made to add something new or deconstruct. It was meant to entertain and be feel good. And that's exactly what it did and it executed well. I don't understand everyone's obsession with wanting everything to be like Schindler's List. Not everything aims or attempts to be that and certainty not this movie, which is relatively paint by the numbers. I will say this, I was debating whether I should rate this a 7.5 or an 8 but that last act pushed me to give it an 8. I'm not the biggest fan of the second act. It was getting boring and a lot of melodrama just wasn't interesting. I only cared about Damson and Brad's characters as well, but their beef was getting a little repetitive. That's probably because of the absurdly long runtime. It felt like the second act dragged a little. Definitely the worst act of the film. You could easily make this 2 hours or less and it'd have similar results.Other than that, I really liked Sonny's and Joshua's dynamic, especially at the end of the film. I didn't really care for any other character but whatever. The action is incredible and shot really well. The editing and sound design is so damn good and sleek. Everything looks and feels nice. This truly feels like a film from traditional hollywood from 20 years ago.I honestly loved this movie toward the ending.Overall: 8/10.

title: best movie 2025
content: For me, F1 is easily the best movie of 2025. Even though I'm not a hardcore Formula 1 fan, I could still feel the passion, intensity, and energy of the sport throughout the film. The racing sequences were thrilling and immersive, truly capturing the speed and adrenaline that F1 is all about. By the time it reached the ending, I was genuinely moved - it left an emotional impact that I wasn't expecting.One of the strongest aspects of this film is how authentic it feels to its title. It doesn't try to overcomplicate things; it delivers raw, high-octane racing moments that put you right on the track.However, if there's one criticism I'd make, it's the inclusion of the romance subplot. It felt unnecessary and a little unrealistic, adding nothing important to the overall story. The film was already powerful without it, and I believe focusing solely on the racing and emotional journey would have made it even stronger.Overall, F1 is a must-watch, not just for fans of the sport, but for anyone who loves an inspiring and heart-pounding cinematic experience.

title: Feel of Cinema
content: Sonny joins the struggling APXGP team, led by his old friend and former teammate Ruben Cervantes. The team's survival depends on him mentoring hotshot rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), a talented but headstrong driver hungry for glory. Their dynamic is the film's beating heart-friendship wrapped in rivalry, mutual respect forged in the heat of competition. The chemistry is sharp, and the writing never sugarcoats their tension.Director Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) brings the same adrenaline-fueled, big-screen craftsmanship to racing. The track sequences are shot with real Formula 1 cars, captured in IMAX, making every gear shift and tire squeal feel bone-deep. You don't watch the races-you experience them. Hans Zimmer's score elevates every lap, blending orchestral swells with pounding, engine-like rhythms that make your chest vibrate. This is peak 2025 blockbuster sound design.The supporting cast adds real weight. Kerry Condon as Kate McKenna, APXGP's technical director, grounds the film with intelligence and quiet authority. Her dynamic with Pitt carries a mature, unforced intimacy-refreshing for a sports drama. Javier Bardem's turn as Ruben adds charm and gravitas, with moments that remind you this isn't just a film about winning, but about legacy.F1 isn't just a racing movie-it's a love letter to the sport. Brad Pitt's aura, Damson Idris's fire, Zimmer's thunderous score, and Kosinski's precision make this the peak of 2025 big-screen entertainment. You leave the theater feeling like you've been in the driver's seat, and honestly? You won't want to get out.

title: Admirable and enjoyable... unless a zealot.
content: Update: 2nd watch and I pretty much stand by my comments below.F1: The Movie a technical achievement and is a very hard film to dislike, unless, of course, you are the most zealous of Formula 1 fans. If that is you, I have one simple reminder for you; this is a movie. Races are not movies and movies are not races. Nor is it a documentary. And like racing itself, movies have rules.What you have is a technically excellent movie, with a proven movie star, given access to the keys of fast machines most of us will never experience. F1:The Movie is very much Top Gun: Maverick with wheels. And like TG:M, everything is on the screen and in your ears, sometimes at the expense of an over complex plot, which is probably for the best.It is hard to imagine two movies that have better been able to put on screen the experience of moving so fast in such precision machines. Which is great.As for the racing? Just like TG:M, F1 has to rely on the tropes of its genre for dramatic effect, and as such the details are not quite so precise as the camerawork. Formula 1 is a precision sport, and as such the driving of star protagonist would in reality be more likely to alienate spectators than is portrayed here. But hey, how many times in a Rocky movie does Balboa touch his gloves to the canvas and not get a mandatory count? Call it poetic licence for dramatic effect.So, perhaps the racing etiquette and the overall drama of the season could be more accurate, but it might not then be quite so cinematic. But so far as being placed inside a racing car at full chat down the straights with the rear wing open, this movie is unsurpassed.Is it the best racing movie? That's a hard one. Both Rush and Ford V Ferrari are better films, but the racing in both does not compare. Both of these films are better written and acted, but are not even in the same lap - or even at the same circuit - in terms of placing the viewer behind the wheel.So, F1: The Movie is what it is. Just like the sport itself it is a technical achievement at the very pinnacle of its game, but that said, perhaps the best forms of racing for the media lies elsewhere. The overwhelming technical aspects of F1 inevitably detract from the potential for a more complex plot or the time needed for character depth and story arcs.But it is what it is, and it is the best of what it could be for a movie which in effect was produced by its subject matter.Absolutely worth watching, and not just for the racing, and though it skirts the with the track limits of self indulgence at times, the star power of Brad Pitt keeps at least one wheel on the track at all times.

title: F1: the movie
content: The film opens with striking visuals and a brisk pace, pulling the audience straight into its core. From the very beginning, it is clear that the story has something to say. Sonny Hayes, portrayed by Brad Pitt, is introduced seamlessly in the opening shots, and Pitt's performance allows us to immediately understand the character's temperament and spirit. Pitt remains one of my favorite actors, and it is impressive to see how he much like Tom Cruise chooses to remain at the top of his craft well into his seventies, while many of their peers have settled for less ambitious projects.The editing deserves special praise, elevating the film's rhythm and style and adding significantly to its appeal. Still, the predictability of the story held it back for me. From early on, I knew how the narrative would unfold, and never felt true suspense regarding the protagonist's fate. While this doesn't make the film weak, it does keep it from feeling particularly fresh or innovative.It is, however, undeniably entertaining. Time passes quickly while watching, and the audience will likely leave satisfied thanks to the strong work from everyone involved. For me, though, it doesn't make the cut for my list of 2025 favorites though I would still recommend it to others.

title: Final Verdict: 10/10 - Absolutely incredible, unforgettable, and truly extraordinary.
content: This film is nothing short of a masterpiece, and it absolutely deserves a perfect 10/10 rating. From the very first frame, it pulls you into its world with breathtaking cinematography that feels larger than life yet deeply intimate. Every shot is crafted with such care and precision that it almost feels like a moving painting, each frame telling its own story. The use of lighting, colors, and camera movement creates an atmosphere that doesn't just show you the narrative-it makes you feel it.The story itself is powerful, emotional, and layered. What makes it even more remarkable is how seamlessly it balances heart, drama, and depth while never losing momentum. The pacing keeps you hooked from beginning to end, with no dull moments. Each scene feels purposeful, building toward a conclusion that leaves a lasting impression.The performances are stellar across the board. Every actor embodies their role so naturally that you forget you're watching a performance-it feels real, raw, and alive. The chemistry between the characters brings an authenticity that makes the emotional beats hit even harder.What truly elevates this film, however, is the visionary direction and cinematography. Wide sweeping landscapes, intimate close-ups, and perfectly timed transitions give the movie a sense of grandeur while keeping it deeply personal. The sound design and background score enhance this visual beauty, immersing the audience into the story's world even further.In short, this film is not just entertainment-it's an experience. It is the kind of movie that stays with you long after the credits roll, leaving you with both awe and reflection. A cinematic gem that reminds us why we love movies in the first place.

content: I know nothing about Formula 1 nor any other racing. Even though he's got all the leading man charm and looks I never though he was all that good of a lead actor, BUT I've always felt that Brad Pitt's wheelhouse was playing weirdo supporting characters. Great camera and a bizarre but fun score from Hans Zimmer.If you can see it in a giant theatre with great sound, that's the way to do it.I have no idea how or why Apple thinks is going to work on their stupid ass streaming service.One last thing to note is that as far as I could tell, the movie is completely devoid of any CGI whatsoever, so everything in the movie seems to have been done practically, with the small exception of maybe a quick flash back shot to a young Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem.

title: Same Old Track, Brand New Thrills
content: I don't watch Formula One. I don't understand Formula One. My vocabulary on the subject barely extends beyond a few shiny keywords - McLaren, Hungaroring, Max Verstappen, slipstream - and most of what I know about how a race works probably comes from some 1980s video game.And yet, as Le Mans '66, Ferrari, Rush and even Gran Turismo have all proved, I'm surprisingly easy to win over when it comes to big-budget motor racing dramas.Once again, the story itself isn't exactly revolutionary: take a dash of Rocky (the underdog fighting his way to the top), mix in a bit of Top Gun: Maverick (the ageing daredevil forced to coexist with a younger, hungrier generation), put them both behind the wheel of gleaming supercars, and you've got F1. Simple as that.There are a few subplots tossed in - the team's scheming major shareholder, Sonny Hayes the grizzled boomer driver entangled with the team's head engineer - none of them essential, but all of them providing decent filler between the high-speed set pieces. I know I can be an easy mark, and that I've grown fond of those "physical" blockbusters - the kind that make you feel something rather than drown you in meta-narratives, cameos and self-aware banter - but F1 is genuinely well put together.Of course, beyond the high-octane spectacle of the racing sequences, what makes them work for me in a film like this - and not at all during an actual Grand Prix - is that sense of embodiment. Everything that happens off-track is there to give emotional shape and consequence to what happens on the track, something you simply can't replicate when watching a real race. Or maybe that's just my indifference to the sport talking: I didn't even notice Lewis Hamilton's cameo as himself, which tells you all you need to know about my motorsport literacy.What also helps is the film's refusal to over-glorify its drivers. Winning the cup isn't the point - keeping the team alive is - and in that context, finishing seventh or tenth counts as victory enough. Sonny Hayes, part daredevil and part tactician, must convince his anxious, image-conscious teammate that he's there as backup, not competition - and ends up helping him win by using methods that are, shall we say, less than sportsmanlike. A totally unflappable Sonny Hayes who even reaches a kind of metaphysical cool in the film's closing moments.Beautifully shot, tightly scripted, and entirely predictable in all the right ways. I knew exactly where, when and how the film was playing me - and I didn't mind at all. At this point, they could apply the same formula to segway racing or competitive spinning tops, and I'd probably still fall for it.

title: A visually stunning ride
content: "F1: The Movie" is a high-octane and emotionally satisfying racing drama. It takes a familiar comeback arc and turns it into a visually striking and engaging ride.The story follows Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a once-promising F1 driver drawn back into the sport to revive a struggling team and mentor a talented rookie. It is not the most original setup, and it can feel predictable at times, but it moves with enough energy and heart that those beats land effectively.Brad Pitt brings a grounded gravitas to Sonny Hayes, making the redemption arc feel earned and believable. Damson Idris as the young teammate and Kerry Condon as the team's technical brain add solid layers to the ensemble, while Javier Bardem brings reliable depth in his supporting role.Where the film truly excels is in its cinematic craftsmanship. Joseph Kosinski directs with flair. The cinematography is breathtaking, the racing sequences have real intensity, and the immersive sound design, paired with Hans Zimmer's score is just brilliant.So in summary: "F1: The Movie" is a visually thrilling and emotionally grounded sports drama. The story may not break new ground, but strong performances, immersive direction, and a gripping racing atmosphere make it a thrilling 8/10 watch.

title: It's all surface, but I was so invested
author: DanLawson146

title: When non-actors try to act
content: I never knew that this happens when non-actors are put in a position to act. Well... I guess we have an answer now.Full disclosure: I have been following F1 since 2007, with some minor breaks, so I am an F1 fan. I am also an avid movie lover.I tried to let go of my F1 fandom and just sit and enjoy the movie for what I thought it would be: an action-packed, exciting car racing movie with a predictable plot.They had some good ideas and there were some good and funny moments -some of which even made me laugh out loud-, but these were few and far between.The plot itself, even for an F1 layman is very one-dimensional. My wife and I both felt that since they tried to appeal to a wide audience (profit above all, yay) with the lenient and mild PG-13 rating, there isn't much depth to the characters. You won't find any trace of character development throughout the film, or, debatably, not much at all. Add in some very cliché plot points, mix in some illogical decisions (from the makers) and you have this movie.As a seasoned F1 fan the movie was even worse than I'd anticipated, based on some prior knowledge, even though Lewis Hamilton had high praise about how accurate this movie is. It's not, looking at the big picture. I understand that they didn't have the capability, or time to explain everything, but the way they tried to feed in bits of information about F1 rules felt unnatural and out of place. They attempted to make it understandable for laymen whilst also trying to make it engaging to F1 fans. You can't have both, in my opinion. There were moments where my inner F1 fan cringed a bit, which is fine by itself; this is not a documentary, which I knew it wouldn't be.A big part of the movie was affected by the two commentators, Crofty and Brundle, who are both actual F1 commentators. Neither of them are actors, and you could feel that. Crofty did an okay job of acting and bringing his usual self, but you could actually hear and see Brundle struggling. His lines were paper dry, lacking emotion and it felt like he was reading it off a piece of paper without believing what he was saying. It was very far from his usual self when he is in the zone.Speaking of the non-actors... The other pilots, team principals, and F1 staff were also thrown into the acting mix, and the results were all over the place. Some of them managed to pull it off, while others looked completely lost, making for a pretty uneven experience. The gap in their acting skills was hard to ignore, and it definitely affected the film's overall vibe.Additionally, I could feel the touch of producer Lewis Hamilton. They mentioned his name so often that it started to feel a bit forced and out of place. And that's coming from someone who was really cheering him on to be F1 champion back in 2007 and 2009.The only couple of things that, in my opinion, salvaged the movie were the 3 actual actors, Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon (Idris did a forgettable job, imo). They earned 3 stars in this review - one each.The fourth star goes to the overall idea and filming/camera work, special effects of the movie. And Brad Pitt, bless him, was so hyped and seemingly excited; the F1 interviews I saw with him about the movie were pretty wholesome and I can tell he is F1 fan for life from now on.This movie attempts to blend the excitement of F1 racing with a broader audience appeal, but it falls short due to uneven performances from non-actors and a one-dimensional plot. While there are a few entertaining moments and solid contributions from actors like Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem, and Kerry Condon, the overall execution lacks depth and coherence, leaving both the seasoned F1 fan and the movie lover in me disappointed. It could have been so much better, so much more...

title: If want a race, wait for the next RAwe CEek. If you want a cinematic experience like nothing else....
content: If this film doesn't win the best Oscar for Film Editing, I swear to god......!!!I can even make a case for the cinematography. I walked into the film expecting some Top Gun-esque shots, and I wasn't disappointed. The sound design was topnotch, Dolby Atmos makes you "feel the seats move" with every contact the cars have with each other/the barriers. This is the closest you'll come to feeling like you're in an actual F1 race. Everybody in the film was good. I didn't care for the choice of songs, OST was...good, expected it to be mind-blowing because it was Hans Zimmer.This was a cinematic experience like none other this year so far, dare I say this was a better overall experience that Mission Impossible was. 👀The story on the other hand, was nothing new. Predictable for the most part, with some dramatic moments added just for the big screen. Nothing against it though, I wouldn't have expected anything else.F1 purists, given how toxic some can be, will say "Yeah, that doesn't happen in real life racing. You liked THIS!?" The film does take a lot of "liberty" (see what I did there?) dramatizing things that won't likely happen in regular F1, maybe not as often. Parts of the film feel unrealistic but that's what you expect from a Hollywood masala movie, which is not meant to show you a proper race. If you want to watch typical race, stay home and wait for the next race week.But if you are a fan and want to see what F1 may look like on a big screen, mixed with a popcorn storyline, or if you're just a regular moviegoer want to have a good time regardless of F1, this is absolutely for you. There's enough strategy talk to make you take it seriously. The finer aspects go unexplained, but that doesn't impact the film IMO, I was hooked for the entire runtime and didn't feel the 2.5 hours pass by.Watch it in Dolby for the sound, forget IMAX. Immersive sound > a bigger screen IMO. A film like this is what the theaters are for, you're in for an experience like nothing else, especially if you grew up racing on your PC.
