title: F1: Maverick on Wheels
content: F1 races onto the screen with high speed ambition and plenty of cinematic flair, but ultimately crosses the finish line just short of greatness. It's hard to ignore the striking similarities to Top Gun: Maverick, with the film swapping fighter jets for Formula 1 cars in what feels like a reimagined, four wheeled version of the same story.That said, the ride is undeniably entertaining. The racing sequences are pulse-pounding and beautifully shot, capturing the intensity, danger, and sheer adrenaline of the sport. Visually, the film delivers. It's sleek, polished, and immersive; exactly what you'd expect from a big budget racing drama.Brad Better is the film's biggest asset. He brings charm, depth, and a grounded emotional performance that elevates every scene he's in. The rest of the cast does well, but few moments outside of Better's screen time truly stick. The script plays it safe, leaning on tropes and predictable arcs rather than taking creative risks.While F1 doesn't reinvent the wheel, it still offers enough thrills and heart to keep fans of the genre engaged. It's a solid watch, but with a bit more originality and narrative depth, it could've been a classic.Worth watching for the spectacle and Brad Better's performance. Just don't expect a fresh track.

title: F1: The Movie - A Surprisingly Respectful Ode to Racing
content:
As a Formula 1 fan since 2009, I've seen the sport evolve from pure competition to a global spectacle where commercial interests often take center stage. So I approached F1: The Movie with a healthy dose of skepticism, expecting a flashy Hollywood take that might sideline the sport's deeper essence. To my surprise, the film turned out to be a remarkably respectful and well-crafted portrayal.Direction and Writing:
The story is solid, grounded, and avoids the typical pitfalls of racing dramas. There's a clear narrative arc, but also room for character-driven moments and believable interpersonal dynamics. The direction feels precise and deliberate, with a strong grip on pacing and tone. Racing sequences are intense, yet not overdone - a testament to the filmmakers' restraint and respect for the material.Acting Performances:
Brad Pitt once again proves why he's been at the top of his craft for so long. His performance is confident, nuanced, and never feels forced. One of the most pleasant surprises was Damson Idris. I wasn't familiar with his work beforehand, but he delivers a compelling performance that feels both fresh and grounded. Definitely a talent to keep an eye on.Cinematography and Editing:
The film's real standout is its cinematography. The camera work during racing scenes is phenomenal - razor-sharp, kinetic, and immersive without becoming overwhelming. Honestly, it's a bit of a shame we don't see this kind of dynamic visual storytelling in live F1 broadcasts. Every frame feels meticulously composed, capturing the raw thrill of motorsport without excessive stylization.Pacing and Music:
The pacing is tight but allows for emotional beats to land. It's not just a rush of engine noise - there's rhythm and breath to the edit. The score complements the visuals well, enhancing mood without dominating scenes.A Minor Shortcoming:
If I had to nitpick, there are a few moments that feel just a bit too polished for long-time motorsport followers - for instance, a character arc that progresses a little too smoothly, or a pitlane scene that feels more choreographed than authentic. These moments don't break immersion, but they do briefly remind you you're watching a film rather than a documentary.Final Verdict:
F1: The Movie is a visually stunning and narratively grounded film that treats the world of Formula 1 with respect. It offers genuine appeal for fans of the sport without alienating newcomers. The performances, cinematography, and direction come together in a package that feels passionate, but never exaggerated. A strong recommendation for anyone who enjoys character-driven sports drama with cinematic flair.

title: Visceral Racing Scenes - Little else
content: Filmed with the cooperation of FI racing, the 4x Oscar Nominated ( including for Best Picture) Brad Pitt film, appropriately entitled F1: THE MOVIE, is a feast for the eyes and ears of racing fans that is visceral and loud and brash in the racing scenes and offers little else in the way of originality, characters and plot.Writer/Director Joseph Kosinski (TOP GUN: MAVERICK) brings the same eye for action to this film that he did for that Tom Cruise sequel and it works well with a subject matter (F1 racing) that is just crying for a big screen, big budget special effects extravaganza and it delivers on that front, bringing the F1 experience front and center making the audience feel like they are in the cockpit of the racing car with Mr. Pitt. This film has, rightfully, been Oscar nominated for Visual Effects, Sound and Film editing and it won't be surprising if it wins at least one of these.It is also nominated for Best Picture - and that doesn't feel quite right, for the rest of the film is a slapdash rehash of previously experienced movie going experiences that features the old pro returning (after a traumatic experience that made him walk away) and coming face to face with a brash newcomer who calls him "old man" throughout the course of the film.Will these 2 eventually learn to get along and team up to, ultimately, bring this losing team to victory while learning life lessons along the way?What do you think?Pitt, clearly, got this movie greenlit when he agreed to appear in this film in the lead role of grizzled race veteran Sonny Hayes, who "just wants to race". Pitt checks all the boxes of the grizzled veteran who pulls a rabbit out of his hat again and again - much to the chagrin of the younger, computer-savvy team. He's a true MOVIE STAR and this film (and this role) needs one in order to hold it together - and Pitt more than delivers on this front.Javier Bardem (NO COUNTRY FOR OLD MEN) is wasted as the Race Car owner who brings Sonny back into the game, while Damson Idris (SNOWFALL) is cocky and brash as the younger racer forced to work with Sonny.Kerry Condon (DEATH BY LIGHTING), Sarah Niles (Ted's therapist in TED LASSO) and Tobias Menzies (Prince Philip in the Princess Diana season of THE CROWN) are all in this film to check the boxes of their characters - the co-worker who becomes Pitt's love interest, the worried Mother of the brash young driver who wishes her son would understand/respect the wisdom of Sonny and the Corporate Head who is looking not for the "love of racing", but rather "the big payday".All of this is just by the book.But, the biggest trope (faux pas) that this film does over and over again is that Sonny pulls a trick out of his hat during just about EVERY race that his team has no idea about. Whether it's a minor rule, trust in the car, a wily veterans glance at the weather conditions, Sonny is the smartest guy in the room over and over again and cockily lets everyone know about it. He pulls a fast one on everyone - including his team - and It's not a very endearing quality and by the time Bardem's character finally says to Sonny "you're fired" (do you think he'll be rehired before the big, final race)? I just wanted to say to him - "about time".Oh, and they also have the "Racing Announcer' voice over telling the audience over and over again the stakes that are in play (in case you forgot).Come (and stay) for the racing footage. Put up with the corny dialogue, character and plot, the racing footgage is worth waiting for.Letter Grade: B (A for the racing sequences, C for everything else)7 stars (out of 10) and you can take that to the BankofMarquis.

title: Goosebumps, Grit, and Gasoline - A Must-Watch Ride!
content: It's been a minute since a movie gave me this kind of bone-chilling, goosebump-filled thrill. F1: The Movie delivers an adrenaline-pumping experience that truly sticks with you. Being a Brad Pitt fan, I can confidently say he nails it here-charisma, intensity, everything on point.Director Joseph Kosinski brings an edge-of-your-seat vibe throughout. There are moments where you feel like you're in the driver's seat-heart pounding, engine roaring-major props for that immersive execution.Damson Idris also shines as the young up-and-comer, full of potential and hunger. I wish his arc had been developed more. Maybe a deeper look into his growth or mentorship moments with Brad's character would've elevated the film further.Still, this movie is worth the ticket. You'll leave the theater thrilled and satisfied. And Brad... I love you.

title: Bonkers terribly brilliant!!
content: Just seen it in IMAX, laugh out loud brilliant. By the way, I'd class myself as an F1 purist so went in wanting to be entertained, and I was. Laugh out loud a few times, the race scenes are epic, some of it is totally bonkers but don't let sniffy reviews deter you. Go watch it and get turned on to the sport I love and have loved for 50 years.It's not Rush so put that to one side immediately. It straddles Hollywood and the sport fantastically so definitely go and see it!Who knows, I'll probably go and see it again.I loved playing spot the drivers and even stayed to the very end so I could see all the formula 1 bods involvement.Brilliant, just brilliant.

title: A brilliant soundtrack trick
content: Halfway through the film, I was ready to type out a quick review: fun movie, atrocious soundtrack. The usual mainstream sludge-cheap pop, generic hip-hop, soulless house-clashing painfully with the film's epic scope.But then, closer to the finale, it clicked: the whole sequence of disposable tracks, the kind you forget the next morning, was intentional. They play during a stretch of easy, almost undeserved victories-exactly like that music itself: talentless but inexplicably popular, carried by chance and the poor taste of the masses.And only at the climax, when the heroes take real risks, suffer, and bend reality through sheer willpower, Zimmer finally kicks in-grand, resonant, and worthy of the film's scale.Maybe the producers of that vocoder trash are proud their "music" made it into such a big movie. But the irony is, they're probably too dense to realize their tracks were used to illustrate the story's emptiest, most meaningless passage.

title: It's the blockbuster of the year and makes you believe in cinema again.
content: If there was a great savior of cinema in times when it seemed doomed post-pandemic, that man is Joseph Kosinski with his tremendous Top Gun Maverick starring Tom Cruise. Today he returns to make us believe again in epic, spectacular, and exciting cinema, led by another megastar like Brad Pitt, and now he takes us to Formula 1 and pays a small tribute to all those who make a living from the king of racing.Joseph Kosinski immerses us in the world of motorsports in an epic and wonderful way, with a simple story, yet embellished with all the spectacular elements, of an adrenaline-filled and exciting sport. For those of us who are fans and for those who are not, this is a film that will invite you to love the sport a little bit. The director once again gives us an immersive experience, now under the adrenaline rush of racing, with spectacular scenes and a stimulating cinematic experience.Brad Pitt gives his all to his Sonny Hayes and his redemption arc as a veteran racing hero. The actor imbues the character with nuances of loneliness, but he takes him on a journey of camaraderie in such an epic and adorable way that we, as viewers, end up more than a little embarrassed for enjoying such a simplistic schtick, but it always works as the best guilty pleasure we could have. He's accompanied by a cast that embodies Brad Pitt's aura, such as Javier Bardem, Damson Idris, and Kerry Condon, who are the best complement to the film's success in all its aspects and story arcs, even if their stories are as stereotypical as we can imagine.The director's excellent work is echoed; Kosinski has a remarkable flair for filming each action sequence, delivering a perfect blend of realism and spectacularity. His work is so detailed that it allows the viewer to feel the adrenaline rush and experience a roller coaster of emotions. It offers vertigo, adrenaline, and an entertainment engine, with moments that are more accomplished than others, but a constant drive that keeps us on the edge of excitement at all times, even if you're not addicted to the sport in question. Kosinski manages to create a spectacle for as many viewers as possible, and he does so with camera work worthy of one of today's best directors at recreating action and adrenaline.The great success, once again in Kosinski's proposal, is transporting us to a moment where we can immerse ourselves and elevate familiar material with the bravado of old-school cinema. It effectively leverages its budget, intelligently and with audience entertainment in mind, delivering the perfect combination of power and star power to enjoy a blockbuster worth seeing on the big screen, turning us into children enjoying a grand gift of images, sounds, and spectacularity.

title: From Track to Screen: Exploring the World of F1 in This Film
content: The F1 film delivers a captivating cinematic experience with outstanding cinematography, a feast for the eyes that successfully builds intense tension throughout the races. Not only that, Hans Zimmer's score is a major strength, adding emotional depth and adrenaline to every scene. The film's storyline is very direct and linear, making the 2.5-hour runtime fly by without a single boring moment. While some might find the presented drama a bit excessive, the film manages to package it in just the right proportion, without overpowering the core narrative.This film is perfect for general audiences or those unfamiliar with the world of F1, as it provides a clear overview without feeling preachy. The viewing experience feels akin to a "Top Gun: Maverick" but with F1 cars, given that Joseph Kosinski also directed this film. The direct involvement of F1 drivers, team principals, engineers, and journalists on screen adds a strong sense of realism, further enhanced by the fact that filming took place simultaneously with actual F1 Grand Prix events.However, there are a few minor inaccuracies that might slightly bother long-time F1 fans. Nevertheless, this is understandable given that the film is a work of fiction, not a documentary, so artistic freedom was certainly a consideration.And finally, my message to those of you who have watched F1 for a long time and will or have watched this movie, don't take it too seriously and take it easy.

title: The Ultimate F1 Experience
content: F1 directed by Joseph Kosinski starring Brad Pitt is a grand formula 1 racing movie with fair share of thrills and entertainment. The plot of the movie is nothing special, it is generic and predictable but the way the movie is shot makes all the difference. It is grand and a film that should be seen in the theatres for best viewing. The visuals, the racing scenes, the unmatched aura of Brad Pitt, the score by Hans Zimmer and the direction by Joseph Kosinski all made this a good experience at the theatre. The film does have it's flaws, there were few things I wish was better like focusing more on the bonding between the two drivers instead of the romance subplot but it didn't ruin the momentum of the film and some parts of the film just failed to engage especially few scenes before the climax. It's not perfect but for the experience and that climax alone, it is worth it. The film is technically top notch and has great performances from the cast especially Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon, Damson Idris and I also liked the Apxgp crew especially Callie Cooke as Jodie. The racing scenes in the movie is extraordinary, the best I have seen, it really felt like we were in that car, the climax scene especially was breathtaking, they did a brillaint job in making the racing scenes thrilling and engaging. Really this movie had one job that was to get that racing scenes right and they did that perfectly, that was truly an experience. Overall, I enjoyed the film and it's racing , it really ended on a high aswell. Good theatrical watch.

title: Good graphics do not a good game make.
content: I understand that there are limitations in just how much realism aspects of true life can be practically depicted in cinema, but in the opening sequences, when I saw the casual handover between drivers in the Daytona race I had to roll my eyes. 'Here we go' I thought. It was like watching Morgan Freeman preparing to 'Drive Miss Daisy'.But, actually once I sat back and just watched, I actually quite liked the movie. True, it wasn't necessarily an compltely accurate portray of F1 but for the purposes of movie magic, everything worked quite well.I'm not going to go into any depth on specifics... direction, casting, performances etc, I'm sure others will give you all the information you need. I'm simply going to focus on what the movie delivers.Quite frankly the movie is pretty good. It's exciting, entertaining and has quite a bit of action. The characters do well bringing the it to life and for the most part, it delivers on what it promises. If you've seen any of the top gun movies they know what to expect as it varies very little in the formulaic production, but that doesn't hamper the experience as the movie makes good on its strengths and doesn't attempt to 'hoodwink' you into thinking this is a masterpiece of story telling.Despite my small criticisms I liked it. I'm giving it a generous 7/10 Pretty good.

title: Entertaining, Realistic, fun.
content: This is the best race movie in years. There was plenty of action, a great story, and you get invested in the characters without them having to do a bunch of drama and side story garbage. I go to see movies to be entertained, and this delivered 100% It was very realistic, typical underdog story but some excellent twists. You like and hate some of the characters, some turn you around, some don't. The camera word was awesome and there was very limited CGI, which was great! Full theater, everyone walked out smiling and happy. This is how a movie should be, I had decent expectations going in and it exceeded those. Hats off to the cast, the director, and apple for finally making a great film.

title: Visually impressive, but too Hollywood for real F1 fans
content: "F1" is a high-budget, action-driven take on the world of Formula 1 - and while it delivers adrenaline and visual spectacle, it often does so by bending the sport's reality to fit the Hollywood mold.Let's get one thing straight: this is not a film made for hardcore F1 fans. It's made for general audiences who enjoy fast cars, personal drama, and big, cinematic moments. If you're expecting technical precision, realistic racing dynamics, and accurate portrayal of F1 culture, prepare to be disappointed.The film presents Formula 1 in a very stylized, almost fantasy-like way. Racing scenes are filled with dramatic overtakes that ignore physics, crashes that would be fatal in real life but are brushed off like minor hiccups, and a general disregard for how F1 cars actually handle, brake, or accelerate. The film takes liberties with speed, track conditions, tire degradation, and even how team radios work - often prioritizing cool shots over logical consistency.One of the most glaring issues is how communication during races is handled. Drivers talk way too casually and frequently, even mid-corner at 300 km/h. It breaks immersion for anyone who's watched a real F1 broadcast. The pressure, the concentration, and the absolute silence drivers maintain in key moments are completely absent.On the technical side, the film throws around terms like "DRS" or "ERS," but they're never explained or shown in action accurately. Pit stops are flashy but unrealistic in execution or timing. There's very little attention given to the strategies, data analysis, or team coordination that define real F1. The mechanical and tactical brilliance behind each race weekend is largely ignored.That said, the movie does shine in its presentation. The cinematography is polished, and the race footage looks slick. Shots from inside the cockpit, low-track angles, and sweeping aerials are impressive. Sound design is powerful - the engine notes, tire screeches, and crowd roars all hit with weight. On a technical production level, the film is undeniably well-made.As for performances, the cast does a decent job with what they're given. The acting isn't the issue - the script is. Characters often fall into predictable sports-movie roles: the talented underdog, the arrogant veteran, the pressure-filled team boss. The dialogue is serviceable but not particularly memorable, and there's a lot of melodrama packed in to fill gaps where real motorsport storytelling could've gone.Emotionally, the film tries to touch on the psychological and physical toll of racing at the highest level, but only at surface level. It hints at depth, but rarely commits to it. The result is a movie that feels like it's imitating the emotional beats of stronger motorsport films (like Rush or Ford v Ferrari) without fully earning them.If you know little about F1 and just want a fast-paced, energetic ride with high production value, you'll probably enjoy it. It's flashy, loud, and keeps moving. But if you're a lifelong fan of the sport, the inaccuracies and simplifications will be hard to overlook.In short: F1 (2025) is more Fast & Furious than Formula 1. It delivers entertainment, not realism. It might introduce some people to the world of F1, but it won't satisfy those who already love it for what it truly is - a defined by precision, strategy, and restraint.

title: A great cinematic experience
content: F1 : The movie is a visual spectacle that delivers big time. It's an absolute blast and one of the most exciting movies to come out this year.Starting with the story, yes, it is formulaic and adapts a cliché trope. The story has good characters but weaker arcs and emotional depth is minimal. Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes is charismatic and Damson Idris as Joshua Pearce is something that was surprisingly good. The other supporting cast were good in their respective roles. Of course it feels dramatic at times, but it never disappoints.The direction by Joseph Kosinski is praiseworthy. Making an edge of the seat film with unparalleled direction and pioneering filmmaking, F1 delivers a great cinematic experience. The score by Hans Zimmer is energetic as well as moving. The cinematography and the visuals are something the makers should be boastful of.To summarise, F1 doesn't bring anything new to the table, however Kosinski's skillful direction paired with absolutely dashing Brad Pitt brings a fresh breath of thrill and joy in this 155 minutes edge-of-the-seat racing drama, that is nothing short of cinematic triumph.My Rating : 4/5.

title: Not just a movie but an experience!
content: F1: The Movie is an amazing experience to witness on the biggest screen possible. Just like Top Gun: Maverick, F1: The Movie is a high octane thrill experience that puts you in the drivers seat of an F1 race car. What I loved about this film so much was how much authentic racing they showed throughout. There had to be over 10 racing scenes in the entire 2 hour and 30 minute run time. Brad Pitt and Damson Idris have a lot of good chemistry here. You have Brad Pitt who is the veteran driver and the new young gun Damson Idris who feed off each other. The non racing scenes kept me so invested throughout. Director, Joseph Kosinski has really crafted a career here with thrilling authentic experiences that need to be witnessed on the big screen. I can't wait to see what the rest of his career brings. F1: The Movie gets a perfect 10 stars out of 10.

title: The Plushie Crew Reviews: F1 2025 - Brad Pitt, Hans Zimmer & A High-Speed Weekend Ride 🚗🔥
content: This weekend's movie night pick came courtesy of Sonic, and what a choice it was. The Crew settled in at home, fired up Apple TV+, and the moment Brad Pitt's name popped up, everyone knew we were in for something special. Pitt brought all the charisma and legendary charm you'd expect - Knuckles couldn't stop calling him the "coolest guy alive," while Amy was quick to point out just how effortlessly handsome he still is ✨.Then Hans Zimmer's soundtrack kicked in 🎶, and suddenly it felt like our living room had been turned into an IMAX theater. The music roared, the cars flew, and the energy was unreal. Sonic, Tails, Knuckles, and Shadow were practically glued to the screen 👀, completely caught up in the speed and spectacle. The racing sequences were pure adrenaline, fast-paced and gorgeous, and even those of us less into Formula 1 couldn't help but cheer at the screen 🙌.Sure, the story leaned on clichés here and there, and the hardcore F1 fans in the Crew (looking at you, Tails 🦊) raised an eyebrow or two at the bending of real-world rules. But none of that stopped us from having a blast together. The vibe was fun, the movie looked stunning, the soundtrack was fire 🔥, and by the end, everyone agreed: F1 2025 turned a simple weekend movie night into something that felt like an event.For the Crew, that's an easy 10/10 ride 🏁.

This is what cinema is made for! What a movie! Finally again a brilliant blockbuster that has everything you need and so far the best movie of the past two years.What a ride. title: Formula one
content: Firstly, the music. Oh boy!I came expecting engine roars... and instead got Hans Zimmer blasting emotions at 300 km/h. I wasn't prepared for this concert. I came to the theatre for Formula 1, not to feel my feelings in Dolby Atmos. The guitars and drums were doing more laps than the cars.Cinematography? A thing to Marvel at!No, no - NOT that Marvel. This isn't a multiverse; this is motorsport. But I'll give it to Kosinski - the man knows how to shoot speed. The close-ups, the helmet cams, the pit lane drama - it's all smoother than Lewis on a soft compound.Kosinski pulls it off again As expected, the Top Gun: Maverick director brings his signature hyper-real-but-just-believable-enough style. Everything looks slick, the tension is palpable, and the racing feels intense. The cars look real, the tracks feel familiar, and the drama is soaked in cinematic polish - and maybe a bit of Red Bull too.Now, dear hardcore F1 fans... You will suffer. In silence.Yes, yes - the pit stop timings are a joke, strategy makes no sense, tyre degradation is apparently fictional, and someone said "DRS" like it's a Marvel superpower. You'll sit there, physically cringing, as your non-F1 friends lean over and say, "Omg I didn't know Formula 1 was this emotional." You'll want to scream, but you won't. You'll smile. You'll nod. Then later, you'll go straight to Reddit, type furiously, and find solace in a thread titled "Things that made no damn sense in the F1 movie - Spoiler heavy".Final thoughts: Is it accurate? Not really.Is it entertaining? Absolutely.Is Brad Pitt believable as an ageing F1 comeback driver? Well... let's just say Fernando Alonso might have notes.Go watch it. Just don't forget - it's not Formula 1. It's Formula Fun-damentally unrealistic, but kinda awesome. Thanks to the director and the actors. We really enjoyed it. It was great. Also, for those who haven't seen Formula One, watch it. It's very spectacular. Thank you for the film you made.

title: A Phenomenal Action Blockbuster
content: As soon as I heard Joseph Kosinski, the director of 'Top Gun: Maverick', signed on for this film, I knew we were in for something truly special! It turns out this film is every bit as awesome as I wanted it to be and in many ways even better.Even though 'F1' feels like it's based on a true story, it is entirely fictional. I find it amazing how you have countless real-world Formula 1 racers like Lewis Hamilton, Max Verstappen, Fernando Alonso, etc. And real Formula 1 crew implemented into the story as it makes everything in the film feel more realistic.As with Joseph Kosinski's previous big-budget action film 'Top Gun: Maverick' (2022), the action is shot spectacularly with camera angles inside the cockpit that provide the audience with a more immersive experience than it already is. It even feels more immersive than any other 3D film I've ever seen thanks to its intense cinematography, excellent sound, fantastic soundtrack and a surprisingly high amount of IMAX shots! Hans Zimmer also did a really good job with the score!Stylistically 'Top Gun: Maverick' and 'F1' feel quite similar especially when considering Tom Cruise and Brad Pitt are "old-timers" coming back for one more big swing before "retirement". Brad Pitt did a magnificent job dedicating himself fully to his character!Joseph Kosinski has pulled off something truly beautiful here with 'F1' to the point that I am unbelievably excited and buzzing to watch it again! 7/10.

title: This is why we go to the cinemas. Top-class blockbuster filmmaking!! [+80%]
content: There was already a lot of talk (read: buzz) around how this was gonna be like Top Gun: Maverick but on the racing track, given F1 is written by Ehren Kruger and directed by Joseph Kosinski. The trailer confirmed much of it, and yes, it's essentially what everyone (and their mom) expected from a plot standpoint -- I'm not explaining it -- if Ehren Kruger has cracked one kind of (tweaked) storytelling formula, it's THIS ONE. And mind you, it still works. BIG TIME!Brad Pitt is still an effing badass on screen, and it's incredibly easy to buy him as Sonny Hayes. I certainly have a soft spot for Kerry Condon, and her pairing with Pitt benefits the film just as much as the Cruise-Connolly pairing did for Top Gun: Maverick. The lead characters carry arcs of their own, and there's more than sufficient breathing space for them to develop. The racing sequences are incredibly nail-biting, and edited with finesse (by Stephen Mirrione) - there are fast cuts done to match the soundtrack too, which I felt, was very stylistic and cool. The sound department is just on another level, making you feel every single vroom-vroom. It puts you right in the middle of the track, making you experience those thuds, crashes, and engine revs.Hans Zimmer also brings his A-game on the score, fully immersing us in this world of corporate decision-making, race driver egos, what the pit crew essentially goes through as a result, and even a sweet slice of romance. Claudio Miranda (a frequent Joseph Kosinski collaborator) captures the action up close to let us know how one feels to be in the driver's seat of an F1 car, but the real winner here is the decision to film almost all of it in real tracks around the world. The podcast with Joseph Kosinski on the F1 website explains all of this, and more, in a super interesting episode.As much as the film revolves around the characters of Brad Pitt and Damon Idris, F1 is very much Javier Bardem's. While he's the rich, corporate stooge, the friendship he shares with Pitt's Sonny Hayes also forms one of the core emotional points. Idris pretty much plays the Miles Teller character from TGM, and he's solid. On the whole, the film is very much an experience meant for the big screens. I enjoyed every minute of it -- the one-liners, the character arcs, the technical flair, and most importantly, the set-pieces.

content: Hollywood took the most basic cliched plot and churned this thing out. I could imagine NASCAR getting a gambling drunk who is 61 to drive the car. As Brad Pitt says in the movie, is the team owner loco? Chiron and Etancelin drove in 50-s... anyway Chiron didn't even qualify for the GP, Fagioli won as a 53-year old but anyway. Aerodynamics and the logics of it aside, the car just flies over the barriers like von Trips when he died...?And then, of course, they win and movie is over after Brad got to boink the only female here.So, who green-lit this thing about F1 - so cheating is good, cars fly over barriers (should be tragic but this got me laughing as to how unreal this looked) and rules are just ignored. I would even like the movie, it looks great, Brad is cool and I can even buy the very basic APXGP but it just shows that the film-makers really didn't care about making something like Rush. Anybody who watched this, just watch Rush (2013) instead. Plot is real, a lot better and hey, the racing also looks totally fine.

title: An unashamedly old-school Hollywood tentpole
content: It's never good to assume but my assumptions would lead me to believe that you enter into an ice cream shop to buy ice cream, a shoe shop for some shoes or head to a music festival to listen to some music.Taking that same assumption approach, I would be thinking viewers are buying a ticket to F1: The Movie to see Brad Pitt drive really fast cars in a formulaic sports drama that has made no false pretences about what it is and what it's trying to offer cinemagoers.One of the summer blockbuster seasons few original I. P releases that also acts as Apple's biggest gamble and last ditch hurrah to pursue big-scale cinematic releases for its feature film products, F1 is a $200 plus million dollar tentpole that teams Pitt up with the masterminds behind 2022's box office smashing Top Gun: Maverick and for anyone wanting big-scale Hollywood entertainment at its most thrilling, crowd-pleasing and at all times unashamedly within the confines of expectations, Joseph Kosinski's latest adrenaline rush is exactly what the doctor ordered.Following the tried and true formula of a grizzled veteran who's seen better days being called back into action by an old friend to help an ailing team and impart their wisdom onto the next generation, Kosinski and his screenwriting partner Ehren Kruger throw Pitt's Sonny Hayes into a scenario that will be familiar to anyone that's ever watched a Hollywood underdog story or partaken in a sports themed drama but that matters little when everyone involved is having the time of their lives roaring around the worlds most famous racing tracks all the while involving the audience into the Formula One scene in ways that's never been achieved on the big screen before.With the full backing of the global brand, F1 has been granted an all-access pass to the world of the high-octane and high stakes sport and while hardcore racing fans may find themselves nit-picking the liberties taken by the film in regards to certain rules and regulations to make the story tick along, going into F1 with an open mind to have fun and enjoy the ride should provide a huge majority of audiences to have an absolute blast with the show Kosinski and his team put on.Utilising the long established charms of leading man Pitt who gives Hayes a hearty dose of Cliff Booth DNA, F1 gets the most out of its main star who collaborates well with the films other key players including Snowfall rising star Damson Iris as young protégé Joshua Pearce, the always joyous Kerry Condon as design specialist Kate McKenna and scene-stealer Javier Bardem as team manager Ruben Cervantes, making F1 a pole position finisher when it comes to winning ensembles.While all these characters are as thinly drawn as you'd like, given small moments of backstories and depth, it matters little in the grand scheme of things as this crowd-pleaser will win you over with a razor sharp script, ample adrenaline rushes in the thick of the petrol fuelled action, a hit heavy soundtrack accompanied by yet another top-notch Hans Zimmer score and a quick-fire education into a world that many audiences members may not have previously had an interest in.A refreshingly old-school Hollywood effort that hearkens back to the glory days of 90's fist-pumping audience pleasers, producer Jerry Bruckheimer and his creative team have crafted a winning big screen offering that provides its audience with exactly the type of experience that's been promoted to them, no baggage attached.Final Say -A wonderfully captured piece of cinematic entertainment that maximises the star power of its leading man, F1: The Movie plays it safe but when it's done this well it does little to dampen the joy of 2 and a half hours of pure Hollywood escapism at its shiniest and sharpest.4 odd pairs of socks out of 5.

title: One of the great action movies from 2025
content: I'm going for it now. F1 The Movie will have these three Oscar nominations: Best Director, Best Editing, and Best Cinematography. And it's not for nothing, because in my opinion, it's one of the great action films of this year. It will surely join the final podium of its genre with another film released in the remainder of 2025.Kosinsky specializes in dizzying, high-budget action films with superstar casts. If Top Gun: Maverick was a complete success, F1 is following the same path. It is, indeed, an absolutely immersive film, which puts the viewer in first-person perspective, including them in a thrilling story. The editing is exquisite, as the film's pacing is incredibly fast, with a couple of breaths of fresh air to keep pushing until the triumphant finale. The script favors 90% of the competition and the racing, because come on, it can't just be two and a half hours of cars; there also has to be some drama and romance to spice things up.Part of the appeal of this plot is that it's written with the charisma of 90s action movies, where the hero and protagonist is a skilled individual and also a heartthrob who comes to save the day. It works? It works. It's Brad Pitt's trademark.Other highlights include the soundtrack, which spectacularly accompanies the images on and off the track, and the production design, which allowed Kosinsky to sneak in and see real F1 races. It couldn't be any other way, given that Lewis Hamilton himself is one of the film's producer, sneaking in several impressive cameos for die-hard F1 fans.In short, a film to enjoy from start to finish. An excellent opportunity to enjoy on the biggest screen possible and perhaps even encourage you to see a real F1 race someday.

title: Entertaining but forgettable
content: The trailers and talent behind this movie would have one hope, that this would be an another addition to great racing dramas that had been coming out 'recently' like Rush (2013) or Ford v Ferrari (2019) but alas it is more like Grand Turismo (2023).The plot is paper thin. The focus is on a failing F1 team that needs to get at least a couple points in the rest of the season, lest they be sold causing a major financial loss for the owner and personnel would lose their jobs. The young driver already in the team is convinced this fate is inevitable hence he tries to do his best to make himself visible to other teams, so that next season he might start under a new team. The owner desperately tries to avoid this fate so he gambles on bringing in Brad Pitt as his second driver. Brad Pitt used to be an F1 driver 30 years ago until an accident ended his potential rise to fame.The movie is shot well and has loads of entertaining parts. Brad Pitt is witty and unorthodox in his interpretation of the F1 rules which leads to some fun situations. I surely had a good time watching the movie, though I never developed any emotional investment as to what was happening on screen. For one, I kept seeing the actors, rather than them being characters and I never really cared about what would happen to any of them. I fear this movie won't leave any lasting impression on me, and I will struggle to recall anything about it a couple weeks from now. If you happen to stumble upon it, it is a good time, but it won't be remembered.

title: needed a fun movie?
content: On days when I just needed a fun movie, I randomly picked Formula 1 (F1)-and I have to say, I was satisfied. With performances by Brad Pitt alongside Sidna and Molana, Formula 1 is exactly the kind of film you want for a weekend with chips and yogurt dip.Although on the surface it's about driving, racing, and everything related to Formula 1, it's still appealing even for those who aren't into cars. Beneath it all, the film carries deeper themes like the importance of teamwork, not becoming arrogant, and never underestimating others.Overall, I enjoyed this movie because it was entertaining, exciting, well-paced, and never boring. I'd give it a 7 out of 10 (its IMDb score is 7.8/10), and I recommend you watch it too.P. S. After watching the film, I found out Brad Pitt trained for four months just to nail this role. Compare that to Javad Ezzati's acting in the series Zakhm Kari and Vahshi-performances so similar that if you didn't know they were two different shows, you might think you were watching the same one!

title: You Don't Need to Love F1 to Love This
content: Brad Pitt plays a retired Formula 1 driver brought back into the game by an old friend running a struggling independent team. He ends up paired with the team's rising star, played by Damson Idris - Franklin Saint himself, now behind the wheel. The tension between the two is sharp and believable, with Pitt delivering that seasoned, ego-driven energy that gives the story real weight. Kerry Condon, known for her work in Better Call Saul, The Banshees of Inisherin, and more, is excellent as the no-nonsense lead engineer. The film blends real race footage with cinematic storytelling, and the result looks incredible. Even if you're not into F1, the characters, pacing, and drama make it engaging. That said, the movie runs over 2.5 hours and sometimes feels a bit repetitive, especially in the rivalry scenes. Still, it's a strong 8/10 - stylish, intense, and surprisingly grounded.

title: A Must-Watch Even If You've Never Watched a Race in Your Life
content: I knew nothing about F1 or car racing but this film completely pulled me in. Felt like I was cheering at a real race.Easily one of the best films I've seen in 2025. A must-watch, even if you've never watched a race in your life.

title: Good Summer Blockbuster
content: F1 is an old school Summer blockbuster, the likes of which we don't get as often.Brad Pitt plays an older race-car driver who joins different teams every season to help them win races, and he has spend the last 30 years of his career winning them all...except the big F1. As a young racer he was in the F1, but he had a terrible crash that ended his career with F1 and it's always haunted him. When brand owner Javier Bardem offers him a spot on his F1 team to help a young driver win the big race...he now has a shot at redemption.Problem is, he and the young racer don't gel well, and to make matters worse, if they don't win the race Javier will have his ownership stripped and the brand will be sold off. In other words, if Brad fails...not only will he lose his shot at redemption but all the other people on the team will meet the same fate...a career of What If's about their time at F1.Brad won't let that happen. He gets the team to re-design their cars to move faster in the turns than the other cars. It's risky, dangerous, but if it pays off...his team will have the speed to pull off the big win. What follows is a series of really intense driving scenes that - while maybe a little too fast and repetitive - do help make sense out of what an F1 race is all about. You feel like you are strapped into a racer going a zillion miles and hour and in one misstep you can fly into the air and crash.F1 is a very formulaic film. We have seen this story told time and again. However, one thing that makes this film a little different is Brad's character is very likable, the Hans Zimmers core hits hard, and as we get to the final race I discovered I had really invested in these people and cared about their race and wanted them to succeed.F1 is a good time at the movies. It's got a good moral message about how sometimes when we are at our lowest, it's just an opportunity to get back up and win.

title: Invigorating, high-octane race car blockbuster
content: Brad Pitt gives a granite performance as a former race car wunderkind who attempts a miracle comeback as a Formula One driver, long after a disastrous accident on the track derailed his career and sent him into gambling and isolation. Javier Bardem plays somewhat against type as his old friend and racetrack rival who recruits him to pull off the miracle for his team's struggling season. Damson Idris is the cocky young racer who has little regard for his older teammate in the beginning. Kerry Condon is superb as the engineer who puts together the race car that will win the race and becomes Pitt's newfound muse. Tobias Menzies is sharp as a shady businessman with greater ambitions for the racing team.To get the full effect, it might be a good idea to see this film in IMAX. The mesmerizing racetrack cinematography and Hans Zimmer's soundtrack keeps going at a heady clip.Director Joseph Kosinski's films tend to have a recurring theme in which a young person gains wisdom by listening to those with more experience. That narrative returns in this film as Pitt and Idris veer from intense clashing to grudging cooperation. This time around, however, the older protagonist has baggage and question marks of his own to overcome, all the while setting the standard for excellence. While the film's premise is familiar, its execution is so propulsive and riveting that you won't even care if you've seen something else in this genre before. The film's storyline is definitely old school and tried and true, but its bravado is not outdated in the least. Grandly recommended.

title: Expected better, but wasn't dissapointed either
content: Opening Daytona sequence was phenomenal and probably set the bar for the rest of the movie too high for me. In general I was expecting a somewhat different story judging by the trailers: something like an underdog team nobody sees coming suddenly has a season of a lifetime - winning both WDC and WCC. What's more frustrating is that similar thing literally happened in 2009 with Brawn GP, so instead of coming with whatever cheesy "we just need one race to survive" excuse writers could've just looked back into F1 history and do the exact same thing. The problem with the movie's premise is that it takes away the suspence and tension that naturally comes from a WDC scoreboard shifting back and forth during the season - once again, something that is very much the essence of F1. Additionally, the movie relied way too heavily for whatever silly gimmick that allows our heroes to win the day (like Sonny basically playing F1 demolition derby) instead of racing speaking for itself - like they did in Daytona sequence. But I guess it is to be expected from a movie aiming for a more general audience. And obviously, in an attempt to pander everyone, writers have manager to literally cram every single cliche dialogue catchphrase and every single sports movie trope into here. Some of the dialogues were really cringe inducing. All in all, it is what it is: a multimillion summer blockbuster that somehow happens to be an F1 movie. It's not meant to be watched seriously, but as a spectacle it works - I genuinely had a couple of shivers moments during racing sequences. In the end, while I have expected better, I wasn't dissapointed either: it's the kind of movie to watch in a theater on a weekend never to come back to it again.

title: Stunning experience visually falls short comapred to Rush
content: F1, the latest motorsport spectacle, delivers high-octane visuals and an immersive experience that brings viewers thrillingly close to the intensity of Formula 1 racing. The film shines with its stunning cinematography, every frame on the track feels meticulously crafted, and the racing sequences are visually jaw-dropping. The special effects and realism are commendable, offering a visceral portrayal of speed, danger, and adrenaline that fans of the sport will appreciate. Some expositions will bore the experienced viewer but the major flaw in logic depicted in the trailer was thankfully executed in a good way.However, where F1 excels in technical execution, it struggles narratively. Unlike Ron Howard's Rush, which balanced racing drama with compelling character development, F1 falls short of adding emotional depth or exploring its protagonists beyond surface level. The story lacks the complexity and emotional stakes that could have made it more than just a visual ride, even though there where multiple chances to do so.Another missed opportunity is the minimal involvement of real F1 drivers. Although a few make fleeting appearances, they don't contribute meaningfully to the story. For a film rooted in such a passionate, personality-driven sport, this absence feels like a letdown.In short, F1 is a visually stunning tribute to motorsport, but its lack of narrative depth and underutilization of the sport's real-world icons prevent it from reaching pole position in racing cinema.

title: Summary
content: I'll keep it short for those who like to hate this movie.This film was never meant to be fully realistic. It mixes realism, story, and fiction, and in my opinion it was made to introduce new people to motorsport and give a glimpse into the world of F1.It's a movie first and foremost, and its job is to entertain. To do that, it needs to balance realism with fictional elements. If you went into it expecting pure realism, you'll probably be disappointed; for that, documentaries or actual F1 races exist.That said, the story could have been better, but overall the quality of the movie was impressive. The inclusion of real-life drivers and personnel was a great touch and added a lot to the experience.

title: Palate Cleanser
content: Movies like "F1" are palate cleansers for devotees of art house cinema like me. You've got to see a movie like this once in a while to keep it real and remind yourself what kinds of movies the average movie goer likes, and why they then find art films so intimidating, or boring, or inaccessible, or whatever other adjective you want to use. Everything you're supposed to feel is telegraphed at every moment in this movie. You're never in doubt as to what message the movie is trying to convey, or what role each character is supposed to be playing, or what the narrative arc is going to be. Movies like this are refreshing once in a while because they let me turn my brain off and just let a movie come at me without having to do any work, but I would feel suffocated if my diet consisted of nothing but movies like this.More than anything, "F1" is a masterpiece of editing and sound design. I might have been more impressed by the racing scenes if I knew anything about car racing, but even at that they're pretty thrilling.And just in case you were worried that a male-dominated film might make the mistake of creating female characters with actual agency, rest assured. The woman in this movie can build space cars, but she still exists mostly as a sexual object for Brad Pitt to win before the movie's over. Whew! Dodged a bullet there.Grade: B.

title: F1
content: I can't be mad at this movie. It was well executed and carried enough weight to keep me engaged. My only real critique is that the story could have been slightly stronger. Still, I was compelled, and I have to give special recognition to the editor, who sifted through all that footage to shape the final product. I would rate it four stars. The film worked, but what it lacked was a truly compelling story that made me care deeply about the characters. I understood them, but I didn't feel invested in their outcomes. The movie itself was memorable, but the people in it weren't. Even so, I appreciate the experience this film delivered, and I respect the craft it took to bring it to life.

title: Running on Empty
content: In 2022, Joseph Kosinski's 'Top Gun Maverick' took the world by storm. A sequel to Tony Scott's 1986 classic, the Tom Cruise starrer took in over $1.496 billion worldwide, the highest grossing Cruise vehicle to date. Praised by critics and audiences alike, it was widely seen as the film that reignited the global box office after the COVID-19 shutdowns, with Steven Spielberg calling it the saviour of "the entire theatrical industry."Kosinski's directorial follow-up, 'Spiderhead,' flew under the radar, not offering much (other than a great Chris Hemsworth performance). His newest effort, 'F1' desperately wants to be 'Top Gun Maverick.' The narrative is essentially the same, but swaps the skies for the pits. It follows renegade racer Sonny Hayes, who is brought out of retirement to mentor a hot-headed young prodigy and help turn a struggling team into contenders. Amidst tension in the garage and friction on the track, can Hayes beat the odds?If it sounds familiar, that's because it is. For a film about one of the most exciting and fast-paced sports there is, 'F1' is shockingly dull and predictable. Clearly aimed at the same audience that loved the electrifying dog-fights of 'Top Gun: Maverick,' it is a non-starter in nearly every regard: a film about speed that never gets out of first gear. The narrative isn't just derivative of the Cruise blockbuster- it also echoes dozens of sports films where a grizzled veteran returns for one last shot at redemption.Full of cliches, stilted dialogue and cardboard cut-out characters, it lacks the excitement of John Frankenheimer's masterful 'Grand Prix,' the tension of Lee H. Katzin's 'Le Mans', the bravado of Tony Scott's 'Days of Thunder' and the fun of Gordon Chan's 'Thunderbolt,' as well as the emotion of Sydney Pollack's admittedly troublesome 'Bobby Deerfield.'Although Pollack's film was- and still is- by no means spectacular, it boasts far more interesting, multi-dimensional characters than Kosinski and co-writer Ehren Kruger came up with. Sonny is a walking cliché, who is about as compelling and well-rounded as a blank piece of paper. Neither he, nor any of his supporting characters, display any growth over the course of the film, and the emotional stakes are contrived.Further, the interactions between said characters lack spark or authenticity, as if torn from a 'Screenwriting Guidebook for Dummies'. Conversations trudge along, weighed down by stilted, on-the-nose lines, hammering home the obvious. Instead of feeling like real people with real conflicts, the characters merely recite plot points, leaving every exchange flat and lifeless. The film's attempts at emotional depth fall embarrassingly short, drowning under a tide of awkward exposition and contrived banter.Technically, the film has a lot more to offer. Fast-paced and frequently gripping, 'F1' boasts dynamic camerawork from Claudio Miranda, brilliantly capturing the adrenaline of a race. Furthermore, the crisp editing from Stephen Mirrione and Patrick J. Smith heightens the visceral nature of proceedings. Some of the races are pulse-poundingly intense and beautifully caught on camera; they'll have you on the edge of your seat.Additionally, the atmospheric sound design immerses one fully in the roar of engines and screech of tires. Hans Zimmer's score is evocative and thrilling, amplifying the tension and excitement throughout. The soundtrack also features a handful of great songs from the likes of Led Zeppelin and Queen (though they're never used to their full potential- often dropped in with little impact and quickly faded out). However, all these technical elements combine to create an undeniably exciting sensory experience- if only the story and characters were as finely tuned.Brad Pitt stars as Hayes, a role that asks little more of him than to look cool. No one can do that better than Pitt, but charisma alone can only carry a character so far. His talents are wasted on a part that offers no real nuance, development or dramatic challenge. His supporting cast- stacked with skilled performers like Kerry Condon, Damson Idris, Kim Bodnia and Javier Bardem (as well as Shea Whigham in a glorified cameo)- is similarly shortchanged, each saddled with one-note roles of little to no depth.Despite all the noise, 'F1' never finds its footing. It's a film that looks the part, sounds the part and moves like a blockbuster- but under the hood, it's running on empty. Director Joseph Kosinski clearly knows how to orchestrate spectacle, but spectacle without soul only gets you so far. With a flat script and hollow characters, not even a star as magnetic as Brad Pitt can steer it to victory. In trying so hard to replicate the triumph of 'Top Gun: Maverick,' 'F1' fails to blaze its own trail, ending up stalled at the starting line.

title: Does Brad Pitt get cooler with age?
content: F1 has been on my radar for quite some time now, and I eagerly anticipated its release since it was announced last year for a few intriguing reasons. Directed by Joseph Kosinski, who did a superb job on Top Gun Maverick, starring Brad Pitt, now in his early 60s, portraying a currently competing Formula 1 driver, and the fact that movies based around this specific sport are surprisingly in short supply. So, how does the movie stand up? Does it cross the finish line with spectacular, high-adrenaline fashion, or does it spin off at the first corner? Well, I'm happy to say the movie is worthy of popping the champagne over the podium. I had a fun time with F1, to no one's surprise, it is incredibly well directed, the races feel authentic and realistic (in a cinematic style), and the narrative for a returning F1 driver competing against drivers a third of his age is comprehensible and enjoyable for the story. Brad Pitt is, well.....Brad Pitt, but this is not a bad thing as the man oozes charisma, equipped with snappy and at times cheesy dialogue that works for this style of film - The popcorn munching kind that it is. The characters are rather two-dimensional, but all are well acted and serviceable to the plot. Overall, I would recommend F1, especially in the cinema. Sure, it's probably going to cause major petrol heads to heavily criticize its inaccuracy to the sport, but as a Hollywood movie, I thoroughly enjoyed my time behind the wheel, sitting on my backside slurping endless Dr Pepper, that is.

title: Well done and entertaining!
content: It's a movie with Brad Pitt about racing. Maybe some folks were expecting a Paul Thomas Anderson four-hour existential journey starring Daniel Day-Lewis as a tortured mechanic searching for the meaning of speed. Not sure why the low ratings here - did people walk into the wrong theater? I came in hoping for a Top Gun on wheels, and guess what? I got exactly that. Fast cars, big egos, shiny helmets, dramatic rivalries, awesome soundtrack, and Pitt being effortlessly cool while casually defying the laws of physics. It's popcorn cinema with horsepower. It knows what it is, doesn't pretend to be more, and delivers the spectacle. No notes. 10/10 if you like your drama served with engine roars, sweeping aerial shots, and enough testosterone to refuel a jet.

title: F1: Maverick
content:
From the writer and director of Top Gun Maverick comes the same formula, no pun intended, but instead of planes it's cars. I didn't mind it though since the recipe worked in Top Gun and seemingly worked here too showcasing a racing veteran passed his prime trying to school the hotshot young buck on how it's done.Brad Pitt was a solid choice for the older racing veteran since he's about Tom Cruise's age and is also aging in reverse like dear old Tom, so it was a good casting choice. How he bounces off his younger counterpart as they both learn the trial and errors of both of their ways culminating in an explosive finale in between some death defying moments in the first two acts meshed well I thought too.My biggest complaints about the movie lies within of course it's long, bloated almost 2 hour and 40 minute runtime. Listen Hollywood, if your movie isn't on track to becoming a timeless classic then you need to cut down your runtime and respect folks time. That is all. Also some of the pacing felt a bit long and drawn out too in light of the lengthiness of this film.Ultimately, F1 was a solid action drama within the racing genre that knew what it set out to do in delivering the goods in a solid action experience
and executed it pretty well for the most part.

title: Good Summer Fun
content: By all accounts this is a high quality summer flick F1 racing movie. A lot of effort was put into the construction of the film which should be appreciated in and of itself considering how rushed some movies are made nowadays. The cinematography and overall immersive experience were excellent and corners were not cut either by having Hans Zimmer in on the music.Brad Pitt is aging like fine wine with arguably some of his best work coming in his last couple of movies. While Pitt carries the show the supporting cast is more than adequate.There are however some glaring weak spots in this movie. The whole bringing a retired washed up arrogant athlete out of retirement schtick has been used many times and this one does not offer anything unique in that area at all. Further the tricks Sonny Hayes uses racing , while admittedly pretty comical at first, become the main theme and strategy of the entire racing teams' story. It got to be a little repetitive and the team needed at least some other new creative way of winning than an F1 version of rope-a-dope. I can't imagine serious F1 fans were thrilled with some of the antics used over and over.Still, F1 offers a much higher than average summer flick quality film. It is unfortunately limited with how good it can be simply based on the fact that it is a pretty mediocre story being told.

title: Unmemorable
content: The fact that this movie currently has over 7 stars shows how unreliable this platform is. Those of us who have used it for a couple of decades have seen a sharp decline in the accuracy and quality of reviews. I wonder why!This movie is a great example. I remember when receiving just under 8 stars was a major win for any movie. Now I feel like so many undeserving films get there. There is barely an enjoyable or deep plot line going on in F1. There isn't much substance to the backstory of the relevant characters, the characters themselves, the dialogue and relationships between them, the events themselves, etc. The movie was frankly boring. I felt uninspired and unmoved while watching it. It felt like nothing was really happening, or that it happened from afar. I wasn't invested in it at all.The romance between Sonny and Kate was underdeveloped and unexciting. Brad Pitt's character did goofy things like lie down on the white couch in his break room with his shirt off making a silly face that I suppose we as the audience were supposed to connect with. Maybe his shirt being off was supposed to make up for the movie's lack of substance.There was nothing deep or immersive about this movie. Sonny made one face practically the entire time. Overall it is unmemorable and I wonder why any production company would invest so much time and money into something that will barely have an impact on its audience. That seems to be a trend these days...

title: Another good film from a good formula.
content: This film was very cool, made you feel like you wanted to be in the seat. Story was generic but well polished - worth the watch. Many moments made me feel tense and got my heart going.8/10 Acting - Obviously, a film with Brad Pitt is likely to have good acting. Actors were into it, and they put on a believable performance. Simply: nice.6/10 Characters - Again, pretty generic characters: a scarred veteran, a newbie with potential, a perfect love interest, etc.6/10 Story - Very gimmicky, just like Top Gun, Le Mans '66 and Ferrari - the average sporting success film formula - but, also like the previous films, very polished and easy to follow.8/10 Sound - It's hard to go wrong with Hans Zimmer. It was nice to be met with a diverse film score (and solid sound design) that keeps you tense and on the edge of your seat.7/10 Cinematography - Although not given much of an opportunity to prove itself with many of those lovely scenic shots, the cinematography was solid and it was easy to tell what was going on. Not as good as Ferrari or Le Mans '66 however.Overall, very entertaining film.

title: Such a great ride
content: This movie could easily have gone wrong for me, since F1 is one of the sports I care about the least, but the massive production value, strong cinematography and score, the star-studded cast, and a soundtrack packed with major industry names made it impossible for this to be anything less than a good experience.They couldn't have chosen anyone better than Brad Pitt to lead it all, since we all have vivid memories of his iconic driving scenes in Se7en, Fight Club, Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, and so many other great films.On the narrative front, it plays safe. The story follows familiar paths of the sports genre, leaving little room for character development to reach more complex emotional territory, but the film works very well in presenting this universe with technical splendor, effectively placing us inside the experience of the competitions, the adrenaline, the danger, and the hard work behind them.

title: F7 out of F10
content: Elevator pitch, "F1" is "Top Gun: Maverick" for racecars. The movie does a great job making the audience feel like they are in the car with the characters. Racecars don't have the same narrative possibilities as jet planes, by design they have one less dimension to work with. But this makes for a good sports movie. You can argue whether Formula One is a sport, but F1 is definitely a "Sports Movie". It's a slobs vs snobs underdog story complete with training montages and announcers explaining the action. The music by Hans Zimmer is great at pumping the audience up. The story does a great job of portraying all the characters, both heroic and villainous, as real people. Make a Brad Pitt stop and give it a watch.

title: Hollywood version of F1
content: As someone who follows Formula 1 closely, I was disappointed by how the F1 movie leaned into a Hollywood-style version of the sport. It's clearly made for viewers who aren't familiar with F1-more of a flashy introduction than an authentic portrayal.That said, it's something to watch if you're bored and in the mood for light entertainment. The movie prioritizes spectacle over substance, offering enough drama and adrenaline to keep casual viewers engaged. But for long-time fans, it misses the mark in capturing the complexity, history, and intensity that make Formula 1 truly compelling.It might serve as a gateway for newcomers, but for purists, it feels more like a highlight reel than a true reflection of the sport.

title: Lights out! And away we go!

title: An Underdog Story with Scruff
content: A former Formula 1 driver and current nomadic racer joins a friend's failing F1 team in hopes of turning it around. While everyone in the sport, including his teammate, thinks he has nothing left, he proves that age is not everything, and winning is not the only goal.F1 racing has become increasingly mainstream over the years, gaining popularity, and this movie capitalizes on that. The story is the standard underdog arc, but with unique characters that make it engaging. The story offers insight into the sport, the training drivers undergo, and some of the science and physics that go into designing the cars. The racing scenes are executed flawlessly and thrilling, keeping the audience on the edge of their seats. The two-hour and thirty-five-minute runtime races by. A must-watch for both racing enthusiasts and non-racing enthusiasts.

title: A Fantastic Encore from Joseph Kosinski - A Love Letter to Speed and Storytelling
content:
"Plan C is For Combat" -Sonny Hayes
Watched F1:T With Brad Pitt(Ad Astra) as Sonny Hayes, Damson Idris(Outside The Wire) as Joshua Pearce aka JP, Kerry Condon(Rome,Star Wars:Skeleton Crew) as Kate McKenna, Javier Bardem(Dune Part 2) as Ruben Cervantes, Tobias Menzies(The Way Up) as Peter Banning, Shea Wigham(Kong :Skull Island, Mission:Impossible-The Final Reckoning) as Chip Hart , Kim Bodina(Killing Eve) as Kasper Smilonski, Sarah Niles(Viewpoint) as Bernadette Pearce, Will Merrick(Barbie) as Hugh Nickleby , Callie Cooke(Doctor Who) as Jodie and Abdul Sallis (Mufasa:The Lion King) as Dodge Dowda.After Top Gun: Maverick (2022), I was eager to see what Joseph Kosinski would deliver next-and he did not miss. This film is an incredible encore, echoing the heart and intensity of Maverick, but swapping the fighter jets for the raw, visceral thrill of Grand Prix racing.The dynamic between Sonny Hayes and his younger teammate was powerful-reminiscent of Maverick and Rooster, but grounded in the world of motorsport. Their tension and eventual mutual respect gave the story real emotional weight. And much like Top Gun: Maverick, this film is built for everyone-not just fans of F1.The romance subplot with Kate McKenna worked beautifully too, in a way that felt natural and earned-evoking the charm of Penny Benjamin without ever copying it. Kosinski brings his signature style and heart once again, crafting a movie that not only thrills but honors the legacy of speed and legacy. It also feels like a fitting tribute to George Lucas, whose love for racing predates even his filmmaking days.This film is not just a crowd-pleaser-it's a moment for motorsport, and it's going to grow the F1 audience even more. I'll be first in line for Top Gun 3, but for now, I'm more than happy to be seated for this. Kosinski continues to prove he's one of the most reliable blockbuster directors working today.Great Music By Hans Zimmer(The Creator), Cinematography By Claudio Miranda(Spiderhead) and Directing By Joseph Konsinski (Oblivion, Top Gun:Maverick) . A Fantastic Encore from Joseph Kosinski - A Love Letter to Speed and Storytelling 8/10.

title: Hyped and Brundell
content: I am an F-1 fan. Visually and technically this was unquestionably good if more than a little long, given the plot was incredibly simple and the script slightly laboured.One had the persistent feeling there were no time for rehearsals- interactions were wooden and forced between the main characters. Which brings us to the really awful part of this movie. Whoever gave Martin Brundell a script should have given him time to practice - maybe a year. As a commentator in real life he's outstanding, a worthy modern equal of Murray Walker. But six of us, all fans, found his scripted faux commentary grindingly unrealistic and turgid. It was so bad it cut through much of the race sequences and made them feel uncomfortable. He had no idea how to make it sound like he does when it's live. Plodding and painful, it cut through scenes like a knife, distracting from the action. His co-commentator was fine given the script. Brundell was an emotionless distracting failure.He ruined the film. I don't think a single voice has been so ruinous in what should have been a thrilling movie.

title: Goosebumps and Gripe
content: As a hardcore Formula 1 fan, I found the film somewhat underwhelming. However, for those who aren't deeply immersed in the sport, it's certainly an entertaining ride. The action sequences are electrifying and capable of giving viewers genuine goosebumps. The cinematography and color grading are outstanding, creating a vivid, immersive experience that makes you feel as though you're strapped into an F1 car hurtling around the track.The narrative is well-balanced, and the cast delivers performances so convincing that it genuinely feels as though we're watching real F1 drivers in action. I would have appreciated appearances or dialogue from actual drivers, beyond brief cameos from team principals, which could have added authenticity for devoted fans.One notable drawback is the SPX GP team, whose livery and attire too closely resemble those of Mercedes, even featuring a similar logo, which could be confusing or distracting for fans familiar with the sport's branding.Despite these minor criticisms, the film strikes a good balance overall. Its breathtaking visuals and thrilling racing scenes make it a cinematic experience worth watching, especially for newcomers seeking a taste of the adrenaline and spectacle of Formula 1.

title: A badass F1 racing movie - fun, well-balanced, with a strong directorial flair and full-on Top Gun vibes.
content: The movie's called F1: The Movies. Now let me ask you - what exactly are you expecting from a film like this? Realism? Logic? Or do you want excitement, adrenaline, and pure style? I'd say it's the latter. And if that's what you're here for, this movie delivers - big time.I keep hearing people say, 'It's not realistic,' or 'It doesn't make sense.' But seriously, are you really watching an F1 action film for realism? Come on. The racing scenes are absolutely top-tier - intense, cinematic, and brilliantly executed. Every element comes together perfectly.Huge credit to Joseph Kosinski - the man nailed the vibe. It's pure fan service for both F1 lovers and Top Gun: Maverick fans. And Brad Pitt? Dude's cool as hell.I'd say the movie shines brightest during the race sequences - they're genuinely thrilling. Sure, some off-track scenes are a bit slow, but they help balance the film overall.Final verdict: this is a must-watch - especially in IMAX. Trust me, you won't regret it.

title: Cool ride, no replay
content: This movie gave me a really good cinematic experience, but I don't think I'd ever rewatch it at home. It's definitely more of an action film than a drama (even though it could have gone in either direction). That doesn't make it bad, though! The cinematography is great, the characters are well-developed, and both the plot and the characters' ideals make sense.Some people say it feels a bit dragged-out, but for me, the time flew by - which is a huge plus. The story feels both real and thrilling, which might be the film's biggest strength.I can imagine this movie being great fun for fans of F1, car racing, or even adrenaline sports. On the other hand, I couldn't help but keep comparing it to Le Mans '66 (which makes sense, I guess) - and that's a film I'd definitely rewatch several times.That said, if you've already seen Le Mans '66, this one shouldn't disappoint. It delivers action, heartbreak, persistence, and more.The only scenes I truly wish had been cut are the romantic ones - and the completely random sunset shots in Abu Dhabi.

title: high speed entertainment
content: Summer entertainment? Well, it's summer, isn't it? I mean it's the time of the year when I have no problem sitting into an air-conditioned cinema with a bucket of popcorn in my hands and a cold drink by my side, to watch one of those films that I'm sure won't join my list of masterpieces, but which I hope will relax me for two hours or so. 'F1: The Movie' fits this recipe very well, for me. It's a film that takes place in the world of Formula One car racing, it's co-produced by Brad Pitt and written for Brad Pitt, and the director is Joseph Kosinski, who also made 'Top Gun', so he can be declared the world master of directing spectacular action films in which the main role is played by a male star who, at over 60, looks like he's at most 40 or so. Another label that could be applied to the film is that of 'crowd pleaser'. Well, if I've already revealed that I'm part of the crowd, I can say that I came away from this movie quite pleased.As I have already mentioned 'Top Gun', the script for 'F1' proposes a similar story. A veteran (aviator there, racing driver here) returns to the elite of his profession after more than three decades to save the world (there) or save the team from last place in the Formula One world circuit (here). Our hero is called Sonny Hayes and he is brought back to the top speed racing tracks by Ruben Cervantes, an old friend and the owner of the team of losers (until he arrives). Just like in the air fighters movie, the veteran must work as a team with an ambitious young man, who becomes his apprentice, partner, friend and rival. The clash between generations and personalities also has effects on the race tracks.The script is quite formulaic and predictable, but, fortunately for the viewers, this does not matter much when watching the film. The races scenes are superbly filmed and the sensation is immersive, amplified by the quality of the image and sound in the room where the viewing takes place. The cinematography, editing, music are exceptional, they give a feeling of continuous dynamism and have enough elements of novelty in each scene to not give the impression of repetition and avoid boring the viewers. Experts and fans of Formula One racing will probably find some mistakes or exaggerations, but for completely uninitiated viewers - like me - the impression is overwhelming. The presence of actors like Brad Pitt himself (to whom I can only reproach that at 61 he looks a little too young for the role!) or Javier Bardem can only add quality. These are not the deepest or most interesting roles in their careers, but it is obvious that they play them with passion and pleasure. Finally, I confess that I'm not a fan of car racing, I don't watch it on TV, and I'm not even interested in video games inspired by it. That is until now. After watching 'F1', I think I may give these a chance.

title: A New Racing Classic
content: A racing movie is a racing movie. There's usually an underdog, some kind of team dynamic, and-without fail-a gnarly wreck or two. To stand out, a film either has to go full-throttle into the ridiculous (Fast and Furious-style) or find its own lane with something unique.Enter Joseph Kosinski. Best known now for Top Gun: Maverick, he's carved out a niche with hyperrealism and edge-of-your-seat tension-and F1: The Movie is another thrilling success.I know almost nothing about Formula 1, but this film put me right in the driver's seat. The POV shots and real-time action sequences make the adrenaline hit hard. The tension is relentless, and it's all grounded by a cast that clearly clearly cares, making every moment feel lived-in and authentic.It takes the familiar structure of a racing film classic and floors it-elevating the genre with style, precision, and heart.Pure movie magic.

title: Top Gun in race cars!
content: You know what you're getting before you go to see F1. It's not entirely predictable though. Brad Pitt's character is the loose yet creaky veteran of the racing circuits and Damson Idris is the up a coming track star. The script is fairly written and I thought a bit sparse, leaving plenty of room for some spectacular racing scenes shot in IMAX.Unfortunately I have some minor criticisms the main one being: MORE RACING SHOTS! And longer too! I felt that they could have put you in the car more. The wide angle shots were great in IMAX though. I found myself looking across the screen, actually moving my head left to right to follow the action.I'm not giving anything away by saying there is a romantic connection in the movie but why is the shot of the couple hitting a wall in a passionate kiss the go to shot in the industry? We've seen that before.Overall a fun, well paced, decent summer escape from the heat wave in the theater. Brad Pitt is always great to watch and the supporting cast are well placed throughout the movie.6.5 rating. Grab the popcorn and check it out!
