title: A reluctant positive review
content: I hate F1.Not the film. The sport. The league. The competition. It's so conspicuously consumerist. The sponsors and advertisng are endless, the harm it does to the planet is immense, the egos are exausting. Let's call this movie what it is: a 2 hour 30 minute advertisment for the sport.The moral of the story is presented as "It's not about the money. We do it for the love of racing and being a part of a team." But Heineken 0.0% alcohol gets more screen time than a lot of the supporting actors. You have to be media illiterate to not see through this.I would never have seen the film had my best friends not invited me out. I expected this movie to be a propoganda puff pieceThe plot was surprisingly good. Joseph Kosinski provides characters that feel 3 dimensional and interact well. The film is targeted at the viewer that has never watched racing (did I mention this is an advertisement?) Which means Kosinski has to really lather the skript in exposition. Not a single thing is done on the circuit without commentators explaining the rules, what is at stake and the tactics that are being employed by the teams on the course. Our hero lays out why he drives in potentially the only soft 5 mins throughout the film. He does so with absolutely zero nuance.This handholding of the viewer led me to be surprised that F1 is genuinely gripping. Hans Zimmer plays a lion role in this. His score is always instrumental in setting tension. A director or editor is almost not necessary if Zimmer has penned the score. But Joseph Kosinski plays his part well. The action was close and fast. With the exception of a comical crash in which a car was turned into an intercontinental missile and sent barrel rolling over the fences - the action felt real, the driving was close and caught myself with my heart elevated and my breathe held despite myself.I still hate F1. But this is a legitimately good action film. I think I'll jump in a go-kart next weekend.

title: Review - F1 (2025) (by rocer7)
content: Hello, i am rocer7, and this is my personal review of F1 (2025)With F1, Apple delivers a sports film that clearly ranks among the better half of the genre. In around two and a half hours, the film tells a story from the world of Formula 1 - fast-paced, visually impressive, and accessible even to viewers without in-depth knowledge of motorsports.The biggest highlight of the film is undoubtedly the visual realization: the racing scenes are excellently filmed, the image is razor sharp, and the camera work is dynamic and impressive - here you can see the high production quality and the effort that went into the project. The music also contributes to the atmosphere, even if it doesn't appeal to all viewers equally.The film is also convincing in terms of acting: Brad Pitt plays his role with routine and charisma, and Damson Idris also delivers a solid performance. The story itself does not reinvent the genre, but it offers a few surprises and remains exciting for long stretches.For die-hard Formula 1 fans, the portrayal may seem too simplified in places - logic and realism have been adjusted in favor of drama in some places. But that's exactly what makes the film accessible to a wide audience, which was probably the goal. The built-in Hollywood love story wasn't strictly necessary, but it doesn't detract significantly.Conclusion: F1 is a gripping sports film with strong visuals, good music, and convincing actors. It's not a milestone in the genre, but it's definitely worth watching - especially for those who are interested in motorsports or want to get excited about it for the first time.My Rating: 7,5/10Take Care.

title: if you like racing + Pitt this is for you
content: I've been around racing for 50 years. Growing up in NC and CLT it's racing country. I've watched many racing movies and will say this is not the worst and it may not be the best.This is NOT a documentary, it's a movie for entertainment so watch and be entertained.Like all movies, it portray's a story on the screen. It's fiction so if you want to pick it a part, have at it.However if you want to get a 'glimpse' into Open Wheels and Formula 1, you might enjoy.Brad Pitt portrays Sonny, a man who has had some racing regrets and early trauma.After 30 years, he gets a 2nd chance to live his dream. Rubin played by Javier Bardem gives him that opportunity.My key take away is that Sony is driven by his early love and passion of racing, it's the common thread of his life.He has an exchange with Kate, played by Kerry Condon that reveals his motivation to race. He is 'flying' it's a moment he's had at various times in his career that he wants to recapture. Maybe, it's only in F1 racing with the extreme speed danger and drama.We learn that owning and operating an F1 racing operation is extremely expensive. We also learn that to be successful it requires team work.As the story develops, it's revealed who is part of the team and who is in it for their own agenda.At 2.5 hours, there is a lot to unpack.I really liked the realism of the race. The camera inside the car, seeing the instruments, the portrayal of the speed and what it takes to maneuver at speeds over 200 mph.It's also about the true bonds of trust. When you sit in the cockpit of the car, you give up an element of control to a team that will potentially guide you to victory.Sony has been in enough F1 cars to immediately realize this team has all the right stuff and potential to get to the finish line at the highest level of racing.I also liked the portrayal of Kate by Kerry Condon. She's the 'Technical Director' who works with the Engineers and Mechanics to 'build a winning car'.It's a critically important role for a winning team and she knocks it out of the park.Watch F1 and get entertained and educated.

title: the live action version of 'cars'.
content: Not being a formula 1 fan nor understanding its rules, what stands out here is a cinematic work that functions on its own, beyond sports accuracy. The film opens with a perfectly calibrated drama, setting the tone and tension from the very first minute that carries through to the ending.Brad pitt maintains his charismatic presence, and his character, sonny, shines with a confident and overbearing personality. The dynamic between sonny and joshua, full of constant contradictions, serves as a strong narrative engine: precise, natural, and meaningful dialogue that strengthens both the plot and the cast. A line like "and if it helps, don't beat yourself up. A lot of people will do it for you" may seem simple, but it becomes a memorable, direct, and resonant moment.The handling of silences and rhythm is another of the film's great strengths. Pauses and sustained tension amplify the drama and create moments of full immersion. The editing is outstanding, showing complete control over the pace of each sequence, while the cinematography and framing reinforce the sense of precision and style throughout.The musical score is the only uneven point: while some tracks work very well, others don't fully convince and contrast with the narrative's intensity. Still, this does not spoil the overall experience, as the film keeps the viewer engaged and absorbed.I'd give it a 6 overall, because although it is entertaining and well-executed, it doesn't reach the impact it could have had to become truly memorable. However, i give it a 7 thanks to brad pitt: his charisma and presence elevate the film beyond a simple commercial blockbuster. Without him, the story would still be solid, but it would lack the spark that makes each scene feel significant and keeps the viewer engaged.The ending, though perhaps not as impactful as it could have been, closes a cinematic journey that blends drama, tension, and entertainment. 'f1: the movie' stands as an attractive and well-executed proposal, capable of engaging both those familiar with formula 1 and those, like me, who simply want to enjoy a well-told story.

title: An Adrenaline-Fueled Journey of Frustration and Triumph
content: Coming from Joseph Kosinski, the visionary director behind Top Gun: Maverick, I went into this film expecting a visual spectacle. I was not disappointed. You can feel his signature style in every shot, placing you directly in the cockpit for a stunningly visceral take on the world of Formula 1.The film centers on a new team, APXGP, as they fight for a place on the championship podium. I have to be honest-this movie was an exercise in frustration, but in the best way possible. I found myself completely invested in the team's journey, rooting for them with everything I had. Yet, the story relentlessly throws every conceivable obstacle their way. Every potential victory seems to slip through their fingers, and you, as the viewer, feel the weight of every single setback. It's a bold choice that builds a tension that is almost unbearable, making you wait until the final, climactic race for any sense of release.As expected, Brad Pitt delivers a compelling performance as the veteran driver Sonny Hayes, grounding the film with his seasoned presence. But the true star here is the almost unbelievable level of detail. Kosinski and his team have seamlessly blended fiction with reality. Seeing the APXGP car on the grid with real F1 teams, bearing authentic sponsorships, and competing alongside actual drivers during live race weekends is a marvelous achievement in filmmaking. It erases the line between movie magic and motorsport reality.Despite the constant on-track disappointments that made my heart sink throughout the film, the sheer adrenaline rush never lets up. In the end, I realized that the frustration wasn't a flaw; it was the entire point. That feeling of being right there with the team, experiencing their struggles so acutely, is what makes the movie so incredibly unique and the final moments so deeply satisfying.I highly recommend it. It's more than just a racing movie; it's an endurance test for your emotions.

title: Why couldn't this be perfect

title: Absolute Cinema!!!! Must-watch in theatres.
content: An outstanding film with an amazing execution fueled by peak Brad Pitt and peak cameos of real legends. Won't spoil it. Go watch it in the theatres. Birthday more wholesome.The film is so entertaining even if you are not into F1 too.This film impacted me so much at many levels being a fan of F1 in my childhood. After lockdown i have not been watching the sport much nowadays. But thing film really piqued my interest to dwell into that again.A masterclass in cinematography by Claudio Miranda. Reunion with Joseph after Top Gun : Maverick. Marvellous camera work. The cockpit POVs and wide shots were outstanding. The goosebumps-inducing score of Hans Zimmer. Man cooked big in this film. Every epic scene got into a whole another level by this man's score. The sound design was outstanding. The editing was pitch perfect.The film employs themes like Redemption & Legacy, Mentorship & Teamwork and Rivalry. Sonny is shown confronting past trauma to mentor Noah and Save Apex Gp.This film also explores the complex bond between a veteran and a rookie.The film has a pretty good narrative structure combining the both spectacle and substance which mirrors Maverick in balancing mind-blowing visuals with character journeys with emotional sequences.The on-location filming of the real race tracks like Daytona, Monza, etc made this film more epic. Huge respect to Pitt and idris for completing the 4 months of racing prep from F3 to F2. They both nailed it. The minimal CGI in the film is a big reason for its success. Most scenes were practical filming during live events. Exceptional film man. Exceptional.Javier Barden was fantastic in his role has ruben. Kerry Condon was outstanding.Ahhh the freaking cameos 😭 😭 😭 🔥 🔥 🔥.The Teamwork in the film was shown with such finesse. Be it the chaos part or the tow one. Everything was perfect.Through goes HamiltonGoosebumps after hearing this in the theatre. A legacy unfolded with that one line.He raced with Ruben which means he raced with SennaThat's it Iam done. Iam already pumped up with adrenaline typing this. This dialogue, the amount of goosebumps i got from this dialogue. Peakkk theatrical experience. There's a tamil phrase we would say which goes like " piravi palan adanjiten " which means I got the fruit of my birth. After watching this dialogue, I felt like that lol.My theatre roared for many scenes which elevated the scenes more.Iam saying this with full heart. The final " He is Flying sequence " will be one of the great scenes in the car-race genre of films. Magnificent sequence.The ending part was emotional and wholesome.Brad Pitt I love youuuu. Who would believe that man is 61 years old? Amazing screen presence.Just go watch F1 guys. Both the movie and the sport. And those who lost the sport in its midway like me, come on, let's start watching it again. Nice initiative by both F1 and apple for getting their own fields promoted.Lewis Hamilton, i watched you and fell in love with Mercedes. Sorry now. This is my time to support the Antonelli. Mercedes all the way...Kudos to the cast and crew of the film.Hooked.Must-watch in theatres.

title: Technically impressive but falls short in some other areas.
content: It's good. I didn't love it, but it was good. Just an all-around solid movie that did what it needed to do alright in an alright way, and then excelled in the areas it had to, and the resulting film is indeed good. The resulting film never feels like it wants to be anything more than a B+ movie at best, but I'll take a B+ movie that's consistently B+ over a filmmaker trying to make something top-tier and falling well short.It also means that F1 is hard to review in any depth. It's just workmanlike, it gets the job done, it has lots of exciting racing scenes, and then all the non-racing scenes play out exactly like you'd expect. It's the same safe feeling I got from Jurassic World Rebirth, but F1 is better made than that movie. I'd say both are equally unambitious on a narrative front, but F1 is just dynamite on a technical front. It looks and sounds great, and I'm glad I managed to catch it at the movies.The most interesting part of the experience was my curiosity getting the better of me, and in turn making the decision to see this at one of those 4DX screenings. I figured it was the right movie to see at that kind of thing. Honestly, I liked all but the super intense shaking. The seats rumbling, the blasts of air when the cars are going fast, and little things like feeling something poke at your back (like when Brad Pitt shoved the younger racer in his back at one point)? They're cool. I like that subtle stuff. But when it was jerking you around like a rollercoaster, that got a tad annoying. I did also get used to it after a while, and I don't think it harmed my experience. It was fun to try, but I probably won't seek it out again unless they make an F2 or something.

title: Excellent I'm so many levels
content: F1 (2025) is a high-octane, character-driven action film starring Brad Pitt as veteran Formula 1 driver Sonny Hayes, a man well past his prime who returns to the world of racing to prove he still has what it takes against a new generation of younger, faster, and hungrier competitors.Directed with sharp intensity and visual flair, the film does a remarkable job capturing the world of F1 racing. The in-car footage puts you right inside the driver's seat, creating a visceral, pulse-pounding experience. At times, the shaky, immersive camera work may feel disorienting - even dizzying - but as the movie progresses, you begin to understand the creative choice. It's all part of placing the audience in the chaos, speed, and danger of the sport. And from a cinematic standpoint, it works.That said, the runtime is a bit of a hurdle. F1 pushes close to three hours, which may test the patience of casual moviegoers. While the story justifies most of that time with strong character arcs, emotional beats, and plenty of racing spectacle, this is the kind of film that feels more manageable on a premium screen or as a home rewatch, where you can pace yourself. The sound design and visuals are best experienced in IMAX or Dolby - every rev, crash, and screech hits hard and immerses you fully in the action.Brad Pitt gives a strong, grounded performance as Sonny, bringing both grit and vulnerability to a role that could've easily felt cliché. He carries the movie with a calm, veteran presence that's refreshing to watch. However, a major issue with the marketing of this film is its over-reliance on Pitt's star power.Damson Idris plays Jordan, the younger and equally compelling racer, but his presence in promotional material was minimal. That's a missed opportunity. Not only does he hold his own alongside Pitt, but his character adds a fresh, dynamic layer to the film. Had he been better promoted, F1 could've attracted a broader, more diverse audience. As it stood, my screening was filled mostly with older fans who came specifically for Brad Pitt, which says a lot about the marketing misfire.Javier Bardem delivers a solid performance as always, and while his role adds some cultural texture, it likely won't be the sole draw for Hispanic audiences. His character, while strong, isn't central enough to truly shift demographics. On the other hand, Kerry Condon is a standout. As the team's strategist and emotional backbone, she brings steel, intelligence, and grace to the screen. She's commanding without being overbearing, and her performance genuinely deserves awards-season recognition. If there's a Supporting Actress nomination to be given, it should go to her.In conclusion, F1 is a well-crafted film that delivers thrilling racing sequences, thoughtful character development, and some surprisingly emotional moments. Its flaws lie mostly in its length and its promotional strategy, not in its content. If you're a fan of racing, underdog stories, or simply want to see Brad Pitt take on a new kind of role, this one's worth checking out. Just be ready for the long haul - and maybe a few dizzy moments.

title: The best racing movie in a long time
content: I love Joseph Kosinski's work on all of his films, and in my opinion; this one's the best. Brad Pitt is amazing as Sonny Hayes, and Damson Idris is amazing as well. The movie just felt real, like Joseph's vision on Top Gun: Maverick, the lack of cgi just brings the whole film to another level. And the story is just amazing.Hans Zimmer also delivers on an amazing score to run alongside the Apex GP journey to the top. Especially the songs created from other artists that were tied in very well with the movie, most of the time these songs by the other artists don't quite feel as great as the other songs, but these ones are an exception.Overall, it's a great movie worth your time. If you love this, give Drive to Survive a try on Netflix, it gives you the same kind of action and drama in a docu-series.

title: I really liked it but...
content: I really loved this film, though at the start it felt way too loud (at least in my cinema). I did get used to it later on. As a book lover, I noticed similarities to Carrie Soto Is Back - both feature a selfish, strong-willed athlete who insists on doing things their own way. Since I loved that book, I also enjoyed this film.There were moments when it felt slow and a bit dull (I actually found myself checking the time), but luckily soon after came scenes that had me holding my breath, hoping events would unfold the way I wanted. I really liked the tension that pulled me in as a viewer.My feelings were mixed until the poker game scene and what followed - from that moment on, I was sold! The sound design and music were both excellent, and I especially liked the female car engineer character. Overall, the film was interesting, but I think two hours would have been enough.When I left the cinema, I was genuinely excited, but two weeks later that excitement has faded a little, and I'm not even sure I remember exactly how it ended.

title: A Full-Throttle Journey That Balances Speed, Emotion, and Spectacle
content:
As someone who isn't a hardcore Formula 1 fan but loves good storytelling and cinematic energy, F1 blew me away in ways I didn't expect. It's not just a racing movie it's a high-octane experience wrapped in heart, ambition, and authenticity.Brad Pitt brings the perfect mix of veteran coolness and emotional vulnerability as a washed-up driver returning to the scene. Damson Idris holds his own too, delivering a fresh, grounded performance as the rising star. Their dynamic felt real, never forced. The movie doesn't just rely on racing to entertain it builds characters you care about, and that's what keeps you hooked between the action.But let's talk about the racing: absolutely stunning. The way the film captures speed is unmatched. The real F1 team involvement? You feel it. You hear it. That sound design? You don't watch the engines you feel them vibrating in your chest. IMAX screens were made for this kind of intensity.Yes, some parts feel a bit formulaic (pun intended): the underdog arc, the dramatic team tension, the "one last race" moment. But it never drags. It knows it's a summer blockbuster, and it owns that identity proudly while still injecting real soul into the story.If you're looking for realism like Senna or the emotional weight of Rush, this one leans more toward entertainment than introspection. But it still respects the sport especially through the subtle cameos and the attention to track detail. It never mocks racing; it honors it./////////////Final Thoughts:
Whether you're here for the roaring engines, the adrenaline, or the characters, F1 delivers. It's a celebration of racing, resilience, and redemption, and it hits the gas from start to finish.

title: A Must-Watch for Fans and Newcomers Alike
content: As a lifelong fan of Formula One, I went into this film with high expectations-and it absolutely exceeded every single one of them.This movie is a breathtaking celebration of speed, precision, and the human spirit behind the wheel. From the opening scene, you're launched into the visceral world of F1 with heart-pounding intensity. The sound of engines roaring, tires screeching, and radios crackling pulls you straight into the action, and you can feel every gear shift and every near miss.The cinematography is phenomenal. Cockpit views, aerial shots, and track-level footage are seamlessly woven together to deliver a racing experience that is both authentic and cinematic. Whether you're a motorsport enthusiast or someone who's never watched a race, the visuals alone are worth the price of admission.What truly sets this film apart, though, is its emotional core. Brad Pitt delivers a raw and compelling performance as a veteran driver with something to prove, while Damson Idris shines as the fearless rising star. Their dynamic is electric-balancing rivalry and respect-and it adds real depth to the story beyond just the race track.The film doesn't shy away from the realities of F1: the pressure, the risks, the split-second decisions that can make or break a career. But it also captures the beauty of the sport-the teamwork, the innovation, and the relentless pursuit of perfection.In short, this film is not just a racing movie. It's a love letter to Formula One. Whether you're an F1 fanatic or simply love great storytelling, this is an unforgettable ride that deserves to be seen on the biggest screen possible.

Joseph Kosinski masterfully blends high-speed thrills with emotional depth, giving us a racing film that isn't just about cars, but about heart, legacy, and the pursuit of greatness.Brad Pitt delivers one of his most compelling performances in years, portraying an aging driver who still has something to prove. His chemistry with Damson Idris is electric, and together they carry the emotional weight of the story while the engines scream in the background.The cinematography is jaw-dropping. Kosinski uses real race footage and practical effects that make you feel like you're in the cockpit, hurtling down the straight at 300 km/h. The sound design? Absolutely insane. Every gear shift and tire squeal is felt in your chest.What sets this apart from other racing films is its authenticity. The involvement of the actual F1 teams and circuits adds an unprecedented level of realism. You're not just watching a story-you're inside the world of Formula 1.Whether you're a lifelong F1 addict or new to the sport, this movie captures the beauty, danger, and raw emotion of racing like never before. F1 isn't just a great racing movie. It's a great movie-period.

title: Still in Formula, Still in Form
content: By Ian Chandler - July 24, 2025F1 marks the first critical success for Apple Studios, whose previous films ranged from forgettable to outright disasters (looking at you, Argylle). The film plays it safe, leaving little room for ambition. Despite that, it still delivers on thrills, emotion, pacing, and a decent story. It's best served as an adrenaline rush during the summer season. It is worth noting that I'll be analyzing F1 more as a film than a motorsport adaptation. Fans of the racing event should know-it's respectful, but it trades deep insight for cinematic polish.If you've seen 2019's Ford v Ferrari or 2023's Gran Turismo, then most of F1's elements are going to be very familiar. At its core, it is simply an entertaining racing drama that focuses more on its characters than cars. As a movie, this is the smart play. As a celebration for F1's vast history, it might leave some fans craving more from its source material. Driving this film's dramatic weight are its two talented stars: Brad Pitt and Damson Idris. But what sets F1 apart from many motorsport dramas is its deeper investment in the ensemble. It isn't all about the racers; it is about the team-which includes the investors, pit crew, engineering team, and more.Regarding the racing sequences, it's clear that several veterans from Top Gun: Maverick had a hand in the action. Cameras shift from closeups, the cars' side views, and the wheels. Far away shots aren't very common, which again gives its characters more priority than the vehicles. The action is more concerned with the strategies Apex must compose in order to outsmart their otherwise superior opponents. This is where the teamwork shines the most. Back-and-forth exchanges-and occasional bickering-between the drivers and crew were some of the film's most entertaining moments. Seeing the squad attempting to improvise when situations become hysterical is simply nail-biting!Where F1's excitement runs low on fuel is its structure. It's competent, practically without major flaws, but it is a bit predictable. Since it's not based on a true story, it's puzzling why the filmmakers didn't take more creative risks. It doesn't need to reinvent the wheel, but even a shred of extra creativity would have made it miles better. With that said, what we have is a reasonably enjoyable movie that, if not the most imaginative, excels at what it sets out to do.F1 may not finish in first place, but it's still a winner in terms of delivering a wildly entertaining (and sometimes funny) picture with a lovable cast and exciting races. It is further enhanced with impressive sound and steady direction. It may not hit the heights of Ford v Ferrari, but F1 still deserves to be celebrated for its attempt to bring energy into an increasingly tired genre. If you're looking for an exhilarating movie that isn't too demanding, F1 is a pit stop worth making.Score: 77/100.

title: Amazing!
content: F1 is an outstanding film that completely exceeded my expectations. From the very first scene, I was hooked. I highly recommend it to anyone who enjoys powerful storytelling, great visuals, and unforgettable moments. A solid 10/10 from me!

title: This movie has gas 🔥🔥
content: Was this a perfect movie? No. But was it perfect? Yes. This is my favorite movie of the year that hasn't made me cry. Nonetheless, it gave me chills multiple times. It was quite compelling, even with instilled drama. I thought Kerry Condon and Damson Idris were really good, as well as Javier Bardem. It was very well done, overall. The racing scenes were great, the shots were awesome, and the needle drops were great, so was Hans Zimmer's score. All in all, this was everything you could ask from a movie. It wasn't too long or too short. Tbh, I could have watched it for another thirty minutes. I wouldn't have minded spending more time in that world. Don't get me wrong, there are parts of the story that were underdeveloped, but it wasn't exactly consequential to the plot. This was one for IMAX, and I'm terribly saddened that I didn't see it in that format. Well worth the price of admission. Highly recommend. One way or another, it's a must watch. Be sure to have a great sound system if you're going to watch this at home.

title: F1: The Movie Delivers A Pretty Good Experience, Even For Casual Viewers
content: 7.5/10F1: The Movie is a really interesting case for me, i think if you're an F1 fan, you're absolutely going to love it, i personally watched it with someone sitting in front of me who was clearly excited throughout the film, and i could tell this was really his thing.As for me, I'll admit i'm not a hardcore F1 fan, i don't fully understand how everything works in the sport, but i do enjoy watching the races, and as a movie, directed by Joseph Kosinski, who also did Top Gun: Maverick, it absolutely delivers in terms of cinematography, visuals, and pure, high-quality filmmaking, that was what i expected going in, and the film delivered on that front in a big way.Brad Pitt leads a strong veteran cast, alongside rising star Damson Idris, and both of them give solid performances, overall, it's a fun racing film with thrilling race sequences and engaging acting.That said, i didn't go crazy over it, partly because i'm not deeply familiar with F1, which i think did affect my experience, some of the film conflicts didn't really land for me, for example, the tension between Pierce and Sonny felt a bit forced, Pierce disliked Sonny almost instantly, with no strong reason given, and that tension dragged on and affected their racing chemistry without being developed enough, that kind of character conflict didn't fully work for me.I also had issues with a few editing choices, and the ending felt somewhat predictable, not particularly hype or surprising, still, for a movie that runs two and a half hours, the pacing was well-handled, i never felt bored, and i enjoyed the racing sequences, the soundtrack, the performances, and a story that was easy to follow.Unlike Maverick, which had a more universal appeal, i do think F1: The Movie feels more tailored to a specific audience, especially F1 fans, but even for someone like me, it was still a pretty good time at the movies.

title: A High-Octane Thrill Ride
content: The F1 movie is an adrenaline-pumping masterpiece that captures the heart and soul of Formula 1 racing. From the roaring engines to the breathtaking cinematography, this film delivers an immersive experience that puts you right in the driver's seat. The stellar cast, led by dynamic performances, brings depth to the high-stakes world of motorsport, blending intense rivalries with heartfelt moments of triumph and teamwork. The visuals are nothing short of spectacular, with race sequences that feel both chaotic and meticulously crafted, showcasing the sheer speed and danger of F1. The soundtrack perfectly complements the pulse-pounding action, keeping you on the edge of your seat. What sets this movie apart is its ability to balance technical authenticity with a compelling narrative, making it accessible to both die-hard F1 fans and newcomers alike. The story celebrates the passion, precision, and courage of the sport, while exploring the personal sacrifices behind the pursuit of greatness. It's a love letter to Formula 1 that leaves you cheering. If you're looking for a cinematic experience that's as exhilarating as a final-lap showdown, F1 is a must-watch!

title: The Trailer Is The Most Entertaining Part
content: "F1: The Brad Pitt Apple TV Show" promised a thrilling ride through the world of Formula 1 racing, but unfortunately, it falls short of delivering on that promise. The most entertaining part of the show is undoubtedly the trailer, which teases high-octane action and dramatic storytelling. However, once the actual series begins, the excitement quickly fades.The show's premise revolves around the high-stakes world of F1 racing, with Brad Pitt as the star attraction. While the idea of blending the glamour and intensity of F1 with compelling character arcs seemed promising, the execution leaves much to be desired. The pacing is sluggish, and the narrative often feels disjointed, making it difficult to stay engaged.One of the major drawbacks is the lack of depth in the characters. Despite Brad Pitt's charisma, his character remains underdeveloped, and the supporting cast fails to leave a lasting impression. The script often feels formulaic, relying on clichés rather than original storytelling. The dialogue is stilted at times, and the character interactions lack the authenticity needed to draw viewers in.Visually, the show does offer some stunning shots of the F1 circuits and the sleek, high-performance cars. However, these moments are few and far between, and they are not enough to compensate for the overall lack of excitement. The action sequences, which should be the highlight of an F1-themed show, are disappointingly lackluster. The races themselves feel more like background noise rather than the central focus they should be.

title: Full heart, passion and excitement... Must watch on the big screen
content: Brad Pitt has always been an incredibly cool actor and in this action packed drama, he plays Sonny Hayes a no nonsense retired F1 driver. At the start of the movie, he wakes up, does some pull-ups in a van and wins the Daytona race in the middle of the night. Next day Sonny is approached by his friend Ruben who asks him to come back to Formula 1, mentor the team's star driver and help them win at least one race before the end of season.Cars isn't something I am generally excited by so I was going into F1 with moderate expectations hoping to see a decent drama with a few cool sequences. What I got was a fantastic experience filled with adrenaline, surprises and action so good that it made me feel as if I was in the car. Normally, in racing films the focus is always on the driver which makes it feel like a solo sport. Here, we get to see that not only do the two drivers need to cooperate during a race, but that there are numerous people in the background who help in achieving victory.The story might be criticized as something that we have seen before and experts in Formula 1 might be nitpicking on the details of how everything works. Personally, neither of these things mattered because the direction was excellent, the story had it's wow moments where I held my breath in anticipation and most importantly it made me feel good in the end.Additionally, the music selection as well as Hans Zimmer's soundtrack perfectly fit every moment creating an even better atmosphere. The acting was great all around and surprisingly, there were a few properly emotional moments and a good number of funny jokes. To me, this is what cinematic movies are about and I recommend you to watch it in the best experience that is available to you..Movieswithoutshmovies on Instagram for honest movie and TV reviews.

title: Bogus
content: I'm istunned that individuals who are actually prominent people actively involved in the sport could produce this farce. It should have been named "Once Upon A Time At The Grand Prix". Clearly, the movie muddles together aspects of NASCAR. Dirt track, and carnival bumper cars without giving any thought to actual F1 rules, regulations, priorities or integrity. The movie assumes that all the viewers are ignorant enough to not be able to see how utterly rediculous the whole scenario really is. I could go on and on with specific impossibilites, but there's no room here. All I can say in a capsulation like this is that if you know anything at all about F1, your intelligence will be insulted start to finish. The film, aside from the cars, bares not the slightest resemblance to actual F1 racing. This stuff just doesn't happen in F1, and most of what was presented is physically impossible or a flagrant misrepresentation of the rules of the sport. BOOOOOOO !!!! Zero Stars ...PS... The plot is silly too!

title: Pitt runs the show in this unapologetically crowd pleasing summer blockbuster.
content: As a person who never watched F1 and has below zero knowledge of this sport, i was very impressed with myself. I got so invested in the characters and racing scenes that i almost forgot it was a sport that i'm totally unfamiliar with. Yes, it is a predictable, clichéd, and corny movie, but who cares? F1 It's a very simplistic, yet very efficient movie that knows exactly what it is, and for what audience it was made for, this is unapologetically a crowd pleaser made for normies, and that's totally fine. Brad Pitt's effortless charisma is the driving force, but Javier Bardem and Kerry Condor also delivered pretty good performances, it's great when experienced actors get together and play of off each other. As far as summer blockbusters go, F1 might not be the universally appealing and brilliantly crafted juggernaut that Top Gun:Maverick was(another Joseph Kosinski movie), but it certainly delivered on most of what it promised.

title: So much fun!!!
content: This movie had some incredible scenes that honestly made me feel like I was right there in the car with Brad Pitt, racing at full speed. The directing and visuals are top-tier pure adrenaline.My only real gripe is that there's no clear antagonist. Sure, there are doubters and even a backstabber, but none of it feels personal or deeply threatening. Surprisingly though, this doesn't hurt the movie much because it's still so thrilling start to finish.Now, the score? Excellent. It really elevated the tension and should've been used way more. The soundtrack, though... not it. Modern trap songs from Doja Cat, Don Toliver, and other mumble rappers totally pulled me out of the moment. I'm not saying ditch the idea of a modern soundtrack altogether-but they definitely could've picked better artists and tracks to match the energy and tone.That said, Brad Pitt still has it. He's magnetic on screen and really sells the role.Overall: a thrilling ride with a few missteps still an easy 8.5/10.

title: A Frustrating missed opportunity.
content: The opening race at the beginning was really excellent and I was grinning during all of it.Unfortunately, all the other races were borderline unwatchable for me. It was the 2nd competitive race when I started noticing it. The overly excited, obnoxiously loud commentator wouldn't never stop talking!! I just wanted to hear the raw power of these cars and the sweet sounds of racing, but it was not there. Extremely disappointing.This was more of a slightly more cinematic version of what you watch on TV than it was a film.

title: Absolute Cinema 🔥
content:
From the very start, F1 grips you with smooth pacing, witty one-liners-mostly from Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes-and that adrenaline-pumping race atmosphere. Every moment feels carefully tuned, just like a real F1 car.Damson Idris shines as rookie Joshua Pearce, and Kerry Condon delivers a grounded, sharp performance. Director Joseph Kosinski, who gave us Top Gun: Maverick, creates magic once again-this time on the track. His style blends intensity, cinematic flair, and emotion in a way that feels almost effortless.The background score is a beast of its own. Every race is elevated by the music, making you feel like you're right there, trackside. The soundtrack features standouts like:"Drive" - Ed Sheeran (ft. John Mayer & Dave Grohl)"Messy" - ROSÉ"Lose My Mind" - Don Toliver ft. Doja Cat"Just Keep Watching" - Tate McRaeThese songs are now permanent residents in my playlist.Real F1 drivers appear too, but their roles feel natural and well-placed-not forced cameos. It adds authenticity without distracting from the core story.And then comes the final race in the second half-honestly, it's an emotion. It gives you goosebumps, pleasure, and that cinematic high that's hard to describe in words. You don't just watch it-you feel it.If I had to sum up the film in two words:
Absolute cinema.

title: F1 is not just a movie, it's an experience.
content: IMAX was created for movies like F1.From the very first scene, whether you're into racing or not, this movie pulls you in and doesn't let go.The story is simple but enough.Cinematography makes you feel like you're right there on the track - engines roaring, tires screeching, adrenaline pumping. Racing scenes are so intense and beautifully shot it's hard not to grip your seat. It's one of the best-looking racing films in years.Acting is great across the board with Brad Pitt in the lead - Sonny knows every trick in the book, every gray area and exactly when to push the limits without crossing the line.Damson as Pearce is great - all swagger, ego and arogance. And his change at the end? Oh that was amazing. I didn't expect him to do what he did.And of course Kerry Condon as Kate - I love her energy, her character never felt forced. She's sharp, strategic and never flinches under pressure.The music? Spot on. Some people didn't like it but for me it's got this great mix of fun, modern tracks and a few surprises that just work.

title: A very good racing - F1 movie of all time
content: I think the F1 movie is one of the best films in the racing genre, particularly F1, to date. It has a coherent, easy-to-understand, and clear storyline that doesn't confuse the audience while delivering high entertainment value, which the film executes very well. The soundtrack is relatively well-crafted, giving the movie a distinct style. The sound effects are decent, but in my opinion, if the film had used more intense bass or deeper sound effects, it could have heightened the thrilling, breathless experience for viewers. The cinematography is quite good, with some scenes immersing the audience in the driver's cockpit, creating an exhilarating feeling, though I still hoped for more creative camera angles and more dynamic editing in the climactic moments. The actors have great chemistry with each other, and for Brad Pitt, this is likely one of his most rugged, charismatic, and cool roles on the big screen. The film evokes a very stylish, cool, and modern vibe. If possible, watch it in IMAX to fully experience the action sequences. I give the movie a 9/10.

title: F1:bumper car racing.
content: So apparently, Brad Pitt has made a Formula 1 movie. At least, that's what the poster said. What I actually saw looked like Brad Pitt starring in Fast & the Fairground: Bumper Car Drift.The "F1 cars" behave less like finely tuned racing machines and more like shopping carts with a grudge. Corners aren't taken with precision - they're taken with a thud, a bounce, and the occasional "oops, sorry mate" as if Lewis Hamilton suddenly forgot how steering works. At times, I half expected the marshals to hand out cotton candy instead of black flags.Brad Pitt, of course, plays it all with his trademark intensity, but when your big dramatic racing moment feels like a demolition derby in disguise, even his charm can't keep a straight face. The pit crew should've been carnival workers in fluorescent vests.In short: if you love Formula 1, you might leave scratching your head. But if you've ever dreamed of seeing bumper cars with a $200 million budget, congratulations - Hollywood has delivered.

title: Buckle up and race to the nearest theater. F1 is a ride worth taking
content: F1 is the kind of high-octane, adrenaline-pumping thrill ride that reminds you exactly why movie theaters exist. I absolutely loved it - a classic summer blockbuster that delivers on spectacle, sound, and pure cinematic fun.From the roaring engines to the heart-pounding race sequences, this film demands to be seen on the biggest screen possible with the loudest, most immersive sound system available. Watching it in a theater isn't just a recommendation-it's essential. It reminded me why no home screen, phone, or TV can replicate the magic of a truly well-crafted theater experience.Hans Zimmer's score is nothing short of electrifying. It elevates every moment, from the tension on the track to the emotional beats off it. You feel every turn, every risk, every victory-and Zimmer's music is the engine driving that emotion.Produced by Jerry Bruckheimer, F1 is another testament to his unshakeable instinct for delivering cinematic blockbusters that know exactly how to entertain. It's a reminder that when Bruckheimer is behind a film, there's no need to doubt its quality or crowd-pleasing potential.And while F1 may not be gunning for an Oscar, not every film needs to be. My high rating comes from the sheer thrill I felt in that seat-the kind of satisfaction that only a great theatrical experience can offer. For me, it was the best bang for the buck, and that's what truly matters.

title: Brad Pitt Steers a Visually Thrilling, Crowd-Pleasing Victory Lap
content: The new F1 movie isn't just a film-it's an immersive, high-octane experience that captures the thrill of Formula 1 racing in a way rarely seen on screen. With dazzling visuals, a surprisingly heartfelt narrative, and a smart integration of real F1 elements, F1 sets a new standard for racing films.Brad Pitt delivers a compelling performance as a fictional former racing legend making a last, improbable return to the grid. Even in his 60s, Pitt carries the role with charisma and conviction, blending grit and vulnerability in a way that makes his character both believable and inspiring. It's a classic comeback tale, but one elevated by Pitt's grounded portrayal and the film's emotionally charged pacing.Where F1 truly shines, however, is in its technical execution. The film doesn't just depict racing-it places the audience in the cockpit. Shot during actual F1 events and featuring real drivers, the racing sequences are breathtaking, seamlessly merging cinematic storytelling with documentary realism. Compared to recent genre entries like Gran Turismo (2023), F1 laps the competition by fully embracing the speed, spectacle, and stakes of the sport.Yet, F1 isn't only for motorsport enthusiasts. The film's romantic undertones and parallel storylines broaden its appeal, delivering enough emotional depth and narrative intrigue to engage even those with little knowledge of the sport. Whether you're a diehard F1 fan or just looking for a crowd-pleasing blockbuster, there's something here for you.Seeing F1 in IMAX is undoubtedly the best way to experience its full visual and sonic power, but the film's quality holds strong across any screen. At 2.5 hours, it never drags, keeping you fully engaged until the checkered flag.In short, F1 is a triumphant addition to the racing film canon-polished, pulse-pounding, and deeply entertaining.

title: Finally someone has captured racing
content: Some of the scenes in this movie are inexplicable to me. I don't understand how anyone would think they'd work, but they're actually brilliant. Some of the racing scenes are put together as if they were previously tested but in fact I've never seen this kind of framing. It really gave me a connection with racing unlike every other racing film I've ever seen. That alone is worth seeing the movie for.But there's more to savor including another great performance by Brad Pitt. He speaks to guys in particular, I think. This time he's joined by Javier Bardem. Almost all of the scenes where these two are together, even when only talking, are as good as the racing scenes. That's also inexplicable to me and I was aghast seeing just how natural the acting was. How do these people do it?Add the score by Hans Zimmer and this movie is going on my list as one of the best I've ever seen. Can't recommend it enough.

title: A really enjoyable sports-action film
content: F1 was a great action movie, especially those looking for non-violent action. If you're like me, I'm a bit bored by the lengthy, excessive choreographed fist fights, unbelievable shoot outs, etc., but still really enjoy feeling pinned to my seat with action and adrenaline. This movie delivered that.Brad Pitt is charismatic as we've come to know him, playing an intriguing character. I appreciated the supporting characters as well, all adding value to every scene they were in.The run time was a bit much, but in a movie about racing it does make sense for there to be a lot of racing scenes mixed in with the other storylines. I will say, near the end there is a beautiful scene filmed from Sonny's perspective that did have me tear up, just imagining the utter inner peace being felt in that moment. So freeing. A very powerful moment.A really enjoyable movie worth the watch, even if it's a little long.

title: Nice Car Movie
content: F1: The Movie" is pure adrenaline on the big screen - a breathtaking fusion of speed, emotion, and human ambition. From the first roar of the engines to the final checkered flag, it never lets you look away.The cinematography captures Formula 1 in a way no broadcast ever could: blistering close-ups of cars slicing through corners, cockpit shots that make you feel the g-forces, and sweeping aerial views that turn every circuit into a work of art. The sound design is thunderous, each gear shift and tire screech vibrating through your chest.But this isn't just spectacle - it's storytelling. The film dives deep into the psychology of drivers, the pressure on engineers, and the sacrifices behind every victory. Rivalries burn hot, friendships are tested, and dreams are chased at 350 km/h. It's both a technical marvel and a human drama.By the end, "F1: The Movie" leaves you exhilarated, moved, and craving more. It doesn't just show racing - it is racing.Final Verdict: 10/10. A cinematic pole position.

title: A balanced racing movie enjoyable for all
content: We saw the early access viewing a few days ago here in the U. S. and had two F1 diehards, 1 casual F1 viewer, and 1 F1 newbie. We all 4 thought it was a very enjoyable 2.5 hour experience and an excellent, exciting representation of the sport.Of course there are compromises that fervent F1 fans will probably nitpick, but they are not so extreme that non-trolls will forgive them. (One throwaway scene shows little kids crawling on the tires of a car mere minutes after a test session...that kind of thing.) For new F1 fans, the movie does a decent job explaining the more technical aspects of the sport--such as tire strategy and new vs. Used tires--with believable character conversations.As a lifelong F1 fan, I appreciated that the scenarios of this APEX backmarker team in the various races were entirely plausible. Kudos to the filmrunners to involve Lewis Hamilton as a Producer before Brad Pitt was even cast. His fingerprints are all over the various race scenarios that play out throughout the movie...they pass the "smell test" as Brad Pitt said in interviews.If you are a racing fan, SEE IT. If you are a Drive to Survive fan who watches races once in a while, SEE IT. If you are an action movie fan who normally couldn't care less about racing, SEE IT. Great visuals, great sound, solid storyline.

title: Vroomm!
content: The best part - Tom Hanks or Mark Ruffalo didn't make a cameo to ruin this great movie! Wow-F1: The Movie truly delivers an exhilarating ride from start to finish. Whether you're a hardcore Formula 1 fan or just someone looking for a fun, action-packed film, this movie has something for everyone. It's a high-speed blend of adrenaline, drama, and spectacle, anchored by none other than Brad Pitt in one of his most unique roles to date.The film centers around the glamorous but grueling world of Formula 1 racing. It dives deep into the intense rivalries, the behind-the-scenes politics, and the personal sacrifices that come with life in the fast lane. Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a former F1 driver lured out of retirement to mentor an up-and-coming racing prodigy while getting one last shot at redemption himself. His performance is charismatic, believable, and surprisingly heartfelt, offering far more emotional depth than one might expect from a movie centered around cars going 200 mph.The best part - Tom Hanks or Mark Ruffalo didn't make a cameo to ruin this great movie!One of the most impressive aspects of F1: The Movie is how it balances several genres at once. Yes, this is primarily an action film-with race sequences that are truly edge-of-your-seat thrilling-but it's also packed with drama, humor, romance, and a healthy dose of unexpected twists. The tension between characters feels genuine, whether it's on the track, in the pit lane, or behind closed doors in the team garages.Visually, the movie is stunning. The racing scenes are shot with a level of realism that puts you right inside the cockpit. You can practically feel the vibration of the engine, the g-forces in the turns, and the heart-pounding intensity of a wheel-to-wheel battle. Director Joseph Kosinski (of Top Gun: Maverick fame) does an incredible job of capturing the beauty and danger of the sport. There's a raw authenticity here, likely helped by the fact that the film was shot during actual F1 race weekends alongside real teams and drivers.Of course, no Hollywood film is complete without a bit of cheese, and F1: The Movie is no exception. Some of the dialogue can feel overly dramatic or cliched, especially in the more sentimental moments. There are a couple of predictable story beats-like the grizzled veteran giving the rookie a rousing pep talk before the big race-that feel straight out of the sports movie playbook. But honestly, it's part of the charm. The movie knows exactly what it is and leans into the fun without ever taking itself too seriously.The romance subplot adds an interesting layer, though it occasionally feels like an obligatory addition rather than an organic part of the story. That said, the chemistry between Brad Pitt's character and his love interest (played by a strong and grounded Kerry Condon) is believable and sweet, providing necessary emotional breaks between the adrenaline-fueled race sequences.And then there's the plot twist. Without spoiling anything, it's safe to say that the story throws a curveball that elevates the film beyond being just a formulaic (pun intended) sports drama. It's a reminder that in F1-and in life-victory isn't always about being the fastest; sometimes it's about resilience, teamwork, and heart.Is it perfect? No. But is it wildly entertaining? Absolutely. F1: The Movie strikes a near-perfect balance between fan service for diehard motorsport enthusiasts and accessibility for general audiences who might not know the difference between a chicane and a hairpin turn.In the end, this is a movie that understands the spectacle of Formula 1-the speed, the danger, the glamour-but also captures the human stories behind the helmets. It's popcorn entertainment at its finest, with just enough substance to leave you thinking about it after the credits roll.If you're looking for two hours of action, drama, romance, and a whole lot of speed, F1: The Movie is absolutely worth the ride.

title: Not Always Real, But Always Entertaining
content: F1 is an exciting and stylish ride that manages to capture the adrenaline and emotion of Formula 1 in a way that feels cinematic, even if not always entirely realistic. I genuinely enjoyed watching it-it's fast, dramatic, and visually stunning.The cinematography is stunning, capturing the speed and thrill of the races with incredible precision, while the music adds energy and emotion to every scene. Whether it's the roar of the engines or the silence before a critical moment, the sound design and score work hand in hand to elevate the tension and excitement. It's the kind of movie that makes you feel the race, not just watch it.That said, like many Hollywood sports films, it does exaggerate quite a bit. Those who actually watch Formula 1 regularly will definitely spot moments that stretch the truth or break the realism of how things work in the F1 world. But even with that in mind, it doesn't ruin the experience-it's still highly enjoyable for fans and newcomers alike.Brad Pitt brings his usual charm, fitting into the world of racing effortlessly, while Damson Idris truly shines-his performance adds a lot of depth and emotion to the story. The director deserves serious praise too; it's clear they understood how to bring the spectacle of F1 to life on screen in a way that feels modern and engaging.In the end, F1 is a film that works because it entertains. It may not always be accurate, but it knows how to deliver a good time-and that's what matters.

title: Doesn't Skimp on Thrills or Character
content: I don't know much of anything about Formula One so a movie about that in and of itself wouldn't excite me, it's the potential for great filmmaking what got me to as excited for this movie as I was. Top Gun: Maverick was achievement in every sense of the word that I still feel like I'm coming to terms with 3 years later and the idea of that team going from aerial sequences to driving didn't feel like a retread but a whole new exciting set of possibilities. You can definitely see the finger prints from Maverick all over F1 but for all the best reasons mainly being how invested I ultimately was in the characters and the situation. I loved it so much and it ends up feeling like one of those movies that should never leave theatres.The film does such a good job at putting you in the head space of these drivers and I truly feel like I learned as much as was humanely about Formula One racing in two and a half hours. They find the most natural means imaginable of delivering exposition and by the time the 3rd act kicked in I felt like I was totally in sync with these characters. For as long as the runtime is there really isn't an ounce of fat to be found and a large part of that comes down to the editing. The pacing is a close to perfect as it can possibly be and I can honestly say that the runtime flew by.The filmmaking on display is astounding and for as much as I don't want to be focused on the technical presentation of a film as opposed to just being wrapped up in the experience F1 struck the best possible balance between both. I couldn't help but be consistently amazed at the wizardry Kosinski pulls off behind the camera and its in a way that feels like it's totally right for the experience he's trying to provide. I usually hate POV shots but they feel perfectly utilised here and I loved how the camera pans between perspectives from within the driving seats of the cars. It's amongst the best sound design of any film I've seen in the last few years and for as much as the term white-knuckled is thrown around these days it's the only fitting way to describe the experience of this movie.But for as impressive of a technical achievement as this movie is all of the prowess won't go far without something to genuinely get invested in and I think this film is much more impressive as a piece of writing than I think it's going to get credit for. It's undeniable that a lot of the tropes this movie plays with are familiar but they not only work in it's favour but because of how well executed everything is it still feels fresh. The dialogue is sharp and witty with a lot humour that felt totally natural and character conflict that feel totally earned and explored in a satisfying way. In a lot of other movies like this a lot of the scenes between the races are just filler to get the characters back into the cars but that's not the case here.There's meaningful character building and real tangible stakes in almost every scene and the racing sequences are raised up even higher as a result. I loved basically every character I was supposed to, even the small bit-parts felt like they added so much, and I got so much out of their interactions. Brad Pitt is perfectly cast in this role and the character is lot more emotionally complex than I expected him to be, his dynamic with Damson Idris might take an expected turn but it's still perfectly played out and even the hints of romance with Kerry Condon's character were a lot deeper than it has any right to be. Like Top Gun: Maverick before it, the script doesn't skimp on character and story and that to me is the difference between being entertained by a film and being actively invested in it.F1 ends up feeling like the best possible version of itself without a single missed opportunity from top to bottom. It is unbelievably entertaining without a single moment of wasted screen time and it's not only a technical marvel but a solid piece of writing. It's got characters I loved spending time with and a world I was left just wanting to be a part of. I loved this movie and it's so so gratifying to seeing something that gave me everything I wanted it to and I truly, truly hope this isn't Apple's last attempt at a full, theatrical release.

title: iSoul: The F1 Experience
content: F1 is not just a movie - it's an experience. From the opening scene, it pulls you into its world with a kind of emotional gravity that few films manage to achieve. It's not about explaining or showing - it's about making you feel. The adrenaline, the silence, the tension, the release - it's all there, not just on screen, but inside you.This is not just a racing film. F1 operates on a sensory level - built with precision, but driven by emotion. Every frame, every sound, every quiet moment serves a purpose. The technical execution is outstanding, but what truly sets F1 apart is the way it connects. It doesn't just entertain - it resonates. And that's why I call it iSoul.Watching it in IMAX isn't just a recommendation - it's essential. The scale, the sound, the impact - they lift the film beyond the screen. It doesn't feel like a viewing. It feels like presence. You don't just sit and observe - you inhabit the world.F1 proves that cinema can still be deeply human, even in high-octane form. It's not driven by spectacle alone, but by honesty, precision, and soul. Whether you love racing or not, this film will move you.You must experience it in IMAX. Just trust me - this is pure iSoul.

title: One of the best Hollywood blockbusters in recent years, the second-best racing-themed film after *Ford v Ferrari
content: At its core, this film is just a commercial blockbuster designed to sell tickets. Its plot lacks the depth of *Ford v Ferrari*, recycling an overused formula. Still, I appreciated the growth of the two leads-from rivals on the same team to friends who support each other on the track. That said, Sonny Hayes' personality remains unchanged throughout, which fits the actor perfectly.****While the storytelling may be weak, the audiovisual experience is absolutely top-tier! Shot entirely with IMAX cameras, the film offers a wider field of view, immersing the audience like never before-especially if you sit in the front row, where it feels like you're the one driving the car. With a score by Hans Zimmer and a mix of rock and rap tracks layered over roaring engines, the 12.1-channel IMAX sound makes every moment pulse-pounding and breathlessly intense. This is a must-watch in IMAX!****The film includes some Easter eggs for F1 fans, but they don't detract from the experience for casual viewers. Seriously-don't miss it in IMAX!"

title: Greatest Racing Movie Ever Made
content: This is easily one of the greatest racing movies I've ever watched. Unlike most racing films that drag with too much drama, this one dives straight into the action-pure machines, speed, and adrenaline. The cinematography is stunning, the sound design is insane, the color grading is top-notch, the cast delivers, and the BGM hits hard. You actually feel like you're inside an F1 car. The only letdown is the story-it's a bit typical. But honestly, everything else makes up for it. If you can, watch it in IMAX. The experience is unreal-it's not just a movie, it's a full-throttle ride.Movie of the year?

title: As a long-time F1 fan: great F1 movie!!!
content: The title says it all. I've been following F1 since the early nineties and this movie is just great. It does justice to F1 and yes, because a movie needs drama, some events are not correct. But that's ok, because this is a movie and not a documentary.Also, I am a big Verstappen fan. I even cheered for his father back in the nineties. And I give me sincere respect to Lewis Hamilton, who was involved as a producer of this movie. He did a great job.The movie flies by. There is not a dull moment. The story is ok, enough for a Jerry Bruckheimer movie. The race scenes are awesome. The behind the scenes at a race team are awesome. The actors do a good job.I also liked the quotes of drivers and team principals in the movie. title: Expected better
content: I was actually pretty excited about it going in. I love racing films when they're done right, and with the talent involved, I thought this might finally be a modern motorsport movie that feels fresh and emotionally grounded.But honestly... this one just didn't land for me.To me, it felt like a lot of old-school tropes wrapped in a very polished package. The whole "gritty veteran comes back to show everyone how it's done" angle feels tired at this point, and the movie leans way too hard into that outdated version of masculinity - the kind that mistakes emotional distance and toughness for depth. I kept thinking, are we still doing this?I don't know if the film is trying to be a counter-reaction to more progressive storytelling in sports movies, but it definitely feels like it's chasing that hyper-masculine fantasy instead of something that actually reflects how a lot of modern viewers see strength. For me, it just felt disconnected/ like it was made for an audience that still thinks being "manly" means being closed off, angry, and unbothered by anything that isn't winning.Now, I'm sure this will absolutely hit for some people. If you love classic sports movie energy, loud engines, stoic one-liners, and that whole chest-out, don't-talk-about-feelings vibe, you'll probably have a great time. And hey, no judgment - enjoy it if that's your thing.But for me? It was a miss. Emotionally outdated, and not really what I'm looking for anymore in a film like this.

title: Movies scenes
content: The 2025 Formula 1 movie, simply titled F1, is a high-octane cinematic experience that brings the thrilling world of motorsport to the big screen with intensity, emotion, and unmatched realism. Directed by Joseph Kosinski, known for his work on Top Gun: Maverick, and co-produced by racing legend Lewis Hamilton, the film follows the story of Sonny Hayes (played by Brad Pitt), a retired Formula 1 driver who returns to the grid to mentor a young prodigy named Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). The fictional team, APXGP, is introduced as an underdog competing against real-life F1 teams, and much of the film's action takes place on actual F1 circuits during real Grand Prix weekends. What sets F1 apart from other racing movies is its commitment to authenticity. Rather than relying entirely on CGI, the film's crew worked closely with Formula 1 management to shoot on live tracks using modified F2 cars outfitted with specialized camera rigs. As a result, the racing sequences deliver a visceral, edge-of-your-seat feeling that few films have achieved before. Every roar of the engine, tire squeal, and high-speed overtake is captured in immersive IMAX, making viewers feel like they're right inside the cockpit. Pitt, even in his 60s, convincingly portrays a weathered veteran who still has the fire and hunger to compete. His chemistry with Damson Idris creates a compelling mentor-student dynamic that drives the emotional core of the film. Idris, portraying the hot-headed rookie, balances arrogance with vulnerability, making his growth throughout the story feel earned and genuine. Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon provide strong supporting performances, rounding out a cast that brings real gravitas to the film. From a narrative standpoint, F1 doesn't break new ground-it follows a fairly familiar arc of redemption, rivalry, and mentorship. However, what it lacks in originality, it more than makes up for in execution. The pacing is tight, the dialogue sharp, and the drama both on and off the track keeps audiences invested. Whether it's internal team politics, media pressure, or the mental toll of competitive racing, F1 gives a well-rounded look at the life of an F1 driver beyond just the races. The film also subtly nods to real-life figures and events, making it a treat for longtime fans of the sport, while still being accessible to newcomers. Hans Zimmer's score adds emotional weight to the film, with booming orchestral pieces complementing the film's biggest racing moments. Visually, the cinematography is nothing short of stunning-sunset-lit tracks, cockpit close-ups, and slow-motion tire changes are captured with a level of polish that reflects Apple Studios' and Warner Bros.' cinematic ambition. The film succeeds not only as a sports drama but also as a character study, delving into the sacrifices, egos, and drive that define F1 legends. While some critics have pointed out its predictable plot beats, audiences have largely responded positively, praising the movie as one of the most realistic racing films ever made, even drawing comparisons to classics like Rush and Ford v Ferrari. At the box office, F1 opened strong, topping global charts in its first weekend and becoming Apple Studios' most successful theatrical release to date. With potential streaming release on Apple TV+ later this year, the film is poised to reach an even wider audience. In conclusion, F1 is a cinematic triumph for racing fans and casual viewers alike-delivering speed, emotion, and style in a slick two-hour package. It captures the beauty and brutality of Formula 1 in a way that feels both nostalgic and modern, and for anyone who's ever dreamt of sitting in a Formula 1 cockpit, this movie comes about as close as it gets.---Let me know if you want a shorter version, an academic tone, or a version in another language like Hindi or Arabic!

title: Great... but felt familiar
content: F1: The Movie" is a thrilling and beautifully shot racing drama that captures the adrenaline and excitement of Formula 1. The director, best known for high-speed action films, transitions from fighter jets to race cars with impressive skill, bringing the same sense of speed and immersion to the track.The story follows a former racer who returns to help an underdog team compete against the giants of the sport. While not based on a true story, it clearly takes inspiration from real racing culture and carries the classic "underdog sports drama" vibe.The film's strengths lie in its exhilarating race sequences and gorgeous cinematography, which make you feel right inside the car. The main characters are believable and add emotional weight, and the variety of racing locations helps sell the global nature of Formula 1.However, it isn't without flaws. Many of the tracks look and feel too similar, some supporting characters are underdeveloped, and the lack of a strong rival or antagonist makes the story less tense than it could have been.It also shares a lot with other sports dramas. Fans may notice shades of Gran Turismo (a similar character arc), Moneyball (Brad Pitt playing a mentor trying to change the game), and Ford v Ferrari (the underdog vs. Giant dynamic).Despite these issues, it's still a fantastic racing experience that captures the thrill of the sport, and Formula 1 fans will likely love it.Rating: 8/10.

title: "Americanized" Formula One
content: A $300M Formula One Commercial, a visual spectacle that lacks a soul.For a die-hard Formula 1 fan as me, for over 45 years, watching this movie is a frustrating experience.While the cinematography is undeniably crispy, the film feels like it was engineered in a boardroom specifically to explain the sport to the American audience that only recently have discovered it via Netflix.The "Americanization" of the Grid: The movie spends too much time over-explaining the basics, treating the audience like they've never seen a DRS zone before. It prioritizes "cool factor" over the technical grit that makes the sport actually interesting.Sonny Hayes vs. Reality: The fictional APXGP team feels out of place. For someone who follows real-time telemetry and strategy, the movie's logic feels "Hollywood-ized"-relying on tired tropes about "driving by feel" rather than the data-driven reality of the modern paddock.A Sonic Mismatch: The constant, thumping techno-disco-club soundtrack is perhaps the biggest offender. It drowns out the one thing F1 fans actually want to hear: the visceral scream of the power units. Instead of the mechanical symphony of a downshift, you're stuck with a club beat that makes the whole thing feel like a two-hour luxury car commercial YouTube video.If you want style over substance, this is your movie. But if you actually love the sport, you're better off re-watching the 2021 season finale or Rush. This isn't a racing movie; it's a Hollywood advertisement sponsored by Formula 1.And non the last - it was too clichéd, quite predictable, Hollywood-style sterilised, and too long. Also and most notable - if it was without that annoying music on background - I would've liked it more and would've give it a 7/10.It somehow reminded me of "Need for Speed" , and also of "Ferrari" - which both lacked to impress because were too americanized and soulless, the same way as "F1".Current rating of 7.7/10 is so overrated. It deserves not more than 7/10 but I'm giving it 4/10 because all of the flaws I pointed out.

title: It's a great movie
content: It's a great movie. I really liked it in this market of sports movies. In a genre often saturated with predictable underdog stories and formulaic triumphs, this film manages to stand out with its emotional depth and compelling storytelling. From the very first scene, it captures the intensity and passion that define athletic competition-not just on the field, but in the hearts of the characters. The cinematography is stunning, immersing the audience in the action while also highlighting the quieter, more introspective moments that make the journey feel personal.What truly sets it apart is the way it balances the thrill of the sport with the complexity of human relationships. The characters are well-developed, each with their own struggles, ambitions, and vulnerabilities. The lead performance is particularly powerful, bringing authenticity and grit to a role that could have easily fallen into cliché. The soundtrack complements the narrative beautifully, amplifying the emotional beats without overwhelming them.In a market flooded with sports dramas, this film doesn't just follow the playbook-it rewrites it. It's not just about winning or losing; it's about resilience, identity, and the sacrifices made in pursuit of greatness. Whether you're a die-hard sports fan or someone who appreciates a well-crafted story, this movie offers something meaningful. It's the kind of film that stays with you long after the credits roll, reminding you why we root for heroes, both on and off the field.

title: Man found his inner peace
content: I watched F1 in German and really enjoyed it.The main character, after his injury, is no longer a "high achiever" in the traditional sense. He was broken for a time, but then pulled himself back together, grew beyond his former self, and found inner peace. He is truly a free man-a master of his craft-living life entirely on his own terms. That quality is incredibly compelling.Soni is someone who has let go of the constant need to prove himself. He no longer chases achievements; he simply needs to drive. And yet, he's not just a truck driver-he still wants to win. But losing is no longer a tragedy or the end of the world for him. He's already experienced the end of his world, survived deep personal loss, and emerged from it stronger. What we see now is his life after that-when he was broken, but rebuilt himself into the man he is today.It was fascinating to watch his journey to the top and how he brought the team along with him. The way he mentored the younger driver-teaching him the value of teamwork, the rules of collaboration, and passing on his calm, grounded mindset-was inspiring. He showed that neither victory nor defeat defines you, and that there is life after loss.

title: F1: The Movie (2025) - Thrilling, Stylish ... With Some Checkered Line Wobbles
content: If you're looking for a high-octane ride wrapped in Hollywood sheen, F1: The Movie delivers. Directed by Joseph Kosinski and led by Brad Pitt, this film plunges you into the visceral world of Formula 1 racing with a visual and aural impact that genuinely impressed me.From the opening sequence you feel the speed, the tension, the roar of engines - it's clear the filmmakers meant this to be an immersive experience, not just a sports drama. The cinematography grips you, the editing punches hard, and the sound design (including the score by Hans Zimmer) gives you the rush of being in the cockpit.Brad Pitt anchors the film with smooth confidence, playing a character whose mastery of the sport is matched by his emotional baggage. Opposite him, the younger rival/mentee injects energy and urgency, giving the story a nice generational contrast. The female lead (portraying the team's tech/engineering role) brings smart presence and helps humanize the high-speed mayhem.For all its strengths, the movie isn't flawless. The story sometimes feels very familiar - mentorship, redemption, legacy - and a few scenes lean on exposition a little heavier than they should. For instance, the race-regulation talk and strategy breakdowns occasionally pull you out of the seat rather than push you deeper in. Still, the trade-off is largely worth it if you're watching on a big screen and just letting the machine of the film take you for the ride.In short: F1: The Movie is a stylish and action-packed tribute to racing that largely succeeds at capturing what makes the sport exciting - even if it doesn't reinvent the genre. I'd recommend it especially in an IMAX or premium format for maximum effect. For me: 9 out of 10 stars.
