title: Built for the Track, Made for the Heart
content: Brad Pitt's F1 isn't just a racing film-it's a full-throttle immersion into the soul of Formula One. Directed by Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick), the film blends real-world racing footage with cinematic storytelling so seamlessly, it feels less like watching a movie and more like strapping into the cockpit yourself.From the first lap, F1 grips you. The roar of engines, the blur of motion, the tension of every corner-it's all captured with visceral clarity. But beneath the speed lies a story of grit, legacy, and the quiet battles behind the visor. Pitt's performance is magnetic: a seasoned driver confronting the twilight of his career, haunted by past choices and driven by something deeper than victory.What makes F1 exceptional isn't just its technical brilliance-it's its rewatchability. This is a film that rewards repeat viewings. Each time, you catch new emotional beats, subtle character shifts, and the craftsmanship behind every frame. It's not just a movie you watch-it's one you feel, again and again.The supporting cast adds texture without distraction, and the screenplay avoids the usual sports clichés. There's no forced triumph, no melodramatic arcs-just raw, human stakes played out at 300 km/h. The sound design alone deserves awards, and the cinematography turns every race into poetry.In a cinematic landscape crowded with noise, F1 is a rare engine of clarity. It's a film that respects its audience, honors its subject, and delivers on every promise. For those who measure greatness by impact and longevity, this one crosses the finish line with style.

title: You Must See F1 This Summer!
content: The new Brad Pitt movie F1 is a must see for the Summer of 2025! You do not have to be an auto racing fan to enjoy this movie. You will learn a lot about the F1 racing circuit, which is a multibillion-dollar industry and extremely fun to be a fan of. I personally learned a lot about the F1 racing circuit and this movie is extremely realistic. The race cars are extremely well done and very authentic, the racing scenes were filmed on actual race tracks from England to Italy to Hungary to Las Vegas and the final race scene is shot in Abu Dhabi. This has to be one of Brad Pitt's best performances. He actually drives these race cars himself at 180 mph! The camaraderie between Brad Pitt and his racing team boss Javier Bardem is excellent, and the competitive nature between Brad Pitt and the young race car driver Damson Idris is also compelling. The female lead is Kerry Condon who works as the Technical Director which is a high tech race manager who helps manage Brad Pitt and the race team. She is a great actress, in fact the last time I saw her she was acting on the show Ray Donovan with a strong Boston accent. She's now using her native Irish brogue, and what a voice it is! There are also some great cameo appearances by current F1 racing champions Max Verstappen, Lewis Hamilton, and Charles Leclerc. Even the British F1 TV announcers from ESPN are integrated into this movie. Their voices are fantastic and the way they depict the racing action is superb. I think you'll greatly enjoy this movie!Take care, Brad P in Cleveland.

title: Punchy, emotive racing film.
content: Some movies feel the need to be complex. This is not one of those movies. Some movies, feel the need to be uber original. This was not one of those movies. And a movie like this can be absolutely sensational, if its execution is sublime and there's a clear driving force. And in this movie, it's the feel good factor.This isn't really a 'drama'. There isn't a rich plot or rich characterization. We the viewers have enough that we can imagine, with maybe our own desires what these characters are like. You've got a 'has been' or as Sonny is perhaps described in the movie a 'never been'. You've got an underdog team, trying to achieve something special. Eh voila. You've got a storyline with a feel good factor.The acting is top class, the dialogue is snappy and riddled with subtle humor, and the filmography I mean those racing scenes *chef's kiss*.This was a movie that was rich in emotion. You had anger, aspiration, love, passion. The whole deal. And it just felt everything was really well calibrated.We like to be sentimental, we like a good story. I thought we got one here.

title: Feel good tripe
content: Ah, it's Hollywood feel good tripe, isn't it. Rocky in F1 cars. The Karate Kid with carbon fibre. Typical Bruckheimer fayre.The storyline isn't particularly groundbreaking, the plot points are completely unbelievable, the characters lack depth, but the cinematography is good and you do find yourself rooting for the team.Martin Brundle's commentary is appalling - completely dumbed down for Americans and presumably people who have no idea what F1 is - and it completely cheapens it. "It's like racing ballet"? Come on, Martin. You're better than that.As for another review that called it a "DEI movie". It's not. There are plenty of black motorsports drivers, even if Lewis Hamilton is the only one to have made it on F1. HAAS have a female race engineer. Claire Williams was deputy team principal of Williams. There have been female F1 drivers, there are female drivers pushing for F1 seats, and there are women involved at all levels of F1. Seems some people just want to be offended and called everything out as DEI if they don't like it.As for the movie, if you're an F1 fan, don't expect much.

title: Old school good
content: This is what films use to be. Some how modern films are more concerned with pushing some political narrative or pandering to certain groups that good old fashion entertainment is the last consideration. This isn't an F1 documentary so those of you thinking "this isn't accurate" or "this wouldn't happen". This is a film that uses F1 as a canvas only to tell a story about triumph over adversity. And to remind people that if you put your mind to it, you can accomplish great things. That all the characters regardless of gender earned their places. One rises, all rise.War and piece this ain't, but it also ain't a bad way to spend a couple of hours and be entertained. Go in with an open mind and be pleasantly surprised.

title: "It's not about the money"
content: The F1 movie blends adrenaline-fueled action with an unexpectedly emotional core. Even if you're unfamiliar with the sport, the storytelling pulls you in with clear exposition and a genuine passion for the racing world.Brad Pitt commands the screen with a grounded, charismatic performance. He adds depth to a character that could have easily been one-note. This is a ride worth taking-whether you're a gearhead or not.

title: Not even Brad could save this movie
content: The only exciting part of this movie was the final race-maybe because it was the only scene that felt remotely realistic. But for a film that dragged on for 2 hours and 20 minutes, that was far too little, too late to save it.Honestly, this was a snoozefest. The acting was weak across the board, and I don't know what happened to Brad Pitt. This wasn't the Brad Pitt of old-maybe it's age, but he came across as stoic, emotionless, and his trademark cockiness just wasn't appealing.Then there's the script. For movies like this to impress, they usually need to be based on a true story or at least inspired by one. This wasn't, which left us with a string of unrealistic, predictable scenes we've seen done countless times before in better films. Ford v Ferrari, Gran Turismo, Rush, and even Need for Speed handled this genre far more effectively.I wish I could find something positive to say about this one, but unfortunately, there just isn't.

title: Only Watch it in Theaters
content: I walked into F1 not knowing much about the sport - but walked out pumped, entertained, and thoroughly satisfied. The film is built for the big screen. If you're thinking of watching it on a mobile or laptop - don't. You'd miss the very soul of it.The 1st half is gripping with high-octane races, sharp cuts, and intense sequences. Even though I didn't catch every F1 technical term or penalty due to audio issues at my theater, I still found myself cheering for the lead to make it from point A to B.The 2nd half dips slightly, with one bar scene and a race that felt stretched, but the final race brings it all back, delivering a punchy climax that makes the ride worth it.Contrary to some reviews, the movie does have a story. It's linear but filled with moments of character development, emotional dips, and tactical ups. And trust me, the engagement never really breaks.It may not be a perfect film for F1 purists, but as a theater experience, it delivers something rare: a movie that races, roars, and lands strong.Verdict: If you're even slightly curious, catch it in theaters. Don't wait for OTT.Final Thoughts: F1 may not be a storytelling masterpiece, but it's an adrenaline-loaded spectacle that's impossible to replicate outside the cinema.

title: Love it
content: Great movie. I haven't enjoyed a movie in the theater this much since Mission Impossible the Final reckoning and Top Gun Maverick, which this movie generates a similar vibe to for F1 racing. Brad pit plays an aging, experienced, racer that blew his chances at F1. By chance an old friend and competitors' desperate need to save his investment in F1 draws Pit back into the circuit. He's teamed up with a cocky rookie who's driven to obtain fame and success in F1 racing and see Pit as a failed, aging team member with little to share, and simply another obstacle standing in his way. What ensues is a journey in self awareness and a bonding that leaves the viewer fully engaged and rooting throughout. The anticipated formula for such a movie endeavor is thrown on its heels from the start, leaving the viewer unsure what will happen, adding to the thrill and increasing viewer engagement. On another note, I dislike movies that force woke items into the story line simply to meet hollywoods obsession with forced equality. This movie checks the box on most of the Hollywoods woke items, and while a few feel obviously forced, they don't in my opinion detract from the story line and while unnecessary actually complement it well. I highly recommend this movie and will be going to see it again soon. Enjoy!

title: Aw, dagnabbit!
content: I don't follow F1, so the racing aspect didn't interest me. The only thing this movie had going for it was that. Everything else-the characters, dialogue, performances, and music-was awful.The script is dreadful. Every emotional scene comes across as forced and contrived. Most of the characters are underdeveloped stereotypes, with no moments dedicated to establishing their backstories, leaving us unaware of how they became who they are and what motivates them to move forward. We know a little bit about Sonny, at least. The rest, not so much. As a result, when these characters faced significant events, I felt indifferent, even though the background score tried to elicit an artificial emotional reaction. For example, I didn't care whether the team was sold or not by the end. The humor was also unfunny and lazy. Overall, F1 is a dud script- wise.Brad Pitt looked bored and tired, reciting the one or two-line dialogue he was given with utter disinterest. If the lead actor gives off a lazy energy and acts as if he doesn't care for the story, then why should I? This will definitely be remembered as Brad Pitt's most lethargic performance.The movie is composed poorly, filled with tired 80s rock and forgettable pop and EDM. Hans Zimmer has for sure done better.I hear loud 80s rock music, Brad Pitt lazily delivering lines with a grin stuck on his face, and the cars going vroom vroom, but I found myself entirely bored and disengaged. Brad Pitt is also far too old for Kerry Condon; their romance was cringe-worthy. At least they let her keep her Irish accent, which was great.All in all, the movie was dull. The writing department needed to grind more to breathe life into the movie because it was all cars zooming in circles while cardboard cut outs awkwardly attempt and fail at talking like a human being.

title: Good Movie
content:
Rating - 8.3:
Overall, an insanely fun watch, as the cineamtography, sound design, and visual effects fuel an adrenaline rush that makes you feel like you are at the edge of an F1 driver's seat from the start of the movie all the way to the finish line; while the characters and performances are enjoyable, the plot and dramatic moments feel very 80s Hollywood blockbuster and predictable, as it is very obvious they are trying to recreate Top Gun: Maverick, subbing the skies for the racetrack.Direction - Good:
The direction on a macroscale is great, as Kosinski takes the scale he operated in the skies on 'Top Gun: Maverick' and places it on the racetrack, as he creates a thrill ride that perfectly emulates an F1 race, keeping you on the edge of your seat as you see how these races and storylines reach their way to the finish line; the direction on a microscale is pretty good, as this is a star-studded cast, but it feels incredibly like a Hollywood blockbuster and very unnatural, as they farm for clips; the storytelling is very 80s Hollywood blockbuster, as it is pretty formulaic and simple; they build tension very well, as the races feel like adrenaline rushesStory - Decent to Pretty Good:
The concept is more about the F1 aesthetic than the plot, as the movie puts you in the driver's seat of an F1 car for the final 9 races of the season; the plot itself is a very 80s Hollywood blockbuster and a little predictable; the conflict is very unrealistic because it begs the question, "what if Strip Weathers and Lightning McQueen joined Haas midseason to turn things around?"; the plot structure is pretty linear and a straight shot, as they go through the final 9 races of the F1 season to get to the finish line; while the movie is a little bloated in plot, they already cut down 2-3 full storylines, so this is about as concise as you are going to get the film; character writing is actually very good, as they create characters with backgrounds that are really interesting and that you are invested in to see what happens to them next, even though they might be backed by traditional Hollywood tropesScreenplay - Decent to Pretty Good:
The dialogue feels very 80s Hollywood blockbuster as it goes for hell yeah moments and tries to farm for highlights, but feels incredibly unnatural when it tries to do so; Aaron Sorkin is credited as an additional writer for this movie, and it is so obvious which scenes he wrote; The humor is present a lot and pretty good as it makes this movie feel very fun, with a lot of it coming from banter that Pitt ignites; The symbolism is a little templated as it is what you would expect from a movie about an old, formerly retired driver being brought on to race with a hot-shot up-and-coming racer; The product placement for this movie is symbolic of F1 because the entire movie just feels like a giant ad for Apple, F1, and their sponsors; The foreshadowing is somewhat present, but it makes the movie feel a little predictable and safeActing - Good:
Brad Pitt - Good to Very Good (Uses his experience as a blockbuster lead to really fuel this performance, as it's clear he has a lot of confidence on screen, and he really immerses himself in the role of being a suave racecar driver; he has good chemistry with the cast, though it is a bit of a slow start with Idris), Damson Idris - Pretty Good to Good (Plays the hotshot character well, but it takes about 20-30 minutes from his introduction to really get into the character; By the end of the movie, I really enjoyed his performance and want to see more of Idris going forward; His chemistry with others is good, and by the end of the movie it is good with Pitt), Kerry Condon - Good (A real scene-stealer as she makes her presence known on screen and has very good chemistry with Pitt as they play this flirtatious tennis match; While I really enjoyed her performance, the love interest made this movie feel a little too Hollywood), Tobias Menzies - Decent (Really isn't given any material to work with, as he does not really have any chemistry and is given very subdued lines), Javier Bardem - Good (Makes his presence known on screen as he makes it clear how established of an actor he is; He has very good chemistry with Pitt as their connection is infectious and makes you wonder if they are this close in real life), Rest of the cast - Pretty Good (Everyone plays their role very well and helps support the main cast in pushing this movie across the finish line)Score - Very Good:
A surprisingly modern and techno score from Hans Zimmer; shows why he is the best composer in the business as his marks on the score leave fingerprints throughout the film in helping make this movie feel exhilarating and fun, especially in the race sequencesSoundtrack - Good to Very Good:
Used well throughout the movie, the classic rock track, when laid across the race sequences, made the movie feel very fun, as you internally yell out "Hell Yeah"; the use of new songs made the movie feel like a Hollywood summer tentpole movie, as they tried to recreate the magic of 'Top Gun: Maverick', and they did a good job of doing thisCinematography - Incredible:
Puts you in the driver's seat of the F1 car, and you feel like a racer on the circuit; A major reason why this movie is what it is, as it creates an adrenaline rush as you race to the finish line; Takes the Top Gun: Maverick cameras and puts them on steroids, as they use even more cameras and cut between different angles to get you a holistic view of the car as it races; Also used well to build the world and the race tracks, and it makes you feel like you are traveling with a real F1 team during their season; The movie does have that grayish color saturation you see from a lot of Apple moviesEditing - Great:
The sharp cuts between the different angles in the race sequences make the movie an adrenaline rush and a fun ride as you holistically get a view of the car and the race; The editing in the non-race sequences felt polished and helped build the worldSound - Incredible:
Helps bring to life the F1 races and the cars, making the movie feel incredibly realistic, as if you were at the race in the driver's seat; Makes the movie feel like an adrenaline rushVisual Effects - Great:
Doesn't feel like they used a lot of CGI, maybe for the big pyro moments, as this movie feels like a realistic F1 race as the actors did a lot of the racing and the filmmakers most likely used a lot of practical effectsProduction Design - Great:
Considering they filmed at real F1 tracks, this movie feels like an extension of the F1 season because it is as if you are following the team during the final straight of the seasonMakeup - Pretty Bad:
The only truly bad part of the movie was that they did a pretty bad job aging down Brad Pitt because the younger version just looked goofyCostumes - Great:
The use of real F1 drivers, coupled with realistic jumpsuits for the actors, made this movie feel like a documentary in the F1 seasonPacing - Pacing is very fast in the racing sequences as it mimics the adrenaline rush and thrill of racing in F1, but the movie feels a bit on the slower side for the dramatic parts as the movie is pretty plot dense; because they already cut out 2-3 storylines, this is probably the best pace we are going to getClimax - Climax is an absolute thrill as you are on the edge of your seat as you see how the final race plays out because of how emotionally invested you are in these characters and their fate at the end of the season, though the ending is very Hollywood and predictableTone - Tone is very similar to other racing movies; steals a lot from Top Gun: Maverick as this movie has the same 80s Hollywood blockbuster feelFinal Notes - The amount of product placement for this movie mimics an actual F1 race.

Lights out and away we go. Really. Just go and watch it. Definitely worth it.

But what really sets this film apart is its heart-the story of redemption, passion, and belief in yourself when no one else does.From the roaring engines to the quiet moments of doubt and reflection, F1 captures it all. It's emotional. It's brilliant. It's action-packed. And most of all, it's inspiring.

title: Does age really matter?
content: By someone who remembers when racing wasn't a spectacle-it was war.Behind the smoke from the burning tyres, F1 isn't just another high-octane film about fast cars and faster egos. It speaks directly to a generation like mine-those of us in our late forties and fifties-who grew up watching racing legends write history, lap by lap. We've witnessed Formula 1 evolve from sheer mechanical grit and human willpower to today's world of data, design, and dazzling tech. And yet, this film reminds us that at the core of it all, it still comes down to heart, hunger, and execution.This isn't a movie chasing social media trends or trying to be fashionable. It's not polished to the point of vanity. It's visceral. Every scene, every turn on the track, every moment in the pit lane echoes a time when victory wasn't defined by brand endorsements but by fractions of a second. It brings back the rawness of the sport-the brutal beauty of speed and the silent power of strategy that doesn't always come from a headset-wearing team principal, but from instinct, legacy, and grit.Brad Pitt delivers one of his most restrained and focused performances yet-no theatrics, just presence. He doesn't try to outrun the story; he lets it breathe through him. And Javier Bardem? Charisma and weight in every frame. He brings a gravitas that feels like the soul of the sport itself-jaded but not broken, calculating but still passionate.F1 is a film for purists. For those who remember when the only thing more important than the car was the driver. It's a nod to a time when winning mattered more than eyeballs and likes, when the goal wasn't just to entertain-but to endure. And in that sense, F1 doesn't just win-it roars.

title: Frankly Shockingly Disappointing.
content: There is really very little to like about this movie. It's obscenely contrived, the F1 details are horrendous, and the story is inconceivably worse. Somehow this movie has fewer women in it than actual F1, and they consist of the promising but ultimately uninteresting first-female-tech-director-turned-1D-romantic-interest, the rookie's misinformed mother, the villain's mislead accomplice, and the incompetent mechanic whose entire story arc involves learning how to properly attach a wheel to an F1 car. Not a single one of them has a conversation with each other.There is a wealth of options for interesting story details in actual F1, and this movie behaves as though F1 is entirely uninteresting, instead coming up with unrealistic, and in some cases thoroughly inconsistent plot points that are frankly comical, and not in a good way. It dodges the details that make actual F1 racing entertaining like it's dodging canon balls, and replaces them with flimsy attempts at imitating the sport done by a person who's only knowledge of F1 comes from their wine-drunk aunt who exclusively watches Drive to Survive.If that weren't enough, the story follows a strange and disappointing plot line that feels like a desperate attempt to give Brad Pitt as much screen-time as possible. Seriously, no movie needs to feature that much of Brad Pitt. Sorry Brad Pitt. Better still, the story could have been vastly improved by a couple of very basic changes. I've never made a movie in my life, and I've only been an F1 fan for 6 months, but I could write a better script than this one. Frankly I could spend half an hour suggesting edits and the result would be more entertaining and accurate than this movie.I've never been more disappointed by a movie in my life.

title: Surprisingly Good, Even If You're Not into F1
content: I really enjoyed this film, even though I'm not familiar with the rules of Formula 1. That didn't take away from the experience at all. I've watched quite a few racing-themed movies over the years-from big-budget Hollywood films to documentary-style productions-but this one truly stood out in how it captured the intensity and atmosphere of the sport. It didn't just rely on flashy visuals to entertain; it genuinely pulled me into the world of racing, making me feel like I was right there in the driver's seat, heart pounding with every turn and roar of the engine.The storyline may be somewhat formulaic, and you might be able to guess what's coming next, but the pacing is expertly handled. It never drags, and every scene serves a purpose. The emotional arcs are also quite well-developed. The way the protagonist navigates pressure, setbacks, and eventual triumph feels believable and deeply human. The performances are strong, and the characters are portrayed with enough depth to make you care about them, which adds weight to every race.The final race sequence is particularly memorable. The tension keeps building, and the editing, combined with the music, creates an exhilarating crescendo. There was a moment where I honestly felt like I was flying right alongside the main character. The way the film captures speed and stakes-through sound design, camera work, and rhythm-is simply breathtaking.Overall, this is a film that can be enjoyed by anyone, whether you're a die-hard motorsport fan or just someone looking for a well-crafted, exciting story. Beyond the racing itself, what really struck me was its deeper message: the passion for chasing dreams, and the relentless push against limits. It's not just about who wins on the track-it's about what it takes to get there. And that, more than anything, is what makes this film truly worth watching.

title: Joseph Kosinski is your dad's type of director.
content: Following Tony Scott's footsteps, Joseph Kosinski brings his high-tech camerawork to the racetrack after the success of his "Top Gun" film. "F1" effectively captures the cutthroat, political nature of Formula One-the pinnacle of motorsports-while highlighting the multidisciplinary teamwork required to keep a car on the grid.It manages to be both an entertaining experience for F1 fans and an approachable entry point for filmgoers unfamiliar with the sport, by containing its plot to be a classic, straightforward sports story. Much of "F1"'s strength comes from its phenomenal, practical racing footage, which helps elevate it to blockbuster status.

title: Go into this with an open mind, and you'll enjoy it
content: A thrilling ride. The F1 movie starring is a high-octane thrill ride that delivers on its promise of adrenaline-fueled action and emotional drama. Director Joseph Kosinski masterfully captures the intensity of Formula 1 racing, with Claudio Miranda's breathtaking cinematography putting you right in the driver's seat. The film follows Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a veteran driver who returns to F1 after a 30-year absence to help his former teammate Ruben Cervantes' struggling team. Alongside talented rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), they aim to save the team from collapse and achieve victory. Pitt shines as the charismatic Sonny Hayes, bringing depth and emotion to the character. Idris impresses as the young and ambitious Pearce, delivering a standout performance. The chemistry between the two leads is undeniable, making their on-track rivalry and off-track camaraderie compelling to watch. The film's racing sequences are heart-pumping and intense, with real-life F1 drivers like Lewis Hamilton, Max Verstappen, and Carlos Sainz Jr. Adding to the authenticity. The use of IMAX cameras and Dolby Atmos sound creates an immersive experience, making you feel like you're part of the action. While the film takes some creative liberties with the story, it's a wildly entertaining ride that will leave you on the edge of your seat. With its stunning action sequences, emotional storyline, and standout performances, the F1 movie is a must-watch for fans of racing and sports dramas. If you enjoy films like "Ford v Ferrari" or "Rush," you'll likely love the F1 movie. Even if you're not a huge racing fan, the film's themes of redemption and teamwork make it an engaging watch. Do your best to overlook the absence of black flags in this film, and you'll enjoy it for what it is.

title: Exciting, Energetic and Intense!
content: Sonny Hayes is a retired Formula One racer who suffered a horrible accident that made him retire. In modern F1 era, Hayes is contacted by the Apex Grand Prix Team owned by Ruben Cervantes and is given the opportunity to come out of retirement. Hayes gets the task of mentoring Joshua Pearce, young but talented driver and helping motivate the struggling team to race better in the championship. However, Hayes and Pearce clash when it comes to their teamwork making this more difficult than it may seem.Joseph Kosinski's F1 has a pretty familiar story with it involving a retired racer coming out of retirement and a young racer who is talented, but a bit arrogant. It has a typical romance story in there that is kind of there for the sake of having one. F1 has a lot of plot beats you might be familiar, but it is the way these beats are executed that makes this movie so refreshing and entertaining.F1 is an exhilarating movie filled with energy and competitiveness throughout. From the opening scene with Hayes racing and the credits rolling, I knew this was gonna be a lot of fun to watch. It has the perfect opening sequence for a movie about Formula One racing.I am not 100% sure how accurate this movie is for Formula One racing, but this movie shows a lot of interesting aspects about the sport especially in regard to the teamwork aspect. The movie is about how teamwork is crucial from everyone involved and it plays a big role with how much Hayes and Pearce are at each other's throats despite being on the same team. These two racers have to work together if they want their team APXGP to do well in any of the races and once they work together better, they perform better. Also, I would like to take a moment to appreciate how many people work on one team in F1, especially when changing the tires and working on the vehicle during the pit stops. This movie shows how Formula One racing is more than just driving a race car well.Beyond the messages of teamwork and loyalty, this feels kind of like one of those old school Hollywood blockbusters we do not see too much anymore. The way the story is structured and how climactic the last race truly is here. A lot of times, movies resolve their climaxes pretty easily or quickly. Joseph Kosinski makes sure that the most exciting and nailbiting part of the movie is the final race and it leads to an epic finish. There is a romance between Hayes and Kate McKenna, the technical director of APXGP that is cheesy, but it kind of works thanks to some decent chemistry between Brad Pitt and Kerry Condon and Condon's sassiness throughout the film. Honestly, all of these elements help the movie feel so fresh because it is a very different blockbuster from what we have been getting lately. It is based off a sport, it is about racing in dangerous ways and the stakes are different here. The movie is about a career instead of saving the world from some sort of evil.While the tone of the movie is nowhere near as cheesy as Tony Scott's Days of Thunder, it does have several moments of humor. I did get some good laughs out of the natural dialogue delivery here. It relies on a lot of snark and I like snarky dialogue so it works for the most part for me. However, the movie does not shy away from how deadly the sport can be, especially a particularly crash scene which is probably one of the scariest car crashes I have seen in a movie in a long time. Even some of the people in the audience I was with gasped when it happened. The script is great despite some familiarity because of how it handles the beats of the story and the development of the characters.However, it is the racing scenes that I think most people will go see this movie for. The Formula One racing in this movie is spectacular. This movie puts the audience in the perspective of a Formula One driver perfectly. The race sequences are exciting, they are nerve wracking and they carry a level of unpredictable. I like how Hayes and Pearce struggle a lot despite their talent which helps make the races less predictable and more thrilling.Visually, the cinematography by Claudio Miranda is immaculate, the camera work is sweeping and the editing is spot on without being too reliant on quick cuts. I love the camera angles this movie uses too, especially with one shot putting us in both Hayes and Pearce's perspectives at the same time. The sound design is excellent too making the audience feel like they were a part of the race. These racing sequences are phenomenal and each one is better than the last leading to an awesome climax.The musical score by Hans Zimmer is amazing. I actually think this might be one of Zimmer's best scores because it feels very different from his other music. There is more of a techno feel to the music and it fits the tone of Formula One to a tee. Also, it is just fun to listen to even outside of the film. I have been listening to the soundtrack while writing this review to be honest. The movie also has some neat song choices, but it is the score that I will remember from this movie music-wise.Performance-wise Brad Pitt delivers a lot of old school Hollywood charm as Sonny Hayes. Pitt knows how to act so natural without coming off as too snarky. In my opinion, the standout is Damson Idris as Joshua Pearce. Idris is so expressive and energetic the young driver compared to Pitt's calm and cool nature as Hayes, but it's Idris' body language that I really like. There are scenes where he has to smile for cameras and it is one of the best "I wish I would disintegrate" smiles imaginable. I really enjoyed Idris' performance here and his character Pearce. Hayes and Pearce are very solid characters who undergo a lot of development throughout each act and they feel like different people by the end of their journeys. Seeing how these two grow to respect each other and work together better is really cool and kept me invested in them.The supporting cast is not too shabby either. Javier Bardem is solid as Ruben Cervantes who is a very chill owner of a teammate and also a good friend of Hayes. Kerry Condon has a pretty simplistic role as Kate McKenna, but I enjoyed her quite a bit for her sarcastic sense of humor and contributions to the team. Condon's character also initiates one of my favorite scenes in the movie where she gets Hayes and Pearce to play poker with her. It is a really interesting scene that shows a softer side to the two drivers and a way for Kate McKenna to bring the best out of them.I also really like Pearce's mom played by Sarah Niles. She is a good mom who is fully supportive of her son and his career, but will go into mama bear mode when something happens to him. I love the speech she gives to Hayes at one point in the movie. Niles' delivery is awesome and would make any mother proud. The cast is fantastic and they bring everything they have to this movie with each person bringing something unique to the table. Even the character development is really good which makes the movie that much more engaging.Overall, F1 is pretty awesome. It is a very long movie and I did feel the length a bit, but not in a bad way. It is paced perfectly and it never slows down entirely. This is one of the best movies to go see on the big screen because the experience is so cool. The race sequences truly did make me feel a part of the action at times. This is one movie I do not think you should miss out on even if you are not a big fan of Formula One Racing.

title: Surplus to requirements.
content: Rush, Ferrari, Lamborghini, Le Mans '66, Drive to Survive - all great films or series based on real events. Drive to Survive offers an insider's view of F1. And then there is this movie.It did not need to be made.It's like serving an Iceland spaghetti bolognese at the end of a seven-course meal in a Michelin-starred restaurant, or Bernie Ecclestone belting out the final song at a Coldplay concert at Wembley. It's superfluous. It's bloatware.The cast seem to realise this. Pitt looks bored half the time; no one would have noticed if he'd Zoomed in his lines while a gaffer held up a laptop during filming. Bardem appears visibly stunned that he ever signed up. Condon plays it like a starstruck debutant who can't quite believe she's in a film with Brad Pitt, determined to deliver a performance that would make any pantomime producer proud.All immensely talented actors, trapped inside a glitter-covered lump of coal. This is an uninspiring, unbelievable fantasy hack, set in a world where genuinely inspiring alternatives already exist - and, crucially, reflect reality.

title: It's Not About Winning. It's About Why You Race.
content: Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) delivered his character perfectly. It truly is a Cinderella story. Sonny has a lot of demons in his bag but has a consistent optimism. A kind that really describes the edge many of us gen X'ers carry. There is a drive in him that you see isn't the same as this current generation. The other team driver who is part of today's generation is very determined but has this cocky arrogance to him. His pride isn't rooted the same way Sonny's is.What's impressive is how the film subtly layers that generational clash with real emotional depth. Sonny's resilience, born from hardship and mistakes, makes his comeback feel earned, not just cinematic. The movie captures the grit, the glory, and the cost of the sport. The racing scenes feel raw and authentic, but it's the human story behind the helmet that really sticks with you. F1 isn't just about speed here, it's about purpose, redemption, and what it means to fight for something when the world thinks you're past your prime.

title: A spectacularly thrilling ride!
content: Apple's big-budget F1: The Movie is a visually magnificent film. It delivers exactly the kind of on-track spectacle you want from a Formula 1 movie, capturing the speed and intensity with incredible realism. While the main story arc follows a fairly predictable formula, the script is packed with numerous surprising twists and turns that keep you engaged.The film focuses on APXGP, a struggling backmarker team. The plot kicks off when the team brings in Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a former F1 racer from the US, to mentor their young talent. Pitt is perfect for this role, balancing world-weariness with flashes of his old competitive fire.In fact, the entire cast is great. Damson Idris convincingly portrays Joshua "Noah" Pearce, the new up-and-coming F1 talent whose drive is a compelling counterpoint to Pitt's veteran wisdom.While there's nothing particularly deep about the film's message, it successfully touches on some of the more unsavoury aspects of the corporate and financial manoeuvring behind the scenes in the world of F1. It's also loaded with good humour and sharp one-liners that lighten the intense racing drama.I absolutely love how they mixed the fiction with the real, actual current F1 drivers and teams; it's very cleverly done and makes the entire experience feel grounded in reality.I'm giving the film a high rating because, at the end of the day, it was immensely entertaining from start to finish. I was genuinely on the edge of my seat throughout the racing sequences. It's an enjoyable watch, even if the story is a bit unlikely and certainly takes some liberties with the operational mechanics of real-world F1.

title: Well, whatever that was, i want more!!!!!
content: F1 is an absolute blast. With Brad Pitt in the lead role, Joseph Kosinski directing, and Jerry Bruckheimer producing, this film delivers on multiple levels - and for Formula 1 fans, it's an even bigger treat.The movie is packed with energy, excitement, and a genuine love for the sport. If you already follow F1, you'll catch plenty of details and references that hit just right. But even if you're new to racing, it still works perfectly as a thrilling, accessible action film.The performances are spot-on. Brad Pitt shines in the lead, bringing both charm and emotional weight to his character. Javier Bardem, playing his friend and team principal, adds humor and depth. The young driver JP (who seriously looks like footballer Ousmane Dembélé) delivers a strong, convincing performance.The film has a clear Top Gun vibe - as if someone took the energy and thrill of Top Gun: Maverick and replaced fighter jets with Formula 1 cars. That feeling comes largely from the way the racing scenes are shot. You can really see Kosinski's signature here, influenced by his work with Tom Cruise, especially in how close and immersive the camera gets to the vehicles, the speed, and the emotion.Visually, the film is outstanding. The racing scenes are dynamic, intense and beautifully captured. The pacing, cinematography and sound design all work together to create something that feels alive. The music lifts it even further, making the whole experience pulse with energy.In short: a solid script, engaging story, fantastic soundtrack, and a film that stands out whether you're an F1 nerd or just looking for a great night at the movies. title: F1: Pure Speed, Pure Cinema
content: F1 is built for the big screen - fast, loud, and relentless. The director knows exactly how to frame speed, every corner and straight turned into pure adrenaline. The cinematography makes you feel the rush of the track, while the music blends perfectly with the roar of the engines, keeping the film in constant motion.Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes is the heart of it all. A veteran returning after three decades, Sonny isn't back for fame or fortune - he's chasing the dream that slipped away. Pitt captures the grit of a man who refuses to fade, turning this into a story of redemption as much as racing.The POV sequences are where the film truly takes off. For moments, it feels like you're in the cockpit yourself - the car tearing through straights at 200mph, every shift of the wheel rattling through your chest. It's pure adrenaline, the kind of thrill you can only experience in a cinema.By the time the final lap arrives, everything has built to this moment. Sonny Hayes, back where he belongs, pushing forward with everything he has. And as the engines roar and the crowd erupts, the film leaves you with one clear image: Sonny isn't racing anymore - he's flying.

title: ¡Ay dios mío!
content: The film is exciting, as it is both funny and engaging, but this is not a comedy; it's about people raising F1 cars. This film stands on its own. I went to the theatre thinking that inevitably I was going to draw parallels between this movie and Rush, or Ford vs Ferrari, but since the very beginning, I was completely persuaded away from that thought. I cannot say the same about the music score. Zimmer's score is beautiful, but it pulls me towards the work of Beltrami and Sanders in Ford vs Ferrari. In music is OK to take a theme from other authors and incorporate it into own own work; Beethoven was influenced by Mozart and copied pages from Mozart's work, and it is not considered plagiarism. I am not saying that Zimmer copied from the Ford vs Ferrari OST, but at times, the music score of F1 reminded me of Ford vs Ferrari, and I would have liked not to be reminded. I loved the characters played by Pitt and Idris, and the psychology of their interaction. I am a great fan of Kerry since the days of "I, Claudius", and Javier, what can I say; we both have the same roots, and when he said "¡Ay dios mío!" I joined one half of the theater in laughter and assumed we all shared the same origins! I think this film broke barriers, and I hope, I have this little tiny hope, there is going to be an F1 II.

title: High-octane and blood-pumping!
content: After quite a few mediocre attempts, Apple finally has a big-budget hit on their hands. F1: THE MOVIE is, you guessed it, a movie about Formula 1 car racing. It features Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), an old-school racer living nomadically who comes back into the fold of F1, convinced by his friend Ruben (Javier Bardem), who now owns the APXGP F1 team. Desperate for a win, Ruben has Sonny and Joshua (Damson Idris), a hotshot rookie, working together to try and save the team from being sold off. At first, they butt heads with their different driving styles. They have a great dynamic here. I was invested in the character drama. It has some of the usual sports movie tropes, but everything is executed so well here, these are easily overshadowed. This comes from the team of producer Jerry Bruckheimer, screenwriter Ehren Kruger, and director Joseph Kosinski, who recently made the mega-hit TOP GUN: MAVERICK. While in that, you felt like you were actually inside the cockpit, flying around doing extremely dangerous stunts. In this movie, I felt like I was actually inside a different kind of cockpit, going blistering speeds, one hand twitch away from death. The camera work combined with the sound design and the electrifying Hans Zimmer score reminded me of CHALLENGERS. Yes the story can be predictable and perhaps a smidge too long for some (not me), but the rest overshadows these small flaws. F1 is a team sport, which is something that's heavily focused on here. They have also really embedded themselves in the infrastructure and lingo of Formula 1 racing, without making the audience feel lost. Pitt's last few roles have been pretty interesting, all involving looking inward and thinking about the future or his legacy. He has now become the old-school mentor-type who follows his own rules, looking for redemption, coming in to try and help out a young whippersnapper who's more interested in social media followers than actually winning. This is a sports movie to a T. Cars go vroom vroom. Tension within the team. Authentic blood-pumping races. Brad Pitt being Brad Pitt. Just like TOP GUN: MAVERICK, F1: THE MOVIE is a high-octane summer crowd-pleaser that is made to be seen on the big screen with big sound. Put the petal to the metal and get to a theater near you!

title: F1: We've Got a Driver-Buckle Up!
content: F1 sets a new benchmark for racing films this is a movie made to be experienced on the big screen.From the opening sequence, it's clear this film is something special. Let's start with the phenomenal score by Hans Zimmer. The man is a legend for a reason, and once again, he manages to surpass his own high standards. His music elevates every scene, adding tension, emotion, and pure adrenaline.Joseph Kosinski proves he's one of the best directors working today. Visually, F1 is breathtaking. Every frame feels carefully composed, every racing sequence is visceral and immersive. It's not just a movie it's an experience.The characters are well developed, each with meaningful arcs and emotional depth. That said, I would've loved to learn more about Sonny's background there was untapped potential there. On the other hand, some characters, like the "board," didn't need to be so prominent in the story. Their involvement felt more distracting than necessary.The performances are top-notch. The chemistry between the cast feels natural and grounded. Brad Pitt, especially, stands out his character brings a raw, emotional edge that truly captures the brutal reality of being a professional racer, and i don't wanna forget the amazing Javier Bardem, Kerry Gordon and the new star Damson Idris.The story blends familiar elements with fresh perspectives. While some moments feel like we've seen them before, the film still manages to surprise. And most importantly, it never loses momentum. Despite its 2 hour and 30 minute runtime, it feels like it flies(if you watch it you what i mean).In the end, Kosinski delivers again. F1 is thrilling, emotional, and nearly flawless. It's a must see for movie lovers and racing fans alike.

title: In a word, WOW
content: There are some movies that should be seen in the theater, and this is one of those. Wow. The sheer excitement of "being there" on the track in an F1 race is enough to go see this film, but the plot is pretty darn good too. It's a classic "underdog" film where all bets are against the guy we like the most, namely a "has been" and perhaps even "never been," named Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), who plays a "Gladiator"-esque role as a highly skilled driver with a colorful history, and a lifelong mission that isn't what it seems. He states several times "it isn't about the money," and he proves it. But what IS it about? That, it turns out, is a question worth answering, and worth waiting for. Sonny Hayes is at once a nobody, and everybody. Emotionally flat, and yet emotionally deep. Driven, and yet a wanderer. A loner, and yet a natural leader. I loved seeing the many contradictions play out. The writing and acting in this film are both terrific. A must see if you enjoy a good action film. At 2.5 hours, it's a long film, but it goes by fast. VERY fast.

title: GPT can output a better storyline
content: ---**Title:** *The Final Lap***Genre:** Prestige Sports Drama**Script Overview:**In *The Final Lap*, former Formula 1 world champion **Jack Rainer** re-enters the paddock after a decade in exile. Once the sport's golden boy, Jack left after a catastrophic crash and the death of his brother-his teammate and closest rival. When he's quietly recruited by **Stratus GP**, the smallest team on the grid, it's not for a title run but to mentor and co-drive alongside their rising star: **Theo Martín**, a fiercely talented young driver with immense potential-but no real leadership around him.Theo isn't reckless-he's *hungry*. A student of the sport, laser-focused and ambitious, but stifled by a team that can't match his aspirations. Jack recognizes in him a reflection of his younger self-not in arrogance, but in the loneliness that comes with chasing perfection. As Jack works with the engineers to refine the car and stabilize the team culture, he also helps Theo navigate the political, psychological, and ethical weight of the sport.What emerges is not a rivalry, but a quiet bond forged through shared sacrifice. Across the season, Stratus GP begins to punch above its weight-winning respect, then points, then races. But in the final Grand Prix, when corporate interests demand one of them stand down, Jack faces a final choice: legacy or legacy-building.With rich cinematography, subtle performances, and visceral track sequences, *The Final Lap* isn't just about winning-it's about what we pass on when the race is almost over.---

title: The Carnival of IMAX Movies
content: This movie embodies the value of the existence of cinemas, especially for IMAX screens. At a time when streaming is starting to gradually become mainstream, this movie deserves to be watched in the cinema rather than on a TV or small screen on a mobile phone.The plot of the movie is very old-fashioned and not worth mentioning, but the effect of the actual shooting in the F1 stadium beats other racing movies. For viewers who have not been to F1 before, the effect of watching in the cinema is enough to arouse their interest in going to the scene. Some of the footage taken from the perspective of a racer is even more shocking than watching it live. For viewers who don't have a concept of F1 races, can also learn some basic rules and some off-field tidbits.Personally, I think that cinemas should focus on movie and television with shocking audio-visual effects and some large-scale sports competitions, live broadcasts of large-scale performances or post-production movies. Other movie and television dramas with average audiovisual effects focus on streaming.

title: For the One Thousandth Second
content: In our current world of desensitization, there're only three things that can stimulate the audience: wealth, extreme action, and Brad Pitt's chest. To start, every single shot in the movie looks expensive, whether the content - the gargantuan stadiums, the high-tech paddocks, the opulent parties, the fancy clothing - or the execution of the shots themselves. I can feel the stacks of dollars burning with every race shown but the budget for cinematography does not go to waste, as we see a textbook display of the car race from first-person povs, frontal close-ups, quick panels, tracking shots, side by the wheels, wide and panoramic, all in surgically clean cuts and enchanted by Hans Zimmer's rhythmic but ambient accompaniment. If there's one word for the film's action sequences, it'd be ravishing. What's sexier than all the maneuvers and the technology is of course Brad Pitt - god this man's the epitome of peak manhood. In terms of characterization, Joseph Kosinski is basically repeating the formula of duo protagonist + belle + friend/foil from Top Gun Maverick but upgrading it. To my surprise, despite all the gasoline and adrenaline, my favorite scene in the movie would be the three-way poker, which is an absolute brilliant letting-go of the characters and allowing their chemistry to naturally shine.Now that only makes an above-average commercial film; what differentiates F1 from the others in my opinion is that it captures the sport's essence and spreads it with sincere passion. Down to the most minute loophole, strategy, teamwork and up to actual history, business negotiation, worldview, the movie is anything short of ambitious. However, with masterful script writing and mesmerizing directorial execution, the story is neither too documentary or fictional, too action-packed or sentimental, too rigidly paced or wildly twisted. Most importantly, F1 is made with genuine love of the sport and that is contagious. You can feel it in the silence and the wind, the friction and the roars.

title: What Walt's Watching
content: I like to think outside the box office when it comes to being a prolific movie reviewer.Funny this is in this new paradigm we live in post pandemic where movies only have weeks not months to make a splash at the box office before they are shipped off to DVD or a streaming platform."F1: The Movie" is definitely going to do that because it's a product of Apple & Warner Bros, but do yourself a favour and see this on the biggest screen."F1" opens with amazing footage from the 24 hour Daytona race.By the end of this flick you will believe Brad Pitt can drive.The running gag in this action movie is just how old he is making a come back into Formula One, one of the most physically challenging forms of race car driving with speeds in excess of 300 kilometres per hour and G forces that are seriously painful.The old man of the track is laughingly referred to by his teammate as "Chuck Norris" amongst other derogatory comments.The silly thing is Pitt (at 62 in real life) is doing the impossible mission (a bit like Tom Cruise) because no one in the history of the sport has ever made a comeback as a Senior.However, Pitt is still in remarkably fit condition and is acting a role a few years younger, which he can pull off successfully.Irish actress Kerry Condon represents a breakthrough for women in her role as "Kate McKenna" and provides Pitt a love interest 20 years his junior...it happens.This all adds to the human drama behind the scenes, but doesn't detract from the action.Watch out for: A stream of real F1 drivers and other high profile people from the world of Motor Sport making cameos."F1"'s featured team is called "APX GP", but they are not the apex predators when this movie opens with Javier Bardem's "Ruben Cervantes" offering a juicy deal to washed up driver Sonny Hayes (Pitt "World War Z") to help tutor this losing teams rookie driver "Joshua Pearce", "JP" ("Everyone's gotta have a nickname") Damson Idris.Idris is definitely a star to watch.TBH: it helps to love F1 going into this film because 90% of this movie is stunningly photographed at a racetrack somewhere in the world from the UK to the final Grand Prix in Abu Dhabi.Only a fraction of this epic 2 hour 35 min motorsport car action is filmed away from the races.I applaud the director Joseph Kosinski ("Top Gun: Maverick") for keeping it so immersive and engaging.You feel like you are at the wheel of one of these beasts as the sound of engines throbs through the cinema.Even if the script is predictable, the acting is fine and the cinematography first class making "F1: The Movie" one of the best in this genre.

title: Edge of My Seat
content: F1 is an intense, exhilarating film that truly puts you in the driver's seat. From the opening scene, it grabs hold and doesn't let go, pulling you into a world of raw speed, split-second decisions, and fierce competition. Every hair-raising turn and breathtaking overtake is shot with such skill that I felt like I was right there on the track. Beyond the adrenaline, the story is deeply inspiring, showing the dedication, sacrifice, and sheer willpower it takes to chase greatness. The driver's perspective is brilliantly captured, making each moment personal and gripping. I found myself on the edge of my seat more times than I could count, completely absorbed by both the racing and the human drama behind it. When the credits finally rolled, my heart was still racing-and I couldn't help but think: I need to see this again. F1 isn't just a movie; it's an experience that reminds you why we love to watch people push the limits.

title: Exhilarating thrill ride
content: I must say I was pleasantly surprised by this film. Sports movies can be quite hit and miss and I'm not particularly an F1 fan, so I wasn't expecting to get drawn into this as much as I did. Despite having the relatively formulaic sports movie story beats, the exhilarating racing scenes, top notch direction, and solid characters give it a distinct edge.The story follows Brad Pitt's Sonny Hayes, a maverick racing driver recruited by his old friend to help a struggling Formula 1 team. As he puts various cars out of commission and noses out of joint, it is to nobody's surprise that he sees personal growth whilst also changing those around him. It's not a particularly ground breaking story in terms of its themes. You'll find similar character development and story beats in dozens of sports movies. That being said, there are a few turns that I wasn't expecting and enough sub plots to keep things interesting.The highlight of this film is really the racing elements though. As mentioned I'm not an F1 fan, but I found them thoroughly engaging and very well executed. You get a real sense of the thrill and danger of racing, and also a great insight into the tactics and teamwork required to win a race. There was also plenty of them, yet they never felt repetitive. Every race scene had distinct character and energy to it, and really told the story on the tarmac.The performances and characters were all pretty intriguing too. Some of them could have done with a bit more depth, particularly given the two hour thirty minute runtime. Brad Pitt was a very solid lead, and Damson Idris matched him well as his teammate / rival. Kerry Condon was great in her role as the team's technical director, and Javier Bardem was also a nice addition.Overall then, this film really surprised me. Good story, solid characters, excellent direction and racing scenes. Worth seeing on your local big screen for sure!

title: AI plot
content: The plot seems to be written on the basis of an outcome of a GenAI prompt, in which the model has been trained with sequences of F1-related movies or videos. To be more explicit: the initial sequence is to me a clear recall of the incipit of the first Diseny's "Cars" movie, wheres the scenes with the accident and the cars on fire are taken by footages of the 1976 Niki Lauda's F1 crash (or from the movie that was made out of it). And those are only examples, there are few other similar cases along the movie.The result is that to me the movie does not have a "soul", it's a relatively cold recollection of ideas from other movies and the plot lacks of originality and "real" twists, meaning twists that you do not see coming...all predictable and, besides the great photography and special effect, rather boring.If my assumption is real, plot writers in flesh and bones are for me unreplaceable, none GenAI will be really as creative as their minds...

title: A gripping and high-speed thrill ride, F1 blends Brad Pitt's commanding performance with stunning race sequences and emotional depth, making it a standout sports drama.
content: "F1" is a turbocharged cinematic ride that blends the raw excitement of motorsport with personal drama, crafted with precision and heart. Directed by Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) and starring Brad Pitt as a seasoned former F1 driver making a dramatic return, the film thrives on both adrenaline and emotion. Damson Idris impresses as the young rising star mentored by Pitt, with strong supporting roles from Kerry Condon, Javier Bardem, and Tobias Menzies. The racing sequences are thrillingly immersive, thanks to real track footage and sharp cinematography, while the human story keeps the audience emotionally invested. With a tight screenplay, electric pacing, and a strong emotional core, F1 crosses the finish line as one of the year's standout sports dramas. A high-octane 9/10 from me - cinematic, compelling, and unforgettable.

title: Came in hoping for a fun, fast-paced racing movie, and that's exactly what I got
content: I'm not an F1 fan, but this film had me hooked from start to finish.The racing sequences are genuinely exhilarating, packed with tension and shot mostly using practical effects. This results in some truly breathtaking camera work that puts you right in the driver's seat. Beyond the action, the film delivers an engaging and well-acted drama that keeps the momentum going off the track. The pacing is spot-on, striking a satisfying balance between character-driven scenes and high-octane races, so it never drags.Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem, Damson Idris, and Kerry Condon all give strong performances, adding depth to a story that might otherwise feel overly familiar. Hans Zimmer's score amplifies the thrill, enhancing both the emotional beats and the on-track intensity.Yes, the plot is formulaic and leans heavily on well-worn tropes, it's predictable and doesn't take many creative risks. But honestly, I didn't mind. I came in hoping for a fun, fast-paced racing movie, and that's exactly what I got.

title: Sometimes when you lose, you win ..
content:
MOVIE REVIEW
"F1 The Movie"
Release :27th June, 2025
Genre: motorsport - F1, thriller, action drama.Great movie and undoubtedly the best so far in the genre.A must one, for real time F1 thrills across global circuits, of high octane motorsport action and great drama that's rocking theatres globally.Stellar performances by all, especially the subtle yet redoubtable Brad Pitt, the astute Javier Bardem and resourceful Kerry Condon, in a movie shot over three years from 2022 in actual locations during F1 grand prix races ..My take on the film is - the gritty and absolute realism depicted onscreen with the viewer always being a part of the action as if in real life. That's what I call - real cinema - of you being one amongst them at all times .. .. and occasionally right in the middle of pulsating action that switches strategies dangerously to achieve goals.The movie is a wonderful landmark in the genre of motorsport, full of real life action/drama/suspense and many others, which qualifies it for a prominent place in the Hollywood hall of fame along with the pioneering one "Racing" (1969) starring Paul Newman, watched at Grand Theatre, Quilon circa 1971.The movie can be introspective too, with quite a few lessons for everyone in life, on .. handling losses, over coming desperation, creating multiple strategies and trying them, the need for focus and resoluteness in overcoming seemingly insurmountable challenges and working towards it with deep conviction .. yes deep conviction.In the backdrop of the above, the broad appeal of the movie qualifies it as one that needs to be screened in all establishments right from defence to mundane banking for a whole lot of justifiable reasons ~ pedagogic and otherwise.The movie runs for 152 minutes.My rating :10/10 (flawless)Titbits
Shot on a budget of around $ 300 million, subsidised to nearly a quarter by sponsorships at the behest of Jerry Buckheimer, the principal producer renowned for a host of block buster big budget movies, the movie created a new benchmark in 'opening weekend earnings' of $144 million, the biggest in the post Covid era becoming a runaway hit in all territories for it's underlying theme of "the underdog story" that resonates with audiences much like the 2019 runaway hit "Joker" which ultimately grossed earnings crossing one billion dollars.Hollywood greats like producer Jerry Buckheimer, cinematographer Claudio Miranda, Director Joseh Kosinski and music Director Hans Zimmer all great names with proven DNA coming together once again for this epic movie.The movie features 4 fast numbers which jell with the theme and performed by great artists life Ed Sheeran. The background score too adds to the real feel of the movie.Unlike our movies where the audio levels throughout are at a deafening level, this movie is as good as it can be to the ears situationally, adding to the natural allure of the theme.An unmissable movie .. ..premnath D

Everything is there: the roar of the engines, the stress of the turns, the perspiration, and the concentration.

title: Give me just a roll of Brad Pitt's best moments
content: It's always interesting to think about the hype for a movie, see it for yourself, and then find out how everyone else reacts-at least the more visible reactions that you come across. F1 had the hype that it would be another Best Picture nominee and possibly make a billion dollars. I doubt it comes close to that amount. I wasn't particularly excited to see it, but perhaps going in with no expectations would increase the likelihood of liking it. Even Brad Pitt couldn't save this movie from being below average.Initially, this movie is getting reviews that range from very good to best of the year. It starts off well. It feels lighthearted, though rarely funny. Then the races start happening. An announcer, who sounds like they're narrating a game of Quidditch, talks over every race almost non-stop. I didn't find the sound to be very good. It felt like the races were happening, but I didn't feel immersed in them. Instead of showing the events of the race, the film only accomplishes this sometimes, and the announcer interferes with both the sound of the race and the ability to work out in your head what's going on during the race. The script makes sure you are told by that announcer exactly what is going on, and it comes off as comical many times.Starting with the first press conference, the script starts to fall apart. It delves into a series of conversations between characters where people switch their motivations randomly and even act out of character, such as when one goes from being sure of himself to acting childish, then super cocky. The characters tell the audience exactly what is going on; then a press conference happens, and the results from the previous scene are repeated through dialogue.The script treats the audience as if they are dumb. The editing inserts some out-of-place songs during poorly timed sequences, with one poker scene involving a song over dialogue-rather than focusing on the game-that feels like messy post-production editing. Many situations were confusing, and the character motivations and backstories were poorly handled. I did enjoy Brad Pitt, not his character Sonny Hayes, but Pitt himself. Even then, I didn't care what happened to his character, and the movie suggests you shouldn't.

title: F-1, the Art of Motion Without Emotion.
content: F-1 is not just a sports racing film but a thrilling showcase of speed, suspense, and human resilience, elevated by top-tier performances and dazzling technique. Brad Pitt shines as Sonny Hayes, a veteran racer pulled back from retirement to save the struggling APXGP team, proving that even in later years he can outpace younger stars with sheer craft and charisma. Damson Idris, known to many from television, steps onto the big screen with commanding presence as rookie Joshua Pearce, giving a performance that holds its own beside Pitt and confirms his transition from small screen promise to major film talent. Javier Bardem, unforgettable as the chilling Anton Chigurh in No Country for Old Men, shows his range here by embodying Robin Cervantes, a desperate but determined team principal, bringing gravitas and emotional weight instead of menace. Technically, the film is a marvel Claudio Miranda's cinematography captures the roar and velocity of real Formula One cars, the editing maintains razor-sharp tension, and Hans Zimmer's score drives the adrenaline home. Yet for all its high-octane energy, the emotional beats, though plentiful, sometimes lack the depth to linger beyond the race track. And curiously, the film chooses not to show Sonny's family at all no angry wife, no rebellious daughter, no love-seeking son as English films often lean toward, leaving the personal side of his comeback curiously absent, perhaps intentionally but still noticeable. Blending authentic racing spectacle with themes of teamwork, skill, and second chances, F-1 is both immersive and inspiring a film that doesn't just show Formula One, it makes you feel like you're in the driver's seat. My Rating is 8/10.

title: Popcorn Flies, Heart Races-F1 in 4DX Is a Lap You Won't Forget
content: F1 delivers a cinematic adrenaline rush that's pushed to new heights by the 4DX experience. Brad Pitt commands the screen as Sonny Hayes, a fallen star given one last shot at Formula One glory alongside rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). The film's racing sequences are already gripping, but in 4DX, you're not just watching-you're right there in the cockpit.The physical sensations-seat movements, wind, rain, and engine vibrations-sync perfectly with the on-screen action. Every gear shift, crash, and high-speed corner puts you in the heart of the race. Effects like air blasts during overtakes, bursts of scent on pit stops, and even mist during rainy races transform the film into a truly immersive ride.Though some sports drama clichés remain, the sheer impact of watching F1 in 4DX elevates the experience well above standard viewings. For fans of racing-or just great cinema-this is as real as it gets.Unmissable in 4DX, F1 will leave your heart racing long after the credits roll.

title: Formula 1!
content: In the 1990s, Sonny Hayes was Formula 1's most promising driver until an accident on the track nearly ended his career. Thirty years later, the owner of a struggling Formula 1 team convinces Sonny to return to racing and become the best in the world. Driving alongside the team's hotshot rookie, Sonny soon learns that the road to redemption is not something you can travel alone."F1," directed by Joseph Kosinski who also made "Top Gun: Maverick," is a fast and exciting movie that pulls you in right from the start. The story is strong and full of energy, with plenty of intense moments that keep you hooked. As a big Formula 1 fan, I noticed that some of the wild or surprising things in the movie would never happen in a real race, but that did not take away from the fun. It still captures the spirit and excitement of the sport in a way that fans can really enjoy.One of the best parts of the film is the racing itself. The onboard shots are amazing and make you feel like you are right there in the car. The way the races are filmed is super cool and fun to watch, especially for F1 fans who will appreciate the details. Hans Zimmer's music adds even more power to the scenes, making everything feel bigger and more intense. The whole movie is packed with adrenaline and keeps you on the edge of your seat. Whether you follow F1 closely or just enjoy action-packed stories, "F1" is a thrilling and entertaining ride from beginning to end.Score:8.5/10.

title: Fueled by Heart: A Ride Worth Taking
content: This Is Why We Go to the Movies.Sonny Hayes (aka Brad Pitt) is an aging, washed-up driver who fell short of glory on the asphalt. Now, he's just trying to make ends meet. Opposite him is Joshua Pearce (aka Damson Idris) an overly confident, self-centered rookie who thinks he can do it all. When the two meet head-on, it sparks a heart-stopping race.From beginning to end, you're propelled straight into the cockpit. But at its core, this is more than just an F1 racing movie it's a story of teamwork, human ingenuity, and the enduring human spirit. It's about ambition, sacrifice, and fading dreams. A story of triumph and failure. And the race against time that we all must partake in.All of this is bundled into a package of thrilling races filled with dramatic, funny, and heartfelt moments.The pacing of the movie is excellent, the story is well-written, and the great Hans Zimmer once again does not miss.Brad Pitt is the star of the movie, but make no mistake Damson Idris, Kerry Condon, Javier Bardem, and the rest of the cast are superb. The chemistry between them is undeniable.This movie brought me back to a time when it was exciting to be in a packed theater, sharing laughter, adrenaline, and emotion with complete strangers. That feeling of camaraderie of truly experiencing something together is something I think has been missing from modern moviegoing.This isn't an Oscar-worthy, thought-provoking film but it's a refreshing callback to when movies were exciting, unique, and fun before the age of endless remakes and repetitive franchises.I implore you all to go see this in theaters. title: "Who's Fighting? I'm Racing."
content: From the moment 'F1' kicked into gear with Led Zeppelin, I knew this film was after my own heart.As someone whose F1 knowledge barely extends beyond having watched the 'Senna' documentary, I was afraid the magic of this film would be a little lost on me. title: Full-Throttle Redemption
content: F1 (2025) is more than just a sports drama - it's a powerful, pulse-pounding ride that blends high-speed adrenaline with emotional grit. Directed by Joseph Kosinski and headlined by Brad Pitt in one of his most compelling performances to date, the film roars past expectations and delivers an unforgettable cinematic experience.The story follows Sonny Hayes, a former F1 legend whose career was cut short by a devastating crash in the '90s. Thirty years later, he returns to the track to save a struggling underdog team, APXGP. Teaming up with a fiery young rookie (played brilliantly by Damson Idris), Sonny must face not just the fierce competition, but also the ghosts of his past.Brad Pitt is magnetic - portraying Sonny with a perfect mix of intensity, vulnerability, and veteran charm. The supporting cast, including Kerry Condon, Tobias Menzies, and Javier Bardem, brings depth and realism to every scene. Director Joseph Kosinski's vision, combined with Claudio Miranda's sleek cinematography and Stephen Mirrione's crisp editing, results in an immersive and visually stunning film. And Hans Zimmer's soaring score injects pure energy into every corner of the track.Even for someone who isn't a die-hard Formula 1 enthusiast, F1 proves irresistible. The racing sequences are masterfully crafted, balancing speed with strategy - offering a glimpse into the technical brilliance behind the sport. It's fast, intense, and thrilling - a true edge-of-your-seat experience.Whether you're in it for the roar of the engines, the drama of redemption, or the spectacle of world-class filmmaking, F1 delivers across the board. It's a must-watch for motorsport fans, Brad Pitt admirers, and anyone craving a cinematic adrenaline rush.Rating: 10/10.

title: Come for the racing (don't worry about story)
content: F1's story is nothing we haven't seen (and groaned at) before, but the racing is unlike anything else that's been put on the big or small screen. The filmmakers (particularly Lewis Hamilton's influence) have achieved something that's never been possible before: making the audience feel like they are in the car with the drivers.The attention to detail, particularly to keeping tabs of where cars are on track throughout a lap and making sure the geography makes sense, the beautiful design and the outstanding editing make this an astonishing technical achievement. Just don't expect an Oscar-winning screenplay with anything more than clichéd over-dramatisation!(Full disclosure: I'm a huge F1 fan, so this may be coloured by that)

title: Fantastic Film
content: This film was as much about Formula One racing as it was about being a leader and living your passion. You may not leave with a burning desire to watch F1 racing but you will have a comprehensive understanding and find yourself watching the POV races with intensity and anxiety. The film is long with a runtime north of 2 hrs and 30 mins, but it doesn't feel long. Maybe they could've trimmed the love interest side as it felt unnecessary and untenable. What grabs you most in this story is the development of friendship, mentorship and teamwork that feels entirely natural.

title: GOOD HOLLYWOOD FODDER...!
content: Oscar winner Brad Pitt stars in this exceedingly breezy entertainment from director Joseph Kosinski (Tron: Legacy/Top Gun: Maverick) which is still in theaters. Pitt is the typical has been in the racing world (at least to the eyes of others) who after an injury early on in his career w/bouts of gambling addiction now plies his trade as a hirable racer (for any medium) where his years on the track & his winning acumen come in very handy. Into this mix comes Oscar winner, Javier Bardem, a former friend/racer who is a co-owner of a F1 team which has yet to see a win even though his star driver, played by Damson Idris (from FX's Snowfall) is being touted as someone to watch. What follows is a narrative very much in tune w/1988's Bull Durham where the seasoned pro must cajole & nurture the problematic, yet headstrong up & comer who starts to bring in some placings in future races as Pitt's strategic maneuvering elevates Idris' standing in the team. Done w/enough visual vim & verve you'll forgive the overfamiliarity of the story but Kosinski amps up the fireworks to the nth degree (must've been a wowser in IMAX) where you admire the craftsmanship on display.
