title: A real adrenalin ride
content: After a nasty incident almost ended his career, Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) has retired from Formula One and now races a Porsche, but when an opportunity comes from the owner of a struggling team, and his old friend Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), Sonny makes a return to Formula One racing, alongside a hot new rookie teammate Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), but soon realises that he can't take the road to redemption on his own.I sat through the almost 2.5 hours of this film and never moved from my seat. I was sometimes horrified, sometimes excited, sometimes sad, I went through a gamut of emotions helped along by a generous helping of American smulch. But it worked.There was only 1 or 2 scenes that slowed the pace down, but the effects and the brilliant photography had me moving my body with the cars as if I was steering them myself. A great cast with great characters helped seal the deal, and we got what was almost like Apollo 13 but in cars.Pitt is at his best too, as was Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon (Kate McKenna). You don't even have to like cars or car racing to enjoy the film: you can't help but get caught up in it all and be carried along in the excitement.A great film and I rate it an exceedingly rare 8.

title: Related To My Tech Life, Great Fun, Even More So In IMAX
content: Great fun. And two small historical notes.One, I was CEO of EverTrac, and launched the world's first moving map tracking and smart vehicle monitoring and management system.... in 2000. That was 6 years before Google Maps, 18 years before some of the current commonly available tools! It used CA's Unicenter management with hardware vendors to do real-time dashboards even of Mercedes-McLaren F1 season-winning cars driven by Mike Hakinen and David Coulthard. So, I was smiling so wide as I saw the automotive tech on display in the movie.Two, I had even spent (wasted?? LOL) marketing funds on product placement of EverTrac tech on displays in the non-F1 but similar racing movie that was typical and predictable of a Sylvester Stallone move, DRIVEN, if I recall the name. How I wish that movie had been about actual F1 cars (which my tech worked directly on) and that it had Brad Pitt instead of Stallone. LOL.So, my opinion of this movie F1 is that while it had the expected predictability of such movies, it also had enough human drama - and actual acting (not something I expected or ever got in the Stallone movie!). To see it on opening night in IMAX was even more fun, and I was delighted with the experience.

title: Who did they make this film for?
content: As a long term F1 and film fan, I wonder who this film is made for. Honestly, if you're a fan of formula 1, and have gathered some knowledge about it over the years, non of the racing in this film makes even the slightest bit of sense. As a film by itself, the story is weak at best, the silly jokes seemed to be more important. The writer didn't seem to have done any research whatsoever about racing, and the world that it's in, but then you could still expect a nice story with characters that you start to care about, however, it seems like they started filming halfway through script version 1. Brad did a decent job, and so did some of the other actors, but even though the cameo appearances might be fun, this gimmick doesn't last, and the believability is hardly ever reached. Apart from some decent scenes, and the production value, I really struggle to find something to like in a film that I was looking forward to. It's a shame that the people in charge were not up to the task, and should've not touched it unless they could do it well, after doing proper research. To me, this film is a huge miss for both F1 and film fans, apart from, to be honest, too many similar racing shots that although cool to see and well made, doesn't cut it. In one word: to me the biggest disappointment in the cinemas this year...

title: Brad Pitt Take Racing to the Next Level
content: F1 is an absolute thrill ride from start to finish, delivering breathtaking visuals, intense racing sequences, and a cinematic experience that leaves you breathless. The VFX and CGI are nothing short of spectacular, making every car chase, crash, and high-speed maneuver feel incredibly realistic. The attention to detail in recreating the Formula 1 world is remarkable, immersing the audience fully into the adrenaline-fueled life of the racers.Hans Zimmer's music elevates the film to another level. Each score perfectly matches the tension, speed, and emotion of every scene. From heart-pounding race sequences to quieter, dramatic moments, the soundtrack amplifies the experience and keeps you fully engaged throughout. It's the kind of music that makes your pulse race alongside the cars on screen.Brad Pitt once again proves why he is one of the best in the business. His performance is charismatic, intense, and effortlessly compelling, giving depth to his character while commanding the screen in every scene. He makes the audience feel every victory, every setback, and every risk as if they were part of the race.A huge shoutout to the director and producers for bringing this film to life. It's clear that every decision, from the visual effects to the casting, was made with F1 fans in mind. The film celebrates the sport while giving a cinematic spectacle that can be enjoyed by fans and newcomers alike.

title: Exhilarating, Fast, Furious, Fun, and High Octane
content: 9/10. That was an exhilarating racing performance. High octane, summer escapism going 230 mph into victory lane with grace and style.Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) gleefully directs with aplomb, frenetic energy, and kinetic racing scenes. Brad Pitt (No Man's Land, No Way Out) gives a charismatic performance and shows great chemistry with co star Damson Idris (Black Mirror, Twilight Zone). The racing scenes are intense and blistering fast. With cinematographer Claudio Miranda (Life of Pi, Top Gun: Maverick) keeping it clean and crisp. The in-car camera shots and drivers' perspective are a phenomenal technical achievement.Seeing this in IMAX was a fun and enjoyable ride. The sounds of crunching metal, squealing tires, chaotic pit stops, and F1 cars whizzing by are punctuated with the glorious sound of 1.6-liter, V6 turbocharged hybrid engines and a phenomenal sound mix. Hans Zimmer's score is epic, adrenaline pumping, and uplifting.Yeah, the story is overdramatized and most of this stuff wouldn't happen in real life. The pacing zips through the runtime fast but could have tightened itself around the apex a little better. The story is more style over substance. But it's a blast to watch. Fueled my brain with dopamine, serotonin, and satisfied my need for speed.I think this is the first racing movie I've watched where the racing and crash scenes are not obviously filmed at around three dissimilar locations. When they go to a track, you know all the shots are from the that track. Fantastic job at editing and continuity. You feel the speed the on screen. In some movies, they are going 200 mph but feels like watching a turtle crossing the street. Great speed immersion and camera tricks.F1: The Movie honors the world's greatest drivers and the legacy of the world's best Motorsport. Way better than taking constant Nascar, left hand turns. If they made Days of Thunder 2: The Revenge of The Road Course and had the drivers taking right hand turns; then the movie would be a soapy drama about the drivers being in therapy. How everyone just DNF'd themselves on the first right hand corner. Oh. A road course and not a boring, cookie cutter track? The heresy.I'm going to see this again in 4DX. Definitely watch this in the best theater format as possible. And this is how you make a racing movie and crash scenes without that shoddy CGI nonsense. Use those practical effects!Thank you, Regal Cinema, for showing an advanced, early fan screening.

title: Satisfied with visuals
content:
It is great to see another fan of Joseph Kosinski's work! Since you're rating it 10/10, you clearly appreciated the incredible technical craft that went into the film.Here is a drafted review (approx. 200 words) that captures why the movie is such a standout:
A Masterclass in High-Octane Filmmaking
F1 is more than just a sports movie; it is a visceral, immersive experience that sets a new gold standard for racing cinema. Director Joseph Kosinski and cinematographer Claudio Miranda have translated the "on-the-grid" intensity of Top Gun: Maverick to the world of Formula 1 with breathtaking success.Brad Pitt delivers a grounded, soulful performance as Sonny Hayes, bringing a weary but determined gravitas to the role of the veteran mentor. The chemistry between him and Damson Idris's Joshua Pearce provides a strong emotional core to the high-stakes drama of the APXGP team.However, the real star is the cinematography. By using custom-built cameras mounted directly on the cars, the film captures the sheer speed and G-force of the sport in a way that feels dangerously real. The sound design is equally impressive, with the roar of the engines practically vibrating through the screen. With a soaring score by Hans Zimmer and the authentic touch of Lewis Hamilton's production, this is a cinematic triumph. Whether you are a lifelong F1 fan or a newcomer, this is a 10/10 must-watch on the biggest screen possible.

title: A roller coaster of film
content: An amazing movie, extremely entertaining, full of adrenaline, and beautiful, dramatic moments. I expected it to be good, but it was extraordinary in almost every aspect: the filming, the races, the characters, and the wonderful music.I really liked Johnny Hayes' character in the film, his story, and his transformation from a talented and promising young driver to someone who lost everything in a horrific accident. However, he returns to achieve a specific goal in his mind, and Brad Pitt gave a good performance in the role.Jogwa Pierce, played by Damson Idris, is a delight to watch. He and Johnny are similar at the beginning of their careers, and as the film progresses, he learns from him and develops into a better driver. The only negative is that his story and vision are not explored in depth when he was trying to reach Formula 1.The character of Kate McKenna, designer of Aero Dynamic, was good, and I really liked the importance of her role in the races, and her relationship with Johnny didn't bother me, despite its insignificance. It didn't bother me, but her negativity, like Pierce's negativity, didn't delve deeply into her story and how she got to her position in the team.The cinematography in the film is amazing, and how they use specialized, lightweight camera rigs built into modified F2 cars, allowing them to move with the race and the music, as I said, is very enjoyable. My only regret about the film is that I didn't see it in the cinema.The film is like a roller coaster ride where you can predict the ending, and most of the film is just a poster, but the journey it takes you on is delightful and beautiful.

title: Strap in!
content: My expectations are low. I'm an F1 fan and a film nut, but do they go together? They should, fast cars are cinematic, but is that enough? Hell yeah it is. We meet Sonny (Brad Pitt), zipping up a race suit, grab a helmet, climb inside a GT car and hits the accelerator as Led Zeppelin bangs out. That's F1 The Movie in a nutshell. Sonny is a renegade. An old school loose cannon, playing by his own rules. He jumps from one race car to another. His next jump being into, you guessed it, F1. He has history there, but is persuaded back into the top tier by his old mate and Apex team boss Ruben (Javier Bardem) to come in and rescue their floundering season. Offer some experience, to the team and their other driver, Joshua (Damson Idris). The team, Joshua, the car, all have potential, but they can't unlock it and careers are on the line. They don't like Sonny rocking up with his sunglasses and rock n roll swagger like he's, well, Brad Pitt! But he's clearly what they need. It's very corny, but knowingly so. F1 is a dense sport that's not easy to penetrate by the casual viewer, but this drawers you in and helps any novice viewer with some heavy background information courtesy of the race commentary. There are nuggets for F1 fans though. Some of that commentary comes from Martin Brundle and Crofty, who of course delivers his signature line. Reporter, Will Buxton turns up (of course he does), Gunther Steiner leans out of a pit wall chair, but doesn't swear, Toto Wolff actually has a line and even Lewis Hamilton (who gets a producer credit) features by way of his dog, Roscoe. Much of the paddock action is shot on real race weekends, so there's plenty of quick cameos from real drivers too, team bosses and the general glitterarty of F1. It makes this film part action movie, part advert for the sport. Not to mention a glut of product placement sponsorship. You can decide for yourself how the percentages break down, but putting Sonny and Joshua on the grid with Hamilton, Verstappen, Sainz, it gives this instant credibility. It's not easy to see where the real race action meets the fake, the blend is brilliant. It doesn't make the mistake of making it look easy either. Apex are the underdogs. Sonny is not the quick fix. This is a two and a half hour film though and yes, it paces itself really well. There's a good cast of characters here. Most notable, Technical Director, Kate (Kerry Condon) who's frustrated with Sonny's unconventional methods, but is the first to see that he has something. Yes it's a crazy dramatically overblown rollercoaster ride. Yes it's probably too surface for the hardcore race fans. Yes it's probably half an hour too long. Yes Pitt carries it. Yes Hans Zimmer's score is full on. It's bloody good fun though. Bardem and Condon both deliver too. It's better than it has any right to be. In fact it's bloody marvellous. In a world that some days feels like it's falling to pieces. Thank god for film, for sport, for joyous escapism. Expectations met and then some.

title: High Octane
content: 8.1 stars.I will put this on my list of movies to watch again and again every few years. It's fun and exciting and the racing segments are well executed.The technical aspects of the film are unrivaled as far as racing movies go. It sits on the top shelf with a few other fantastic racing films. I loved 'Ford v Ferrari', which I feel was a little better than this, but 'F1: The Movie' is similar because it tells the same story, just a little differently. Both films are about aging racers and are great for overall acting and depth of the narrative. Of course who can forget 'Days of Thunder', also an intense racing movie at the same level, but more formulaic.'F1: The Movie' the human element is not as believable, because it features a more aging Brad Pitt at the helm. He's not the usual forty-something aging driver, his character is obviously in his fifties (even though Pitt is in his 60's) and it shows, but Pitt looks REALLY young for 62. In the story, his character is a rolling stone, but he inspires people and helps a losing F1 team to win Grand Prix. It's emotional and inspiring and I enjoyed it.

title: A lot of fantasy...very little reality
content: Those familiar with F1 and who watch its races on television will find dozens of errors and lapses that are impossible to imagine in reality.From the physical preparation of the protagonist, especially given his age, to maneuvers that would be penalized for both drivers and teams.Overtaking in areas where DRS should allow for greater overtaking is not activated until driving in the rain on dry tires, where braking and cornering would be impossible.Good cinematography and acceptable acting (in addition to the appearance of true stars of the category like Zak Brown and Frédéric Vasseur in a fun interview) are not enough to excuse so many differences between reality and a simple popcorn story.

title: Predictable, but fun
content: F1 is a fun ride, even if the destination can predicted before arrival. But there were a lot of things I enjoyed/appreciated along the way. First up, the cinematography for the racing is great, enhances the intensity of the action sequences. And if you're like me and unfamiliar with F1 racing, the film does a really good job of "explaining" what is happening what is going on during the racing with an overlay of the play-by-play announcers holding your hand along the way. I appreciated it; felt almost like a documentary at times. And finally, who doesn't mind a Hans Zimmer score to go with everything? I felt he was a great choice for this film.Are the typical tropes there? Yep. You can pretty much take the cliches from Days of Thunder and plug them in here. But the final package was polished enough that I was able to be immersed to the point where I never rolled my eyes.

title: F the Haters
content:
I had a hell of a time. It's pure Jerry Bruckheimer entertainment. Do not wait for streaming. You will regret not paying the $20 to $30. It's a packed house party at the multiplex. Avoid the first seven rows. I was in the eighth. F1 fans ripped it a new one, as expected. It's not a documentary. It's giving Ted Lasso vibes and is more of a long F1 commercial, but I can live with that. Product placement aplenty. The pre F1 Daytona scene at the start was gold, with that Led Zepellin rock music and high adrenaline. He took the $5,000 and declined the trophy, hat and watch spoils of victory. I wish I could just wake up, plunge my face in an ice bath, get coffee from a crew guy and perform. It set a high bar. Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes won't be nominated for any awards, but he capably put the film on his back, which the camera often lingered on. After almost two hours, it is revealed just how serious the underlying condition is that the scar actually signifies. That stunning crash was made possible for us via documentary footage of the aftermath of Martin Donnelly's 1990 crash! I'm sure the family appreciates the shout out.His old teammate Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), now owner of the APXGP Formula One team, tracks him down in Orlando. He's on the verge of bankruptcy, $350M in the red and persuades Sonny to come to London and be the Savior. Josh Pierce (Damson Idris) is their brash rookie. His role is underwritten, but the exposure will relaunch his career at 220 miles per hour. Kate McKenna (Kerry Condon) is the technical director, the first female to do so in F1. Tobias Menzes from The Crown is a cookie cutter villain on the team board cooking up a deal to sell for a big profit if they can finish out the season winless and block a three year extension for Ruben. He also has a scar, on his face, which should be a red flag right from the start.We're pretty sure what will happen, just not how. It's a very DEI cast. A young blonde woman is even in the pit crew. She screws up, but stands up for herself at the end in yet another cliché this film dishes out by the minute. Condon was great. I loved her dress in Las Vegas and her sexy accent. The Oscar nominee doesn't need more exposure, but will get tons of it via F1. The Menzes machinations were actually a surprise, since we're focused on what happens on the track and not behind the scenes. The Hans Zimmer score is his typical top notch work. I loved the movie. Only serious impactful films get the highest grade. But as for unabashed Hollywood Entertainment, this rates merely a notch below.Viewing 2
I snuck into this for the second time. IMAX is such a treat. Ice cold theater on a summer day, huge screen, great sound even if not the majestic Dolby. Patrons who give a damn about the experience because they paid so much for tickets. It was nice to sit sixth row dead center.It starts with such a blast of energy. Led Zepelin's "Whole Lotta Love" is blaring as Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) awakens at the 24 Hours of Daytona. He's got the night shift and must take the team from worst to first. No problem. They eventually win. He declines the trophy, but takes off with a $5,000 share of the winning prize. While doing laundry in Orlando, he is surprised by Ruben (Javier Bardem), a former driver whose APXGP team is $350M in the red. The board will force a sale if they don't win one of the remaining nine races of the season. Sonny considers it at a diner. Waitress gives her blessing. Off to London he goes. He blew out his back and neck in a terrible crash 30 years ago, but nobody knows how bad. On the team, he must deal with British rookie Josh Pierce (Damon Idris). He's cocky as hell and building his brand with his perfectly named manager Cash (Samson Kayo). Mom Bernadette (Sarah Niles) is also around. Sonny then meets technical director Kate McKenna (Oscar nominee Kerry Condon). Of course she doubles as the obligatory love interest. Tobias Mendes (The Crown) is the evil board member who is trying to set up a very lucrative team sale. How do you think this old barnburner winds up? Everything is great. Director Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun) sends us on a wild ride. Kerry Condon is fantastic as the lone female on the brains team, looking to prove her worth. And she gets to keep her accent. Idris is wonderful as Sonny's foil. Bardem is such a warm presence, capable of great fury. Mendes is appropriately deceptive. Pitt works his movie star game and is always nothing short of a commanding presence. Hans Zimmer is the man. How do you not love this film if you are a male? Or female, since there is plenty to consider. Only potential Best Picture nominees get the ten, of course.

title: The Hype is real!
content: I LOVED this movie. Saw it in Imax and was totally worth the Imax price. I was on the edge of my seat for a good chunk of the movie and at one point I audibly said oh my god. One of my all time favorite movies. Highly recommend this even if you are not familiar with or a big fan of racing.

title: Top Gun Maverick Reskin (w/out the prequel)
content: As few can argue, the camera work and footage are stunning. The story, however, fails to find any semblance of a meaningful pulse until nearly an hour. Even then, the murmur of it is starved to sustain.At it's core, the bones of the story are much akin in structure to how Top Gun Maverick plays out: the old field hand returns to show the greenhorns how it's done and the values necessary to do so - not to mention who eventually completes the mission in the end. Too bad it's Kosinski himself who has chosen to repackage his prior formula, indicative of a lack for storytelling inspiration at its value base.5/10 - as much as five due to how well the action maintained my attention despite my opinion on it's shortcomings.

title: A Movie Worth Watching Second Time On Cinema
content: F1: The Movie is an exhilarating journey into the heart of Formula 1-both the sport and the people behind it. As someone who's followed F1 casually over the years, I found this film not only informative, but deeply emotional and inspiring.What truly stands out is the film's ability to balance breathtaking race footage with intimate, human moments. You feel the adrenaline of every corner and overtake, but also the quiet determination in the drivers' eyes, the pressure behind every team decision, and the weight of legacy. The cinematography is spectacular-especially the onboard shots and slow-motion sequences that highlight the raw speed and precision of F1.The film doesn't shy away from the dangers and sacrifices involved. It honors the sport's history, including tragic moments, without ever feeling exploitative. Instead, it pays respect to the courage it takes to sit in that cockpit, lap after lap.Even if you're not a die-hard fan, you'll walk away with a newfound appreciation for what it means to be part of Formula 1. It's not just about machines-it's about passion, rivalry, innovation, and human endurance.Highly recommended for sports fans, documentary lovers, or anyone who appreciates stories about pushing limits.

title: An Immersive Experience
content: The F1 movie offers a one-of-a-kind view into the intricacies of the highest level of motorsports, that had never been done prior to this. This fact, coupled with the masterful performances provided by Pitt and Idris make for a very engaging viewing experience. title: The Film That Made Me Feel Again.
content: I haven't watched a movie like F1 in a long time and honestly, I didn't expect it to hit this hard. From the very first frame to the final shot, I was completely locked in. Not a single moment of distraction, not a second where I felt the urge to look away. This film gripped my senses and healed something in me I didn't know needed healing.The roar of the engines is not just sound design... it's emotion. Every rev, every screech, every high-speed corner pulls you in deeper. Combined with an original score that swells with adrenaline and depth, the film balances pulse-pounding intensity with subtle emotional weight. The music and the motors never clash -they move as one, like a well-oiled machine.And the cinematography? Absolutely breathtaking. Each race sequence feels like art in motion. The camera dances along the track, capturing both the chaos and the calm in perfect balance. Close-ups, drone sweeps, helmet cams... it's all shot with such care and intention that you feel like you're in the cockpit, racing for your life.More than just a racing movie, A visceral experience...raw, beautiful, and strangely therapeutic. It doesn't just entertain; it immerses, uplifts, and races through your soul.If you've ever felt disconnected from cinema lately, this might be the film to bring you back.

title: Not a bad film but felt a bit cheap and dull
content:
I heard great things about this and after Ford vs Ferrari I was excited.Unfortunately, this film has some things about it I don't like.Although this is not a cheap film my any means when you stand it next to others it looks a bit shabby.Every scene is unnaturally bright and clean making every scene look like it was filmed in a studio or inside a computer.The acting is uneven to say the least and some of the chamoes probably sounded a lot better on paper than on film.I watched F1 for some years but stopped as the races became progressively more boring, this film emulates that perfectly.All the main plot points highlight what a flawed sport F1 is:If it rains too much the racing stops.If two cars clip each other the racing stops.If an overtake is judged illegal the judges force it to be given back later in the race.If a wing is four degrees off kilter or a car has too little fuel the race results are overturned.Even if you are the best racer, it doesn't mean much:
Don't manage your tires: you lose.Your manager chooses the wrong tires: you loseYour pit crew drops a spanner: you lose.Your manager decides your team mate should win: you lose.I didn't really warm to the characters and Brad Pitt's character is alright but a bit nothing.He turns up and mooches around winning races and making quips.His counterpart is more interesting but doesn't come across as a great actor and a lot of his scenes are a bit awkward.This is not a bad film and I had some fun watching it - but I feel this has a lot of wasted potential.

title: Making your heart beat
content: This film centers on Formula 1, a motorsport. While its subject matter might seem niche, it naturally explains technical terms and situations, making it perfectly accessible even to those with zero F1 knowledge.The film's visuals and sound design are truly outstanding. You can visually feel the sheer speed of the racing, while the allure of the internal combustion engines delivers a powerful auditory experience. However, the OST, which blends perfectly with every scene, is particularly impressive. While watching, you might find yourself tapping your foot to the rhythm without even realizing it. The near-perfect visuals and sound, combined with the OST, create an illusion that you're racing yourself, sending shivers down your spine along with a rapid heartbeat.Story-wise, it follows a classic conflict-resolution-collaboration plot, a somewhat traditional progression. But you'll fully understand why classics are classics. Some might call classics cliched and predictable, while others will revel in the guaranteed dopamine rush. This film confidently belongs to the latter category.However, as the ending approaches, a significant plot point concerning the protagonist, Sonny Hayes, emerges with considerable presence. It's somewhat disappointing that this thread isn't resolved in subsequent scenes before the film concludes.Nevertheless, it's a remarkably well-crafted film, one where the ticket price feels entirely justified. I strongly recommend seeing it in theaters.

title: Car crash of a movie
content: I think I must have seen a different movie than those reviewers who seemed to regard F1 as a cinematic masterpiece. While the race scenes are undeniably exciting and put the viewer at the centre of the action, the story is so ridiculous, with more holes than a piece of Swiss cheese, that it soon descends into silliness that insults your intelligence.Brad Pitt is old enough to have a bus pass never mind compete in Formula 1. This might explain why having been brought in to save an ailing race team, he proceeds to wreak havoc, constantly crashing his car, deliberately smashing into other racers at every opportunity and spraying the track with debris and broken car parts like he was competing in a demolition derby. However, instead of being sacked on the spot by the FIA, he proceeds to tell his boss, all the technicians and his race team mates how to do their jobs properly and guess what - he's right about everything. What a guy!The story gets more and more preposterous as it goes on, while becoming increasingly tedious and repetitive. The end is so drawn out that I felt like I'd actually been watching the 24 hour Le Mans endurance race - in real time! Others obviously enjoyed this nonsense but unfortunately for me, I found it to be complete tripe.

title: Great theatrical experience!!!
content: F1 is a riveting film from start to finish, just like an high octane formula one race. Brad Pitt plays an ex car racer who returns to the grid after thirty years, with hardly any takers save his friend who runs Apex racing team, that's in jeopardy and needs a saviour.Enter the suave and charming Brad Pitt, who makes acting so easy with his cool as cucumber mannerisms and takes to the wheels as a fish takes to water, just that here he is swimming troubled waters.The movie is a tribute to formula one and people who are already hooked to this adrenaline pumping sport will lap every second of the action, while those who don't still get to see a technically well crafted movie with mind bending visuals, cinematography, stunts and off course a chilling musical score from the stables of legendary Hans Zimmer.Kosinski who has given Top gun Maverick earlier has helmed a eminently watchable movie that's not so easy to make, infact not so easy to imagine too, that's the level of action, drama and sophistication the movie drenches you in and the fleeting seconds of the climax with no sound and just the outstretched winding roads leaves us spellbound.One of the best movies on racing, very close to the Ford vs Ferrari...though that movie is slightly more taut and connects emotionally better.Do watch a movie that has been made with a big heart and for the big screen!!!

title: Best Of 2025
content: "F1: The Movie" is hands down one of the most electrifying sports dramas I've ever watched. From the very first scene, it throws you right into the heart of Formula 1 racing with a level of realism that makes you forget it's a film. For me, this is easily one of the best films of 2025 so far.

title: It's done by a formula - still great
content: Yes, the movie is quite formulaic. All the elements you're seeing, you've seen before - if you've seen a few sports movies. Guy way past his prime, underdogs who gotta win, shady opponents, tensions inside the team...It's still done in a very, very good way. I really liked the way the - we all know they'll come! - gut punches were delivered, I anticipated them, but most of them came without warning or much foreshadowing. Which nicely emulates real racing: everything's fine in one second, smelly stuff hits the fan in the very next.Also, the dynamic between the two male leads is done greatly. For at least half of the movie, you don't know how it will play out - will they become friends or enemies?Then there is the finale, and even if you're sure that the happy end will happen (will it? The movie is grounded enough that you also contemplate the possibility that it won't happen)... because we have more than one guy to root for (no spoiler, because I'm not saying that it's gonna be the same guys as in the beginning of the movie), it's really exciting to not know who will prevail.It's formulaic, but it's done to perfection, and I can't think of a better sports movie.(Ever since I was a teenager, I loved Major League. While the comedy is stronger in ML, the "will they/won't they win" side makes me love F1 even more.)Side note: I'm not even a big F1 or motorsports fan.

title: An incredible visual experience, but one that can be improved.
content:
An incredible visual experience, but one that can be improved.F1 is a movie that keeps you hooked, watching the competition, the characters and everything around it, but leaves something to be desired in terms of script.The Good:
Joseph Kosinski's direction is incredible, generating tension, combined with an incredible visual and aural experience. The pacing is fast despite the length, and the film never gets heavy. Claudio Miranda's cinematography is incredible and Hans Zimmer as always delivers a great soundtrack.The Formula 1 racing scenes are impressive, they keep you hooked and are impressively filmed. The tension that is generated is felt.The characters were very good, with great performances from Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem and an amazing Damson Idris.The story is basic but effective, with clichés, but it delivers a satisfying movie. The movie never gets boring or tiresome.The Bad:
The plot is the weakest point of the film, which can be unpolished in some aspects, which could have been refined. There are some script conveniences and lack of development in scenes. One scene in particular was when Sonny returns to the Apex team as if nothing happened and they let him go back to driving despite what he was told earlier.The movie falls into clichés, and emotionally it can be poor, even though it has its moments, as in the final scene of the last race. Overall the movie could have been perfect if the script had been better polished and added more context and refined script conveniences. Also the romance between Sonny and Kate feels rushed and forced. But overall it is a recommendable movie.In short:
F1 is an incredible movie worth watching, but could have been a masterpiece if the story had been deeper and more polished.8.0 Stars.

title: Overtook my expectations
content: F1 is an excellent sports drama film, delivering on non stop thrills both of and especially on the track, accompanied by great performances, music, and timeless themes.The easiest winning factor of the film is without a doubt the cinematography; it is stellar in placing audiences in the car alongside the characters. Moreover, the geography of the races and where each car was located was sublimely choreographed and made easy to follow. Additionally, the performances were all great with Brad Pitt delivering an incredibly compelling lead role of Sonny Hayes, a man more willing to help than to win, despite his own simmering ambitions that gradually take the wheel as the plot progresses. This is not to undermine the deuteragonist Joshua Pearce, played surprisingly well by Damson Idris; he is able to convey an impressive amount of emotional conflict towards Pitt's Sonny, showing anger, respect, recklessness, ambitious drive, and an earnest desire to win all sometimes in the same scene. And the supporting cast all had their moments too, from Javier Bardem to Callie Cooke. Furthermore, the story felt like a throwback to a simpler time, conveying timeless themes of taking the ambitious road to success without completely understanding why you want it in the first place, and that paired with the score made for a more impactful execution. And the cuts between the races and the drama felt excellently balanced, with the few liberties taken with the rules of racing benefiting the story phenomenally well.However, a red flag is the lengthy runtime, and it is only as long as it is likely due to repeating certain plot beats but for different characters, and even more likely to create the appropriately lengthy race sequences.Ultimately, F1 is an excellent film that not only focuses on perhaps the greatest motorsport in the history of car racing, but delivers on a compelling drama with real drive behind the direction and overall execution.

title: Another phenomenal adrenaline fueled racing film
content: Would definitely put this up there with Rush and Ford Vs. Ferrari as some of the best modern racing films.Yes, it has some formulaic sports movie tropes, yes it probably ends exactly how you think it does. But these kind of movies aren't meant to surprise you (in that way), they're made to keep you on the edge of your seat and make you feel the rush of the sport which F1 2025 does EXCELLENTLY.Shot really good, the camera work really makes you feel like you're in the driver seat and had my heart pumping the whole time. Really great cast with phenomenal performances. The rivalry between the two main characters feels intense and the actors make it incredibly believable and adds a layer of intensity besides just winning the actual race.It was even nice seeing some of the minor characters also have agency and get character developments and minor arch's which isn't as common for these kinds of movies.The soundtrack was great with a good mixture of licensed music new and classic and this movie also did the impossible for me. It made me actually like an Ed Sheeran song haha.Highly recommend if you are a fan of racing movies or the actual sport of F1 as it features multiple *minor *cameos of real F1 drivers.

title: Lessons I have learned from F1: The Movie
content: Life isn't so different from Formula 1.I just watched the new F1 movie, and it didn't feel like just a film - it felt like a lesson in how life, business, and leadership really work.Out there on the track, you realize that strategy always wins. You don't need the fastest car; you need the smartest plan. The perfect pit stop can beat raw horsepower any day. And while a driver may cross the finish line, it's the team that wins the championship - every tire change, every calculation, every hand behind the scenes.Experience plays its role too. The veterans don't just drive; they guide, channeling the wild energy of the young into something lasting. Passion keeps the whole machine alive - because without fire in the chest, speed is just an empty number. And when the purpose is greater than profit, that's when legacies are built, long after the paychecks fade.But the track also reminds you: risks are the ticket to the podium. Play safe, and you'll stay stuck in the middle of the pack. You don't even need the best car - guts, creativity, and daring can turn "average" into "legendary." The real winners are the ones who stand out, who refuse to blend in with the grid.And in the middle of it all - gratitude matters. You never forget the people who built something good for you, who lifted you when you couldn't do it alone. Because even when you get hit, even when you spin out, that doesn't mean the race is over. The true champions? They keep driving.

title: Review F1 the movie
content: "F1: The Movie is an absolute cinematic thrill ride that left me glued to my seat from start to finish. The visuals are breathtaking-the cinematography, the dynamic camera movements, and the way the cars roar through every frame are nothing short of perfection. Hans Zimmer's background score elevates the tension and emotion to another level; every note felt like adrenaline pumping through my veins.The direction is top-notch, delivering not just a racing film but an immersive experience. And that final race? Pure cinematic gold. I didn't move an inch-I was on the edge of my seat thinking, 'What's going to happen next?' The ending ties it all together in a way that feels both powerful and satisfying.Honestly, this is one of the best racing films I've ever seen. A true masterpiece for motorsport lovers and movie fans alike.The only thing that could have been better: The explanation of racing strategies. As someone who isn't a motorsport fan, I felt a little lost during some strategic moments. A bit more clarity here would make the film even more accessible to new viewers."

title: The New F1 Movie - A Disappointment for Real Fans
content: I didn't enjoy the new Formula 1 movie at all. It lacked realism and was drenched in typical Hollywood fantasy. The storyline was completely unbelievable - Brad Pitt playing an ageing F1 driver just didn't work. It felt forced and disconnected from reality.The only part of the film I thought was worth watching was the Daytona segment - everything after that descended into cringe and chaos. The plot was full of nonsense, dramatizing Formula 1 to a ridiculous degree, far beyond anything grounded in the real sport.One of the worst aspects was how the movie glorified crashes and accidents. Considering real drivers have lost their lives in F1 due to crashes, turning that into dramatic entertainment felt tasteless and disrespectful.This is a film that simply didn't need to be made. Formula 1 isn't something that translates well into a fictional movie format, and frankly, it shouldn't be forced to.Some of the racing visuals were decent, but even that couldn't save it. The cameo appearances by Stefano Domenicali and Toto Wolff felt unnecessary, flat, and awkward - they would've been better off using actors.I wouldn't recommend this movie to any real Formula 1 fans. And for newcomers? The overly chaotic plot and constant technical jargon about tyre strategies and race tactics would just confuse them. It tries to please both camps and ends up pleasing neither.

title: A High-Octane Triumph on and off the Track
content: F1 is a thrilling, finely crafted racing film that hits all the right notes, whether you're a die-hard motorsport fan or just in it for a good time at the movies. Helmed with exceptional technical precision and cinematic flair, the film delivers a dynamic, high-speed spectacle that's grounded in a surprisingly authentic portrayal of the world of Formula 1.The screenplay strikes an impressive balance between adrenaline-fueled action and grounded human drama. It captures the spirit and intensity of F1 racing without veering into cliché or melodrama. The film's narrative flows smoothly, punctuated by sharp dialogue and well-paced emotional beats that make the story resonate beyond the roar of engines.Brad Pitt is simply outstanding. Charismatic and utterly convincing, he brings depth and gravitas to his role as a seasoned driver making a final stand in a fiercely competitive world. It's a performance that's both understated and magnetic, anchoring the film with a maturity that elevates the entire ensemble.What truly sets F1 apart is its remarkable fidelity to real-life racing culture. The attention to detail-from the paddocks to the politics, the rivalries to the raw danger of the sport-is spot-on. The film benefits enormously from its close collaboration with the F1 world, capturing not just the spectacle, but the ethos of a sport that demands everything from its athletes.Technically, F1 is a marvel. The cinematography immerses you in the race like never before, with heart-pounding, beautifully choreographed sequences that rival anything ever put on screen in the genre. The sound design, editing, and visual effects are all first-rate, contributing to an experience that feels as visceral as it is cinematic.Even if you're not particularly drawn to motorsport, F1 is an exhilarating, entertaining ride-smartly written, superbly acted, and executed with passion and precision. It's a celebration of speed, ambition, and resilience that will keep you engaged from start to finish.

title: A Perfect 'Pitt' Stop
content: Some movies come with great credibility of cast and crew and you sense that there is hardly anything that could go wrong about them. The movie F1 definitely belongs to that group of films. The movie boasts of great casting, and the technical departments firing on all cylinders ;)The story of the movie isn't something that most of us haven't really seen before. It is a typical story of the comeback of a long lost legend in the sport. The screenplay is so gripping that the movie barely gives you any breather to think and not in a tiring way. There are very few movies in recent years that can keep your eyes glued to the screen and F1 definitely takes the podium in that aspect ;) For someone who barely knows anything about this sport, I was totally intrigued and credit to the director for the way he balances sport and emotions. I don't think I have to say much about cinematography because it is exactly how it should be to make this movie top notch and the adrenaline rush that you get with some racing scenes is unmatchable. Hans Zimmer's score along with some cool tracks make Although some dialogues feel pretty cheesy, the callbacks are executed to perfection.Brad Pitt is the heart and heartthrob of this film. He has played the role to perfection and just does a lot with his smile in this film. Javier Bardem's role feels a bit cheesy initially but as the movie goes on the character gets stronger and he is a treat to watch. The chemistry between the two makes this movie more special. Kerry Condon's role is well written and performed by her even better. Damson Idris goes toe to toe with Brad Pitt throughout the movie and brings a different energy to the movie. Rest of the cast and 'crew' have done a very decent job. Then there are the racing world guest appearances which surely bring some hoots and cheers.F1 is another prime example of a well made movie with a tight screenplay can cater to different types of audiences without having to try too hard. It sticks to its guts and never goes off the track ;)

title: EXCITING FILM WITH GREAT TRUNS BY PITT & IDRIS
content: Joseph Kosinski's dazzling and engaging motion picture epic about Formula One racing. It concerns a retired veteran F1 driver named Sonny Hayes, brilliantly played by Brad Pitt at his charismatic best, who is called on by his old friend and former F1 teammate Ruben Carvantes, wonderfully played by Javier Bardem, to drive his Formula One car and team up with his young talented rookie driver, Joshua Pearce, sensationally played by Damson Idris, to help him save his racing company APXgp from being sold by the board investors. What follows is a compelling human drama mixed with spectacular racing sequences that are truly breathtaking. Skillful direction by Kosinski, with magnificent cinematography by Claudio Miranda that is absolutely astonishing, and a rousing original score by Hans Zimmer that enhances the film's excitement. Hands down one of the most entertaining films of this year. Highly recommended.

title: A High-Speed Disappointment
content: "F1: The Movie" promised a thrilling dive into the world of Formula 1 - the speed, the glory, the rivalries - but instead, it skids off track before the first lap is even complete.Visually, the film tries to dazzle with sleek cars and big-budget race sequences, but even those feel oddly sterile and over-CGI'd, lacking the raw intensity real F1 fans crave. The sound design, which should have been a highlight given the iconic roar of F1 engines, is oddly muted - as if someone turned down the volume on the very thing we came to hear.The real problem, however, lies in the script. The story is a formulaic, cliché-heavy mess: an underdog with a troubled past, a cocky rival, a love interest shoehorned in without purpose, and a motivational speech just before the final race. It all feels like a patchwork of sports-movie tropes with no authentic emotional payoff. There's barely any depth to the characters - even the legendary figures they try to reference come across as one-dimensional fan service.Worse still, the film seems unsure who it's made for. Hardcore F1 fans will be disappointed by the lack of technical realism and historical accuracy, while casual viewers may struggle to care about a drama that assumes a level of emotional investment it never earns.In the end, F1: The Movie is like a flashy prototype that looks good on paper but crashes on debut. A wasted opportunity to tell a truly compelling story from one of the most adrenaline-fueled sports in the world.

title: Made for the summer
content: F1 is a straightforward summer blockbuster that needs to be seen on the big screen.Brad Pitt leads a great all-around cast as the lovable Sonny Hayes. He plays it exactly how you'd expect Brad Pitt to play a cool racecar driver-and it works perfectly, with everyone delivering solid performances across the board.Now, the story isn't anything too deep or revolutionary, but it does what it needs to do. It's here to entertain, and I was absolutely entertained!The real heart of the movie is the racing, and it is awesome-intense, engaging, and obviously fast! Not only are the races beautifully shot, but the whole movie looks fantastic from start to finish.F1 is just a flat-out fun time! It's the kind of movie that's made for summer and gives you exactly what you came for. I'll admit it might hit a little harder if I knew even a bit more than absolutely nothing about Formula 1, but honestly, that's the beauty of a movie like this-you don't need to know a thing going in. Joseph Kosinski makes it totally accessible, and the excitement pulls you right in.Definitely recommend checking this one out-and trust me, it's a must-see in theaters!Am I going to start watching Formula 1 now? Probably not... but I did think about it-for like a second.

title: F1: The Movie" Delivers High-Octane Thrills Despite a Crowded and Cluttered Storyline
content: The much-anticipated "F1: The Movie" has roared onto the screen, offering a visually stunning and adrenaline-pumping glimpse into the world of Formula 1. While the film succeeds in capturing the raw intensity of the sport, a hurried narrative and a misplaced romantic subplot prevent it from taking a permanent place on the podium of racing classics.Starring Brad Pitt as the seasoned but retired driver Sonny Hayes, who is coaxed back to the grid to mentor the prodigious rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), the film boasts a compelling premise. The on-track action is where "F1: The Movie" truly shines. Director Joseph Kosinski, of "Top Gun: Maverick" fame, once again proves his mastery in orchestrating breathtaking practical effects and heart-stopping racing sequences. The roar of the engines, the blur of the scenery, and the visceral sense of speed are all palpable, offering audiences an experience that feels remarkably close to the real thing.A significant highlight for any Formula 1 enthusiast is the authentic depiction of the racing world. The inclusion of real F1 drivers, teams, and circuits lends an unparalleled layer of realism to the film. Seeing familiar faces on the grid and iconic tracks rendered in cinematic glory is a genuine treat and a clear nod of respect to the sport and its dedicated fanbase.However, the film's narrative engine sputters under the weight of its own ambition. The storyline feels compressed, as if the writers attempted to cram several seasons' worth of drama into a two-hour runtime. Character arcs feel rushed, and significant plot points are introduced and resolved with a swiftness that undermines their emotional impact. The corporate intrigue and team dynamics, while interesting, are not given enough room to breathe and feel fully developed.Adding to the narrative clutter is an unnecessary and underdeveloped love angle between Pitt's character and a brilliant engineer on the team. The romantic subplot feels tacked on and does little to advance the central story of mentorship, rivalry, and redemption. In a film already brimming with high-stakes drama on the track, this diversion feels like a needless pit stop that only serves to slow the momentum.Despite these narrative shortcomings, "F1: The Movie" remains a largely enjoyable and exhilarating ride. The stellar performances from Pitt and Idris, coupled with the awe-inspiring technical achievements in its racing sequences, make it a worthwhile watch. For fans of the sport, the authenticity and respect with which Formula 1 is portrayed will undoubtedly be a major draw. While the story might have benefited from a more focused and patient approach, the sheer spectacle of the racing is enough to get the adrenaline flowing. It's a good movie that, with a more streamlined story, could have been great.

title: Good racing film & not too formulaic!
content: This film is good to see in a theater; the visceral feel of racing is well depicted both through point-of-view driving shots and long views. The editing is good. The film is fairly long, but didn't feel long, a testament to good editing and pacing, with engaging characters. Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, a middle-aged driver who is skilled, but not serious or orthodox, and who has been bouncing around less prestigious race circuits for years. An old racing friend recruits him for his struggling Formula One team. Sonny is more than a little off-putting, especially at first, to the tech crew, pit crew and other drivers. The team really needs success in the Grand Prix season, which is half over and a dud thus far. This might seem an obvious set-up for a predictable story arc, but it wasn't; as a viewer, I was surprised by many of the developments (and not so much by others). Character arcs were well handled, and new revelations about the characters made sense and worked. The insights into the technology (lots of it!), rules, and stakes of Formula One racing held my interest. Plus, there was some fairly subtle material about the challenges women face taking part in the still man-dominated sport. A good and exciting film experience.

title: A Thrilling Ride Through Speed, Passion, and Rivalry - 8/10
content: You don't need to be a sports fan to appreciate what this film delivers. F1 is not just about racing - it's a story of ambition, pressure, ego, and resilience. The film is grounded in strong storytelling, layered characters, and intense emotional conflict, making it accessible even for those unfamiliar with the world of Formula 1.The direction is tight, the pacing is well-balanced, and the screenplay does a great job of building tension - both on and off the track. Performances are solid across the board, with the lead actors bringing nuance to roles that could've easily been one-dimensional.Visually, the film is impressive. The racing scenes are immersive but never overdone, while the quieter moments add real emotional depth. If there's any critique, it's that a few subplots could have been developed further. Still, it's a compelling film that respects the viewer's intelligence.

title: F1: a high-octane spectacle
content: F1 is a high-octane spectacle that transforms a familiar comeback story into first-rate entertainment through sheer craft and charisma.Kosinski's technical precision and unprecedented track access make the racing feel almost shockingly real; it's the rare film where IMAX feels essential. He's engineered something loud and elegant, built to thrill without losing polish.Brad Pitt's star power-gray at the temples, lived-in and magnetic-is a major asset. The film hits the expected sports-movie beats, with just enough character work to avoid dullness, though some dialogue slips into cheese. Still, the brisk tempo keeps it irresistible.Zimmer's score and the film's sound design are thunderous, while Chris Stapleton's song lands perfectly. For me, F1 is a must-see and one of the year's best: a crowd-pleaser that feels fast and lean despite its long runtime.

title: Vintage Bruckheimer and Cutting-edge Kosinski
content: F1 is maybe the best kind of summer blockbuster we are likely to get in this day and age...engineered by the sure hands of veteran producer Jerry Bruckheimer, the man behind most of the biggest, most bombastic, most entertaining American films of the past 40 years; and Joseph Kosinski, the man responsible for some of the slickest action movies ever made. Like all of Kosinski's films, even the regrettable Netflix/Covid thriller Spiderhead, he tells a fairly simple story that nevertheless doesn't lose focus on the human, emotional themes amidst all the pyrotechnics and world-building. What many critics ascribe to his 'cliches' are actually proven story beats which, if told effectively, will hold universal appeal after all the fads and supposedly 'daring' films of today are forgotten. (Nothing ages more horribly than the hip movies).The core of the story here is about collaboration, showing how the Formula One sport works, as well as (similarly to Nolan's Inception) serving as a metaphor for filmmaking. Brad Pitt and Damson Idris (more perfect casting on Kosinski's part) ground the story in their hotshot rival forced-teammates dynamic. Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon give solid supporting performances. Everyone in the film works together like a well-oiled machine, or maybe like those hyper-coordinated pit stop crews. On a technical level it's quite close to flawless.Which leads into the absurdly clueless commentary on this film. First off I knew nothing about Formula 1 going into this, so can easily discount all the neurodivergent arguments from racing fans about the length of the pit stops or whatever. Obviously the film had to generate some sort of appeal outside this very niche fanbase, and Brad Pitt's cool detachment manages to prevent the whole thing from becoming a nerd-fest. Online criticism that stupid American filmmakers could never hope to understand such an intricate sport as Formula One laughably ignores the fact that Lewis Hamilton, a champion F1 racer, had a very hands-on role as producer.When this became apparent the criticism instead metastasized around the script, with many reviews calling the dialogue terrible, even unbearable, with the only high point being the racing scenes. I don't buy that after seeing the film. The script is serviceable. It gets the job done. It's quite humorous at exactly the points it needs to be and knows when to cut back to allow serious moments. It's what you'd expect from a Bruckheimer film. And compared to today's Marvel movies/shows it's like Billy Wilder himself rose from the dead to write it.There are only a few criticisms I would consider legitimate. The first is that the romantic element really is woefully underdeveloped. Brad Pitt and Kerry Condon's team engineer develop a relationship which ultimately feels transactional: Pitt beds her, then in the end just leaves still chasing his racing high. The cars come first. And the inner romantic in me was left quite disappointed. Meanwhile, though the film's style and themes are still recognizably Kosinski's, I noticed the Bruckheimer touch starting to overwhelm the proceedings. There are far more montages and quick cuts here than in the director's other, more stately films; sometimes it feels like Tony Scott made it. Not much of a complaint, but the movie is about as precisely, scientifically constructed as those race cars.

title: Must See in Theater
content: Thoroughly enjoyed this movie. I believe it's as close as you'll ever get to being in one of those cars. It's the thrill of the speed and camera work that makes this a theater must see. Loved the actors as well; perfectly cast. You don't have to be a racing fan to enjoy it. It had a real "Rocky" type vibe of rooting for the underdog for those old enough to have seen Rocky. You can just sit back and enjoy the actors' camaraderie & the soundtrack. Wish it had developed a tad more of a story aside from the racing, but was still enough to recommend it. Pitt's charm was on full display as was Javier Bardem's and Damson Idris. A treat!

title: Appreciated the high quality of the production and the effort.
content: This was such a Thrilling Experience at the theatre , The Dubai mall theatre was so good and so well maintained and that experience was Worth the money , Seeing this film in the big screen IS the way you should see this film because the experience and the feel of the action and the vibration from the crashes and the engines and everything else makes the experience so much more immersive and thrilling.What makes this a 9/10 for me is the LEGENDARY production quality , there is a lot of movies with great production but this was Just so impressive , the way they placed those cameras in a perfect way in the formula 1 cars was so impressive and the way they captured the best angles and point of view's , The production was something really , the way they arranged the filming into filming with actual F1 drivers in an actual F1 race only made the film more immersive, and the locations wow the VAST locations that they shot in was so incredible seeing this globetrotting journey with the formula 1 drivers was amazing the way every race takes place in those circuits and just experiencing every new turn and track was so incredibly thrilling , The cast really took the movie seriously and really expanded on this whole "Retired F1 driver returns to his glory" and they really nailed that so well , the production as a whole has been taken seriously by the cast like every scene was so well done. The editing alone deserves a freaking award , how the HELL did they film a scene with like 15+ cameras and STILL edit it to make it so good like I'm so impressed by that Espically the in-car cameras that were placed in like choosing the best angle and the best view is really something extraordinary and I do think this should atleast be given an award for this incredible editing it is so impressive.The plot is definitely good as well it's a simple one but it really has a lot to offer, A retired legendary F1 driver is brought up to come race back again to revive a team that is on the brink of being sold , and I think a simple plot was the way to go since the film focused on the races and the action.The cast were incredible and As always Brad Pitt is such an awesome Actor , he literally got 30,000,000 to play this role ( yeah Apple producers are rich ) but he really took that role seriously and I really loved that about him , the fact that he trained and did a lot of work to perfect this "Formula 1 legend" is definitely amazing , and his performance alone is enjoyable, Damson Idris was also a great teammate I really liked his character development and the way he slowly developed into liking Brad pitt's character , Samson Kayo was definitely a great dude I know him from the movie "Puss in boots:The last wish" I really liked his involvement in this film as JP's friend and assistant, Javier Bardem was such a good character I really liked his performance in this film the way he handles the team and just overall a really great character , the rest of the cast also did a great job.What I also absolutely LOVE about this film is the way they made the Last and BEST race in the Yas Marina Circuit in Abu Dhabi UAE 🇦🇪 which is where I'm from , I absolutely appreciated that they included the national anthem it really made the film more likable , and as a Yas island loyal visitor I was definitely familiar with the place and locations and stuff like that and I absolutely loved that they used this awesome place for the final and best race but I wish they inlcluded more shots of the Ferrari World it definitely fitted the atmosphere of the film but eh some shots were good.Overall SUCH an incredible experience at the Dubai mall cinema I absolutely enjoyed this absolutely THRILLING film , such a high quality film really really high quality stuff , Amazing production , and absolutely amazing cast and acting , and that final race scene was AMAZING!! Joseph Kosinski really did his best with the directing job in this film and I think that this film deserves atleast an Oscar in any category because the film DESERVES it. 9/10.

title: Great if you are not a F1 fan
content: You need to be able to go into the movie knowing it is not going to be factually correct and leave your F1 knowledge at the door. Women are not portrayed the best and you can clearly see where some parts are CGI. Dramatically it is fun but is that, a dramatised version of a race. If you go into it knowing you will be entertained then great but don't expect it to be a realistic depiction of a standard formula 1 race. The story is a bit far fetched in that after being retired for 30 years the main character could just pick it straight back up again and race with no issues and be fit enough to do so.

title: A Review That Overshot the Pit Stop (But in Style)
content: Firstly, the music. Oh boy!I came expecting engine roars... and instead got Hans Zimmer blasting emotions at 300 km/h. I wasn't prepared for this concert. I came to the theatre for Formula 1, not to feel my feelings in Dolby Atmos. The guitars and drums were doing more laps than the cars.Cinematography? A thing to Marvel at!No, no - NOT that Marvel. This isn't a multiverse; this is motorsport. But I'll give it to Kosinski - the man knows how to shoot speed. The close-ups, the helmet cams, the pit lane drama - it's all smoother than Lewis on a soft compound.Kosinski pulls it off again As expected, the Top Gun: Maverick director brings his signature hyper-real-but-just-believable-enough style. Everything looks slick, the tension is palpable, and the racing feels intense. The cars look real, the tracks feel familiar, and the drama is soaked in cinematic polish - and maybe a bit of Red Bull too.Now, dear hardcore F1 fans... You will suffer. In silence.Yes, yes - the pit stop timings are a joke, strategy makes no sense, tyre degradation is apparently fictional, and someone said "DRS" like it's a Marvel superpower. You'll sit there, physically cringing, as your non-F1 friends lean over and say, "Omg I didn't know Formula 1 was this emotional." You'll want to scream, but you won't. You'll smile. You'll nod. Then later, you'll go straight to Reddit, type furiously, and find solace in a thread titled "Things that made no damn sense in the F1 movie - Spoiler heavy".Final thoughts: Is it accurate? Not really.Is it entertaining? Absolutely.Is Brad Pitt believable as an ageing F1 comeback driver? Well... let's just say Fernando Alonso might have notes.Go watch it. Just don't forget - it's not Formula 1. It's Formula Fun-damentally unrealistic, but kinda awesome.

title: A Return to the Racetrack
content: F1: The Movie is a high-octane sports drama that successfully combines the spectacle of Formula One with a classic, crowd-pleasing story. Directed by Joseph Kosinski, the film puts you directly in the cockpit with a level of authenticity and speed that is truly breathtaking. The film follows Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a retired F1 driver who returns to the sport after a 30-year absence to save a struggling team and mentor a hotshot rookie (Damson Idris).The film's greatest strength is its incredible visuals and commitment to realism. By filming at real F1 race weekends and using cutting-edge camera technology, the movie puts you closer to the action than ever before. The sound design is thunderous, and the in-car shots are immersive, making the audience feel the sheer speed and G-forces of the sport. It's a film that truly delivers on its promise to be the most authentic-looking racing movie ever made.While the plot of the "old dog returns for one last shot" is a familiar one, the performances elevate the material. Brad Pitt brings a quiet charisma and a sense of world-weariness to Sonny Hayes, while Damson Idris is excellent as the ambitious and cocky rookie. Their dynamic is the emotional core of the film, and it's what grounds the high-speed action in a story about redemption and partnership. The movie is a bit long and can sometimes get bogged down in technical details, but it never loses sight of the human drama at its core.F1: The Movie is a thrilling and well-made film that serves as both a fantastic introduction to the world of F1 and a satisfying piece of blockbuster entertainment. It's a must-watch for racing fans and a great ride for anyone looking for a visually spectacular cinematic experience.

title: A solid racing film, more for those new to the sport :)
content: If you're a hardcore F1, long time racing fan, you're going to be underwhelmed by this film. Not for the cinematography, it's spectacular, but for the content. This film isn't for those of us that can name every world champion or reserve driver since 1981, like 'Drive to Survive' this film is aimed at growing F1s fan base and for those who are brand or relatively new to the sport, it does a great explainer on simple things like race formats, strategy, and terminology whilst interlacing it with your classic old dog new tricks comeback story, and some cameos for those who love F1.It's Lewis Hamiltons chance at film, Brad Pitts chance to be Steve McQueen, and Formula One's chance to sell you itself.Enjoyed it, would watch probably once more for the cinematography and sound, but you know the plot, you know the players, but if you've got kids, partners, or friends on the fringes it will bring them in.Solid movie :)

title: Perfectly Tuned
content: F1 is a technical marvel and an emotional ride. The Dolby Vision imagery brings the paddocks and racing circuits to life with razor sharp contrasts, every frame sleek and immersive. Hans Zimmer's score pulses through the film with perfect restraint, amplifying the tension without overwhelming it.What elevates the story is the arc of Joshua Pearce, a rookie who begins as arrogant and unlikable but gradually evolves into a teammate worth rooting for. His transformation feels natural, paced with care. Brad Pitt as Sonny Hayes anchors the film with his trademark charisma, a calm presence that steadies the chaos around him. Their dynamic carries the film beyond spectacle into something human and resonant.The racing sequences are stunning, both thrilling and readable. Strategy is shown with clarity, detailed enough to feel authentic while remaining accessible. The film never loses momentum, flowing like a continuous lap that never stalls.

title: Racing as the True Protagonist
content: As someone passionate about cars, F1, Le Mans, and GT racing, this movie absolutely blew me away. Joseph Kosinski nailed it again. Just like Top Gun: Maverick put us inside fighter jets, F1 puts us inside the car. The POVs, cinematography, background score, and sheer realism made every scene feel alive with adrenaline.I've watched Ford v Ferrari and Rush, both top-tier motorsport films. Ford v Ferrari had deep emotional storytelling and Rush captured rivalry perfectly. But F1 surpasses them in pure immersive spectacle. My only slight letdown was not seeing much about team operations or technical politics, but that would have stretched it over 3 hours.Overall, this isn't just a racing movie - it's a cinematic experience where racing is the true protagonist. Despite its lower IMDb rating than Ford v Ferrari and Rush, for me, this is the greatest motorsport movie of all time.

title: Most boring movie I've ever seen
content: Extremely DisappointingTerrible storyline. Tremendously and unnecessarily prolonged duration. The movie felt like it was trying too hard to be deep or dramatic, but instead, it came off as flat and uninspired. The characters lacked development, the dialogue was painfully generic, and the emotional beats missed the mark entirely. It's almost impressive how a film about one of the most thrilling sports in the world managed to be this boring. Yes, the graphics were stunning-absolutely top-tier-but great visuals alone can't carry a film with no heart. The pacing was atrocious, with scenes dragging on far past their welcome. There was very little actual racing, and what little action there was felt oddly disconnected from the characters. It's a shame, because the potential was there, but it was completely squandered on slow storytelling, dull monologues, and style-over-substance direction. I walked out of the theater feeling more tired than excited. Not even worth watching on streaming. This was not the F1 movie fans deserved.

title: VROOM VROOM
content: I'm not an F1 fan and don't know the rules of the game and how accurate the details were but this film was A HELL of a ride.I watched it in the standard 2.39:1 aspect ratio but I enjoyed this film enough; maybe on IMAX it would be the same, just with more vertical information. Dolby Atmos elevated the whole experience. It was so loud that my seat was trembling. The sound design nailed the "vroom vroom" energy.Claudio Miranda put great care on camera work. Cinematography is stellar with good lighting and well-composed, grounded shots. I am wondering how it was filmed, especially in the race sequences which looked clean and dynamic. The film felt authentic and immersive that you can't even say that there was CGI.Now let's talk about Hans Zimmer - he is the GOAT. His compositions felt grand and the vibe had a mix of Inception (e.g., track "F1") and TDK at the same time. The original music blends rock and indie, afrobeat, house, trap beats which complements the whole atmosphere and pace.The story is simple but effective. There's a clear focus on teamwork, ego, and ambition. I liked how they showed strategy in racing - it was smart and insightful. The character dynamics has some flaws, but they're good enough to keep you invested.Brad Pitt plays his usual charismatic self. Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and Kerry Condon all bring great energy to their roles.My main concern is that the film is fast paced (no pun intended) due to quick editing choices that made it a bit tough to fully absorb everything, especially in the first part of the movie; but then again - it's Formula 1; it's all about the speed baby.Overall, this film delivers a thrilling, authentic experience (even for non-F1 fans). This whole ride was worth it.

title: High Energy Vroom Film
content: F1 is one of the highest energy films you will ever see, right up there with the Mission: Impossible series. It is a modern-day adaptation of an aging star who was once a promising young buckaroo. Then, for various reasons, they got sidelined. This movie describes their second and possibly last chance at redemption - one last shot at the big prize. Similarly themed films are On the Waterfront with Marlon Brando, Real Steel with Hugh Jackman, or perhaps The Pursuit of Happyness with Will Smith.In F1, the movie focuses on our star Brad Pitt, playing the role of washed-up motor world driver Sonny Hayes. He's of a certain age because people in the movie keep telling you how old he is, even though he's Brad Pitt and seems to never really age. Pitt wears this role comfortably - He's a kidder with a devil-may-care attitude. He's got nothing to lose, and he shows that in every aspect of his life in this movie.Enter Javier Bardem, who was terrific as his old friend Ruben, who is in charge of the racing team. He's heavily in debt and wants to take a chance that Sonny would join his team as a second driver to mentor the young and upcoming superstar Joshua Pearce, played by Damson Idris. That's the crux of the story.The crux of the story is about how the young and old buckaroos deal with each other. In some ways, it's a bit Rocky-esque because Pitt doesn't back down, whereas the young kid feels invincible like all young kids do, until they learn they are not. Does Pitt have enough in the tank to drive this relationship to success, or will he drive it off the road into complete failure?There are some side stories, too. The young kid's relationship with his Mom, explored only thinly. Pitt's relationship with a woman on the team. Pitt's backstory. And a nefarious team ownership that keeps appearing in rumors about a potential team action that could affect everyone.Fortunately, those are only backstories, tho the bad guys show their faces eventually, which leaves you rooting for the good guys.There are no major downsides to this film. Every week, they race the F1 circuit all over the world. You get a cool view of what that is like in several countries. Pretty cool. But the highlight is the racing! Fantastic scenes of fast cars, unbelievable scenes, heartpounding energy with an equally loud, supportive musical score. You will leave the theater breathless at the end as we get a Hollywood-esque finale of scenes with a climactic do-or-die race.I highly recommend F1. I never watch this racing in real life, but I enjoyed spending a few hours in this high-energy, thundering movie. Enjoy.

title: In Case You Missed It in the '90s
content: This is an ambitious film, designed to dominate the box office, bringing together heavyweights: Apple, Jerry Bruckheimer, and Joseph Kosinski, the team behind Top Gun: Maverick. Its goal is for the viewer to experience, in some way, what it's like to be a pilot.It's classic-style commercial cinema, using every industry tool to deliver a two-and-a-half-hour spectacle that's overwhelming but not very imaginative. The film works as a joyful escape on the big screen, celebrating the magic of theaters, even if that comes at the expense of soul or lasting impact.Ultimately, it's a tribute to '90s blockbusters, with all the energy and spectacle of those years, but with the same limitation: leaving the audience with little more than pure visual amazement.

title: Terrible script, mediocre movie
content: Get ready to roll your eyes, this was a let down. A very generic script is what kills the experience. A 30 year old plot that we have all seen a dozen times before. It's delightfully ironic that despite our main character being presented as Mr take that risk; fearless but reckless driver with a gambling addiction to boot. The movie cant take any inspiration from this and adheres to a safe and formulaic narrative that had me losing interest at a few points.The film is of course technically solid. But even still it didn't blow me away like top gun mav did. I felt like they were holding back. The soundtrack is good and cinematography nice. Theres some style but not enough to save it. You won't watch it again and nobody will be talking about it in a month. Theres too much budget, brands and money so the film played it too safe and made something uninspired.
