title: Amazing movie
content: Buckle up for the most electrifying racing film in decades! Brad Pitt delivers a career-best performance as a grizzled F1 veteran lured back for one final season. Director Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun: Maverick) revolutionizes racing cinematography with mind-blowing practical effects - cameras mounted on real F1 cars at 200+ mph create unparalleled realism. The thunderous Dolby Atmos sound mix puts you right in the cockpit.The gripping story balances white-knuckle racing with compelling human drama, exploring the dangerous allure of speed through Pitt's complex mentor relationship with a rookie driver (Damson Idris). Authentic F1 details shine, from accurate team politics to real-life driver cameos (including Lewis Hamilton as producer). While some racing movie tropes appear, they're executed with such visceral intensity and emotional weight that they feel fresh.A perfect blend of character-driven storytelling and technical mastery that will leave both gearheads and casual viewers breathless. The definitive racing film of our generation demands to be seen on the biggest screen possible.

title: Awesome Movie
content:
Review of F1 (2025) - 8/10 StarsJoseph Kosinski's F1 roars onto the screen with the high-octane energy of a Formula 1 race, blending authentic motorsport thrills with a character-driven story that mostly hits the mark. Produced in collaboration with Lewis Hamilton and featuring Brad Pitt in the lead role, the film is a cinematic adrenaline rush that balances spectacle and substance, even if it occasionally leans too hard on genre conventions.Brad Pitt stars as Sonny Hayes, a retired Formula 1 driver lured back into the cockpit to mentor a rising star and help his struggling team compete against the sport's modern titans. At 60, Pitt exudes charisma and gravitas, delivering a performance that is both rugged and reflective. His chemistry with Damson Idris, who plays Joshua Pearce-the young prodigy-adds emotional depth to the otherwise fast-paced narrative.The heart of F1 lies in its commitment to realism. Filmed during actual Grand Prix weekends and in collaboration with the real F1 paddock, the movie captures the intensity, danger, and glory of racing like never before. The use of real footage-mixed seamlessly with choreographed sequences-immerses viewers in the sport's visceral experience. Kosinski, known for Top Gun: Maverick, once again proves his mastery in capturing motion and speed with clarity and impact.The technical elements are top-tier. Hans Zimmer's score pulses with energy, matching the rhythm of the racetrack. Cinematographer Claudio Miranda delivers sweeping shots of circuits across the globe, from Monaco's narrow streets to the high-speed corners of Silverstone. The sound design is thunderous and immersive, bringing the audience as close as possible to the roar of the engines.Where the film slightly falters is in its predictability. The mentor-student dynamic, the comeback arc, and the underdog story are all familiar territory. While executed well, the script doesn't break new narrative ground. Some supporting characters, including the team principal and rival drivers, feel underdeveloped, existing more as archetypes than fully realized personalities.Despite these limitations, F1 thrives on its sincerity and love for the sport. It's not just a racing film; it's a tribute to the passion, sacrifice, and obsession that drive the people behind the wheel and behind the scenes. Fans of Formula 1 will appreciate the attention to detail-from accurate team dynamics to nods at current F1 culture-while newcomers will still be swept up by the universal themes of legacy, mentorship, and redemption.Verdict:
F1 is a thrilling ride that offers both speed and soul. While its story may be familiar, the execution-both visually and emotionally-is undeniably impressive. It's a crowd-pleaser with genuine heart and a must-watch for motorsport fans.Rating: 8/10.

title: An Enjoyable Ride

title: An engaging racing movie
content:
F1 turned to be an engaging movie and although it took around 155 minutes, I barely felt bored while watching the movie (maybe felt bored only for 10 minutes).I watched it with D-Box (tried for first time) and Dolby ATMOS technology to let me feel it most.Here I'd like to highlight some of the key points and also tell about my D-Box experience:1- First of all, the SOUND of movie is great! Cars sound really alive, musics are so cool, the audio in races are so immersive and you can feel the racing vibe. I really liked the sound of movie (although I'm not sure how much of this perfect audio experience was due to Dolby ATMOS. I think the movie should sound really great even without ATMOS)2- The competition is really tight! You feel the high urge for competition during the entire movie and the story meet this expectation.3- Everything is really fast in the F1 ring, so you (as an audience) should be fast too. And I liked that :)
Conversations are fast, plots are changing dynamically, you can clearly hear the rush in the field, and (again) sounds make you believe that everything is at top speed. I couldn't even understand some of the conversations and unable to realise what happened for some parts during the race. It's hesitating to be unable to follow the story but I think it is part of the feeling of the movie as everything MUST be fast. So, the audience should be fast too :)
And that's what make you feel the mental pressure during the F1 drivers.Do you see the point?4- D-Box made it feel like vibrating on the engine, sudden (but light) crashes, and the left/right turns. It was sometimes unsync and for some sections, there was no movement or vibration even if the scene was in the race and there was a turn (and you'd think that it should now work but didn't)!So, it was not really consistent during the whole movie. But It was cool. It was definitely better than nothing. However, it was not something to say "Wow!".It would be interesting for first try, especially if you're fine with the extra cost, but if you couldn't get the chance to watch it on D-Box, don't worry. It wasn't something seriously magnificent. I'd recommend it but not "highly" recommend it.

title: Waste of time
content: I went into F1: The Film expecting an adrenaline-fueled celebration of one of the most thrilling sports in the world, but what I got instead was a flat tire of a movie. Despite boasting incredible access to the world of Formula 1, the film feels more like a stitched-together highlight reel than a coherent story.The pacing is erratic-dragging in places where there should be intensity-and the attempts at drama feel forced, with manufactured rivalries that pale in comparison to the real stakes of the sport. Visually, it squanders the chance to capture the true speed and danger of F1 racing; instead of breathtaking shots, we're left with uninspired cinematography that could just as well have been from a generic sports documentary.Even the soundtrack, which should rev up the excitement, feels mismatched and oddly lifeless. By the end, the movie feels less like a celebration of Formula 1 and more like a missed opportunity. For die-hard fans, it will frustrate; for casual viewers, it will bore.Verdict: A disappointing pit stop that fails to capture the thrill, passion, and beauty of Formula 1.

title: F1
content: I've been watching regularly Formula 1 since Vettel started winning championships (on and off since Schumacher) - screw Drive to Survive, it got boring haha. He (Vettel) is still one of my favourite drivers because he is a true fan of the sport and showed it a bunch of times.I don't know Vettels opinion regarding this movie but what I saw, as an avid watcher of the sport but not one that knows all the in and outs of the sport, is that film is a great commercial for Formula 1 and I think that was the point. Not the relationship between Sonny and JP or the relationship between Sonny and Kate. Btw, JP's arc was undercooked and unsatisfying.I wanted to watch a race on the big screen with Hollywood level sound design and I kinda got that. Left wanting more of it, still. All I got was the spirit of a race plus a bit more, the minimum necessary to satisfy a fan, not a uber-fan. And that's for the best. They will never make a F1 movie for uber-fans. Nobody wants to watch that.Even Hamilton through this film critics the excessive amount of rules this sport has and through the script he's bending them or ignoring them. We all know that this sport will be a lot more fun without a bunch of its rules.The film was fun, great to watch on the big screen. The script needed more work, it has clear minuses. Some characters over-used (JP), others under-used (Kaspar). I overall liked it.

title: A great remake!
content: First off, let me get this out of the way. I love this movie, however, as I watched it, I couldn't escape the feeling I've seen this before.Turns out, I have. title: Pure Adrenaline
content: From the very first minutes, F1 makes it clear that it wants to grab you with the roar of the engines. Starting with Daytona, accompanied by that classic Florida radio station, is a brilliant touch that instantly pulls you into the racing world.Joseph Kosinski proves once again that he knows how to capture speed and danger. Just like in Top Gun: Maverick, he turns the races into an unforgettable visual spectacle. The camera hugs the track, the tires, and the drivers, creating a sense of vertigo rarely seen on screen. It's not just technique: there's storytelling in every overtake and every turn.Brad Pitt carries the film with magnetic presence. His character, a veteran with more wrinkles than illusions, works because the actor embraces age instead of hiding it. Watching him behind the wheel conveys both experience and risk. The supporting cast delivers, though it's clear the film is built around him.The plot isn't groundbreaking. Familiar tropes are everywhere-from the aging racer to the inevitable rivalries. Yet, the execution, with spectacular staging and relentless rhythm, keeps it all engaging. What matters here is the emotion, the tension of each race, and the adrenaline that spreads to the audience.What stands out is how the film balances spectacle with personal epic. It's not just cars circling a track: it conveys the sacrifice, solitude, and fleeting glory of the sport. This is big-screen cinema, designed to be felt with booming sound and sheer scale.In the end, F1 doesn't reinvent the genre, but it raises it to rare heights. It's a film made to be enjoyed, with sequences that glue you to your seat and with Brad Pitt proving why he's still a star. A movie that floors the accelerator from the start and never lets up.

title: F1: The Movie" - A High-Octane Thrill Ride!
content: From the deafening roar of the engines to the heart-stopping precision of a perfectly executed overtake, "F1: The Movie" isn't just a film; it's an immersion. Director Joseph Kosinski, fresh off his triumph with "Top Gun: Maverick," proves once again his unparalleled mastery of capturing kinetic energy on screen. This isn't merely a drama about racing; it feels like you're strapped into the cockpit, experiencing every g-force, every whisper of tire on tarmac, every agonizing decision made at 200 miles an hour.Brad Pitt delivers a nuanced performance as Sonny Hayes, the "greatest that never was," a seasoned driver pulled from semi-retirement to mentor the fiery prodigy Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). Their dynamic forms the emotional core of the film, a blend of mentor-mentee rivalry, respect, and the fierce dedication to a struggling team, APXGP. Javier Bardem is compelling as Ruben Cervantes, the team owner fighting to keep his dream alive, and Kerry Condon shines as the sharp technical director.What truly sets "F1" apart is its commitment to authenticity. Shot during actual Grand Prix weekends, with real F1 cars on real tracks, the racing sequences are breathtakingly real. You'll witness maneuvers that defy belief, captured with a visceral intensity that outstrips anything CGI could ever hope to achieve. The sound design alone deserves an Oscar - the engines don't just rumble, they scream directly into your soul.Beyond the spectacle, the film delves into the human drama of Formula 1: the immense pressure, the split-second decisions, the camaraderie, the rivalries, and the sheer mental and physical toll it takes. It beautifully portrays the fragile balance between individual ambition and team success. While the narrative might follow a somewhat familiar underdog trajectory, it's elevated by the exceptional performances, the stunning visuals, and the unwavering dedication to making the audience feel the speed and danger of the sport. "F1: The Movie" is a thrilling, white-knuckle ride that will leave you breathless and utterly captivated, whether you're a lifelong F1 fanatic or a newcomer to the world of motorsport.

title: A passionate racing film
content: F1: The Movie is an adrenaline-fueled celebration of motorsport, blending Hollywood spectacle with the heart of Formula 1. It captures the speed, danger, and emotion of racing with a sharp eye for drama and cinematic flair.Brad Pitt shines as Sonny Hayes. Damson Idris brings raw intensity as the arrogant prodigy, Joshua Pearce, whose growth arc is one of the film's best surprises. Add a strong supporting cast Kerry Condon, Javier Bardem and you get a team effort that fires on all cylinders.Yes, the film takes creative liberties. It's not a documentary, nor does it pretend to be. But if you're heading in just to nitpick realism, you're missing the point. The racing is thrilling, the stakes are personal, and the final act is pure cinematic magic.The racing in this film is nothing short of breathtaking each scene packed with heart-pounding action, beautiful cinematography, and emotionally charged drama. From daring overtakes to crashes that make you wince, it delivers everything you want from a racing movie and more.This movie is a love letter, to motorsport, to Formula 1, to the machines that defy limits. A seamless blend of drama and racing.I never wanted this movie to be perfect, I wanted it to be real and nothing real is ever perfect. It made me feel just like how the sport does, full of passion, heart, and chaos.And that's all I've ever really cared about.

title: Boys and their toys
content:
Seeing "F1: The Movie" with my 11-year-old grandson reminded me of my own motor racing career when I was about his age more than a half-century ago.It took place on a go-kart track at the Marlboro sports car raceway outside Baltimore. My dad would pack my kart in the trunk of his Pontiac Bonneville, and we would go racing a couple of Sundays a month.That go-kart was the greatest joy of my junior high school wonder years in an era when a young senator from Massachusetts was inaugurated president of the United States one sunny snow-covered Friday morning a few miles from our home in the Washington suburbs.Feeling like that kid again is one of the many pleasures of this joy ride of a movie.In my youth, drivers named Sterling Moss, Juan Fangio, and Jackie Stewart raced the Formula One circuits of Europe. Although their vehicles wore the storied auto dynasty badges of Lotus, Ferrari, Porsche and others, they look like comical cartoons, compared to today's perfectly aerodynamic, $15-million F1 rocket ships designed to never leave the ground.From me and my go-kart to the biggest names throughout the history Formula One, the sport has always been about boys and their toys.Beyond asking if you're Brad Pitt, is cosmetic work really necessary?, no further thinking is required to navigate "F1: The Movie." The cars aren't the only products of formulas in this project. So's the script. This is precision-engineered entertainment.It helps to have Brad in the driver's seat. Veneers or not, as he rounds the bend of 60, the Oscar-winning actor and producer still has more charisma in the tank than any ten normal mortals of the male persuasion.The role of coulda-been-a-contender driver Sonny Hayes fits Pitt as comfortably as Capt. Pete "Maverick" Mitchell fit Tom Cruise in the latest "Top Gun." That's no coincidence. Both blockbusters were directed by Joseph Kozinski, whose signature is getting superb performances from fine actors and the sleekest machines imaginable.Javiar Bardim is reliably excellent as Reuben Cervantes, Sonny's former teammate and rival who now owns the APXGP Formula One team. APXGP is in last place in the standings, and Reuben is hundreds of millions in debt. With one driver out for the season, he's stuck with a cocky rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) to lead the team. In Hail Mary desperation, Reuben seeks out Sonny - currently a beach bum living in his van - to save the day.Stop me if you've heard this one before ...
Kerry Conden - who all but stole the show from her manly fellow Oscar nominees in the wonderful "Banshees of Inisherin" - does something similar here. She plays former NASA engineer turned APXGP's chief designer Kate McKenna, who's got a lot to prove to folks, beginning with herself.Interestingly, considering this is a story of cutthroat competition, there's no arch nemesis driving for another team. All the dynamics - the rivalries, jealousies, treacheries and betrayals - take place inside APXGP world. Ostensibly a star turn for Pitt, what "F1" is ultimately about is the team.The team ... and product placement.From the cars to the drivers to the backgrounds whizzing by at 200 mph, any somewhat flat surface is a potential billboard. Not just the form-fitting jumpsuits and futuristic helmets, but the flame-retardant, body-sculpted underwear worn by Pitt and company are covered with logos. Not just for Mercedes and Geico, but for products ranging from Shark vacuums to to Tommy Hilfinger outfits. Ads for Expensify - whatever that is - not only adorn cars' roll bars (always in the POV shots) and Pitt's chest, but filled the Megaplex screen before the movie even began.Pitt and Idris actually learned to drive their F1 cars for the film. He and Idris can probably thank Tom Cruise for upping the ante for being a prettyboy movie star anymore.But the real star of the show is director Kozinski who fills the screen with such glorious action. Whether POV shots from the cars, high-speed jockeying wheel to wheel, or action seen from above, it's all visual poetry, making the pyrotechnics at the end feel redundant.As he did with "Top Gun," Kozinski makes technical details - the strategy of pit stops, the chemistry of tire rubber, the split-second negotiating of hairpin turns - understandable to general audiences.Along with the exotic machinery and high-octane testosterone behind the wheel, the gorgeous settings in places like Abu Dhabi where moored mega-yachts flank part of the course add reminders that "F1" isn't just about speed.It's also about wealth... power ... glamor... style ... excess. It's one more diversion mostly for rich men, for whom fast is never fast enough.In other words, boys and their toys.

title: Exactly what you sign up for!
content: F1 The Movie is an instant thrill ride from the first frame. Seeing it in IMAX is definitely worth it, the sound and the visuals grab you right away and don't let go until the credits roll. This was a very fun watch. Don't let the 2 hour and 45 minute run time alarm you... there are no parts of this movie that drag. Brad Pitt is great as the lead. Nothing new from Pitt... just the classic charm, coolness, charisma that you just can't take your eyes off. Javier Bardem also does a great job and his chemistry with Pitt feels very natural. Damson Idris does feel a little out of place at times surrounded by seasoned vets... but does his job well. No memorable scenes from Damson but he doesn't take away from the film. Story is fairly linear and some scenes are predictable BUT you still are hooked the entire way. Really fun watch though.

The exhilaration is constant-every engine roar, tire screech, and pit crew shout is rendered with an intensity that pulls you into the cockpit. Beyond the spectacle, there's a raw humanity in its characters-drivers chasing legacy, teams on the brink, families grappling with risk. That human connection fuels the excitement, making the races more than just battles for pole position-they become metaphors for resilience, ambition, and sacrifice.

title: Maverick on Wheels
content: It is pretty clear from the trailer of F1 that Joseph Kosinski is trying to do with Formula 1 cars and Brad Pitt what he did extremely successfully with Super Hornet Jet fighters and Tom Cruise in Maverick.....and he does come pretty close. F1 literally puts its audience in the driver's seat and is a highly entertaining and adrenaline infused sports thriller.Brad Pitt is perfectly cast as the over the hill, erstwhile F1 racer who was bubbling with talent and potential before his career got cut short after a dreadful accident on the racetrack and just like Pitt, his character gets another chance to get hailed as one of the best in the world. Javier Bardem, Damson Idris, Kerry Condon do a great job in their respective roles and support Pitt's protagonist very well.The real star of the movie is its great VFX and state of the art race stunts that keeps its viewers on the edge of their seats. The story is solid, the script is tight and pacy, the background music is great and just like Maverick, F1 is technically a very strong movie. However unlike Maverick, the audience needs to have some basic level of understanding and affection of the sport (F1) in order to fully appreciate the movie. Those who have never watched F1 may find themselves a bit confused for a couple of mins here or there but should quickly find themselves rooting for the team.This is a movie made for the big screen....the bigger, the better. If possible to be experienced on IMAX. This is a must watch for Action Junkies, F1 Afficionados and Brad Fans. I was fully engrossed in the movie, feeling every curve and bump on the racetrack and enjoying all its twists and turns. Highly entertaining. Loved it. 8.5/10.

title: Pure Speed, Pure Cinema

But honestly, if you're watching it for the thrills, the emotional payoff, and the sheer spectacle, it delivers pretty consistently.

title: More than just about F1 Racing, although that's incredible too
content: I've seen this movie twice already, and it's not just for guys, it's for all people who want to feel emotions in their movies again and not just be bombarded with CGI.The music is incredible, both the score and the songs they chose, it really delivers on the emotions during the races scenes! The cinematography is just amazing, and as for the accuracy of the F1, who cares. I used to watch F1 in the 90s, but I didn't go see this movie for its technical aspects.A lot of people won't understand since it's not just another concept-driven superhero story where the good guys win relatively easily and the story is predictable, you'll actually have to think a little to understand the values and reasons behind the story and, let's face it, most people today don't want to have to do that.Some moviegoers say that Brad Pitt did a bad job acting, but it's clear that the goal of this movie wasn't to win an Oscar. Sylvester Stallone was a bad actor and yet he made countless Rocky movies that fans rave about because of the heart of the main character, his morals, ethics, values and integrity. The reasons why Pitt's character makes his choices are clear to some, but won't be for all - why not just race for the fame, money and glory? Isn't just being in the F1 enough? The answer is clear : No, it's much more than that.So imagine some of the greatest scenes from Rocky, mixed with the struggle and instrumental score of The Martian, with a little thrown in from Forrest Gump, and the feel of Top Gun Maverick - some of the greatest drama/action movies from the past 40 years - and if you like these movies you'll love F1.

title: Fast, Loud, and Ultimately Forgettable
content:
F1: The Movie had the potential to be a gripping, high-octane tribute to the world of Formula 1-but instead, it stalls out somewhere between a glorified highlight reel and a soulless commercial.Visually, yes, it's sleek. The cinematography does a decent job capturing the speed and spectacle of the sport. But beyond the shiny surfaces, there's little substance. The film seems more interested in brand placements and slow-motion pit stops than in telling a story that actually matters. Characters are paper-thin, emotional stakes are practically nonexistent, and for a movie about one of the most adrenaline-fueled sports on Earth, it somehow manages to feel... boring.The script feels like it was assembled by a marketing team rather than screenwriters with passion for racing or storytelling. Dialogue is cringeworthy, and any attempt at building drama feels forced and formulaic. You don't feel like you're watching a human journey-you feel like you're being sold a lifestyle.Even hardcore F1 fans may leave disappointed. Instead of exploring the fierce rivalries, mental strain, and the personal sacrifices behind the scenes, the film opts for generic hero worship and cheap thrills.In the end, F1: The Movie is all show and no soul. Like a car with a stunning exterior and no engine, it looks the part-but it never really goes anywhere.Rating: (2/5)
For visuals and engine sounds only. Everything else? Pit stop required.

title: A Stylish Return to the Track with High-Octane Thrills
content: "F1" (2025) delivers a visually stunning and exhilarating ride into the world of Formula 1 racing, marking a strong return for Brad Pitt in a high-octane spectacle. The film boasts exceptional production values and showcases the sheer speed and intensity of the sport, making for a truly immersive cinematic experience.Brad Pitt is, as always, incredibly stylish and charismatic in his role. He brings a seasoned gravitas to his character, effortlessly commanding the screen and adding a layer of star power to the fast-paced narrative. His presence alone elevates the film.Director Joseph Kosinski, known for his work on "Top Gun: Maverick," brings his signature flair to the racing sequences. The action on the track is nothing short of stunning, with dynamic camera work and breathtaking visuals that put you right in the cockpit. These sequences are truly the highlight of the film, delivering a visceral sense of speed and danger.The film's plot centers around a veteran driver returning to the demanding world of F1, providing a solid framework for the racing spectacle. While the narrative may adhere to some familiar sports drama tropes, it's executed with a high level of technical proficiency and passion for the sport.Overall, "F1" (2025) is a stunning and thrilling experience that largely succeeds in bringing the excitement of Formula 1 to the big screen. Brad Pitt's iconic presence and the film's incredible action sequences make it a must-watch for racing enthusiasts and fans of high-octane cinema.

title: To be the best of the best in Formula One
content: To be the best of the best in Formula One, you're not just racing against other drivers. You're racing the machine itself. Victory demands unmatched speed, strength, and strategy. Does this film capture that reality? It absolutely does, as it offers but a glimpse into the thrilling intensity of the real sport.What makes this film particularly compelling is its immersive execution. It places the viewer right in the driver's seat, delivering a visceral sense of the tension and exhilaration that define each race. The cinematography is outstanding, masterfully shifting between cockpit views and dynamic race footage to create a strikingly authentic atmosphere. The sound design further enhances the experience, using realistic audio and racing jargon to make you feel like a true member of the pit crew.While the storyline follows a familiar arc, a fiery rookie and a renegade old-timer, the performances from Damson Idris and Brad Pitt bring undeniable charisma and chemistry. Their emotional interplay adds depth to the narrative and keeps the audience engaged, even as the plot treads well-worn paths. The emotional highs and lows of competition are palpable, and the audience's reactions, including audible gasps, speak to the film's dramatic impact.In the end, this is a film for those who crave the thrill. Its standout cinematography and sound design offer a gripping portrayal of the racing world. While it may not convert everyone into lifelong F1 fans, it will undoubtedly spark some curiosity about the sport. And if you have the chance to see it on an IMAX screen - take it. As I would have no doubt it would be amazing. The larger the format, the more electrifying the experience.

title: Not your typical sports thriller!
content: F1 The movie is a breath of fresh air in the sports drama genre. The movie revolves around Sonny Hayes played by Brad Pitt, and how his character gets back into the racing championship.You might find some similarities between F1 and Ford v Ferrari but, I think F1 is holistically a better movie than Ford v Ferrari. The cinematography is exceptional and the makers have used lights and the fireworks brilliantly. The story is unique and gives depth to the characters. The race sequences are shot fabulously and makes the audience sit on the edge of their seat! The movie has some breath-taking visuals which makes the audience go crazy!The background score by Hans Zimmer complements the visuals and builds some real tension. The actors have done their characters justice. Overall, the movie is a cinematic experience and should be enjoyed on the biggest screen possible! Inspired of not being a F1 fan, I enjoyed the movie very much!

title: Great Racing Movie
content: I'm not a big fan of F1 or racing in general. Not because I dislike it, I've just never really followed it or been that interested. That said, I do love Brad Pitt, and when I saw Joseph Kosinski was directing a Formula 1 movie with him in the lead, I knew it was going to be worth watching.I really enjoyed this one. The cinematography is incredible and the racing sequences feel intense and grounded. There's a real sense of speed and danger to it, and the tension works really well even if you're not familiar with the sport. The story arcs are solid and everything feels surprisingly realistic.I hadn't seen Damson Idris in anything before this, but he really impressed me. He has great screen presence and definitely feels like someone with a big future ahead of him. Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon are both fantastic too, which isn't surprising, and they add a lot to the emotional weight of the film.Overall, this is a really well made and exciting movie. It actually made me want to watch more racing in real life, which feels like a pretty good sign they did something right.

title: Great for action/race fans!
content: "F1" - I saw this one with a friend while we were in Austin. First, a brief comment on the Ipic theater, which is a high-end theater in the "Domain" district. It is very nice (and expensive) theater with "pods" so you can talk without disturbing anyone, and have dinner and drinks delivered. It was late, so we didn't have dinner, but the seats were great! As for the film, Brad Pitt stars as a former F1 driver coaxed out of retirement by Javier Bardem. Of course, there is a young driver (Damson Idris) who resents Pitt's appearance on team. The direction and action are both first rate, and you will feel that you are in the car while Pitt and Idris are driving. The cinematography is fantastic along with the special effects which are excellent. There are some scenes that perhaps could have been edited down a bit (in particular, an obligatory love scene) as the film runs a bit long at 2 hours, thirty-five minutes, but it is still a great experience that I highly recommend for race and action fans. 8/10.

title: Let's crank the Rush to eleven!
content: It's not easy to make an original movie about racing. The temptation to throw every known twist and plot cliche into one single movie is really hard to resist. Well, this film is no exception.Without going into details the story is as predictable as it has little relevance to reality. There's simply too much of everything. Drama, people losing it, more drama, "I had enough" twist and then a triumphant comeback as melodramatic as it could possibly get.Brad Pitt's character is like a continuation of his "Once Upon a Time..." stunt guy who's keen on getting on top of others by outracing them.Javier Bardem is great as always just like most of the cast is.The racing itself is shot great, but some of the innovative camera work gets repetitive and slightly annoying.PS. I'd rather see the 2013 Rush one more time...

title: Three Laps Is a Lifetime
content:
F1: The Movie is more than just a sports drama, it's an emotional, high-octane journey that dives into the heart of racing and the soul of a driver. From the very first scene, the film pulls you into the fierce world of Formula 1, not just through the roar of engines, but through the story of Sonny and JP, whose bond and relentless drive to win keep the audience on edge every single second.As the intensity peaks during the movie, the tension explodes with a voice asking,
"The question is... do they have the car?"
And then the line that shook the entire theatre:
"WE HAVE THE DRIVER."
One of the most powerful moments comes in the final act - and in that moment, everything clicks. What follows is pure cinematic poetry: a long, silent shot showing only the track. No music. No sound. Just the road. And then, Kerry Condon's voice breaks the silence with one unforgettable line:
"He is flying."
It's one of the greatest shots I've ever seen on screen - pure emotion, pure cinema.Adding to the magic is the presence of real F1 legends like Lewis Hamilton, Max Verstappen, Charles Leclerc, and Toto Wolff blending the film's fiction with the real world of racing. Their appearances make it feel authentic, like you're watching a piece of racing history unfold. Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem are phenomenal, their chemistry driving the emotional weight of the film.Visually, the movie is stunning. Every lap, every overtake, every corner is shot with breathtaking precision. But what lingers long after the credits roll aren't just the visuals - it's the emotion. F1: The Movie is a story about speed, belief, and what it means to push beyond limits. Because sometimes, three laps aren't just a race - they're a lifetime.

title: A Great Theatre Experience
content: While I don't love all of his movies, director Joseph Kosinski has earned my trust as a filmmaker who knows how to deliver a thrilling cinematic experience, thanks largely to his masterful work on Top Gun: Maverick. So naturally, I was looking forward to F1: The Movie, and now that it's finally in theatres, I'm happy to say it didn't disappoint. It may be a bit too straightforward for its own good, but it's also undeniably impressive in how it's made.Kosinski knows how to shoot action and scale, and that skill is on full display here.The story centers on Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a retired driver who's persuaded back to drive for Formula 1 by team leader Ruben (Javier Bardem) to help revive the struggling APXGP team. Along the way, Sonny must mentor a young rising star, Joshua (Damson Idris), and their clashing personalities inject just the right amount of tension into the otherwise familiar underdog sports narrative. It's the kind of dynamic we've seen before, but it works well thanks to strong performances and sharp pacing, at least for most of the runtime.That said, the film does overstay its welcome by about 20 minutes. A tighter edit would've elevated the emotional beats and kept the momentum stronger in the second half. Still, F1: The Movie is a visceral experience. The racing sequences are shot with a thrilling sense of speed and realism, and the sound design is nothing short of spectacular. It practically puts you in the driver's seat. This is a movie meant to be seen (and heard) on the big screen.Brad Pitt delivers yet another charismatic performance, bringing charm and charisma to the role of Sonny. But it's Damson Idris who really stands out. His charisma leaps off the screen, and it's easy to see major roles in his future. While the story itself is predictable and one you've likely seen before, it's executed with enough energy and sincerity to make it a worthwhile ride. F1: The Movie is very, very good and I'm glad I got to experience it in a theatre.

title: Nice Movie
content: As a long-time Formula 1 fan, I truly enjoyed this movie because it captures the spirit, energy, and intensity of the sport in a way that feels real and exhilarating. What stood out the most for me was not the cast, but how incredibly well the movie portrayed the world of F1, both on and off the track. The character that the main actor plays is absolutely wild in the best way possible. His personality, confidence, and racing instincts bring a fresh kind of hype to the story, and watching him handle the pressure of competing at such a high level is really fun. What impressed me even more was the way the film showcased the cars. The sound, speed, design, and camera angles made every race sequence feel powerful and immersive. As an F1 fan, seeing the cars move like that on screen genuinely gave me goosebumps. The film does a great job of making viewers feel the intensity inside the cockpit, the tension before a race, and the thrill of overtaking at high speeds. It's the closest thing to being on an actual F1 track without physically being there. The behind the scenes portrayal of the paddock, the team strategies, and the constant pressure to perform makes the story even more engaging. The movie doesn't just focus on racing; it also shows the emotional and mental battles that drivers face. That balance between action and character development is one of the reasons the film feels so complete. Overall, this movie is a must-watch for anyone who loves Formula 1. It celebrates the sport with respect, accuracy, and excitement. The racing scenes are crazy good, the atmosphere feels authentic, and the characters bring a lot of energy to the story. As a fan of F1 myself, I can honestly say this film gave me everything I hoped for speed, drama, emotion, and stunning cars.

title: Absolute cinema! A top contender for best racing film ever.
content: Wow...!I had high hopes, but this absolutely cooked.Easily the best racing film ever made on a technical level, possibly allround as well.Growing up, I loved Days of Thunder (1990), another Jerry Bruckheimer racing film. I rewatched it after seeing F1, which I really liked, for the first time.I had a good time with the Tom Cruise flick, but saw a lot of weird choices and flaws. Then, I watched F1 again.There is no contest. F1 is a much better film on all levels. I love this film now.There are other contenders, of course. Grand Prix, Le Mans, Ford vs Ferrari to mention a few.I will rewatch these classics soon, just to be sure.That said, my feeling is that F1 at least will be on the podium.The only nitpick is Pitt's age. He's too old to practically manage the physical strains of driving those cars. There's a reason why real racers retire while still relatively young. Their reaction time, strength and speed deteriorate as they get older. Yes, experience and talent can balance it out a little bit, but it's not realistic. If you can accept this suspension of disbelief, this is a magical cinematic experience.This feels like a mix between Any Given Sunday and Days of Thunder, with a dash of Moneyball and Top Gun: Maverick.In short, you have to watch in IMAX or Dolby Cinema.It's great!

title: A Thrilling Lap around the track - with some fictional pit stops
content:
As someone who has been watching Formula 1 for over 40 years, I went into F1: The Movie with both excitement and caution. Hollywood and motorsports have had a bumpy relationship over the years, but I'm pleased to say this film delivers a strong performance-especially on the technical side.The racing scenes are incredibly well done. The realism is palpable-not just in the speed, but in the choreography of overtakes, pit stops, and wheel-to-wheel action. The use of actual Formula 1 tracks and behind-the-scenes access to development labs brings an authenticity that longtime fans will appreciate. The sound design is outstanding-the roar of the engines, the subtle mechanical feedback, the spatial cues during races-it's a thrill to experience. And the soundtrack hits the right notes throughout, energetic and elegant when it needs to be.There's also a touching homage to Ayrton Senna that stood out to me. When Sonny describes how he feels when driving-how time slows down, how he and the car become one, and how he can almost watch it from outside-it's a clear and respectful reference to Senna's famous reflection about entering a different dimension while racing. As a lifelong fan, that moment gave me chills. It's subtle, but meaningful, and shows the filmmakers did their homework.The driver cameos are fun for F1 enthusiasts, and it's a treat to see so many familiar faces in the paddock and garage scenes. That said, Verstappen does seem to steal the spotlight a bit too often as the unbeatable standard-bearer. I would've liked to see more balance with other modern-era greats.The script does lean into a few sports-movie clichés, and of course, the central premise-that a 60-year-old former F1 driver can return and instantly compete at the top level-is pure fantasy. But hey, if we can suspend disbelief for Mission: Impossible, we can do it here too. It's part of the fun.In the end, F1: The Movie is a visually and sonically stunning tribute to the world of Formula 1, with just enough heart and reverence for the sport's legends to keep longtime fans like me engaged. It's not a documentary, and it's not meant to be-it's a celebration.8/10
Technically brilliant, emotionally charged, and a respectful nod to F1's past and present-even if the plot occasionally shifts into fantasy gear.

title: Cinematography 10, story and accuracy 1
content: While there is no doubt that this is a cinematographic masterpiece, the story as well as the accuracy when it comes to F1 rules, strategy, tactics, etc. Are, quite frankly, just bad. title: F1 (2025) - A Slick Spectacle, But Where's the Soul?
content: For a Hollywood film, F1 is... fine. It's polished, it's loud, it's fast. It delivers the glossy visuals and big emotional beats you'd expect from a blockbuster. But for anyone who truly loves the sport-who lives and breathes the real F1-it feels strangely hollow.Early reviews praised it as "authentic"-and honestly, I can't understand why. Authentic? To what? Certainly not to the sport, its culture, or its history. It's like someone skimmed through a few Drive to Survive episodes, threw in a dash of Top Gun bravado, and called it Formula 1.Worse still, the message of the film feels off. What are we supposed to take away here? That the key to winning is to ignore safety, bend every rule, and ram your opponents off the track so your teammate can take the win? It's not passion-it's recklessness. It's not rivalry-it's sabotage. Was Briatore ghostwriting the script?In the end, F1 is a decent movie for general audiences, but a disappointment for real fans of motorsport. It had the chance to show the true heart, drama, and complexity of Formula 1-and instead, it crashed at turn one.

title: F1 Hits the Track with Style and Soul
content: Brad Pitt brings undeniable charisma and a cool, cowboy-like attitude to F1, leaving a strong imprint on the film. His presence adds grit and heart, making the story resonate well beyond just the racetrack.As a fan of Rush and Days of Thunder, I found F1 holds its own character among the above movies.It delivers high-speed thrills with a human soul.But F1 isn't just about the roar of engines and the race to the finish line. It's also about character, values, and the strength to stand for something bigger than yourself. The film delivers beautiful messages-about standing up for others, recognizing that money can't buy your soul, not being the one who is always looking to be infront of cameras and acclaim, and the quiet strength of humility. These themes give F1 emotional weight and elevate it beyond a typical sports drama.The soundtrack, scored by Hans Zimmer, is stunning. His music perfectly matches the speed, pressure, and glory of the racing world, pulling you deeper into the experience.F1 is more than just a racing film-it's a fast, soulful ride that hits both the heart and the senses.A film, that may even boost interest in the Formula 1 championship from now on.

title: F1: A Film That Turns Driving into a Dream
content: The choice of actors and the way they act the roles in this film is one of its positive and impressive points. The performances were so natural and harmonious that the audience could easily identify with the characters and follow the story path.From a visual perspective, the set design, environment, and components in it are completely realistic. The atmosphere tangibly conveys a sense of naturalness to the audience, so that the film not only does not seem artificial, but also multiplies its involvement.The film's greatest strength is undoubtedly its story. This film shows that there is no need for aliens or superheroes to create excitement, emphasize morality, or arouse the audience's emotions. A coherent, simple, and realistic narrative can alone fulfill the mission of cinema well. It is noteworthy that, unlike many similar works, the film does not inject false hope into the viewer and instead presents a reflection of reality.The film's music is at an acceptable level. Although it is well-integrated with the atmosphere of the story, it does not reach a point where it can be kept in mind as one of the lasting elements of the film. In contrast, the script is designed professionally, accurately, and without exaggeration. The absence of unnecessary dialogues and secondary stories has made the film's narrative clear and effective, and it well conveys the feelings and moods of the characters.From a technical point of view, the design of costumes and vehicles has also been very accurate and realistic. The correct and balanced use of digital tools and CGI has helped the film appear at a completely convincing level in terms of visual effects.In addition to the story and artistic aspects, the film has also succeeded in arousing the viewer's enthusiasm for driving and adventure, A feeling that few films have been able to create at this level.Ultimately, we can say that this film is one of the best cinematic works of the year; a spectacular and enjoyable film that fills the void of such an approach in cinema. Even the continuation of the story in the form of a sequel (number 2) can be extremely attractive and expected.

title: Fast, Fierce, and Full of Heart
content:
"Formula 1" delivers an adrenaline-fueled cinematic experience that brilliantly captures the speed, rivalry, and human drama behind the world's most elite motorsport. Whether you're a die-hard racing fan or just a casual viewer, this film grips you from the opening scene and doesn't let go.The cinematography is absolutely stunning - the high-speed sequences are shot with such precision and realism that you can almost feel the G-forces pulling you into the driver's seat. The sound design deserves special mention too; the roar of engines, screeching tires, and pit lane chaos are all perfectly balanced to create an immersive audio experience.What sets this movie apart, however, is its storytelling. It doesn't just focus on the races but dives deep into the personalities, pressure, and sacrifices behind the helmets. The rivalry between the main characters is intense, emotionally charged, and rooted in real, relatable struggles. The screenplay does a great job balancing technical accuracy with emotional depth, keeping both motorsport purists and general audiences engaged.Performances are strong across the board. The lead actors bring charisma and intensity to their roles, making you care about their victories and heartbreaks. The direction is tight, the pacing fast (fitting for the subject), and the editing keeps the tension alive throughout.If there's one minor flaw, it's that a few supporting characters could've been more developed. But that's a small quibble in what is otherwise a top-tier sports drama.Verdict:
A must-watch for anyone who loves speed, competition, and stories of ambition and resilience. "Formula 1" races to the finish line with style and substance - a thrilling tribute to the human spirit behind the sport.

title: A thrilling, well-crafted sports drama - not groundbreaking, but full of energy, style, and emotion. Sharp pacing and sound design lift it above typical underdog clichés.
content: A thrilling and well-crafted sports drama - not groundbreaking, but full of energy, style, and emotion. Strong sound design and pacing elevate it above typical "underdog" clichés.Entertainment. That is how I would describe this movie. It is not going to win oscars most likely, but it it is a movie where I am not bored from start to finish. I think there is originality to the plot. There are some cliches here and there, and sure often you have a movie about how an underdog wins, but that is not what this movie here is really about. It was much more about the characters, so that felt refreshing. I was not even sure how it was going to end. There is one scene about halfway where something happens to the character JP, where I find what happens afterwards to be very unrealistic and how quickly the character recovers from it as it something major did not just happen. That killed it a bit for me as it felt wholly unrealistic and as if there was plot armor for the character that any other character would not survive well. For the structure and pacing of the movie that felt good. Never did I feel lost and never did I feel bored. The dialogue especially, can easily be cheesy or over the top in this kind of movie but I felt it was just right. I almost felt inspired by many parts. It did leave an emotional impact. Not the kind that the best of the best movies do where you feel changed or very sad or very happy but still I did feel like I am a little bit better after watching the movie. The plot is engaging and well-written. Not crazy with plot twists and turns and twists or amazingly beautiful, but for someone not interested in F1 I actually felt interested and that is a big credit to this movie. It flowed very naturally the movie. Acting wise most performances were decent or just above. No one is winning an oscar, probably not even nominated, for any of their performances, but Brad Pitt felt absolutely perfect for his role and he did an amazing job in it. Maybe it could be better, I don't know, I just know that I enjoyed it a lot. Supporting characters very also above average and I think overall all actors did well. The chemistry between the characters was very good. Only negative thing is one of the romantic relationships which happens in the movie felt very pushed, and a hollywood thing to have at least one romantic scene, which added nothing to the plot. That is a minus in my book. I believed that characters. The plot itself I am not sure could actually happen in real life, the whole strategy of the team felt weird but I am just pulling the card that I don't know enough about F1 to know if it is off or not. I think people who know F1 might find it annoying but then again from what I understood Lewis Hammilton was part of making the movie so I will trust it is in good hans. Performance were convincing and I did care about the lead performances but not about any supporting roles. Cinematography was good. It is hard making a movie where things movie so fast. I felt the cisual composition was good. Camera movement was excellent. Atomostphere was good. It visually looked good. It is not that I was blown away by the cinematography but I think they did an excellent job. The rhythm of the scenes were good, scene transition neat, the flow was good never too rushed or two slow. It overall felt just right. The sound and music was amazing! If it does get an oscar nomination it will be for the score and soundtrack, which was amazing and really added to the movie. I will check it out afterwards but it really made the movie much better so top marks for that. Lastly for production design and effects, I think it would be easier to say if I understood F1 better, but I have no complaints. Sets, costumes, and props all felt realistic, the effects were good, the world-building was belivable. Lastly there were just some writing moments which I felt were great. When a movie takes something that was said earlier in the movie and that some even might forget and brings it back, and one goes like "oh yeah that is a reference to this earlier in the movie" and when it is done not so much in your face, that is really satisfying.

title: A Masterpiece on Wheels - F1 is Pure Adrenaline and Emotion
content: As a longtime fan of Formula 1 and someone who appreciates great cinema, F1: The Movie absolutely blew me away. It is not just a movie about racing-it's a powerful story of passion, competition, sacrifice, and the human spirit. Whether you're a die-hard F1 fan or someone new to the world of motorsport, this film will leave you in awe.From the very first scene, the movie grabs your attention with intense cinematography, the roar of engines, and the high-stakes energy of the racetrack. The sound design is phenomenal-every gear shift, tire screech, and cheer from the crowd pulls you right into the experience. It's not just background noise; it's a part of the storytelling. You feel like you're sitting in the cockpit, racing at 300 km/h, risking everything for glory.But what really makes F1 stand out isn't just the action-it's the emotional depth and character development. The film manages to humanize the drivers, showing us the struggles behind the glamour. We see the pressure they face, the constant fear of injury or death, the politics of the sport, and the sacrifices they and their families make. One scene in particular (no spoilers) had me genuinely emotional-it reminded me that behind every helmet is a person with dreams, fears, and relationships.The lead performance is absolutely stellar. The main actor (or actors, if the film focuses on multiple drivers) brings authenticity and intensity to the role, perfectly capturing the confidence and vulnerability of a top-level driver. The supporting cast is just as strong-whether it's the team principal, the rival driver, or the family member watching from the paddock, every character feels real and necessary to the story.The directing and editing are also worth mentioning. The race sequences are some of the best I've ever seen on film. They're fast-paced but never confusing. The camera angles give you a unique view of the action-from aerial shots of the circuit to close-ups inside the cockpit, everything feels visceral and real. There's even a perfect balance between track action and off-track drama, which keeps the story grounded and engaging.Visually, the movie is stunning. The cinematography makes full use of F1's global nature-showcasing iconic circuits from Monaco to Monza, under the sun, rain, and even night lights. The lighting, the colors, the slow-motion moments-it's all crafted with care and artistry. And let's not forget the soundtrack. The music blends so well with the mood of each scene-uplifting, tense, heartbreaking-it elevates every moment.As someone who's watched every F1 documentary and series out there, I can confidently say this film is on another level. It respects the history and spirit of Formula 1 while telling a personal, emotionally powerful story. It's not just about who wins the championship-it's about what it takes to be a champion.To sum up: F1: The Movie is a cinematic triumph. It delivers on every level-adrenaline, emotion, character, and heart. It's a celebration of human ambition, pushing limits, and the beautiful chaos that is Formula 1. I laughed, I teared up, I held my breath during the race scenes-and the moment the credits rolled, I knew this was an instant classic.10/10 without a doubt.If you love sports movies, racing, drama, or just great storytelling, you need to watch this film. It's fast, it's raw, it's real-and it's unforgettable.

title: Production of a lifetime
content: This movie is designed for anybody who already loves Formula 1 or barely knows anything about it as it contains great racing while also trying to be educational.The characters are well-likeable and have some smooth development over the course of the movie as it follows this enjoyable and fairly realistic story which does not push limits the of practicality too far. One complaint about the story I do have is including a love interest as it does not do much for the story, neither does it feel well built and overall is just unnecessary in this sort of movie.Surprisingly there are plenty of great punchlines and consistent banter which allow the movie to lift off the pressure of the fierce racing.In complete honesty, the main reason for watching this movie was always to see the quality of production it brings, and it delivers in this aspect beyond any expectations. The quality of the racing scenes feel unreal and are so immersive as if Apex GP are actually the 11th team on the grid competing with the rest of the constructors.But realism does take some hits as they have to rush some aspects slightly due to it being a movie.Overall this movie was a cinematic masterpiece and suitable for more than just F1 fans.

title: A Wild Ride from Start to Finish
content: F1: The Movie was an awesome experience. The racing action had me on the edge of my seat-it was exciting and fun to watch. The dialogue, especially Sonny's lines, was hilarious and gave the movie a light, entertaining vibe. Plus, Sonny's backstory added depth and made his growth feel even more meaningful.I loved how both Sonny and Joshua grew as people throughout the film. They learned not to focus only on themselves and started putting others first. That growth, along with their discovery of the importance of teamwork, really hit home. It might seem like a cliché, but it's still a valuable lesson.The romance between Sonny and the technical director felt a little sudden, but it didn't take away from the movie as a whole. Even though I don't know much about cars, I still really enjoyed it-and I think car lovers would enjoy it even more. title: Never A Truthful Moment
content: Small cameras can now be fitted inside and outside racing cars to provide the audience with many "you are there" moments and the roar of engines may please them too. All we need is some rock music to keep us in high spirits while zooming along at 150+ mph. Other than that, there's nothing to see here. "Ford vs. Ferrari," the 2019 hit probably convinced "the powers that be" that there was a market for this stuff, but that film had a cogent screenplay and theme. This movie is mainly "a noisy place," with a mishmash script, with characters that are not likeable and actions that are not credible. The racing resembles demolition derby more that F1 racing. The big point comes when Javier Bardem tells Brad Pitt that "nobody drives forever." Do tell! Kudos to director Kosinski for pulling in $200-300 million to make this. I suppose everybody made out like bandits. When it was over, the crowded theatre, that I was in, was as quiet as a cemetery.

title: More than just screeching tyres and high-octane circuits!
content: Speed has always been thrilling-but here, it also has soul. From the very beginning, F1: The Movie promises more than just screeching tyres and high-octane circuits-it promises immersion. And for the most part, it delivers.Brad Pitt's portrayal of Sonny Hayes, a seasoned racer whose life has long been fused to the track, is both grounded and charismatic. The opening sequence locks you into his world immediately-someone who doesn't only chase podiums anymore, but the pure feeling of racing. What's remarkable is how the film manages to communicate this to any viewer-even those who've never watched a Formula 1 race in their lives.The racing sequences are a visual treat-thrilling, clean, and uncommonly easy to follow. Director Joseph Kosinski avoids stylized clutter, instead choosing realism. The decision to keep the camera grounded and often locked to the driver's perspective pays off immensely. It gives viewers a visceral sense of speed and danger without overwhelming them. More than just the cars, the circuits get their due-each track presented with unglamorous honesty, making the races feel more human than mythic.The narrative has a strong emotional spine: whether you see it as a comeback story or simply a man's lifelong obsession with racing, it works. Brad Pitt owns the screen, but Damson Idris brings quiet intensity, and Javier Bardem adds surprising texture. The chemistry between Pitt and Kerry Condon is believable, though that subplot is the film's slowest gear. It slightly dampens the pacing and could have been trimmed without losing much.What truly elevates the movie, though, is the sound. Engines growl, tires scream, and the music doesn't try to dominate-it amplifies. The sound design draws you in without ever being flashy. That restraint is a theme here: F1: The Movie doesn't over-glorify its subject. It respects it.Sum up - For fans of the sport, it's an authentic representation of F1's competitive spirit and behind-the-scenes reality. For everyone else, it's a gripping character-driven drama that just happens to be set at 180 MPH.

title: Feel the Speed of F1
content:
F1 (2025), directed by Joseph Kosinski and starring Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Kerry Condon, Tobias Menzies, and Javier Bardem is a high-octane sports drama that follows aging American driver Sonny Hayes (Pitt), who returns to Formula One after a 30-year absence. Hayes, once a prodigy whose career was derailed by a devastating crash, is recruited by his former teammate Rubén Cervantes (Javier Bardem), now the owner of the struggling APXGP team. With the team facing collapse unless they can clinch a win in the remaining season, Hayes joins forces with ambitious rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). The film weaves together themes of redemption, rivalry, and the relentless pressure of elite motorsportBrad Pitt delivers a charismatic performance as Sonny Hayes, capturing both the character's weary swagger and competitive fire. His portrayal of an aging racer balancing regret and hope is both believable and compelling, especially in quieter, introspective moments and during the film's tense media scrums Damson Idris stands out as Joshua Pearce, skillfully embodying the modern F1 driver equal parts raw talent and social media savvy. Kerry Condon impresses as Kate McKenna, the technical director fighting for respect in a male-dominated paddock. Javier Bardem injects charm and desperation as the embattled team owner, while the supporting cast, including Tobias Menzies, rounds out a strong ensembleJoseph Kosinski, known for Top Gun: Maverick, brings his signature style slick, immersive, and kinetic to F1. The narrative moves at breakneck speed, echoing the intensity of the sport itself. Kosinski's collaboration with real F1 insiders, including Lewis Hamilton as producer, ensures a level of authenticity rarely seen in sports dramas. The pacing rarely lets up, and while the story occasionally leans on familiar underdog tropes, Kosinski's direction keeps the viewer engaged from start to finishCar Stunt Action and Camera Shots
The film's standout achievement is its revolutionary approach to racing sequences. Filmed on actual Grand Prix weekends with Pitt and Idris driving real cars, F1 uses helmet-mounted and car-embedded cameras to put the audience directly in the cockpit. The result is an unprecedented sense of speed and danger viewers feel every gear shift, tire screech, and high-speed overtake Claudio Miranda's cinematography is both frenetic and elegant, capturing the visceral chaos of wheel-to-wheel racing. The film F1 enhances the adrenaline rush of racing through its exceptionally realistic racing footage, which immerses the audience directly into the high-speed, high-stakes world of Formula One. This realism is achieved by filming during actual Grand Prix weekends throughout the 2023-24 seasons, integrating a fully functional fictional team (APXGP) seamlessly into real races alongside established F1 teamsHans Zimmer's score amplifies the adrenaline, matching the rhythm of the race with pounding emotionF1 is a visually stunning and immersive ride that blurs the line between fiction and documentary. While film can be shorter because the plot sometimes lapses into predictability and the technical details may overwhelm non-fans, the film's authentic racing action and dynamic performances make it a must-watch for motorsport enthusiasts and general audiences alike. Kosinski's direction, combined with Pitt's magnetic turn and groundbreaking camera work, ensures F1 is less about strict realism and more about capturing the thrill, spectacle, and heart of Formula One.

title: Speed, Sacrifice, and the Cost of Racing
content: The pacing is one of its strongest points. It goes up and down nicely, keeps things exciting, but never becomes too overwhelming. The film is engaging without feeling exhausting. The cinematography is very strong, especially during the race scenes. You really feel the speed and pressure of Formula One.The story is not only about racing. There is a love story, themes of sacrifice and patience, and a mentor-student relationship that works well. The film does not try too hard to be deep or philosophical, which I actually appreciated.That said, the movie has a clear weakness. If you do not already understand Formula One, some parts can be confusing. The film assumes you know how teams work, why there are two drivers per team, and how race strategies and tire changes function. A short explanation at the beginning would have helped a lot.Some character choices also felt underexplained. For example, the constant presence of the young driver's mother at every race felt strange and unnecessary, and the film never really explains why she is so central.I also would have liked to see more of the other teams. The movie focuses heavily on one team, and showing at least one rival team's perspective would have made the Formula One world feel bigger and more realistic.Overall, it is a well-made, entertaining film with great visuals and solid emotional moments. Even without deep Formula One knowledge, it is enjoyable, but viewers with some background in the sport will probably enjoy it even more.

title: absolute cinema
content: The movie took me to another world a true cinematic escape that left me speechless.It's not just a film; it's an experience. From start to finish, I was captivated by its energy, intensity, and emotional depth.It moved me deeply and even made me feel ashamed of myself for not being talented at anything.This is absolutely Oscar material - without question, the film of the year. It moved me deeply, stirring emotions I didn't expect, and even made me reflect on myself, feeling a sense of inadequacy compared to the brilliance I had just witnessed. That's how powerful it was. If this masterpiece doesn't win the Oscar, it would be nothing short of a crime.The direction was flawless, the pacing perfect, and the storytelling was crafted with a level of care that you rarely see in modern cinema. Each scene felt purposeful, each moment charged with meaning.And of course, Hans Zimmer's score elevated the film to another level. He truly is the maestro of modern music. Every note, every crescendo, every quiet pause - it all blended seamlessly with the visuals to create an unforgettable atmosphere. He has once again proven why he stands unmatched in the industry.Honestly, I can't find a single flaw in this film. It was visually stunning, emotionally gripping, and musically transcendent. I'm already waiting with great anticipation for a sequel, because one film simply doesn't feel like enough.My only regret is not watching it on the big screen in the cinema, where I'm sure the impact would have been even more overwhelming. This isn't just a movie it's a once-in-a-generation cinematic triumph.

title: You're right there in driver's seat, movie better than expected
content: This was a really enjoyable movie with great acting all around. The script was well written and subject matter a lot more interesting than we expected. F1 racing has come a very long way. Brad Pitt does a great job here bringing his character to life. Javier Bardem, Tobias Menses and Idris, as well as all the other actors really delivered. Not an academy award winner, but a really good solid movie. About human beings with their struggles, failures, flaws and triumphs, with the adrenaline of F1 racing and how it works up close. Every drivers delight who likes the thrill of being behind the wheel of anything fast. We'll never get the chance to drive one of these, but we can get the feeling and experience of it with this movie. We loved it.

title: Good film - suspend belief!
content: Just a quick review - I went to see this today with my wife and we both really enjoyed it. As someone who is a bit of an F1 fan (watch races when I can and have binged Drive to Survive) there are obvs plenty of things which would either never happen in real life or which would lead to massive penalties, but it's a movie!If they made it realistic, nothing would happen. It usually takes 2 hours for just a few moments of excitement in a real race! (It's a good job they skipped out Monaco!)So if you are new to F1 you'll really enjoy it and may be tempted to watch the sport for real, and if you are an F1 fan there's still lots to enjoy and you can just sit and feel quietly smug when you notice all the oddities.The cameos were fun, the action is great, and my car engine has never sounded so quiet on the drive home!

title: Flop: The Movie
content: Film spin off of 'Drive to survive' mixed with a campaign scenario from a sports computer game and Hollywoodland script mechanics, 'F1: the movie' should be parked. This needs more than a pit stop, it needs a full rebuild.The actors look lost without a road map, and unable to power their way through no matter how much they put their foot down. There isn't a good performance in the film. Brad Pitt looks amazing for a 60 year old, playing a nearly 50 year old beaten up and stuck back together racing driver and he can shift gears for comic relief but he hasn't got much else to do. The rest of the cast are tested by their own evident boredom, induced by their under-direction by an almost absent director who will probably blame a poor script and poor production standards.The overall effect is simply a waste of time. This is just moving money around. It isn't really a film it's a market missing attempt to mash together F1: a TV sports show, 'Drive to Survive', and motorsport video games, over the slightest skeleton of film plot padding and production craft.I can't give credit for anything but the in-car camera work, and some editing and sound mixing. The score has a few moments. This film really needs those moments. It needs lots more than the score and sound design can muster.I rate at 2.5/10 and the only reason that I've rounded up to a 3 instead of down to a 2 is because Brad Pitt clearly put in the effort, that's apparent throughout: he can play the role for pathos and for hints of comedy, but mainly his physical performance is the impressive element of the film. The other star is for the sounds and sights but I can get most of those elsewhere to be honest.I don't recommend to anyone; not to F1 fans, not to non-motorsport fans, not to general film fans, not to anyone other than absolute Brad Pitt hero worshippers from his most hardcore fan base.As a final reflection, I wonder if this film has been heavily re-edited and is a Frankenstein of a movie: hacked together from all sorts of limp lumps. In one instance a major character saves the life of another major character in the full public view of a Formula 1 race after a massive crash. This act, which has been set up in a previous scene of the film, where the saving character had himself been left unaided immediately after an F1 crash at the start of his career, is never once mentioned or even alluded to again. Not by any character - even in passing. It's ignored completely. By everyone. Forever. To me this suggests complete re-editing of a film in post production or the script was about as big as my weekly shopping list and they just forgot about paying off something which they had set up previously in the film. It made every part of the second half of the film seem even more like a computer game campaign scenario.

title: I really enjoyed watching it
content: They did a good job with this movie. However, there were quite a few cringy moments. Of course, it wasn't realistic, the two drivers from Apex had one of the best starts in F1 history in every race. The fact that they went from having the worst car to one of the best with just a single upgrade didn't sit right either. The overtakes in the final race, where they worked together multiple times, also felt unrealistic, I don't think I've ever seen teammates coordinate like that even once in a real race, let alone three times.That said, the rest was really well done. Overall, it was a good movie, and I wasn't bored at all in the cinema.

title: Formula 1
content: First off - yes! As both an F1 fan and a diploma-level AV specialist/videographer, I absolutely loved this movie.The story is fun and engaging! I purposely avoided watching the full trailer, so I went in blind, and honestly, that made the experience even better. These days, trailers often give away the entire plot, so skipping them is worth it.The cinematography is fantastic - great shots, well-crafted scenes, and a visual style that really pulls you in. The film grabs your attention from the very start and doesn't let go. It's just a genuinely fun ride!That said, if I had one critique: where were the qualifying sessions? It's a big part of F1, and apart from hearing about it maybe twice, we never actually see it. That felt like a missed opportunity.Still, the movie delivers: the teammate rivalry, the real F1 driver cameos, and the overall atmosphere are just awesome. A fun and solid watch for any motorsport fan!

title: We are the best in the world
content: If Joseph Kosinski took you to the skies with Top Gun, this time he takes you flying in cars in an unbelievably thrilling way.A remarkable film with a solid and well-crafted story that keeps you glued to your seat for two and a half hours - and the closer it gets to the end, the harder it becomes to stay seated.The innovative and fresh cinematography for this film completely puts the audience inside a Formula 1 car, making it feel uncannily like you're experiencing the film firsthand.Hans Zimmer's music, as always, is phenomenal, giving the film an extra layer of excitement.And absolutely, absolutely, absolutely this film must be seen on an IMAX screen - do whatever it takes to get to the nearest IMAX and don't miss out on two and a half hours of pure adrenaline."Sometimes there's this moment when I'm in the car when everything is peaceful and no one can catch me. title: Great, but No Top Gun: Maverick
content: F1 is an amazing summer action-drama flick and in some aspects it is even better than Top Gun: Maverick.The writing is not an aspect like that. The characters are quite stereotypical, but this is the kind of movie where that absolutely works. The dialogues on the other hand sometimes felt forced or just cheesy. I also think they should've cut some of the commentator's lines, because he is sometimes explaining something everyone can see and understand. That's just not cinematic. I also didn't like when a conflict I thought was over, the characters dumbly started it again. The story is inspirational and a little predictable, but not as much as thought.Joseph Kosinski is one of my favourite directors. I do think Top Gun: Maverick is better, but the cinematography was very limited there and they were doing it in Tony Scott's style, but here he could play more with his tools. The cinematography is creative, sometimes even beautiful and the lighting is good. The editing is great, it didn't feel long at all. The sound design is amazing, silence was used very well too. The soundtrack is epic, creative and very diverse, it's probably the most unique thing about this film. The visual effects were absolutely convincing. I just know they used CGI, because some things couldn't have been filmed without doubling the budget.F1 is great, but I'm tired of hearing people declare every summer movie the "new Top Gun: Maverick", because that creates high expectations.

title: One of the Most Realistic Racing Films Ever Made
content: Honestly, I went into F1: The Movie thinking it would just be a bunch of cool racing shots and Brad Pitt looking cool in a car. But it's actually way more emotional than I expected. The chemistry between Brad Pitt and Damson Idris is really good; they feel like two people from totally different worlds trying to meet in the middle, and it works.What I loved most is how REAL the racing looks. You can tell they shot a lot of this on actual tracks, and it makes a huge difference. It's one of the few racing movies where the action actually feels intense without looking fake or CGI-heavy.Overall, it's a great mix of action and story. I'm not even a big Formula One fan, and I still enjoyed it a lot. Definitely worth watching, especially on a big screen if you can.
