title: Not what I expected.
content: I recently watched this movie not because I wanted to, but my friend persuaded me to. Personally, I am not a huge F1 fan, I don't know much about it and only heard a few famous names in the industry.Before I watched this film I expected it to be a typical start from scratch and work it's way up type of plot. However, It wasn't. The plot was rather unique. It is a plot that has that sense of emotional depth to it as well as having great tension. It is amazing to have something different. Not only that, the actors were outstanding. Brad Pitt's charisma was captivating to me as well as the intense moment he really shines, same goes for the other actors. In addition, the movies have some decent humor which get me to giggle quite a bit, nowadays humor in movies are just so bland and lacks creativity. Cameo of some of the world best f1 drivers also got the audience excited in the cinema. This has to be one of the best racing film ever.Though it most of it was good some wasn't. For instance, the pacing in some scenes was slow, making it a bit boring. Another one is the editing, I remember watching a clip in the movie where it was in the middle of something and energetic tone(without dialogue) and got cut into the next scene all of the sudden with calm tone. I not sure if that was a editing error. Or I could have seen wrongly, but I remember seeing a clip like that. I will not explain what the clip is to aviod spoilers. Last of all, some scenes in the movie felt useless, it doesn't add on the the story nor the tension build up. It just felt like they added it to make the movie longer.Overall, the movie is good. In fact, I will go as far as saying is the best racing movie ever. Better than Gran Turismo. If you are a f1 fan or not, I highly recommend you watching this movie, almost everything is chef kiss.

title: A Thrilling Ride from Start to Finish - A Perfect 10/10
content: F1 is not just a movie; it's an experience. From the very first scene, it pulls you into the raw adrenaline of the track and the powerful emotions off it. The storytelling is fast-paced yet deeply emotional, balancing the intensity of racing with the personal struggles and triumphs of the characters. The cinematography is breathtaking, making every race feel like you're right there in the driver's seat. The sound design, the visuals, the performances-all come together flawlessly. This film celebrates passion, speed, and the human spirit in a way that leaves you both breathless and inspired. For me, it's a solid 10/10.

title: Shane is driving a Formula 1
content: I really enjoyed this modern take on the old western gunslinger format, wherein a mysterious gunslinger rides into town and is called upon to save the day, inspiring the locals to stand up for themselves before riding off alone into the sunset after emerging victorious. It's a classic story of how one generation passes on their wisdom and experience to the next while coming to terms with their own past and maybe having one last moment in the sun.F1 subverts expectations in a good way for once by avoiding a few tropes that, while not bad necessarily, have been done in a slew of other movies. There's the scene, for example, where a character's friend wants him to go out partying but the character has matured and realized that it's time to end that part of his life and says so in coded language. The character's friend pauses for a few beats and you wonder if this will be another 'sometimes those around you can't grow with you' moment before he surprises by accepting the change without drama. It's a nice moment that acknowledges that sometimes our better angels can win out, as is the case with another character who you might expect (rich guy fighting a cut throat political battle with reputation and millions of dollars on the line) would selfishly put his need for a win ahead of his friend's health but instead shows that his concern and love for his friend is genuine and more important to him than winning or money.The story comes together nicely with plenty of exciting action and twists that keep you on your toes and ends on a positive note that feels true to the characters as they've been portrayed. It's not the greatest movie ever made, but it's fun and well constructed, has heart and, I think, holds up to repeat viewings which has been pretty rare of late.

title: Best movie the year so far
content: F1: The Movie is not just a film; it's a full-throttle cinematic experience. From the second the lights go out, it grabs you and doesn't let go until the final chequered flag.While the story is entirely fictitious, the writers and director have built a world that feels incredibly authentic and convincing. The attention to detail is staggering, from the behind-the-scenes politics of a top team to the visceral, heart-pounding intensity inside the cockpit. You completely forget you're watching a scripted drama.The direction is masterful, balancing high-octane track action with deeply human moments off it. The racing sequences are arguably the best ever put to film, making you feel every bump on the kerb and every gut-wrenching overtake.The acting is superb across the board. While the star power of Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem is undeniable and they deliver exactly as you'd expect, it is Damson Idris who truly steals the show. His performance is raw, emotional, and utterly captivating, holding his own against legends and often shining the brightest.Overall, this is a must-watch. It has everything: drama, incredible action, technical brilliance, and heart. You will love every minute and won't regret buying a ticket. Without a doubt, this is the best movie of 2025.

title: "F1: The Movie" - A High-Octane Thrill Ride
content:
"F1: The Movie," directed, written, and produced by Joseph Kosinski, generated considerable interest before its release, attracting both devoted F1 fans and casual moviegoers. The film successfully captures the speed, challenges, and cultural impact of the sport. The involvement of Brad Pitt's Plan B Entertainment, along with Apple Studios, Dawn Apollo Films, and other production companies, added to its anticipated success. Even without a deep understanding of Formula One racing, the ambition and the cast, including Brad Pitt (Sonny Hayes), Javier Bardem (Ruben Cervantes), Damson Idris (Joshua Pearce / JP), Kerry Condon (Kate McKenna), and Callie Cooke (Jodie), were captivating.A Story of Redemption and Rivalry
The film's story focuses on Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), whose journey is inspired by the career of former Irish motorsports racer Martin Donnely. A promising F1 career in the 90s was cut short by a serious accident. Decades later, Ruben (Javier Bardem), a long-time friend and former teammate, who now owns the struggling APXGP racing team, offers Sonny a chance to return to racing alongside rookie Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris).The film shows the contrasting priorities and racing styles of the two drivers: the veteran's experience versus the newcomer's modern approach. Their relationship starts with friction due to their different styles. However, their storylines converge as the team faces pressure, leading to mutual respect and a shared goal of winning the championship.Immersion and Character Dynamics
"F1: The Movie" also explores the Formula 1 ecosystem. The film shows the roles beyond the cockpit, including JP's personal team, training, and the interactions between drivers and technicians. Sonny's relationships within the racing environment and the demands of team ownership add depth to his character.Kerry Condon's portrayal of Kate McKenna, the team's technical director, is notable. Her efforts to improve car design and performance are shown, highlighting her importance to the team. The narrative shows how the team slowly trusts Sonny's insights, enriching the character dynamics and driving the main story.The film's visuals are also a highlight. Cinematographer Claudio Miranda creates an immersive experience. On-board cameras and unique angles put the audience in the cockpit, conveying the thrill of high-speed racing. The use of real F1 cars and footage, along with the actors' performances at high speeds, adds authenticity to the racing scenes. Forbes highlighted the production's effort to push cinematic boundaries to immerse the audience in the action. Forbes highlighted the production's commitment.Final Lap: A Must-See for Speed and Story
While some critics noted the film's use of familiar sports movie elements and a predictable plot, these are often balanced by the visuals, the realism of the racing scenes, and the performances. Rotten Tomatoes reports a Certified Fresh critic score of 84% based on 255 reviews, and an audience score of 96% Rotten Tomatoes reports. Joseph Kosinski's direction and commitment to capturing the essence of Formula 1 with realism, particularly in the driving sequences and team dynamics, improve "F1: The Movie." It blends a classic underdog story with innovative cinematography and character development. "F1: The Movie" is a recommended film for those who appreciate competition, a story of second chances, or well-made movies.

title: A Familiar Yet Satisfying Story
content:
I walked into F1 The Movie not knowing much-other than that it came from the team behind Top Gun: Maverick. Having missed Top Gun in theaters, I felt like I owed it to myself to catch this one on the big screen. And I'm glad I did-it was absolutely worth the watch.At its core, F1 is a classic underdog story. You've seen it before in films like Creed, Rocky, The Karate Kid, and even Drum Line. The movie hits all the expected narrative beats of an underdog arc-but that's not necessarily a flaw. There's something undeniably uplifting about watching a group of people rise above their circumstances, especially in our world today that often feels unforgiving. These kinds of stories offer a simple but necessary reminder: perseverance pays off.I'm not an F1 fan. I knew very little about the sport before watching this. Still, the film managed to make the world of Formula 1 feel accessible and engaging. It doesn't drown you in jargon or assume prior knowledge. Instead, it builds a narrative that allows newcomers to understand the stakes, the environment, and the thrill of the sport without oversimplifying it.Brad Pitt delivers a grounded and heartfelt performance as a former racing star trying to find his way back after a major fall from grace. He's joined by Damson Idris, who plays a young, ambitious driver with just the right mix of arrogance and vulnerability. Together, they form a compelling and often dysfunctional mentor-mentee dynamic. The supporting cast is strong-Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon, and the actor from Killing Eve all bring depth and personality to what could have easily been one-note roles. What's notable is that the film's underdog arc doesn't just focus on one character-it encompasses the entire team, each of whom is battling their own setbacks. Their collective victory becomes all the more meaningful because of it.Coming from the same creative minds behind Top Gun: Maverick, I expected the technical aspects to be excellent-and they are. The race scenes are incredibly immersive. The cinematography captures the speed, danger, and precision of Formula 1 in a way that feels immediate and visceral. You can distinguish each international circuit not just visually but emotionally. The score, too, is outstanding. When I saw Hans Zimmer's name in the credits, everything clicked. His music adds intensity and emotion to every moment, from pre-race build-ups to climactic finishes.There's no major plot twist here-you more or less know how it's going to end. But with stories like this, the destination isn't the point. It's the emotional journey that counts. F1 The Movie is about redemption, second chances, and rising above doubt. It's a feel-good film in the best sense-well-crafted, emotionally resonant, and rewarding to watch.So take the time to see this on the biggest screen you can. As Brad Pitt's character says in one of the film's standout lines:
"Don't be shitty to yourself. Others will do that for you."This movie will make you feel better about yourself-and maybe the world too.

title: A Thrilling Masterpiece That Ignites a Passion for F1
content: I walked into the theater with no real knowledge of Formula 1 - just a general curiosity. I walked out completely transformed. This film didn't just entertain me - it awakened a deep, emotional connection to a sport I had never followed before.From the very first frame, I was hooked. The sound design is so intense, so immersive, you feel every engine roar deep in your chest. The cinematography puts you inside the cockpit - not just as a viewer, but as a driver. You can smell the burnt rubber, feel the wind, and taste the adrenaline.Brad Pitt was an absolute revelation. His presence on screen is magnetic - he brings a raw, grounded energy to the role, making you feel like you're watching a real-life racer, not an actor. He's not just acting in the world of F1 - he is F1 in this movie. Every emotion, every glance, every reaction feels genuine.But what truly elevates the movie is its attention to realism. The racing sequences don't feel staged or over-stylized. They are gritty, dangerous, and authentic. It's almost like a documentary in moments, but with the cinematic brilliance of a blockbuster. The use of practical effects and real-world F1 settings makes it feel like you're watching actual races unfold in real time.The score and soundtrack deserve their own review. It's powerful, pulse-pounding, yet beautifully restrained where it needs to be. The music doesn't just accompany the scenes - it drives them.This film didn't just teach me about racing; it made me fall in love with it. I'm now genuinely excited to follow F1, learn about its history, its drivers, and maybe even attend a live race one day. That's the mark of a great movie - one that changes you.To the entire team behind this - thank you. You created a film that not only entertains but inspires. I came for a movie, but I left with a new passion.

title: A great watch if you aren't a stickler for the rules
content: I walked into F1: The Movie knowing full-well that it wasn't going to be entirely by-the-books. It draws inspiration from real-world events that happened in F1's history, but sometimes skirts the line in regards to what is actually allowed to happen in modern F1.BUT that's okay.Ultimately, they liberties they took can be seen as minor (?) and help give the movie a more interesting narrative ... but this isn't to say that the movie doesn't have its flaws. A couple moments in the movie can come off as quite cringe-worthy and the choice of dialogue could be more visceral to add to the authenticity of the acting, but I can understand that a bunch of swearing and certain phrases might not rub off well on some audiences as this is a mass-market movie with the intent of drawing people into the F1 motorsport.I would have liked to see other F1 drivers and teams play a larger acting roles, perhaps intertwining their radios and POVs more within races, but it has to be commended that this film did leverage a lot of in-season filming, so that may have played a factor in what they were able to record.In regards to the actual quality ... near perfect. You really do get to see some great shots on track and it does kind of feel like you are really racing in an F1 car - and personally for me it was elevated by watching it in 4DX which was stellar in the first half, though dropped off a bit in the second; some environments and what not could have been implemented to greater effect.I do feel like the movie will lose some of its appeal when watching at home after it goes to Apple TV+, but I can say that the iMAX and 4DX experiences are great, and that the movie does well in a lot more places than it does poorly.Highly recommended for people who especially used to daydream as cars about driving fast cars, but grew out of it; can really help bring back that same nostalgic feeling.

title: Fun Popcorn Entertainment
content: It's a fun and exciting movie, and I recommend it if you're looking to be entertained. A lot of the comments mention that it's formulaic with cliches, which I can't disagree with. But it's still fun to watch. It's not a documentary.One of the more interesting things about the movie - for me at least - is that my wife liked it better than I did. So did my neighbor's wife, to the point where she now wants to go to an F1 race.The only drawback for me was Brad Pitt's acting. His performance wasn't bad, but he seems to be relying on the same mannerisms in a lot of his roles. They were the same in Once Upon A Time In Hollywood, Inglourious Basterds and this movie. Which is disappointing. He gave great performances in 12 Monkeys, Burn After Reading and Fight Club. But that's just nitpicking on my part and it doesn't detract from the fun of the movie.

title: Undeniably cool, but it has flaws
content: F1 was incredibly cinematic and it had a pretty engaging story with fun, albeit a bit over the top, characters to boot. I am not a racing fan at all and i was interested at all the hype around this movie as, 'epic even for non fans'. I had a really good time watching this film, but i do not think it does anything groundbreaking and it has drawbacks.First off, the movie is LONG and you definitely feel that toward the middle. Some of the parts in act 2 feel way too drawn out and repetitive. I love me a good long film, but a film like that has to not waste my time. The main character, played by Brad Pitt, is very overwritten and several things pertaining to his character just go nowhere. Brad Pitt is also as wooden as ever. Poor guy needs to just retire already if he is gonna phone it in this hard. That being said, it sorta works for a character that is supposed to essentially be a washed up lunatic. What the film lacks in these parts it does make up with cool cinematography and an entertaining rivalry between the two leads. Its written very well, i just wish that middle was cut down.All that said, the ending of the film is immaculate. Its a feast of speed and has some really satisfying narrative payoff. I only really *got* this film in the end. If you can hang on through the middle, the ending very much makes up for it. I do recommend!

title: F1. (2025)
content: A pure adrenaline rush-that's the only way to describe this absolute masterpiece. F1 is, without a doubt, one of the best racing films ever made. From start to finish, the film delivers speed, emotion, and spectacle at a level that few films can even attempt.The cinematography is phenomenal. It genuinely feels like you're sitting trackside, witnessing the race live. The realism is insane-the camera work places you right inside the action, making every turn, overtake, and collision feel breathtakingly real. The background score and music are pure extravaganza-loud, powerful, and perfectly synced with the speed and intensity of the races. Every rev of the engine hits hard.The film is packed with goosebumps-worthy moments. Your heartbeat races as the competition heats up, and the climax is jaw-dropping, emotionally charged, and incredibly satisfying. It doesn't just thrill-it makes you feel.John's direction is top-notch, supported by a razor-sharp screenplay and a perfectly balanced pace that never slows down. The technical departments shine bright-cinematography, BGM, and VFX are the true highlights and elevate the film into an experience rather than just a movie.Performance-wise, this is perfect casting across the board. Brad Pitt is the soul of the film, delivering a magnetic and deeply engaging performance. Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and Kerry Condon are equally impressive, each bringing depth and authenticity to their roles.Overall, F1 isn't just a film-it's an event. This is a cinematic experience that must be watched on the biggest screen possible. Miss it in theatres, and you'll miss the real magic.

title: A Minardi rather than Mercedes
content: It took 20 minutes for me to tick almost every box in the "how to make a film covering all politically correct stereotypes", by which point i'd given up expecting any surprises in a film genre that is,like the actual sports race results,predictable!So it's about a driver at the end of his career, grabbing a cheque,,,with a race attached to it. He then joins a mediocre team, with the token gesture minority group of individuals, and in less time than it takes to change the livery to include his once infamous name, turns everything to gold. For me the constant in car/out car camera, made me throw up, not the effects of motion sickness,,it just made me puke!I don't know what i wanted from this film, as it had been so hyped up by the inclusion of the exact same stereotypical/scripted players from the other circus act!Making a movie based on reality is great, but the reality of this sport is, you cannot make a racing car/driver win overnight, no matter how pretty you are in front of the camera.And that's where this film "under steered" off into the harbour at Monaco, and became a damp/wet running predetermined outcome, that deserved a stewards enquiry, at least a ten second "stop and don't go on" penalty,,,or even a black flag!Overtaking moves that just left me shouting "oh for ,,,,,,, sake get real",,,and the cliche infected last race/lap,,, I'm absolutely positive Lauda, Senna, Hunt and all the greats are spinning in circles of laughter and sarcastic comments.It's not nearly as bad as Sly's farcical scene in "Driven", but sorry this is a Lada of a film.

title: 2.5 hours of Sponsorship.
content: My outlook on F1 is pretty similar to all the other reviewers here who scored this no more than 5 stars. It's a very familiar plot design with cliche' ridden characters. Since the storyline lacks significant depth and character development, this film simply drags on a threadbare script and mediocre screenplay. Brad Pitt has matured into the aging pro who is on a comeback mission, but his cool and smooth demeanor just can't carry this film in an enthralling way. I honestly didn't care what the outcome would be once they tried to build up the climax which isn't that exciting. I can also say that I am not any more interested in F1 racing than I was before. And I still think it's a cool and exciting sport. Unlike Top gun which spurred a widespread interest in flying. Some people have remarked on what a cinematic achievement this is and how breathtaking the action sequences are. I would say the action sequences are competent, but nothing extraordinary. Another aspect that deserves criticism is that they spend too much time on things that don't really matter to the average audience. Showing how the Racing team operates with all of their High-tech equipment, simulators, Virtual LED's, communication gadgets, etc. Is okay to show us briefly just to give us an idea of the nature of their business. But they show all of this to the point where it grows tedious.What amazes me most, is the magnitude of brand name sponsorship being revealed. I don't think I saw one scene that didn't feature a Brand name advert from: Oracle, Ferrari, Mercedes Benz, BMW, Porsche, Pirelli, iCloud, Red Bull, Tommy Hilfiger, Expensify, McLaren, Workday, Aston Martin, etc. Etc. The list goes on.See Ford Vs. Ferrari instead.

title: A Scenario Lost Between Speed, Noise, and Visual Spectacle
content: The cinematic equivalent of Formula 1 has always been problematic. The adrenaline-fueled races, dazzling speeds, and the magic of the tracks, no matter how exciting they are in real life crash when they hit the silver screen. The 2025 F1 film starring Brad Pitt doesn't fare much better in this collision.The idea of Brad Pitt getting into an F1 car sounds quite appealing at first. Hollywood's golden boy, that ageless genetic marvel is stepping on the gas this time. In this sense, the film is reminiscent of Driven, starring Sylvester Stallone in 2001. Yes, that film wasn't loved by critics either but at least there was some character depth, some sincere attempts at racer psychology. After all these years, Driven still serves as the benchmark for F1-themed films, which is a sad state of affairs for cinema.Why can't F1 seem to find proper representation on the big screen? Perhaps the nature of the sport isn't conducive to it. Maybe the backdrop of on-track competition, political games, and technological warfare is incompatible with cinema's narrative structure. Or perhaps Hollywood prefers to turn this world into a 'speed'-themed action show instead of portraying it with faithfulness to reality. This film does exactly that.Of course, the visuals are magnificent; we can't deny that. The race scenes are breathtaking; the ear-splitting roar of the engines, the blurry frames of speeding vehicles and the sound mixing that offers an almost physical experience turn the film into a technical spectacle. Kosinski and Miranda have managed to transform the cinema hall into an F1 garage. However, immediately following this success is an overly predictable and cliche-ridden screenplay written by Ehren Kruger. Kosinski's visual talents are not enough to compensate for Kruger's screenplay shortcomings. Is visuality everything? Only a Transformers fan would answer "yes" to that.In conclusion, while this film visually satisfies F1 enthusiasts, it offers moviegoers a weak narrative, artificial character relationships, and a forgettable story. It's a slight improvement over Driven, but it's still difficult to say it has "won the race." Formula 1 remains an unexplored territory for cinema; this 2025 film is still very far from that discovery.

title: Cliche and boring
content: This movie is beyond cliche. The plot irregularities makes you wonder if Jerry Bruckheimer Films even read the script before backing this movie or they simply fainted at the thought of another Brad Pitt project? I work railroad, so I know when something goes off the rails.This movie is a lot like going on a Tinder date with me. On the surface it looks pretty good and the write up is interesting but once you show up in person it's just a disappointing bore you can't wait to end!

title: F1 (2025) - Style Over Substance
content: F1 is all eye candy but no soul. Visually, it's slick and adrenaline-charged, but once the novelty wears off, it quickly becomes a repetitive loop of cars drifting left and right - with little else to offer. The races, though initially exciting, soon blur into each other to the point where reused footage wouldn't surprise me. There's barely any emotional depth or meaningful tension between characters, making it hard to care about what's at stake. The storyline feels paper-thin, almost non-existent, with no real edge-of-the-seat moments to hold onto.Ironically, the most thrilling part of the experience wasn't the film itself, but Apple's immersive haptic trailer. That teaser delivered all the excitement the full-length movie promised but couldn't sustain. Honestly, if you've seen that trailer, you've pretty much seen the best the film has to offer.Unless you're here purely for the visuals and engine roars, F1 is a flashy but forgettable ride.

title: Cringe festival
content: Whether you're an F1 fan or not, this is a movie - and as such, it's a very bad one. Its main flaw is the script, which is as predictable and generic as it gets. title: Good but lazy
content: Well... where do I start? The movie is pretty good when it comes to shots and cinematography. Besides being well-filmed, it has some good moments where you enjoy the characters and their interactions... but that's about all the good I can say about it. If you're a recent fan of F1 or the motorsport world in general, you'll probably enjoy it, but that's not me. If you've genuinely liked motorsport for years and understand how it works, you won't be able to watch it favorably. It has a bad script aimed at a casual audience, largely avoiding the world of racing in general, flat characters who don't truly understand what's at stake, and many, many script flaws. These might not be super serious, but once you spot them, they pretty much ruin the experience because you'll stop and think, 'that's not how it works' or 'that wouldn't really happen that way.' In conclusion, I wouldn't pay to see it. It's the kind of movie I'd put on one afternoon at home when I'm eating nachos and scrolling through reels with something on in the background.

title: F1 is decent film yet not top notch driver film
content:
F1 is a decent film that will not win any awards but sadly enough there are not many good films to watch these days in the cinema.Brad Pitt and Javier Bardem carry the entire film and the few supporting actors are just eye candy and Damon Idris does a decent job yet is a B actor compared to these super stars. The directing is top notch
However the biggest issue is the script - which clocks in at a bloated 2 hrs and 35 unnecessary minutes.Maybe Producers thought the $30m salary for Brad required him in almost every scene but the romance does not start until 1.5 hours which is terribly slow because it needs some spice.The pros are that there are endless cool driving scenes and the music is great. The backstory is decent yet gets old fast and the plot twists are predictable with a cliche finale.The cons are the story is bloated, and there is not much sense of purpose or rivalry to merit high accolades so it's not closely in the realm of great driver films like Rush, Ford/ Ferrari, LeMans or Days of Thunder but it's enjoyable. The film should be less than 90 mins and it would have been so much better.Go in with super low expectations and buy lots of soda and popcorn because you'll need it.5 stars and that's being generous.

title: Miscalculated concept
content: This film is well made from a technical point of view, but other than that it's pretty lame. For example, Ford vs Ferrari (2019) or Rush (2013) are good films because they are based on a true story, so they are interesting dramas that show the rivalry between Niki Lauda and James Hunt, and between Ford and Ferrari. But here we get a completely unrealistic fictional story. It's ridiculous from the point where a Gt driver is signed up for an F1 team who is about 60 years old. This would never happen, single seater cars are completely different, especially at that age, no one could perform in F1 at a level worthy of being in F1, let alone be fast, without many years of experience in single seater cars. This story is so uninteresting to me, because it's not realistic enough, but the film isn't so bad that it's funny, so it's basically just boring. Even that old Stallone movie was better than this, because at least there were silly but fun things in it, but this movie tries too hard to be realistic, and is full of unrealistic nonsense. It's just lame and boring honestly.

title: Not what F1 is about
content: As someone who grew up during the Häkkinen-Schumacher era, this film feels like Hollywood fantasy, not Formula 1.Real F1 is about precision, strategy, and respect, about staying in control at 300 km/h - not reckless crashes, childish decisions and heroic clichés.The movie overdramatizes everything. If it was a movie about Nascar: Great; For a movie about F1: an embarrassing Hollywood caricature, including the typical heroic romance scenes that nobody asked for.Overrated.

title: Easy Watch Lacking Creativity
content: Great Sound and Visuals but Bad StoryThe racing is awesome! They reused actual race footage at times and it was amazing seeing it on the big screen.My friend who I watched the movie with, doesn't watch a lot of movies and knows nothing about F1and he really liked the movie. I think people like my friend is who the movie is advertising to.I'm not going to gripe about the inaccuracies with the racing because the story is just... not good. Bland, unimaginative, incomplete, arbitrary, and redundant. There's more words that could be used but that's the gist. Why have an arbitrary bad guy that adds nothing to the story? What ended up happening with the character's injuries? Why is a successful female technical director risking her career by sleeping with a driver? Why is the only female mechanic shown to be messing up and need encouragement? Why should I care about any of the main characters? The movie's story has been told several times before and at times much better than here.Pinnacle of motorsport? You wouldn't tell that from watching the movie. A 60-something year old man hopping into an F1 seat and competing with the likes of Alonso, Verstappen, and Hamilton is absolutely insane.I would've liked to see what it takes to get into and stay in F1, show how cut-throat the sport is, I'm thinking Whiplash meets F1. Take influence from real-life stories, there's a lot compelling ones to choose from. They could have gone the route of Hamilton and Rosberg, best friends getting into F1 and then their relationship deteriorates.Just so many missed opportunities upsets me the most. The biggest take-away from the movie for me is that my friend might now watch an F1 race with me.

title: Looks good..but boring and predictable.
content: I start by saying I'm not a Formula 1 fan..never have been, never will be. My parents used to watch it every Sunday and it was just 70 odd laps of cars droning round a circuit again and again, maybe somebody would try and overtake...dull as dishwater. So I'm probably not the best person to review a film about formula 1, it looks great and has a kicking soundtrack, Bras Pitt is his usual self and the other actors played their parts well..I did have trouble picking up a lot of the dialogue..the talking scenes were very quiet compared to the rest of the movie and the accents were quite thick on some of the characters so just sounded like mumbling. As for the F1 aspect of the film..this was the problem for me..I guess I knew it wouldn't get my adrenaline pumping..heck the real F1 bores me to sleep..the film did once..I did nod off for about ten minutes during one of the quieter scenes. The terminology and strategy of racing in the film was lost on me..not understanding the stuff I should have expected this. I just found the movie overall about boring..too long and a predictable ending..the underdog yada yada..I kind of was hoping for it to hurry up and finish before I fell asleep again to be honest. If you're a fan of racing you may enjoy it more..if like me you ain't..don't bother you lose a good couple of hours and feel like you could have done something more productive with your time.

title: Formulaic 101
content: A losing F1 team running around like headless chickens despite having a prodigy of a No.1 driver and a hottie of a female genius aerodynamics engineer, can't understand why they're dead last in the F1 Championship. The solution? Seek out mans man and white saviour, has been serial loser in life, Sonny Hayes, to put everyone in their place, tell them how their jobs are done, and bed the female DEI hire aerodynamics engineer, and at the same time redeem himself from his past losses and tragic accident (stolen from a real life crash, the depiction of which is frankly insulting to Martin Donnelly, as they use it distastefully to pretend it'sBrad Pitt's character, Sonny Hayes spread all over the track!), and then walk off into the sunset while everyone whose life he just corrected and put in their place looks on in the wake of his rapture inducing aura sighing, "What a guy..." In Once upon a time in Hollywood we saw a certain type of no nonsense white saviour ficticiously putting Bruce Lee in his place. Here we have the same thing, but it may as well be a ficticious account of Lewis Hamilton's rookie year in F1 if all those Lewis Hamilton haters out there could rewrite history and put him in his place. The car scenes in this film look amazing but if you know anything about F1, it's total nonsense, and will make you cringe at the naivety of the tactics. The F1 drivers that endured watching this must have had to bite their lips trying not to laugh their t*ts off out loud! My surprise is Lewis Hamilton's involvement with a storyline like this, as the F1 story he has lived would have been so much more entertaining, real, and show things as they are, and not how certain insecure types out there craving a white saviour to desperately reassure themselves of their masculinity, want them to be. This is a homage to those terrible white saviour Ed Zwick movies Summary: Awful. Just AWFUL...

title: Not as bad as Stallone's Driven Indycar Movie
content: As an F1 and motorsport fan I had low expectations of this film. And got pretty much what I was expecting.Switching off any real knowledge and understanding of the sport (Drive to Survive doesn't count by the way...), you're left with a plucky underdog story where a new character helps galvanise the group to success and escape from a negative situation. And at that level it works.Getting more into the detail, it doesn't work so well. 1) Sonny Hayes is too old and doesn't have enough current/ recent experience driving a modern F1 car quickly (+ the technicalities of having no Super Licence) 2) some of the driving tactics are questionable at best and unrealistic 3) things like designing the car 'for battle' would add extra weight in strengthening components that would make the cars uncompetitive v the opposition. Plus many more...The whole thing about Sonny being 'in the zone' driving speech was a rehash of Ken Miles narration from 'Ford v Ferrari.' " There's a point at 7,000 RPM... where everything fades. The machine becomes weightless. Just disappears. And all that's left is a body moving through space and time. 7,000 RPM. That's where you meet it...."From an equality point of view I'm surprised that producer Lewis Hamilton and F1 Management would be happy that it's a young female mechanic that has the pit stop issues and that the female Technical Director ends up bedding the driver. Not good looks and totally superfluous to the story.The action scenes were meant to be a big seller for the film, but for me they weren't anything special. And having now seen the film it's tough to see what the crew were doing following F1 around so many circuits for so little end product. A Second Unit could have caught most of the paddock, atmosphere shots etc without the need for the whole team, garage, pit setup many times.So for the casual moviegoers and uninformed fans it's a decent (albeit overly long) film, and for real motorsport fans, at least it's not as bad as Stallone's Driven Indycar movie...

title: Glossy, but shallow and aggressively asexual
content: Prior to 2018, F1 teams had teams of grid girls, models typically dressed in body-flattering outfits incorporating the team's colors and logo who performed dance routines during opening ceremonies. However, in 2018, the teams inexplicably pandered to woke groups that disparage internal combustion engines and dispensed with grid girls, much like the useful idiots on college campuses who protest in favor of Palestinian terrorists who would gladly enslave or murder them for not being Moslem.Banning sexy models seems strangely emblematic of the problems with F1: The Movie (2025), starring Brad Pitt as veteran driver Sonny Hayes and Javier Bardem as team owner Ruben Cervantes. F1 is a thoroughly macho male-dominated sport. Since its inception in 1950, there have been only five female drivers who collectively scored half a point, and no female drivers at all since 1992. One would expect these macho drivers earning huge salaries and traveling to exotic locations would attract throngs of beautiful female fans.And yet, the filmmakers seem to have been determined to make the film as sexless as possible. None of the other drivers, including protégé Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris) seems to have a girlfriend or any love interest. Sonny's love interest is a divorcée techno geek whose love scene and wardrobe would likely pass scrutiny of a Taliban movie censor. At one point, Sonny asks a waitress a question intended to establish motivation. Max Bialystock would recognize this bit part as ideal for the girlfriend of a producer, but instead they cast a middle-aged world-weary working-mother type.The filmmakers seem to sacrifice drama for social awareness in numerous other details. The chief design engineer is a female trying to establish herself in a male-dominated world by being an expert in race-car aerodynamics without ever actually driving one. In its seventy-five-year history, F1 has had only one black driver, so the team's protégé is naturally black. A wealthy corporate owner is a disreputable sort with ulterior motives.There is great energy between Pitt and Bardem, but most of the characters, including Sonny, seem shallow. Racing is depicted as a team effort, with half a dozen people monitoring hundreds of sensors in the car and on the track. Plot points often revolve around obscure rules and their consequences. We don't see much of drivers from other teams and the races seem technical challenges rather than personal competitions. Sonny's driving is a compulsion, not a passion. Cervantes is $350 million in the red and in danger of losing his team, but seems unconcerned when Sonny and Joshua crash several $20-million cars.The film offers a fascinating behind-the-scenes view of F1 racing and seems very authentic, but dispassionate to the point of seeming antiseptic.

title: Cool cinematic sequences, sad characters
content: F1 screeches into theaters with the promise of intensity, style, and a throwback underdog arc-but stalls before it ever really takes off. Slick visuals and roaring engines may fill the screen, but they can't mask a hollow core and characters that fail to earn their presence.Brad Pitt plays a seasoned driver brought back into the game as the "man for the moment." Classic setup. We expect the roguish charm of a grizzled veteran, maybe a bit of Han Solo swagger. But here's the problem: he talks like a legend, acts like he's still got it, but fails nearly every time he's given a shot. It's like watching a highlight reel of near-misses, edited with the triumphant music of a hero. Eventually, you stop buying it. If Han Solo never made the Kessel Run and just kept crashing the Falcon, no amount of smirking confidence would win you over. That's the dissonance that sinks Pitt's character - the performance says "icon," but the script never gives him the moments to prove it.His co-star - a young, brash talent positioned as the future of racing - starts off even worse. All arrogance, no grounding. He's insufferable at first: condescending, smug, and grating. Yet somehow, the film expects us to care. He eventually warms up to Pitt's character (in a turn that feels more like studio necessity than earned growth), but by the time that happens, the damage is done. It's hard to recover from so much screen time spent with a character so thoroughly unlikable. When the bond finally forms, it's too little, too late.Then there's the dialogue - or more specifically, the way the film jams in topical quips that feel completely disconnected from the world it's trying to build. The so-called "feminist" one-liners don't feel genuine or relevant to the characters or story. They're just there - stale, overused, and shoved in without context or emotional weight. They don't deepen the characters, they just pull you out of the movie like a pop-up ad during a race replay. It's cinematic virtue-signaling, and frankly, it's exhausting.And that's what makes F1 so frustrating. There's a fantastic movie somewhere in here. The idea of a washed-up driver facing irrelevance, teamed with a rival-turned-ally in the brutal world of Formula 1, could have been gritty, emotional, and thrilling. But what we get instead is a story too afraid to let its characters be flawed in ways that lead to real growth. Instead, it throws buzzwords, half-baked redemption arcs, and hollow bravado onto the screen, hoping it will all feel like something more.That said, the racing sequences are undeniably impressive. The cinematography is immersive and the sound design hits hard - especially in IMAX. You feel the speed, the tension, the blur of tires on tarmac. But the film never builds a human story worth rooting for around those moments. It's like watching a beautifully rendered cutscene in a racing game where you don't care who wins - just that it looks cool.In the end, F1 is all flash and no fire. It wants to be an emotional comeback story with edge, but delivers a formulaic ride with characters you never fully connect to. For all its setup and style, it lacks the most important gear in the engine: stakes that matter, and people worth believing in.

title: From F1 Fanatic - Better than Driven, Worse than Cars
content: I've watched every F1 race since 1980. Given that this was essentially produced by F1 itself as an advert for the sport in America, I expected a thin silly plot compensated by amazing visuals and Hans Zimmer's soundtrack. On the first measure it didn't disappoint - the plot is about as thin and silly as it could be. One example, on his first lap in the car, Hayes is within one second of the established driver, and that was a lap on which he crashed destroying the car. Compensations? The visuals were completely unrealistic, which is a huge acheivement given how many cameras were placed on cars. In the real world, relative speeds between cars are very low - passing another car usually feels like formation flying. They don't zip around, relative to each other. Music was fine though. Verdict: better than Driven, not as good as Cars. Nowhere near Le Mans or Grand Prix.

title: Gorgeous to Look At, Painful to Believe
content: F1 the Movie is one of those films that makes you go, "Wow... and huh?" - often at the same time. The cinematography is absolutely stunning; every racing shot feels like you're right there on the track, engines roaring, tires screaming, adrenaline pumping. The camera work, lighting, and sound design are pure art. But the plot? Let's just say it takes more turns than the Monaco Grand Prix - and none of them make sense. The story tries so hard to be emotional and epic that it ends up feeling unintentionally funny. Characters make absurd decisions, the drama feels forced, and by the end you're left wondering if the writers have ever actually watched an F1 race. Still, visually, it's a masterpiece - just don't go in expecting logic, because the story hits the brakes way before the visuals do.

title: Made to Formula.
content: F1 centred on aged washed up former driver ( Pitt) through an old connection ( Bardem) gets a shot at the big time. There is the standard set up and cliche' reminiscent of Top Gun. The strength of the film firmly centres on the racing car scenes - these are exhilarating, realistic and intense. This is also elevated by the amazing score ( as usual) by Hans Zimmer The story that is threaded is ok but misses a beat. Notions of rivalry, redemption and passion are touched on but are not fleshed out as well as it could have been. There is also a sense of predictability to the story. Worth a watch - especially if you are a Racing car fan.

title: Grand Prix Poser.
content: Paying Brad Pitt $30 million to sit in a simulator and putter around a track for promo shots seems even more wasteful than usual. Saying the actors drove the cars at 200 mph is like saying the actors flew the planes in Top Gun: Maverick. The sped up footage aside, it's obvious any actor footage other than getting in and pulling away was shot on a virtual stage or with a tow rig since the camera would have blocked the actors' vision for the closeups.The rest of the movie only makes it worse with it's drawn out melodrama and unrealistic relationships and depiction of racing rules and etiquette.The only thing it gets right is the fact F1 is the latest sport to become a trendy hobby for Wall Street douchebags who have used internet influencers to boost its popularity so they can make even more money.

title: Slow, yet it felt jumpy and allover the place.
content: So I watched F1, and honestly... it didn't do much for me. The soundtrack? Great. Some of the acting? Solid. But Brad Pitt's character was a letdown. It felt like he was trying really hard to sell a tired "grumpy mentor" vibe, and instead came off forced and kind of irritating.As someone who actually follows Formula 1, the film felt weirdly disconnected from the sport. It's called F1, but it's not about the teams or the season or even the thrill of racing-it's about one struggling team clawing its way through with some seriously dodgy strategy (still cringing at those tactics Pitt's character pulled). Not cool, not clever, just kind of sad.The races were rushed, placements barely tracked, and the whole thing felt like a blurry season recap with no stakes. Character arcs were all over the place too. It ended up being another typical "older guy guides hotheaded rookie" setup. If you're into F1, you'd probably have way more fun watching Drive to Survive. At least that actually respects the sport.

title: So Bad it ends up Semi Okay-ish!
content: This film has every Hollywood cliché there is, and more. It is corny as hell. Predictable, poorly written and shallow with big actors getting easy money. You could take the story (such as it is) and dump it into any other sport - athletics, football, golf, tennis, darts!! It would be so easy to do.The standout feature of the film is the cinematography. It is stunning and holds the whole film together. The cinematography during the races ARE the film and are what get the film the 'Okay-ish' of my title. They are completely thrilling. Also, according to the film there appears to be no rules in F1, but who cares about such minor inconveniences as realism!What really annoyed me and made me downgrade my score to 5 is the terrible way we viewers are treated. I have never seen so much product placement in my life. The film is a shameless money grab. We are treated like mindless sheep. I wouldn't be surprised if the film was in profit before it was released due to all the income from the brands. It is simply shameful.I enjoyed moments of it, but the film is now seen, will never be watched again, and will be rapidly forgotten.

title: My toes got cramp as I was cringing so much.
content:
This film is so bad. I'm sure Americans will probably thinks its a documentary, but when people say its Top gun for Cars- its even worse . Its more embarrassing then watching your Mum and Dad walk hand in hand on holiday, but if you can handle the cringe factor you too can play cliche bingo. Washed up cowboy has-been: check! Annoying rookie who's from 'Da streets innit bro'?: check! Old cowboy has been wearing 80's t shirts living the nomad life in the Scooby-Doo van:check. England is all countryside and farmers walk cows down the road! Check! And you can go on...I originally started watching it but only lasted 20 mins as it was so awful- but as a treat me and my son watched it as we both know and understood F1 which appears more then the people who made this do. We watched it just for bantz through our fingers and needed premium lager to see it through to the end. The start see's Brad in the pits at Daytona, where he drives his car likes its MarioKart and the racing as as realistic as Disneys 'Cars'. (If you look real close you can see Mater in the crowd scenes)
But Brad gets a lucky break that only seems to happen in American films where probably next to being an astronaut getting an F1 drive is probably the most competitive occupation out there. Baring in mind someone has actually pitched this to the studio..'yeah we've got a washed up cowboy with a broken neck who hasn't driven an F1 car in 3 decades but as a mate of a mate he's walked into a F1 seat despite the difference in F1 cars from now and 30 yrs ago being like the difference between night and day he's competitive too, not only that he drives it like its NASCAR but don't worry, everyone is cool with that too'.If you know anything about F1 you will either cry with laughter or die with embarrassment at this rubbish. Even the special effects of Brad driving an obvious F2 car superimposed onto race footage with a go-pro on each corner can't save this mess. Brad goes through front wings like my wife does wing mirrors, but thankfully the misses wing mirrors don't cost 200k, but not only does he go through them like he's playing on an xbox, he then casually just wanders into the love interests design studio and asks if she could knock him up a new wing for 'combat' - now at this point I'm thinking he wants it to fire out bananas like in MarioKart, but no- its for circuits with 'bends'. Which is instantly forgotten as he and his rookie then tears it up against the big boys in Italy at a track that relies solely on straight line speed. Predictively the drive to survive fanboys will lap this up, but trust me when I say you'll never get the 90mins back again. If you want a comedy then fill your boots its hilarious but for all the wrong reasons, especially as Brad bizarrely not only drove F1 30 yrs ago- he still insists on getting paid by cheque like it was 30 yrs ago? I mean, when was the last time you knew anyone getting paid by cheque...

title: More than one F in the chat
content: The team is supposedly on the verge of being sold, yet they keep demolishing their own multi-million-dollar cars like they've got an unlimited budget and zero accountability. Every crash feels less like high-stakes racing and more like a "oopsies".Brad Pitt can't drive without a card in his pocket, which tells you everything you need to know about the film's emotional depth. He hasn't raced in 30 years, yet somehow believes he's smarter than the team's lead engineer - one of the only women in the entire film - and proceeds to explain to her how to design the car, aka do her job. She loves this and decides to listen to his advice and also sleep with him? An disrespectful insult to the intelligence of engineers, women and F1 fans.The film is over two hours long, and I there isn't a single two-second stretch where Brad Pitt isn't on screen. It's suffocating. Damson Idris - a phenomenal actor - is clearly muted to make space for Brad's ego, and Javier Bardem, another powerhouse, gets maybe 5 cumulative minutes of total screen time.Cinematography? Basic. Emotionally, visually, spiritually lifeless. Muted colors, cloudy skies. Some good shots of vegas and racing which are immediately overburdened by long, static shots of Brad doing nothing while the lighting is so flat you could iron your clothes on it. The Hans Zimmer score is practically inaudible, and when it is, the scene is so rudimentary the score sounds comedic.The dialogue in this movie is so excessive and mundane it makes you forget this was supposed to be about racing. You could've cut half the conversations and just shown cars speeding around a track, and it would've been infinitely more entertaining.Formula 1 fans will be horrified - drivers intentionally creating debris to sabotage others, lying about upgrades, smashing up cars every race with no consequence. By the time it ends (mercifully), you realize this wasn't made for Formula 1 fans, or women, or anyone with a brain. I honestly don't know who this movie was made for other than Brad Pitt himself, or 13-year-old boys who think arrogance is character development.It's an overbudgeted, overhyped, self-congratulatory ego trip of Brad Pitt, written by Brad Pitt, directed by Brad Pitt, produced by Brad Pitt, and you guessed it, starring main actor Brad Pitt. His character is overdone, unlikable, tone-deaf, and exhausting. By the end, I completely understood why his entire family doesn't speak to him.

title: Unrealistic and pretty boring
content:
Overview:
Did not really enjoy this movie. The plot was almost like a fantasy with played out tropes. A major problem for this film is that there is not really a clear antagonist amongst the racing community which makes the points of tension not very interesting for the audience.Plot:
The plot follows Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) a failed F1 racer now later in life racing on a smaller scale. He gets recruited by his friend Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem) to join hi F1 team in order to save it from being sold. He encounters resistance from his younger teammate Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris).The plot is quite convoluted with not much focus on the thrill of racing. I'm not a fan of F1 and to the casual watcher, there is a lot of boring going round and round the track. There is also a shoehorned love story that has no point in the movie other than to give Brad Pitt a love interest.Acting:
Acting quality is generally good without being particularly amazing.Conclusion:
With a cast of this talent this movie was a bit of a let down.4/10.

title: Drive to Survive Meets Hollywood: A Formula 1 Movie for the Masses
author: davidfranz-90798

title: An Adrenaline-Fueled Triumph
content:
From the first roar to the final podium moment, F1: The Movie delivers a cinematic ride that lives up to its title. The heart-pounding race sequences-captured with real IMAX cameras and actual F1 events-are a visceral plunge into the high-stakes world of Grand Prix racing. Sitting in the theater, I could practically smell the burning rubber and feel the G-forces.Highlights:Performance & Chemistry
Brad Pitt excels as former champ Sonny Hayes, bringing grit and charisma in equal measure, especially during his mentor-mentee dynamic with Damson Idris's rookie Joshua. The emotional high of their bond adds just the right spice to the high-octane action.Technical Realism
The production spares no detail-F1 and F2 cars were modified for actors to drive, and engine sounds were carefully synced in post-production. Cameos from actual drivers like Alonso, Hamilton, Verstappen, and Norris further ground the film in authenticity.Visual & Sonic Spectacle
Joseph Kosinski delivers another "whoosh"-driven masterpiece (following Top Gun: Maverick), with Hans Zimmer's thunderous score and explosive editing enhancing every twist and turn.Minor Flaws:Scripted Simplicity
True, the story follows familiar sports-movie tropes-a washed-up hero, improbable redemption, team turmoil, rivalry-but it never loses steam. The dialogue is serviceable, if not groundbreaking.Character Depth
Female roles and supporting arcs, while present, could've been more fully developed. Still, Kerry Condon's Kate stands out as more than just arm candy; she's the technical backbone.The Verdict: 9/10It's rare to find a sports film that delivers both on visceral spectacle and emotional resonance. F1 strikes the perfect balance: Immerse yourself in one of the most thrilling portrayals of racing ever caught on camera. Feel the emotional journey between veteran and rookie. Forgive the formulaic script, because the sheer joy of speed and cinema makes it more than worthwhile.

title: F1 the movie should've been F1 the series
content: "F1 the Movie" (June 2025, 150-mins, $610M w/w) Brad Pitt stars as aged, has-been racer who's an F1 team's Hail Mary chance to save it. Now you're seeing review ratings all over the place for this film and I'll tell you why. The story is good, the production values are good. But they misconceived the vehicle for this, yes, pun intended. Whenever a film is over two hours long, something is wrong. They either didn't edit it down to get rid of all the boring/flat scenes or it should've been a mini-series instead of a movie. In this case, it could've been either of those. There were MANY boring/flat scenes that - and thank gahd I did NOT see this in the theatre - you want to have a 30-sec skip button on a remote because, wow, I used it a lot over that 150 mins! There were also too many races depicted which lends itself for this having been better conceived as a mini-series where each episode could focus on one of those many races. So, this could've been a much better film experience if they edited it down to 75-90 mins OR it could've been a decent 6-episode mini-series. But because they made it a 150-min long, stuffed with fat film, it lost a lot of shine it otherwise should've had.

title: Another vacuous Hollywood product
content: This pointless drama-free flick feels less like a film than a precisely engineered Hollywood product. Every beat is calibrated so you never have to find your own reaction: someone crashes . . . Cut to a close-up of someone's disappointed face. A smart move by Sonny . . . Cut to approving smile. Pitcrew nails a tire change . . . Cue montage of a multiracial crew cheering in unison. No need to feel: the movie is engineered (pun intended) to tell you what to feel.Brad Pitt's Sonny Hayes gets a backstory (a wreck, dreams, regrets) that is introduced only to be glossed over. Of course he has a fling with a strong, beautiful (Irish, blonde) mechanical architect, because the template demands it. Of course there's the Black rising star, the Slavic mentor, the African with a Nigerian accent, the redheaded supporting character (gotta make sure every shade of hair colour is accounted for too!) . . . Everyone given a token identity, yet very little room to breathe.The cinematography is muddy in many moments; the visuals try for grit but often end up as overlit gloss. But full credit: it moves at a good clip. Editing is tight; sound design often rumbles impressively. Those parts work. Still, when you leave you feel you saw the thing the film told you to see, and nothing more. No involvement, no frisson, no worries. It's like an A. I. product that seduces you with the promise of a new experience, only to leave you feeling hollow and used.

title: Fast, Flashy, and Fundamentally Flawed
content: If this movie weren't so heavily branded as an F1 film, it would probably sit at a respectable 7/10. Unfortunately, the relentless F1 branding, combined with its obvious borrowing from the Tom Cruise NASCAR movie Days of Thunder, makes it feel more like a copy than a celebration of motorsport.The premise itself stretches believability. The idea of a struggling F1 team rising from the very bottom to win the final race of the season is already hard to swallow, but adding scenes where drivers use F1 cars to crash into competitors without serious repercussions pushes it into the realm of absurdity. Realistically, Sunny would have been banned long before the finale for accumulating penalty points alone.The biggest issue, however, lies in the film's wasted potential. With the full partnership of Formula 1, Lewis Hamilton as a producer, and access to official F1 resources, expectations were high. Yet, despite building a custom F1 car (albeit with an F2 engine), the movie consistently misrepresents the sport at nearly every turn.It's a shambolic execution of a concept that could have been brilliant. Let's be honest, if Brad Pitt weren't attached to this project, very few people would have bothered watching.Final Rating: 5/10 - a high-budget misfire saved only by star power.

title: Speed Without Story: F1 (2025) Under the Hood
content: F1 (2025) is a visually striking film that captures the raw intensity and technical spectacle of Formula 1 racing. The sound design and cinematography are impressive, placing the audience in the heart of high-speed action with visceral impact.However, the film struggles to translate that adrenaline into a compelling narrative. Its story leans heavily toward die-hard fans of the sport, relying on insider knowledge and reverence for Formula 1 rather than creating stakes that resonate with a broader audience. For viewers unfamiliar with the racing world, the plot often feels distant and the emotional beats underdeveloped.Pacing is another significant issue. At over two hours, the film lingers in slower moments while rushing through sections that should carry dramatic weight. The tension often appears in the wrong places, making the overall rhythm feel uneven.In the end, F1 (2025) works as a tribute to the sport, showcasing its speed and spectacle, but falls short as cinema. Fans of Formula 1 may enjoy the technical prowess, but general audiences are likely to find it overlong, uneven, and lacking narrative depth.Verdict: Technically impressive but narratively uneven, F1 (2025) mistakes fan devotion for drama.

content:

From the immaculate sound design to the top-tier cinematography and flawless direction-everything felt crisp, powerful, and immersive. Every race felt real, fast, and dangerously thrilling. The editing and pace elevated the intensity, especially in the first half-easily the best part of the film. Watching it in IMAX made the experience even more immersive.Now for the minor negatives:
The second half of the second act had a brief dip in pace. Around 20% of the film slows down slightly compared to the otherwise high-octane momentum. But honestly, that's a small dent in an otherwise.

title: Sorry to go against the grain: I was disappointed
content: Aside from Kerry Condon's surprising chemistry with Brad Pitt, this film doesn't separate itself from the standard sports cliches to do much. The best thing a sports movie can do with a niche sport (which I define as being outside the big four in the US) is try to elucidate and excite the viewer about said sport. Your mileage will vary, but I didn't find myself caring or even understanding the plot besides broad outlines. The action could have either used enhanced graphics in the manner of Guy Richie or Edgar Wright, or more wide shots that showed the action in broader strokes.It's ironic that the film is directed by Joseph Kosinski who had insane commercial and critical success with Top Gun: Maverick. That Oscar-nominated film had a spectacular emotional impact, but it was ultimately a sequel to a film that leaned on cliches itself. It's also hard to replicate the sense of a Top Gun type work when you don't have an actor of Tom Cruise's intensity. Brad Pitt was playing more of a static role with a James Dean/Marlon Brando vibe.Considering Brad Pitt is practically synonymous with matinee star sex appeal, it seems almost impossible for any woman of Kerry Condon's age to play a convincing option for a Brad Pitt character to want to be monogamous with. After all, the actor himself has trouble being monogamous despite landing some of the most beautiful women on the planet. Considering all this, credit goes to Pitt and Condon for their chemistry.

title: Very entertaining but far fetch
content: The film is very entertaining and truly well made from a technical perspective. The cinematography is breathtaking, and the entire production is polished to perfection-this is top-notch blockbuster cinema. However, the story is far-fetched, and a Formula 1 team would never manage driver selection this way, not even for a replacement. Brad is in great shape, of course, but he's far too old to be driving a Formula 1 car. He has the charisma to draw crowds and fill theaters with popcorn munchers, but no man his age would realistically race in an official F1 event. A younger actor-perhaps ten years younger-would have made the story more believable. Brad's acting was fine, but nothing more. Some of the dialogue left me wanting, especially in how it was delivered. There are also a few small plot holes-such as the resolution of the bet amount, for example. Still, it's a very good summer movie, and I think we're all hoping for an F1 2 in a few years, with Brad's character returning as a team principal.

title: F1; Come for the Action, Stay for the Acting (and the Action)
content:
I watched F1 (in Imax) over the weekend and wanted to share some spoiler-free notes for people considering it. TLDR:
F1 is a very entertaining movie with positive values (e.g. Work ethic, teamwork, friendship, loyalty, integrity, etc.) that help enhance the movie's entertainment value as do the (both very strong) action cinematography and acting which serve as its hallmarks.More brief spoiler-free notes if you're still interested:
1) I'm an IRL F1 fan; there are some liberties taken with real world technical elements, racing rules, logistics, etc. BUT the movie is always clear about its underlying assumptions, team motivations, etc. So as long as you can NOT nit-pick these things the movie experience is still great2) Great acting from the leads almost across the board but Brad Pitt owns the screen in almost every scene he's in and his very strong performance elevates an already good screenplay3) At its core, F1 is not breaking much new ground in story beats: it actually feels a lot like a classic western (in a good way). A 'washed up gunslinger' rides into town to help defend and lift up his downtrodden yet reluctant patrons (i.e. 'townfolk') using, among other things, his talent, experience, wisdom and at times sheer force of will to help the team members to achieve some measure of victory and also to restore or elevate them individually and collectively through the shared experience4) It certainly feels PG-13 and I think it can be understood by appropriately mature people under 18y with parental determination. What I can say is there are serious real world stakes that aren't shied away from but no explicitly graphic or shocking imagery with the notable exception of a non-gory and bloodless yet sometimes sickening depiction of a horrifying on-track racing accident that's revisited a few times as it's crucial to the plot and story; this may be the litmus test for whether someone under 18 will understand and is "ready" to see this movie5) Despite being a PG-13, car racing movie that's presumably made for broad and global commercial appeal (especially given how truly global the sport of F1 is), the writer and director deliver a thematically consistent vision that doesn't shy away from promoting positive values allegorically or through its character actions / implied motivations. They're not controversial, and in fact would generally be universally accepted as positive so it's nice to see that the filmmakers weren't so afraid of offending someone, somewhere as to dilute or half-meaure the film into mediocrity or worse. To be clear, they didn't do that and to me the film is much better for it, both in the viewing experience now and its likelihood of standing up over time.That's all folks. Anything more would be spoiler-y. Spoily? Whatever.I hope you enjoy your next movie, regardless of which one it is!

title: F1
content: F1 is a fast and intense movie where Brad Pitt plays Sonny Hayes, an ex-driver who comes back to help out a struggling team and mentor a young rookie. The story is pretty straightforward, but the way it's told makes it exciting. Pitt is great as the veteran who still has that fire in him, and his chemistry with Damson Idris, who plays the rookie, makes the film fun to watch. The racing scenes are insane. You can feel the speed, the noise, and the danger of Formula 1. It's shot in a way that makes you feel like you're actually on the track which keeps the energy high. The movie is a little long and some parts are predictable, but it's still a really fun watch. It's less about the technical side of F1 and more about the people, the drama, and the comeback story. If you like sports movies with big action moments, this one delivers.

title: Pitt is the only thing going for it
content: The man who wrote the 'Transformers' sequels writing an 'F1' movie. What could go wrong? If F1 is a sport designed for simple people (nothing wrong with that, they need sports aimed at them as well), then making the movie the same way would make sense, right? And they did. This movie is often insulting to the viewer.The movie has no real antagonist. They try and force one later in the movie, but it is very tacked on and fake feeling. We are simply told that this is the team we are to root for and that's that. I imagine there were a lot of behind the scenes politics going on, but get creative and give us a bad guy of some sort to root against.The movie also has almost no human drama. We get little hints at Brad Pitt's character's past life as a professional gambler. Every time that was brought up all I could think was that that would've made a much more interesting story.We follow the usual template that almost every sports film ever made has used as a bible. You could write down every step of the film before it begins and tick them off along the way. They're all in there. It doesn't dare to be different in a single way.Finally, the movie is way too long. Two and a half hours of brutal pacing feels more like five. The only thing this movie really has going for it is Pitt's star-power and charisma. Otherwise it is an arduous watch. 4/10.

title: A complete missfire
content: F1: The Movie feels less like a racing film and more like a glossy cultural mistranslation. Imagine Europeans making a movie about American football: the helmets are right, the stadium is loud, but the soul of the game is somehow missing. That's the persistent aftertaste here.Visually, it's polished to a mirror shine. The cars roar, the circuits gleam, and the editing tries hard to simulate adrenaline. Yet beneath the spectacle lies a strangely hollow understanding of Formula 1 - its politics, its technical chess game, its ruthless margins. The sport is reduced to cinematic shorthand: speed as noise, rivalry as cliché, danger as decoration.Brad Pitt delivers what he always delivers: charisma on tap. Javier Bardem brings gravitas and texture, hinting at a deeper film that occasionally flickers into view. But even their combined acting horsepower can't compensate for a script that treats F1 like a backdrop rather than a living ecosystem. Characters talk about legacy and risk, yet rarely feel shaped by the brutal realities that define the sport.For fans, it's frustrating. For newcomers, it's misleading. The film mistakes proximity for authenticity - standing close to the paddock without truly understanding it. In the end, despite two powerhouse performances, F1: The Movie stalls on the final straight: loud, shiny, and ultimately off the racing line.

title: Immersive but cliche-ridden
content: If you want to get a taste of the thrill of being in the driver's seat of a Formula One race car, look no further than this film. Its main appeal is in its cinematography and all of the races on tracks around the world that it takes us to, even though they were spoiled a bit by the incessant voiceover narration. I liked the casting as well, and I'd be lying if I said this didn't have entertainment value. What held me back from loving it, however, was just how packaged it all felt. There are loads of clichés and predictable moments here. It's really too bad some creativity couldn't have been applied to the script. Brad Pitt's character gets the woman who says she never gets involved with members of the team, be the honorable one who sacrifices himself in the final race, AND yet still be the one who wins the final race? Wait, and his life has been at risk all this time? Good grief. This film had no business being 155 minutes long either. It felt very bloated and yet somehow also pretty shallow. I can see why people love it - clearly a lot of its big budget was geared towards providing an immersive ride, and it delivers on being a Hollywood blockbuster - but I can also see why people hate it. For me, it was somewhere in the middle.

title: Maybe there's a soul here, cut up into ten thousand camera shots.
content: A real dad movie for real dads, a concoction I will not be tempted by.There's fast editing to keep your attention, soap opera dialogue that's passable with 3+ beers, epic music that you will not get a break from, Kerry Co- ooo Kerry Condon. Ok I'll bite.Its production value is impressive, this must have been a logistical nightmare but it never once looks cheap or rushed. The slickness works well for the venues and studios, especially when the camera is allowed to roll for more than two seconds.The editing and writing infuse whatever scope they were going for with a rather narrow, and subsequently dull focus. It's all about the one team; other drivers barely serve antagonistic roles, and the commentary is obnoxiously focused on our two drivers.I think they had the footage for a visual treat, and the 2023 writers strike apparently gave them too much time to tinker with it.Let it be known that the film was written before said strike, so it's baffling that they created a screenplay with nothing to prove and nothing to say.

title: "One lap. That's all it takes."
content:
I honestly got lucky being able to catch this one in the cinema. Missed it when it first came out, but because of the Oscar buzz it was back on the big screen and I went straight for IMAX.And you have to say it:
this is cinema.The visuals, the sound, those cockpit shots it pulls you in. You're not just watching, you're right there in it. In IMAX especially, everything feels bigger, more intense.And that's easily the film's biggest strength.After Top Gun: Maverick, I was already curious what Joseph Kosinski would do here and you can tell right away he knows how to stage this kind of film. The races feel fast but never messy. The editing, the camera work, the sound it all clicks.Brad Pitt fits perfectly into this.That aging driver who still wants to win, who doesn't care about anything else as long as he's behind the wheel you buy it. A lot of the film works because he sells that so naturally."You don't get second chances in this sport."And that feeling runs through the whole film.Every moment matters.Story-wise, it's nothing new.You've seen this kind of story before.But that's not really the point."You either go all in, or you don't belong here."The film works because of the experience.The speed, the sound, the way the races feel.And that's exactly why it's so much fun to watch.
