title: Speed without jolts
content: Driver and machine a race against the clock the ghost of the past and the need for redemption The formula is the same as Top Gun Maverick, the veteran who returns to teach and find himself the young sharks ready to devour him the final race as catharsisWhere F1 truly delivers is in the visceral experience The sound work is simply monumental every gear change the howl of the engine at high revs the muffled sound of the world when the helmet closesThe cinematography with its cameras glued to the asphalt and the singleseaters fantastically translates the weight of those machines the physical gravity pressing on the drivers necks You feel the race in your bonesThe music guided by Hans Zimmer but probably created entirely by the team operates competently on autopilot taut strings at the starts vibrant synthesizers during overtaking the familiar anthem on the podiums It works but never finds its own theme never latches onto the identity of a character or moment in a memorable wayJulian Days costume design in turn is a fascinating silent character With his background in Rush he brings beauty to the functionality of the fireproof suits the architecture of the helmets and the way the veterans worn uniform contrasts with the impeccable shine of the sponsored rookiesThe civilian clothes however tell fewer stories they are functional generic as if the drivers only fully exist inside the car which is partly true for many of these drivers who only see purpose in life on the trackThe problem is that outside the car the film dies It lacks the same impetus It lacks the flashy style and dramatic tension that transform each lap into an act of a play as even Ferrari did And it also lacks the refined character development that elevated Ford vs Ferrari beyond the sport transforming the industry and the rivalry into human dramaHere the drama is functional almost bureaucratic The conversations are delivered with sharp moral lessons the conflicts are resolved with the precision of a wellrehearsed pit stop but without the spark of unpredictability Its a film that seems afraid to take its foot off the pedals and simply let the story unfold freelyIn the end F1 is an impeccably built race car with an engine that roars like a god but only drives in a straight line The emotion is entirely auditory and visual the heart however fast it races needed more time in the pit lane.

title: Your Standard Sports Film
content:
This was my first time watching the movie F1 but I feel like I've seen it a dozen times. It had every sports movie trope you can think of: old guy comes out of retirement for one more shot at glory, his untraditional ways don't work at first and they piss off every one around him, but wait, all of a sudden things start turning around, even the young whipper snapper starts to believe in him, just in time for the championship race. Throw in some sexual tension between the old vet and the first ever female to lead a Formula 1 technical team and F1 had it all.Which doesn't mean I hated the movie. I love sports films, even when the sport, car racing, isn't one I follow, and F1 was a reliably solid, if a bit predictable, sports film. It had quality performances from the lead, Brad Pitt, as well as supporting roles by Damson Idris, Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon. Throw in an uplifting score from Hans Zimmer and this movie didn't disappoint. I gave it 7 out of 10 starsI've now seen all 10 films up for Best Picture this year and I rank them thusly:Hamnet
Sinners
Frankenstein
Bugonia
One Battle After Another
F1
Sentimental Value
Train Dreams
Marty Supreme
The Secret Agent.

title: Big budget, Bad acting
content: F1 is a masterclass in how to spend a blockbuster budget and still end up with a cast that looks like they were recruited from the queue outside Elstree Studios. Brad Pitt is the only one who turns up ready to act. He carries the film with the weary grace of a man who knows he's the only thing standing between this production and complete collapse.Javier Bardem gets a pass. The man could glare at a steering wheel and still outact half of London actors.Kerry Condon also gets a pass. She brings actual emotional intelligence to scenes that would otherwise have the depth of a puddle in a car park if any of the other actors on this cast stepped into her role.And then there's everyone else. Third rate EastEnders wannabes, the lot of them. Not even the good EastEnders roles either, too bland I'm afraid. Every time Pitt leaves the frame, the film nosedives.The movie tries to be high octane but ends up feeling like a learner driver stalling at every junction.Watching any F1 race is better than this movie. Don't waste your time on it.

title: Racing at 300 km/h, Story in Second Gear
content: Few sports seem as naturally cinematic as Formula 1. The combination of extreme speed, sophisticated technology, and constant risk creates an environment where drama hardly needs to be invented-it already exists within the sport itself. F1, directed by Joseph Kosinski, starts from precisely this premise: attempting to bring to the screen the physical and psychological experience of being inside a racing car.It quickly becomes clear that the production invested heavily in visual immersion. Much of the racing footage was captured using cameras specially adapted to be mounted directly on the cars, allowing angles remarkably close to the real perspective of a driver. Instead of observing the action from the outside, the viewer often finds themselves inside the cockpit, watching the steering wheel vibrate, the track rushing forward at brutal speed, and the cramped environment surrounding the driver during a race.This technical care is not merely a behind-the-scenes curiosity; it defines the way the film is experienced. The race sequences carry palpable energy and intensity. At times the camera conveys an almost physical sensation of acceleration-as if the film constantly wants to remind us that in this sport, any mistake can happen at well over two hundred kilometers per hour.At the center of the story is Sonny Hayes, played by Brad Pitt, a veteran driver who unexpectedly returns to Formula 1 to help a fictional team called APX GP. Around him emerges a younger generation of drivers, represented by the character portrayed by Damson Idris. The contrast between the two forms one of the narrative's main dramatic axes: on one side, the accumulated experience of someone who has already faced the limits of the sport; on the other, the ambition of someone who still believes talent and speed alone can conquer everything.This kind of conflict-youth versus maturity, daring versus caution-has appeared many times in sports cinema, yet it remains fertile ground when explored well. Here, however, the screenplay seems less interested in deepening these relationships than in quickly moving the story from one race to the next. The characters function well enough, but they rarely achieve significant dramatic depth.There are also certain narrative liberties that depart from the reality of the sport. Some situations involving driver substitutions or race procedures would not unfold exactly that way in real Formula 1. For most viewers this will likely go unnoticed, since the film's clear priority is the dramatic impact of the scenes.Interestingly, the production pursued a high degree of authenticity in many other aspects. Filming took place within real Formula 1 environments and involved cooperation from the Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile and the championship's teams, something that helps create a convincingly realistic atmosphere. The feeling of being inside the world of the sport is constant.Another element that stands out is the score composed by Hans Zimmer. The musical concept attempts to reflect the relationship between man and machine that defines Formula 1, blending orchestral instruments with electronic layers. The idea itself is interesting, but the final result can feel uneven. Rather than heightening the tension of the races, the music sometimes comes across as overly mechanical or somewhat generic.Despite these imperfections, F1 has clear strengths. The film understands that Formula 1 is, above all, a sensory experience. The roar of the engines, the tense silence before the start, the psychological pressure inside the cockpit-all of this appears on screen with a level of intensity rarely seen in films about motorsport.In the end, the film works better as a visual spectacle than as a deeply developed drama. When the cars are on the track, the movie comes alive with speed and energy. When the narrative slows down to explore its characters, the rhythm becomes less compelling.Still, there is something fascinating about watching cinema attempt to keep pace with a sport that seems to exist permanently at the limits of physics and courage. And F1, with all its virtues and flaws, at least manages to convey a small part of that vertigo.Among the films nominated for the 2026 Oscars, this one ultimately seemed to me the least bad of the group-which says as much about its qualities as it does about the mediocrity of the rest of the competition.

title: I Still Can
content:
GIST
A story about aging ambition and the difference between proving you did it and proving you still can.THINK
The Natural meets The Old Man and the Sea.~~~~~~~~~~My dad used to turn regular cars into race cars and take them to the track. I remember the obsession. I never quite caught it. I delayed watching this movie because of it. I respect the skill. I respect the physics. But it's not something I would choose to watch in real life.So going into F1, I was a little apprehensive. Luckily for me, the racing is the backdrop. Brad Pitt is the movie.Now if you are into racing, you will love this movie as it gets deep into the weeds. Even so, without his performance it's a different movie.There's something different about him here. Not the pretty-boy charisma that made him a star. That's still there, sure. He'll be handsome at eighty. But that's not what carries this performance.It's the stillness, honestly. That's what surprised me.He doesn't reach for emotion. He doesn't oversell regret. He doesn't romanticize the comeback. He just stands in it. There's a grounded authenticity that makes the protagonist feel lived-in rather than written.That's what elevates this movie.Without him, this is a familiar sports template: aging veteran returns, young prodigy with something to prove, team culture needs recalibration, everyone improves one percent and suddenly last place becomes first.But with Pitt at the center, it becomes something quieter. There's a hard-earned calm in what he's doing here. He doesn't overexplain. He doesn't grandstand. When pressed, he answers simply. "Yep." "Yes." It's the energy of a man who doesn't need to narrate his own mythology. He just occupies it.The younger driver needs to win. Winning is identity for him. It's arrival. It's "I did it."For the protagonist, it's something else.It's "I still can."That's a different kind of dragon. A much more interesting dragon in my opinion.When he talks about having "a couple more dragons to slay," it reframes the entire story. The race isn't just about trophies. It's about time. Relevance. Proving to yourself that you haven't slipped past your own edge.The win feels triumphant ~ but there's something strange in it. Not sad exactly. Just complicated. Maybe bittersweet.It doesn't feel like the beginning of something. It feels like confirmation. A reminder. A stay of execution.The film makes sure we see the cost. Even in phenomenal shape, even at this level, everything costs more when you're older. Recovery takes longer. Effort compounds.And then there's the line near the end: "It's not about the money."That's when it clicks. If it were about money, this would be a comeback fantasy. If it's not about money, it's about purpose. About needing something to push against. About measuring yourself against the machine one more time.This is a perfect example of a movie that understands structure. It starts strong. It ends strong. And when a film nails its beginning and lands its ending, you forgive more than you expect to in the middle. There are stretches that feel formulaic. A romance that arrives right on schedule. Familiar beats.But the center holds.Pitt's performance is the anchor.Strip him away and you have a serviceable sports drama. With him, you get something mythic enough to linger.In the end, it's less about the trophy and more about what you can still carry.RATING: 7.5Solid. Occasionally moving. Elevated almost entirely by Pitt's presence and thematic undercurrents.One Word:
Proof.

title: Days of Blunder
content: I'm not a fan of racing or racing centric films but I have enjoyed a few in the past. The recent Gran Turismo film was entertaining though forgettable. I enjoyed Iron Man 2 which had a short scene with F1 racing. But Days of Thunder was a surprising classic. Was F1 intended to be a sequel to that Tom Cruise film?The team that made Days of Thunder 30 years ago made F1. In fact it starts out looking like the sequel to Days of Thunder with a story about a former hot shot race car driver coming out of retirement for another race. But since Tom Cruise was unavailable they got Brad Pitt and turned F1 into a stand alone Brad Pitt film.While the team that made Days of Thunder also made F1, it couldn't replicate the magic that made Days of Thunder so appealing to everyone, not just racing fans. Days of Thunder had the great chemistry between Tom Cruise and Robert Duvall and it had Hans Zimmer's memorable score. F1 had none of that. Even Zimmer returned to score this but didn't write anything nearly as memorable as his Days of Thunder score which was much more melodic and emotional.F1 looks great visually. But it has the look of a film set in the future with its mostly black and white colors. I felt like I was watching Oblivion with some racing in it. Is this how F1 racing is supposed to look? I thought it should be grittier.Anyway, F1 The Movie will probably appeal to only fans of car racing. That's because the mentor-mentee dynamic and the awesome soundtrack that carried Days of Thunder don't exist here. Brad Pitt and the young actor don't have the same chemistry that Tom Cruise and Robert Duvall had. Zimmer's new score is passable but not as memorable. Non-fans might appreciate the visuals, the racing scenes and feel good story. But they won't appreciate the long two and half hour runtime and the confusing technical jargon that failed to explain how F1 racing works. I didn't know who won until the winner rose his hands. And it really should've been cut down to 90 minutes as others have said.Bottom line: you will probably enjoy it if you like auto racing. If you don't, your mind may wander at times. Mine did. How F1 got such high ratings, higher than Days of Thunder, is beyond me.

title: Something of a gulity pleasure.
content: Perhaps about the best thing you can say about "F1" is that it's something of a guilty pleasure and like a lot of guilty pleasures it's undeniably entertaining. What it isn't is one of the ten best pictures of the year (Oscar, take note). It's another highly cliched and formulaic movie about an old-timer, (Brad Pitt, he's over sixty now), and a young hotshot. (Damson Idris, showing real promise), teaming up together to win, in this case to win in Formula One. Cue in a lot of fairly exciting racing sequences which should keep the motor-racing aficionados happy and some off-track romantic shenanigans with Ireland's Kerry Condon and naturally it's edited to within an inch of its life. Highly professional, this is the kind of movie on which no expense was spared save that of the script but like a lot of junk food, once in awhile, it can prove to be pretty tasty.

title: Great but flawed
content: I'll be completely honest, I was not expecting to like this movie. I'm not much of an action girlie, and I especially know nothing about Formula 1 racing.That being said, this movie was really captivating to me. I loved the action sequences, and the story line as well (saw some reviews saying the story line doesn't make sense but for a viewer with no background in this field, it seemed fine to me).However, I did find the script to be a little bit lame at parts, and this is very very far from Brad Pitt's best work, but Javier Bardem was great.I would also like to mention how much I love anything that comes from Apple. The cinematography was great and the palette of the whole movie just felt so crisp and clean. Also, loved the wardrobe styling for Damson Idris!Anyways, I think it gives what it's supposed to give. This isn't supposed to be some deep think piece (like most action movies I believe?), it's just supposed to be a fun time. And it was!8/10.

title: It's fine. It's not special.
content: I very plainly did not have high expectations. I'm not a sports person generally, and I have an especial active dislike for automotive racing and the culture surrounding it. I also harbor no special favor for sports films, as they too often tend toward tired formula, and there are sorrily few counterexamples. As if all that weren't enough, I didn't realize this was directed by Joseph Kosinski, whose biggest claim to fame is 2022's 'Top Gun: Maverick.' I don't think it's altogether bad, but it's definitely bizarrely overrated and undeserving of the acclaim it received. To top it all off, I remember hearing about 'F1' being released, and I also remember hearing nothing about it afterwards; suffice to say that the awards nominations it has subsequently garnered came as a considerable surprise. Still, how is it, actually? Could Kosinski overcome my skepticism with this 2025 sports film?Everyone in the cast looks great, a fine credit to the hair and makeup artists, and to the costume designers. Composer Hans Zimmer can always be relied on for good music, and this is not an exception, even if his score doesn't specifically light sparks in my eyes. What does delight is Claudio Miranda's cinematography, for I distinctly recognize a marvelous artistry in middle-distance shots of vehicles careening down the racetrack, and of other select instances; I favor natural landscapes and animals in art or photography over people or man-made creations, but I can appreciate excellent composition as much as anyone, no matter the subject, and there are certainly shots here and there that are brilliant. The filming locations are fetching, and so is the production design, and in terms of overall production values this unquestionably meets modern standards of film-making, not least of all in terms of sound and image. The stunts and effects are swell.The cast are uniformly charming in their performances, which are otherwise duly suitable; some stars, like Kerry Condon, Callie Cooke, or Javier Bardem, are particularly commendable. Some dialogue or scene writing is a minor joy in its cleverness, and does a good job of catching our attention. This is all well and good, but apart from the pleasure I derive as a cinephile from Miranda's cinematography, it's also kind of the bare minimum that any of us should anticipate from any given title. What else does the picture has to offer? Is the writing strong enough, such as in Ashutosh Gowarikers' 'Lagaan' or Gavin O'Connor's 'Miracle,' to earn merit on that basis? Does the picture in some way lean into what makes the sport special, endearing it to outsiders, as we saw in 2009's 'The wrestler,' to some extent in Ryan Coogler's 'Creed,' and in some measure even in Pixar's 'Cars' trilogy?Joshua (Damson Idris) is an arrogant, somewhat naïve young pup who wants to make a name for himself and who is deeply frustrated with circumstances. Sonny (Brad Pitt) is an experienced but arrogant and jaded veteran with something to prove. Their team, APXGP (I think they need more letters), has been floundering and desperately needs a boost, but Joshua and Sonny readily butt heads. To this tried and true plot we also attach the obligatory romantic element ("when a man and a woman share the screen together, they MUST become involved") that's less than fully convincing, and obligatory scenes of team-building cheering. Along the way there are occasional sequences in which Kosinski and co-writer Ehren Kruger (also returning from 'Maverick') somewhat spotlight the complexities and technical variables that make modern racing unique, though I'm not sure if it's enough to emphatically remark on it as a viewer, and they don't necessarily do so with consistency. And oh, look, a scene in which characters have a serious conversation while ostensibly engaging in recreational activity! Who doesn't love poker, especially if an oh-so pretty lady is involved?I like Condon, but I can't help but feel that this part is beneath her, and there's a part of me that gets the impression Kosinski instructed her to be "extra Irish" in her delivery, just in case American viewers didn't know she was from the Emerald Isle. In addition to Zimmers' score, some of the popular songs on the soundtrack are a blast, others have worn out their welcome over time (the result of "classic rock" FM stations playing the same songs at least once every other day), and still others aren't as much of a fit as the music supervisor thought. The fact that Sonny seems to know All The Right Moves, and how to exploit rules, gets tiresome after a bit, and such writing may mean more to non-racing fans if Kosinski or Kruger did more to ingratiate us to racing in the first place; likewise for some aspects of the story. There is drama between characters, and stakes for them, but I don't know if characters or their relationships are developed enough to foster any major investment. I do also think there could be real thrills to be had from racing sequences as drivers jockey for position at a couple of hundred miles per hour, if they were written and executed shrewdly enough, but since Kosinski favors a lot of first-person shots that limit our perspective - or worse, positions the camera to look back on the person in the driver's seat - there is less potential for thrills than if we were simply relying on play-by-play description.I do think the story is pretty okay, albeit not flawless, and despite cookie-cutter elements it's not a total "copy and paste" job. Anyway, who doesn't love an underdog? For everything that genuinely is done very well across two and one-half hours, however, there is nothing here so substantive and grabbing as to capture our imagination in the way that cinema is specially capable of. I believe 'F1' is more admirable than 'Maverick,' showing that Kosinski and Kruger have grown in their respective crafts in the time since, but that's not necessarily saying much. I believe 'F1' is earnestly enjoyable, and better than I expected with all my cynicism, yet at its best there's nothing that stands out so much as to sustain a fire in our bellies; "passively enjoyable" might be the most apt phrase. Maybe what it ultimately comes down to is that it's a feature most likely to appeal to those already enamored of the sport, though as we've seen with other examples in other genres, I wonder if such safely formulaic odds and ends in pursuit of fan service don't mean that the end result is watered down compared to what racing fans would really want. I'm not one, so I couldn't say. All I know is that I didn't suppose much of this flick when I sat to watch, and against all odds I sort of like it. The problem is that I "sort of like it" in the same way one likes a regular meal of lunch meat and a piece of fruit, something that's baseline satiating of one's hunger without expense, but also without flavor or excitement. If that's the type of viewing experience you want then have at it, and I'm glad for those who get more out of 'F1' than I do. For my part, decent as it may be, I don't think it's any wonder why I didn't hear any word of mouth about this after its release.

title: Driven to Attraction
content: I'm no petrolhead, I only drive a car out of necessity and I'm not into Formula 1 in any way. Not a great lead-in I'll admit, to appreciating this particular movie. And then, to get it at all, you have to get around the clichéd everything about it, the stereotypical characters, the scarcely credible narrative set-ups and pretty much every line of stock dialogue uttered by anyone and everyone involved. And yet, was I entertained? Well, yes I was, in fact pretty much all the way through its two and a half hour running time, coincidentally about the time it takes to watch a F1 race, which itself is something I've never actually done.I was aware of the "Top Gun"-on-wheels comparisons (1 or 2, it's all the same!) but some movies you just have to disengage from and simply strap yourself in for the ride, otherwise you'll gag on every hackneyed plot point you could think of for this type of film.A one-time rising star of F1, whose career literally crashed and burned without ever winning a race, Brad Pitt's Sonny Hayes character, some thirty years on, succeeds in the Daytona 24 Hour Race at his unstated but clearly advanced age, after which he's sought out by his old co-driver, Javier Bardem's Ruben Cervantes, now the independently wealthy owner of Team APXGP, after a disastrous "Nul points" start to the season. With his organisation in apparent disarray, he's been threatened by his board of directors that he'll be voted out of his own company unless the team wins one of the remaining nine races in the season.Which is why Cervantes tilts at this particular windmill by turning to old chum Hayes who he thinks can be the desperately needed catalyst to change the team's fortunes and in particular bring on the talented but precocious young rookie driver Joshua Pearce, played by Damson Idris.So there you have it, will the old-timer, turn around Team APX? Naturally his laid-back and yes, I'll stoop to use the word, maverick ways initially upset everyone on the team including the team's sceptical, female technical director Kate McKenna, played by Kerry Condon but especially Idris and eventually even Bardem himself. He even has health issues which he keeps to himself, but gradually the results start to improve.Ahh, but behind the scenes there's skullduggerry afoot in the shape of a conninving conspirator on Bardem's own board out to sabotage the team's prospects for his own nefarious ends. Threatened with disqualification over a design technicality on the eve of the final race in Abu Dhabi, will they even get to drive in the race? And if they do, what are the chances of pulling off that essential but elusive win? Well, what do you think?Like I said before, the screenplay manages to lap almost every recognisable motor racing cliché in its wake, with Pitt's character's "deus ex machina" intervention set up from the start to deliver the miracle for which Bardem was praying. It actually became fun for me to count off one "no way" but somehow inevitable plot-jump after another, the narrative even taking a pit-stop for a sexual frisson between Pitt and Condon.Where the action is of course is in the depiction of the races themselves. Using real life locations, we get to see the F1 circus in all its glitzy, gaudy glory especially at Las Vegas and Abu Dhabi - I guess Monaco wasn't available for some reason or other! On the track itself, you get to feel every twist, turn, acceleration, bump, shunt and yes, crash as you're put in the car with the driver and also get to share in the tense lows and euphoric highs of the support team on the sidelines.This film isn't "Rush" or "Senna", both of which I've seen and appreciated more, but as good old-fashioned movie entertainment, "F1" certainly zooms along to break the chequered flag by the end.

title: Beautiful and heart pounding
content: F1 is a film that encapsulates the spirit of a sports movie through a familiar yet effective formula. The storyline revolves around an aging racecar driver, Sonny Hayes, portrayed by Brad Pitt, who embarks on a mission to save a struggling team alongside a cocky rookie driver. This core narrative is enriched with classic elements, such as a romantic interest and the journey of a once-great driver. Director Joseph Kosinski skillfully employs the well-loved tropes of sports films, demonstrating a clear grasp of the genre's dynamics, ensuring that the film resonates with audiences despite a lack of originality. The film excels in its technical execution, benefitting from the expertise of Kosinski and Academy Award-winning cinematographer Claudio Miranda, who utilize innovative camera angles to immerse viewers in the thrilling world of Formula One racing. The editing, handled by Stephen Mirrione, is adept, alternating between serene and chaotic sequences that reflect the racing experience. Notably, a tight edit during a tire change serves as a perfect metaphor for the film's pacing and rhythm. Brad Pitt's portrayal of Sonny is both charming and nostalgic, embodying the archetypal veteran character with a nuanced performance that contrasts well with the younger cast, including Javier Bardem and Kerry Condon. The chemistry among the casting complements the film's engaging narrative, which thrives on the joy of the filmmaking process itself. While F1 showcases technical brilliance and an enjoyable narrative, it suffers from an excessive runtime that may leave viewers fatigued before reaching the climax. The film's ambition and adherence to the beloved sports-movie formula contribute to its entertainment value, making it a visually stunning experience that, although not groundbreaking, delivers an exhilarating ride in the world of motorsport cinema.

title: Good, not great.
content: I watched this hoping for a compelling story set in the world of Formula 1, but there really was no plot to this movie. It felt as though the film existed largely because of the current popularity of F1, rather than being driven by a particularly strong narrative.That said, there are elements the film does very well. The racing sequences are exciting and well executed, and the soundtrack adds real energy to the on-track action. Visually and technically, the movie delivers a strong performance.But it's hard to overlook the weakness of the plot. The story was thin and predictable, and I never really felt invested in the characters or the direction the film was heading. Because of that, the overall experience felt more like a way to pass the time than a memorable film. While the production quality and racing scenes were impressive, the weak storyline ultimately brought my score down to a 6/10. Given that, I was a little surprised to see the average rating sitting above 7 at the time of writing. Perhaps I'm too harsh, or perhaps others are too impressed by the fact that the movie had F1 racing action in a time when the sport has never been more popular.

title: 6.8/10. Recommended.
content: My rating is not that high but I won't say F1 is overrated : This is a very "honest" movie which doesn't pretend for a second to be something else than it really is. I would probably have liked it more if i was into formula 1, but the thing is, I have never watched a race. Yet, being a formula fan is not a prerequisite in order to enjoy this movie. F1 is a good sports drama, technically impressive, playing by the rules of the genre. If there is something I can sayas objectively as possible is that it should have been shorter. 155 minutes running time is far too excessive for a movie in which not too many things happen to keep you interested for so long.Alongside Kosinski's high octane directing, Brad Pitt's performance is the film's greatest strength : He was exactly as he should be. There is no overacting here as is the case in similar movies, all the actors were sort of restrained and while that's a positive, I might have actually preferred the opposite : It's a sports drama movie, "cringefest" is allowed if not expected. But that's just me. Overall, this is an entertaing movie, there's action, drama, humor and a satisfying ending.

title: F1 2025 - Speed with a Soul
content:
This is not just a racing film; it is a story about pressure, redemption, and the fragile line between victory and collapse.F1 2025 succeeds by balancing high-octane spectacle with emotional depth. While the racing sequences are technically impressive (capturing speed, sound, and risk with near-documentary realism) the film's true strength lies in how it humanizes the world of Formula One. Instead of treating drivers as untouchable heroes, the narrative exposes their doubts, injuries, and personal conflicts, making each race feel like the climax of a psychological battle rather than a mere sporting event.The pacing is carefully structured: intense race scenes are followed by quieter moments of reflection, allowing the audience to process both the physical and emotional consequences of competition. The script avoids simplistic rivalries and instead builds tension through long-term character arcs, particularly in how ambition clashes with loyalty and self-preservation. Visually, the film uses sharp contrast between the sterile environment of the paddock and the chaotic energy of the track, reinforcing the theme of control versus unpredictability.What makes the film memorable is its emotional payoff. Certain scenes are designed not for adrenaline, but for empathy-showing the cost of pushing beyond human limits. These moments elevate the movie beyond a sports drama into a meditation on identity and resilience.Rating: 8/10
A powerful blend of thrilling races and heartfelt storytelling, F1 2025 proves that speed alone is not what drives this film-emotion does.

title: Old Fashioned Blockbuster!
content: F1This was a big budget production of epic proportions ( 200 million dollars to make and took 3 times that at the box office) and certainly had the Steve McQueen feel, the greatest of compliments.Brad Pitt was certainly on top form and it is a rare person that has the believable heft to carry this type of movie. (he was paid 30 million for undertaking this role) It was a very physical role that projected a testosterone machismo from the get go, this was entirely intentional and reflects the inner world of competitive racing.The action sequences were exceptional and held the viewer's interest even if that viewer had little interest in formula 1, that's me. Of the Oscar nominated movies this film has got the best action sequences.We had so many stars in minor cameo roles to contribute to the strength of the characters, this was without doubt inspired casting.I know I shouldn't but I will, Brad Pitt has perhaps the best plastic surgeon on the planet, looking about 40 in face and body he is 61, and it all moved in a mostly naturalistic way.This was the most old fashioned traditional movie that you would have watched in the cinema along with Jaws and Towering Inferno whilst eating popcorn and again this is a great compliment.I'm going to give this movie a firm 8 outta 10, I'm glad it was nominated for best picture this year but I'm not giving it the Oscar as I'm not a fan of motor racing lol. But it can have it for editing or action sequences.

title: Good, but definitely not Oscar nomination worthy
content: Very fun, very cheesy. Definitely shouldn't have gotten nominated for best picture over Bugonia. The racing was very enjoyable to watch and I felt a lot of pressure. This follows the basic layout of sports films with its ups and downs. No outstanding performances, just decent. I liked all the shots, it made it feel like a racing video game. I wasn't a big fan of the music choice, it felt corny at times, but I did like some of the instrumentals. I also like the relationship between Sonny and Joshua they had a lot of back and forth. Overall a good film.

title: The most immersive racing epic!
content: F1: The Movie tells the story of Sonny Hayes(Brad Pitt), a former Formula One driver who comes back to guide & partner with a younger driver, named Joshua Pearce(Damson Idris).F1: The Movie is a technical masterpiece of a film. Director Joseph Kosinski has given us a movie that's filled with edge of the seat, heart-pounding, thrilling & completely entertaining racing scenes. Though the story is quite basic, it doesn't really matter because all the technical elements from the cinematography by Claudio Miranda, the background score by Hans Zimmer, to the sound design & visual effects are awe-inspiring. The racing set pieces are mind-blowing in IMAX & makes you feel like you're sitting in an F1 car, with the driver. Brad Pitt is outstanding as Sonny Hayes. Pitt's charm & charisma effortlessly carries his performance as the mysterious protagonist. Damson Idris is spectacular as Joshua Pearce. Idris brings his own effortless charm & charisma that makes his supremely confident character, very likeable regardless. Javier Bardem is brilliant as Ruben Cervantes. Kerry Condon is superb as Kate McKenna. Tobias Menzies is impressive as Peter Banning. Sarah Niles is excellent as Bernadette. The supporting cast is amazing. F1: The Movie is a must watch for fans of Brad Pitt, F1 enthusiasts & racing film buffs. A must watch on the big screen & looking forward to the recently announced sequel!

title: Live Action Cars 3
content:
Live Action version of Cars 3.Visually impressive. The plot was predictable. The characters were fine. The dialogue was borderline atrocious at times.I'm honestly not sure why this was nominated for Best Picture. It's not a terrible film but it's highly forgettable. title: The definition of good movie
content: The Formula 1 film is an excellent movie in many respects. It has an outstanding technical side that is truly a 10/10; in fact, in my opinion it will win an Oscar for editing (which is really incredible because it's done in a masterful way). I also think it could have won for cinematography, but it isn't nominated (do you really want to go against the Academy???).The plot is enjoyable, with Brad Pitt proving that he is still capable of making incredibly action-packed films and acting excellently. At times it feels a bit like Top Gun, but it doesn't ruin things the way they did with Tom Cruise's film (I mean that Tom Cruise's movie at a certain point is basically just a love story, whereas the F1 film only includes small moments related to the romance, without ruining the film as a whole).Maybe 2 hours and 30 minutes is a bit too long (if it had been 30 minutes shorter, that would have been great), but it's still worth watching for the kind of movie it offers: a quality film, very well made and very enjoyable. Javier Bardem was also excellent in the film.I'm satisfied even though I'm not a Formula 1 fan, but I really liked this movie.

title: F1 is an amazing experience
content: F1 is a full-throttle cinematic experience that captures the soul of Formula One with rare precision. From the opening lap, the film throws you straight into the cockpit, delivering breathtaking race sequences that feel visceral, dangerous, and alive.The sound design alone is worth the ticket-engines scream with raw intensity, immersing you in the chaos and beauty of the sport.What truly elevates F1 beyond a typical racing film is its emotional core. The story balances speed with humanity, exploring pressure, rivalry, legacy, and the cost of chasing perfection at 200+ mph.The performances are grounded and convincing, making you feel the weight of every decision, every mistake, and every victory. Even if you're not an F1 fan, the film makes the stakes crystal clear and deeply personal.The cinematography is stunning, blending real-world race footage with cinematic flair, while the pacing never drags-every lap matters. It respects the intelligence of the audience and the authenticity of the sport, avoiding cheap drama in favor of earned tension. F1 isn't just about winning races; it's about identity, sacrifice, and the fine line between greatness and self-destruction.

title: Fantastic Thrill Ride
content: F1 The Movie starring Brad Pitt, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon and Damson Idris tells the story of the owner of a struggling Formula 1 Team who convinces a veteran driver to return to F1 to mentor a rookie driver and save the team. Directed by Joseph Kosinski (Top Gun Maverick), F1 looks absolutely fantastic and the racing scenes are breathtaking. Pitt is good as veteran Sonny Hayes and his natural charm and charisma are ideal for the former bad boy of the track. Bardem is assured and gives understated authority as the owner of the team and Idris as the rookie is good, though at times a little bit overbearing. Perhaps the best performance of the film is by Kerry Condon as the technical director of the F1 team. She is suitably headstrong, and in the relationship scenes with Pitt really adds gravitas to the dynamic of the couple. F1 The Movie is 2 hours 35 minutes long and barely pauses for breath. You really get drawn into the teams battle for survival and want them to succeed. Interestingly for me, when watching F1 it follows similar plot lines to Stallone's under rated Driven film, from veteran driver tutoring a rookie, to accidents on the track affecting primary characters, to boss of the team calling in a favour from his old racing buddy, but F1 probably does things just a little better. Ultimately F1 The Movie is a fantastic film, and it really was a breath of fresh air watching a recent film which succeeds in entertaining in an old school way and standing tall and proud next to classics of yesteryear. An exhilarating thrill ride F1 is a film worthy of your time and attention and certainly gives weight to my opinion that Kosinski is one of the best directors of action in recent times. I highly recommend this film.

title: Rightfully an Oscar Contender... and the Award for Best Sound It Won Was Absolutely Deserved
content: The film truly grips you from beginning to end. There's hardly a single minute where you feel bored. I really liked the way the characters were written. Visually impressive and excitingly staged, this is genuinely one of the few films that completely captivated me.Racing films portray a tough sport that can hardly exist without rivalry in its storytelling. The racing scenes are breathtaking, and the rivalry is portrayed in a very believable way. Even away from the racetrack, the film works exceptionally well as a drama. Brad Pitt is a wonderful actor and perfectly cast for this story.The female role - played here by Kerry Condon as an aerodynamic engineer - also does a fantastic job. Overall, the chemistry between all the actors is so strong that the film feels very authentic.I also found it interesting that the film was shot at real racetracks during actual race events. Of course, the actors didn't participate in the real races, but this made it possible to include real crowds in the scenes, which greatly enhanced the sense of authenticity.An outstanding film that also shines thanks to Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, and Tobias Menzies. The entire cast delivers a remarkable performance. The two hours and 35 minutes flew by - rarely has a racing film entertained me so brilliantly. This is one of perhaps 3-5 films that have completely drawn me in.A huge hit for Formula 1 fans, but even general action audiences will definitely get something out of it. Powerful engines, a gritty sound, and stunning visuals. I loved this film - truly a recommendation for a wide audience.

title: Lord have mercy to anyone who drives with the F1 theme playing!
content:
No seriously, the moment that bass hits in the opening credits, I told myself, ok this is gonna hit hard and the film's gonna be great. The track's such a banger and I've had it on repeat since.The high-octane intense racing action sequences are top notch, giving us crazy camera shots from even the driver's POV that make us feel immersed in the races and quite literally put the audience on the driver's seat. Honestly, this was such a big miss to see on a gigantic IMAX screen with the expanded aspect ratio scenes like, I have major beef with Apple for not releasing the IMAX version on digital for us people who missed it on the big screen :(
I wish I saw this; the true perfect summer blockbuster at least on a regular screen instead of meh Jurassic World Rebirth💀😭😭I was enthralled throughout the entire film and loved the film for its brilliance in the action and Hanz Zimmer's score, which is just crazy as usual. Some tracks are so good that they make me feel like I'm on a late night speed drive trying to outrun my past.I felt moved with the overall story but what's a bit dissatisying about the film is also sort of the lack of originality, story wise. It follows a pretty formulatic sports film plot featuring typical Hollywood cliches and stereotypes and overall feels like the writers smashed together Top Gun and Pixar's Cars films with an F1 setting.I'm just giving my honest constructive criticism here, considering the film even got an Oscar nomination for best picture.Yet the film's not bad though cause it does what a film should do in the most grand way possible: entertaining its audience.Also, I'm rooting for the film to snatch awards in Sound and Film Editing categories at the Oscars cause those were just masterfully done.Overall, the film's brilliant and is a super entertaining fun watch despite the overall script lacking a bit of originality.Favourite scenes:
When 'Lose My Mind' played in full length.'He's flying' scene.Favourite lines:
"Box, box, box."
"Plan C is for combat."
"Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast."

title: A thrilling but flawed ride
content: OverallF1: The Movie is a motorsport drama about a fictional former F1 star driver who comes out of retirement after three decades away. Director Joseph Kosinski is most recognized for Top Gun: Maverick, but the quality wasn't the same here. The amateurish story, character development, and over-dramatization impaired what could have been an excellent movie. Four Oscar nominations are on the line for later this year, which surprised me. Best Sound, Best Visual Effects, and Film Editing are well-deserved, but I don't agree with Best Picture.Would I recommend this?No, you are welcome to skip this unless you're an F1 fan. It's heavily cliched from the first scene to the last, making it cringeworthy and difficult to endure. This was second-tier to the brilliant Ford v. Ferrari (2019) and Rush (2013), which featured intricate stories and poignant characters. I can't believe Apple Film Studios earned more than $650 million from this. My rating is 7/10.Plot (spoiler-free)Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) was a gifted star Formula One racer in the 90s until a major accident sidelined him. He returned thirty years later after his former teammate, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), asked him if he wanted a shot at the F1 world title. Bardem's team was struggling and needed a veteran driver to team with the egoistic rookie, Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). The two have vastly distinct personalities and must work together against stiff competition. Time and money are on the line.Interesting TidbitsMost of the track incidents mirrored actual ones from various decades and drivers. Ten real Formula One teams joined the filming after negotiations with the Federation Internationale de l'Automobile (FIA). Lewis Hamilton, one of the greatest Formula One racers, helped produce this movie. Apple designed the lightweight car-mounted cameras used and only 10cmx10cm. They tried to maintain the racing realism. Production occurred in 2023 and 2024 during Grand Prix weekends, including the British Grand Prix where they set up their own garage between the Mercedes and Ferrari garages. They emulated these real garages and engineers to capture the live atmosphere. Idris and Pitt, the focal actors, did their own driving throughout in Formula 2 cars made just for this movie (and Formula 3 cars to prepare before filming). Pitt garnered over 6,000 miles of driving in his car during the production. The corporate sponsorships and deals with brands generated $40 million. They filmed over 800 hours of total footage, and editing took almost two years. Hollywood studios were involved in a bidding war for this script that had Brad Pitt confirmed until Apple Studio won the bid. This film topped the box office on release weekend and since has generated over $650 million total globally. The Formula One champion, Max Verstappen, skipped the private screening and world premiere of F1 because he preferred authenticity over dramatization.TechnicalsF1: The Movie has much going for it, but significant shortcomings. The best aspects were the performances, direction, editing, score, cinematography, camerawork, and pacing. Many shots were impressive, especially at the speeds they were driving. It reminded me of Top Gun: Maverick and the excellent shots in that. The authentic sounds, savant Hans Zimmer's score, and high-speed racing kept you on the edge of your seat. The atmospheric races fill you with tension and blood-pumping thrills, even though they are unrealistic. Pitt's remarkable charisma and Idris' flamboyant personality demand that you keep watching. Visual effects, tight editing, and sensational cars dominate the scenes. If we stopped here, this would yield a legendary film. Sadly, I can't stop here in the name of impartiality. The rest of this review will tear this film apart.The clichés are predictable and overly dramatized until they become ridiculous. Although I don't expect realism, I still expect a modicum of plausibility. This mocks the time, training, dedication, and hard work athletes put into their respective sports to the point of humiliation. Verstappen made the correct call not showing up for the world premiere. Next, let's focus on the abysmal script. The superficial narrative is shallow and without substance. There is no character development outside of the hero, Brad Pitt, because, of course, only the hero matters (sarcasm). The dialogue is unnatural and uninspiring. I experienced little empathy for the characters. They throw in a romantic arc that doesn't fit. The buildup lacks sufficient depth. The film leaves you feeling nothing by the end. Finally, what were the childish tactics they used? I don't watch motorsports, but I've watched racing shows like Top Gear, documentaries on motorsports, and racing clips for over twenty years. Hamilton was involved, so I'm baffled to see this.

title: Spectacle, speed, and controlled emotion
content: F1: The Movie is a technically impressive and highly polished sports drama that thrives on immersion and realism. The film's greatest strength lies in how it captures the physical intensity of Formula 1 racing - the speed, the danger, and the razor-thin margins between control and catastrophe. Every race sequence feels heavy, loud, and consequential, making the experience visceral even for viewers who are not deeply familiar with the sport.Narratively, the film plays things relatively safe. The character arcs and conflicts follow familiar sports-drama structures, prioritizing clarity and accessibility over surprise or complexity. While this limits its emotional depth, it also keeps the story focused and efficient, never losing sight of momentum.Ultimately, this is a film driven more by precision and atmosphere than bold storytelling. A strong, immersive experience that delivers excitement and authenticity, even if it avoids taking major narrative risks.

title: One of the best films of 2025.
content: Initially, I knew this was a film about Formula 1 starring Brad Pitt. I didn't need anything else-I had decided for myself that I was definitely going to see it, so I didn't watch trailers or any additional materials. My initial attitude towards the film was neutral, as towards just another solid Brad Pitt movie.My opinion completely flipped during the very first opening scene of the film. The Daytona 24-hour race was shot and presented so incredibly, dynamically, and thrillingly that I was instantly immersed and simply enjoyed the next two and a half hours. Every subsequent race is a personal small dose of adrenaline or a high, however you prefer. It's shot fantastically: there are many shots and entire episodes where, visually, you as a viewer get aesthetic pleasure from watching. One of the few modern films where the $300 million budget is visible on screen.I think it's worth mentioning the plot as well-it's genuinely good. The no-longer-young racer Sonny Hayes once competed in F1 30 years ago, but due to certain circumstances (we'll avoid plot spoilers), he had to quit the sport. Now he's more of a touring racer: he shows up at various races, wins there, gets a check, and disappears. But then his old friend, with whom he once raced in F1, appears and asks for his help. If there are no results this season, the team will be sold, and Ruben (our main character's friend, played by Javier Bardem) will be ousted from his position as team principal. This is where our journey through the remaining stages of Formula 1 begins.Naturally, there's a small conflict-in the form of a young, talented but arrogant guy who thinks less about racing and more about pleasing sponsors and which party to attend next to trade on his image. And even in this moment, the clash of the old school and the new is shown quite well. Somewhere closer to the middle of the film, I started running through possible ending scenarios with all the data I had at the time-and honestly, I couldn't figure it out. Moreover-the variables changed quite significantly, so the film keeps you in suspense right until the very end.The film also features a large number of star cameos: for example, Tiësto, various motorsport stars, and Jeremy Clarkson himself.I've come across comparisons of this film to Tom Cruise's Top Gun somewhere on the internet. And I can partly agree with that. I was also glued to my seat: watching, worrying, and thinking about what might happen next. If I recall other films-Ford v Ferrari immediately comes to mind. Undoubtedly a magnificent picture-and all three of these films work equally well. Not many films leave a pleasant aftertaste of memories after leaving the theater-but this film succeeded in doing that.Perhaps, I was also lucky with the cinema where I watched this film. Even though I go there quite often and everything is usually good-this time the sound was especially great. If there's a Dolby Atmos theater in your city-it's definitely worth it.Conclusion: We have an incredibly driven, nerve-wracking, and dynamic film. Even if you're not interested in cars and motorsport in particular-this movie won't leave you indifferent. Definitely recommended viewing, and on the big screen if possible. I think it has every chance to compete for the most important film awards this year.10/10.

title: Velocity and Vision
content: In an era of blockbuster cinema increasingly suffocated by weightless digital artifice and manufactured narratives, director Joseph Kosinski has emerged as an unexpected champion of the physical world. Following the spectacular, tactile triumph of Top Gun: Maverick, Kosinski turns his lens to the asphalt in F1. On its surface, this is a crowd-pleasing sports drama about an aging driver seeking one last shot at glory. But beneath the roar of the engines and the cinematic sheen of its star, Brad Pitt, F1 operates as a philosophically robust celebration of human competence, the assumption of responsibility, and the unforgiving nature of the realities of the world. It is a popcorn movie with a remarkably grounded soul.The genius of F1 begins with its methodology. By placing his actors into actual race cars and capturing them at speeds exceeding 180 miles per hour, Kosinski is making a statement that transcends mere technical spectacle. He is returning cinema to the roots of its tradition. A Formula 1 car does not negotiate. It cannot be appeased by good intentions or flattened by bureaucratic overreach; it responds only to absolute competence and the immutable laws of physics. In a cultural moment that often prioritizes subjective reality, the racetrack in F1 stands as the ultimate, refreshing meritocracy. The danger is real, the G-forces are heavy, and the consequences of failure are immediate.Within this high-stakes arena, we find Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt), a driver who crashed out of the sport in the 1990s and has spent decades outrunning the specter of his past. Pitt delivers a wonderfully weathered performance, playing Sonny not as an arrogant hotshot, but as a man intimately acquainted with his own limitations. Psychologically, Sonny has been living in self-imposed exile, terrified of the razor-thin border between structure (the engineered perfection of the car) and unpredictability (the catastrophic variables of the track). His return to the struggling APXGP team is not merely a quest for a trophy; it is a necessary confrontation with his inner demons.This internal journey is catalyzed by his relationship with Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris), the team's prodigy. Pearce possesses raw, unbridled talent, but lacks the orienting wisdom required to survive the ladder of competence. Here, the film gracefully pivots from a story of individual glory into a meditation on mentorship. Sonny must lay down his lingering ego and assume the heavy burden of responsibility for another person. This transition-from the isolated, self-serving individual to a mentor who sacrifices his own spotlight for the sake of the future-marks a profound change of heart. Sonny's spiritual growth is measured not in lap times, but in his willingness to guide Pearce through the very psychological wreckage that once derailed his own life.Furthermore, F1 beautifully captures the communal nature of what appears to be a solitary sport. While the driver sits alone in the cockpit, his survival and success are entirely dependent on the crew-represented with gravitas by Javier Bardem as the team owner and Kerry Condon as the lead engineer. The film treats the pit lane with an almost sacramental reverence. There is a deep perception here that human beings are not mere resumes or isolated hyper-individuals, but persons who find their truest meaning in communion with others. The roaring engines, the meticulously timed pit stops, and the shared risks become a physical manifestation of human striving, pushing against mortal limits to achieve a shared vision.F1 is undeniably engineered for the masses-it is loud, kinetic, and structurally conventional. Yet, it is precisely within this accessible framework that Kosinski smuggles in a deeply resonant worldview. By demanding physical authenticity, the film reminds us of the beauty of the material world. By focusing on the grueling demands of mentorship, it reminds us that true fulfillment is found in responsibility rather than resentment. It is a thrilling, expertly crafted reminder that true greatness requires us to face our fears, master our craft, and ultimately, drive for something greater than ourselves.

title: A High-Octane Sensory Overload
content: The 2025 F1 movie is a technical triumph that finally captures the terrifying, high-speed reality of Grand Prix racing. By utilizing custom-built cameras inside real cockpits, the film delivers an immersive experience that makes you feel every vibration and gear shift at 200 mph. As an editor, you'll likely marvel at the seamless blend of practical track footage and invisible VFX, creating a sense of speed that CGI alone could never replicate. While the "veteran vs. Rookie" storyline follows a fairly predictable Hollywood circuit, the sheer visceral impact of the sound design-the screaming hybrid V6 engines and the whip of the wind-elevates it into a must-watch spectacle. It's a sleek, polished, and adrenaline-fueled tribute to the sport that prioritizes the roar of the track over complex drama, making it a perfect high-definition experience for any racing fan.

title: Pure Adrenaline on the Big Screen
content: Watching F1 in the cinema was an unforgettable experience. From the moment the film started, it completely pulled me into the world of Formula One racing and never let go. I loved every second of it. The film is simply exceptional, an exhilarating blend of speed, emotion, and cinematic brilliance that feels perfectly designed for the big screen.Brad Pitt delivers an exceptional performance. He brings a calm confidence and emotional depth to the role that makes the character instantly compelling. His presence anchors the film, and he carries the intensity of the racing world effortlessly. Pitt proves once again why he remains one of the most captivating actors working today.The direction is outstanding. Every scene feels carefully crafted, especially the racing sequences. The film captures the raw speed and danger of Formula One in a way that feels immersive and authentic. Instead of feeling like typical racing scenes, the sequences feel visceral and real, as if the audience is right there inside the car.The camera work deserves special praise. It is absolutely stunning. The cinematography places viewers directly in the action, capturing the roaring engines, the blur of the track, and the tension of every turn. The angles, movement, and pacing make the races feel electrifying and incredibly cinematic.What makes the film stand out most is how perfectly everything comes together: the acting, the direction, the visuals, and the energy. Nothing feels out of place. It is rare to watch a film where every element works this well together.In the end, F1 is exactly what a racing film should be, thrilling, emotional, and visually spectacular. Watching it in the cinema made the experience even better. For me, it felt close to perfect, and I can honestly say I enjoyed every single moment of it.

title: Faster please...
content: F1 is a slick, immersive racing film that understands the appeal of Formula 1 goes far beyond speed. While the technical side of the sport is impressive, the movie's real strength lies in how it captures pressure, competition, and the psychological toll of racing at the highest level.Brad Pitt delivers a strong and charismatic performance, bringing experience, restraint, and quiet intensity to his character. Rather than playing the typical action hero, he comes across as someone shaped by victories, failures, and the constant awareness that in Formula 1, everything can change in a fraction of a second.Visually, the film is outstanding. The racing sequences feel authentic, dynamic, and visceral, clearly benefiting from real circuits and a documentary-like approach to speed and sound. The sound design, in particular, adds a lot to the immersion, making the viewer feel inside the cockpit rather than just watching from the stands.The story follows familiar sports-movie beats, but it handles them with enough confidence and craftsmanship to stay engaging. It's not just about winning races, but about teamwork, legacy, and finding purpose in a world where margins are brutally thin.Overall, F1 is a well-crafted, exciting, and emotionally grounded sports drama, elevated by strong performances and impressive technical execution. Even for viewers who are not hardcore Formula 1 fans, it's an entertaining and rewarding ride.

title: Wow, they can still make decent movies in the 2020's
content: First, I'll be honest, 10-20 years ago I'd have rated this movie 7-8. In the last five years, this is in probably the best five movies I've seen. The magic of this movie is it tells a good story, imparts a good message without beating it down your throat. Yep, we got the older white guy and the young black guy and I'll admit, as I first started watching I said to myself, " here we go". To my surprise, the fact of white and black never came into the movie. To tell the truth, I'm conflicted by the ending. I'm half wishing it went the other way. (If you watch you know what I mean). Then there's Kate. Obvious love interest. This day and age, should have been the obvious "Girl Boss". They blew it. Yep, a women and an engineer and she succeeds but it feels real and organic, like yes, a women can do great things and yes, people doubted her because of her sex but it all felt real. Personally I think they did more to push "the message" with this movie than most of the movies they've made in the last five years. Except, what is the message "they" have been trying to impart? Is it "Black, White, Man, Women, it doesn't matter"? Or is it "Divide, Divide, Divide"? If the former, then F1 knocked it out of the park. If the latter, then F1 failed miserably. Great movie.

title: Relentless momentum and technical precision
content: F1 delivers exactly what its subject promises: speed, precision, and sustained forward momentum. The film's pacing is outstanding, mirroring the rhythm of competitive racing and keeping the narrative in constant motion.Visually, the cinematography is controlled rather than flashy. Lighting and composition rarely draw attention to themselves, but they consistently support the storytelling. Race sequences are the clear highlight - dynamic editing, side-by-side POV shots reminiscent of simulation games, and a strong sense of spatial clarity make the action both exciting and readable. Flashbacks are clearly differentiated through color and lighting, helping orient the viewer without overexplaining.Sound design is particularly impressive. The race starts are immersive, the engine notes carry weight, and the controlled use of silence after crashes creates emotional impact. The score remains functional throughout, stepping aside when necessary, though a classical cue after a crash stands out effectively.The screenplay is solid but conventional. The narrative moves forward efficiently, yet leans into familiar genre beats. Themes center on teamwork, rivalry, perseverance, risk, and the tension between age and ambition. While it avoids deeper societal commentary, it remains focused and coherent. Performances are strong, with Brad Pitt bringing credibility to the lead role and the female engineer character providing emotional and strategic grounding.A technically accomplished and highly engaging sports drama that prioritizes momentum and execution over originality - and succeeds on its own terms.

title: An adventure of passion and redemption with a great visual impact
content: Impressive visual effects, surrounded by careful editing, make this film a real visual adventure. The masterful interpretation of Brad Pitt who naturally plays the role of an experienced and fascinating driver with a tormented past. A layered character, whose sarcastic and disillusioned nuances cannot fail to recall in part Quentin Tarantino's iconic Cliff Booth in "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood". Alongside him, Javier Bardem accompanies his colleague with a careful and measured acting performance, capable of restoring pathos in the shared scenes. Kosinski's meticulous direction together with the technical sector work with careful precision to the thousandth of a second per lap, to the rhythm of the sequences, measuring themselves in a cinematographic construction of extraordinary workmanship. The result is a high-impact experience, the viewer ends up perceiving the roll of the engine, the whistle of the turbo, the aerodynamic tension at every downshift: a dialogue between visual and sound language that manages to immortalize on film the emotion of a passion that pulsates at high revs. In some moments, the breath itself seems to synchronize with that of the driver, immersing the viewer in a story where the expressive force of passions and the desire for personal redemption become the real engine of the story.

title: A rare racing film that understands restraint, age, and obsession
content: F1: The Movie succeeds where most sports films fail: it respects the intelligence of its audience and the danger of its subject.This isn't a flashy montage-driven celebration of speed. It's a controlled, mature film about discipline, aging skill, and the quiet obsession required to survive at the highest level of motorsport. The racing sequences are shot with restraint-speed feels heavy, mechanical, and genuinely dangerous, not stylized for spectacle. That realism grounds everything.Brad Pitt's performance works because it avoids hero theatrics. There's a deliberate restraint in how his character carries himself-someone shaped by years of pressure rather than bravado. The film understands that in Formula 1, confidence isn't loud; it's internal and earned.Technically, the sound design and editing are outstanding. Engines don't scream for attention-they intimidate. The camera placement prioritizes immersion over exaggeration, making the audience feel inside the cockpit rather than above it. It's one of the few racing films where silence and buildup are used as effectively as noise.Thematically, the film shines when exploring legacy and relevance. It asks a simple but powerful question: What happens when experience collides with a sport obsessed with youth and data? That tension gives the story weight beyond the track.If the film stumbles, it's only in moments where familiar sports-movie structures briefly surface. A few emotional beats feel slightly guided rather than discovered. Still, these are minor compromises in an otherwise confident film.This isn't Rush, which thrives on rivalry, nor is it pure spectacle. It's closer to a character study set at 300 km/h. Viewers expecting neon energy or exaggerated drama may be disappointed-but those interested in mastery, restraint, and the cost of excellence will find this deeply satisfying.F1: The Movie proves that racing cinema doesn't need to shout to be powerful.

title: Quintessential popcorn flick: Flashy looks but formulaic narrative
content: Words I would use to describe this movie: exciting, beautiful, ostentatious, clichéd, predictable.I didn't bother to watch F1 at the cinemas purely because I had a feeling the movie would be more style than substance and that is precisely how it turned out to be for me. In hindsight it would've been great to watch this in a dark theatre on a large screen with surround sound purely to experience the thrill of the racing scenes, for that reason it's a good popcorn flick.Overall the movie is extremely stylish with gorgeously striking cinematography, beautiful sets and wonderful sound design. It's overly-edited at some points which makes for frustrating viewing. That about sums up the good parts of the movie.The major disappointment is the overall narrative. The first half of the movie is fine albeit clichéd. The second half ramps up the clichés and adds some strange, silly and illogical plot points which steers the movie (no pun intended!) down an overly-dramatic contrived path that ultimately ends up becoming somewhat predictable.Weighing all of the above I give it a 6/10. If you want to watch a quintessential popcorn flick centred on F1 check it out, but if you're keen to watch an exciting F1-centric movie with more depth that really pulls you into the story I would suggest watching Rush (2013) instead. Senna may also be a good watch but I haven't seen it.

content: When going to see this film in the cinema I had no idea what to expect; maybe an Oscar sweeping film with elaborate crashes and an amazing plot and lots of drama, or just a bit of fun with people and cars flying everywhere with some of the best special effects anyone has ever seen. title: A Thrilling and Authentic Formula 1 Experience
content: The film has so much to offer. From a Formula 1 perspective, it feels very realistic - even if it leans slightly into clichés at times. However, with some knowledge of F1, you can clearly see that many situations are inspired by real events from the sport's past, which adds authenticity and depth.It may not be the absolute best in any single cinematic category, but it consistently performs well across many aspects - and that adds up. As an F1 fan, the film gives you a certain cool, electrifying vibe that makes the whole experience even more enjoyable.

title: Pitt Stop
content: Sonny Hayes, a rising star, had a terrible incident in an F1 race. Thirty years later, he races as an independent pilot for the love of driving, not for money or fame. One day, he was contacted by Ruben Cervantes,his long-date friend and manager of a bankrupted F1 team. Ruben's aim is to give visibility and success to his team and to continue in F1 for another three years. Sonny Hayes initially faces some behavioural incompatibilities with his teammate Joshua Pearce, but he has a sympathy for the chief technical director, Kate McKenna. Not directly, that relationship between Sonny and Kate will help all the team to reach the goal.The plot of the film is quite linear with some flashbacks that remind us why Sonny Hayes stopped racing. Personally, I really like the role of Kate (the CTD), because in F1 it isn't so common that this difficult role is covered by a woman. So, we can find along the plot that from the incompatibilities and misunderstandings of the drivers will be born collaboration and strategy. After arguments, incidents and strugglings, will Sonny and Joshua be able to raise their team?

title: A Must-Watch for Motorsport Fans
content: I really loved this movie and I think it is a perfect film for motorsport fans. The racing scenes were breathtaking and full of adrenaline, and they kept me interested from the beginning until the end. The sound, visuals, and atmosphere of Formula 1 were done very well and made the movie feel realistic and exciting. The characters were interesting to follow, and Brad Pitt did a great job in his role. Even though the movie is quite long, it never felt boring. I enjoyed every minute of it and I would definitely recommend everyone to watch this film, especially if you like cars, racing, and action movies.

title: Fine
content: "Formula 1" is really exiting and involving movie with amazing racing scenes. The sounds and quality are great and make you dive into the atmosphere. The plot is simple. An old driver comes back of racing after serious injure to help a young ambitious man and save his team The team work is fine, I like it. However, the main character's lifestyle is ambiguous and cringe. He parties too much, hangs out with different girls, and makes mistakes on the track. He don't care of his health and even other's one. It worth to watch the action, really modern and gripping work, but don't expect a perfect hero.

title: BEST MOVIE EVER!!!!!!!
content: This is one of the best movies I have seen in a long time. The shots were breathtaking, the music was incredible, and the characters were super endearing.I do not understand the hate on the story. I feel like everyone can resonate with an underdog story, even if it's a little predictable. And even then, I was literally on the edge of my seat.And as a woman engineer who has felt like they have to prove themself, it was awesome have those feelings represented in a movie by Kate. Not that you have to be a "woman engineer" to relate to that... everyone has felt that way at some point, right? I thought all the characters had incredible development and were relatable in one way or another.And okay, again, the driving was AWESOME!! Seeing a movie with real visual effects is incredible. This is an underrated movie.

title: Very enjoyable
content: Really fun, really enjoyable movie. The tension of the race is really thick and the stunt work is amazing. It kind of reminds me of the NASCAR version of the Top Gun lol. The romance subplot and the sports trope is kind of cliche tho but it ended really well with the focus on the flow an athlete gets driving. I'm also surprised by the complexity and the strategy behind the sport and now I'm kind of interested in it.
